I have 4♥6♥ and completely missed this flop (8♣ J♠ 2♠). There are no hearts on the board so a flush is impossible, and my only realistic outs are runner-runner or pairing a low card — very low equity. The pot is already ~1000 and there’s no bet to me. Pro opened from MP with two callers preflop and the action checked to me on the flop, which looks like weakness or pot control. I’ll check to keep the pot small and see a free turn. I will fold to any sizeable continuation bet (no strong draws or pair to continue with), and only consider calling very small bets where the pot odds and implied odds justify it. If checked through, I’ll reassess on the turn and only continue with a clear improvement or a very favorable runout.

I have flopped Top Set (JJJ) on a board of 8c-Js-2s. This is a very strong hand, essentially the current nuts. The board texture is somewhat wet with a flush draw (two spades) and some straight connectivity (T9, etc.). With three opponents in the pot, it is risky to slow play, as giving a free card could complete a flush or straight. My goal is to extract value from flush draws, straight draws, and weaker Jx hands (KJ, QJ, JT). A bet of half the pot (500) is a solid size to charge draws while keeping the range of calling hands reasonably wide. Checking would be a mistake as it allows opponents to realize equity for free.

I have Ah5s on a 8c Js 2s board - complete miss. I have only ace high with a single backdoor spade. Pro's c-bet of 500 into 1000 is standard and likely connects with their range (Jx, 88, overpairs). With Elon and Minni still to act behind me and no real equity, floating here is too speculative. Easy fold.

Facing a 500 bet into a 1500 pot (3:1 odds) on a flop of 8c Js 2s with pocket 3s, which has only two outs (~4% equity) against Pro's MP raising range (likely pairs 66+, AJs+, KQs, overpairs/draws that hit this board well: broadway, flush draws, pair+draw combos). Preflop set-mining was correct multiway deep but flop misses completely, and board texture favors opponent's c-bet range heavily (spades flush draw, jack connects with many AX broadway hands). Calling OOP kills playability—no semi-bluff value, reverse implied odds if we hit set on further scary cards (e.g., spade/ace turn), and pot commitment risks escalate in tournament. Raising is a pure bluff with poor equity/fold equity OOP vs Pro's sticky range shown in prior hands (fights pots postflop). Standard fold preserves stack (~114BB deep) for superior spots; no value/draw to continue.

I have 4♥6♥ and completely missed this flop (8♣ J♠ 2♠). Pro opened from MP and now C-bet 500 into a ~1000 pot; calling 500 would require ~25% equity to break even. My actual equity (only small pair outs and backdoor hearts/straights) is well under that vs an MP c-betting range that includes Jx, pairs and spade draws. Out of position, with deep stacks and no real redraws, calling or attempting a bluff-raise is poor EV. Fold and preserve stack for a better spot.

I am in UTG position with Queen-Six offsuit (Qc 6d). This is a very weak hand to play from early position, as it is easily dominated and plays poorly post-flop. With a stack of 81 BB, there is no pressure to make loose plays. The standard and correct play is to fold.
Q3 suited from middle position is too weak to play. Poor hand strength with 4 players yet to act behind me. Easy fold.

I am in the Cutoff with a pocket pair of fives (5d 5h). After the first two players folded, it's standard to open-raise here to pick up the blinds or play the hand with initiative. A 3x big blind raise to 300 is a consistent sizing for this table.

J9o is a profitable call from the button against a cutoff raise, given our deep stacks (114BB) and excellent post-flop position. Flash's raise to 300 (3x BB) suggests a wide range, likely 25-35% of hands, against which J9o has around 40% equity and plays well multi-way or heads-up due to straight potential and dominance over worse broadway. Folding is too tight; 3-betting risks playing out of position OOP if re-raised. SB and BB may call/3bet but our position mitigates that. Pot odds are favorable (calling 300 into 450 for immediate odds of ~1.5:1, improving post-flop). Prioritizing long-term EV over avoiding multiway pots.

Facing a CO open to 300 and a button call, my K♠5♣ is a weak, easily dominated hand with poor post‑flop playability. The pot is 750 and calling 250 (to make 300 total) requires roughly 25% equity to break even; against an opener plus a caller my real equity will be well below that and I'll be out of position to both. A squeeze raise here would be a pure bluff with poor blockers and risks being called or 4‑bet by deeper stacks — not worth it with a comfortable stack. Preserve chips for better spots, so I fold.

We’re getting a strong price (200 more into a pot of 750) and closing the action in the big blind. A7o is too strong to fold here, but not strong enough to profitably bloat the pot out of position versus an opener plus a caller where we’re often dominated by better aces. I’ll take the cheap defend, then play cautiously postflop (prioritizing strong top pairs/two pair+ and folding weak ace-high pairs under pressure).

We flopped top pair but with a weak kicker (A7) in a 3-way pot, out of position versus the preflop raiser and a caller. This is too strong to start folding, but not strong enough to bloat the pot by donk-betting and then facing a raise (where we’re often dominated by better Ax). Checking keeps the pot manageable, lets the raiser c-bet worse hands/bluffs, and we can continue versus reasonable sizing while re-evaluating turn/river.

I am the pre-flop aggressor with pocket fives. The flop comes Ad-6c-8s, which is a very dry board that heavily favors my perceived range as the raiser. An Ace on the board is a classic spot to continuation bet (C-bet) to represent top pair or better. By betting here, I can often take down the pot immediately against opponents who missed the flop. If I check, I allow them to realize equity with overcards or potentially bluff me later. A sizing of slightly less than half the pot (450 into 950) is effective and consistent with a balanced strategy on this texture.

J9o completely whiffs the Ad-6c-8s flop: no pair, no flush draw, and only a marginal gutshot to the 7-10 straight (needing runner-runner essentially for value). Flash's CO raise continuation bet into three players is standard with a wide range including Ax, pairs, sets, and some draws. Pot odds require ~24% equity to call 450 into 1400 (effective pot post-bet 1850), but vs Flash's range J9o has closer to 15-20% heads-up, less multiway if FiveTwo (loose preflop caller) continues. Position on both opponents is a plus, but no improving draws or fold equity for a raise-bluff on this Ace-high board where calls are likely. Deep stacks mean better spots exist; folding preserves stack for premium hands in this tournament phase.

Heads-up now versus the original raiser on a very dry A-6-8 rainbow board. With top pair but a weak kicker (A7), raising mostly gets action from better Ax/sets and folds out the bluffs/worse hands we want to keep in. Flash’s sizing is reasonable, so the best line is to call and keep the pot controlled, then re-evaluate on turns (continue versus small/medium barrels, be cautious versus large multi-street aggression on bad runouts).

We’re still heads-up, out of position, with top pair but a weak kicker (A7) on Ad-6c-8s-Qs. The Qs is a mild overcard to villain’s 8x/6x holdings and also introduces more semi-bluffs (spade draws, Qx turns) that we want to keep in. Leading here mostly folds out the air we beat and risks getting raised by better Ax/sets, bloating the pot when our hand is in a lot of thin/value-catcher territory. Best is to check, let Flash continue bluffing/thin value, and then decide based on sizing and river runout.

The Queen of Spades on the turn is another overcard to my pocket fives. After FiveTwo called my continuation bet on the Ace-high flop, it is likely they have a piece of the board, such as an Ace with a weak kicker, an Eight, or a Six. By checking back here, I control the size of the pot and realize my equity with a small pair that still has some showdown value against missed draws. Betting again would likely only be called by better hands and exposes me to a potential check-raise.

We have top pair with a weak kicker (A7) in a spot where villain’s range contains a lot of better Ax (AT+), and this river (J) also completes some two-card hands that can now value-bet (KT, T9, AJ, QJ). Flash checking back the turn caps his range somewhat, but leading the river is still thin: worse hands that call (some A2–A5, occasional 8x/Qx) are limited, while we risk getting raised by better value and bloating a pot where we’re mostly a bluff-catcher. Checking keeps our range protected, allows Flash to bluff his give-ups, and lets us decide based on sizing if he bets.

The board runout of Ace-Six-Eight-Queen-Jack is quite unfavorable for my pocket fives. After FiveTwo called my continuation bet on the Ace-high flop, it's highly probable they hold at least a pair. My check on the turn signaled weakness or a pot-control line with a marginal hand. By checking the river, I am opting to take the hand to showdown for free. While I likely lose to any Ace, Queen, Jack, Eight, or Six in the opponent's range, betting as a bluff is unlikely to force a fold from those hands given the pot size and my perceived range after the turn check.
2s3h is one of the weakest hands in poker, and I'm in UTG - the worst position. This is a clear fold.

I am in middle position with Jack-Nine offsuit. This is a weak starting hand that is generally outside of a standard opening range for this position in a 6-max game. With a 70BB stack and players behind me yet to act, I should prioritize stronger hands or those with better playability like suited connectors. Folding here preserves my stack for more profitable situations.

Preflop in cutoff with 4h6d offsuit, a complete trash hand with no high card value, poor connectivity, and no suitedness. Deep stacks in a tournament with 150 hands total, no need to play speculative garbage from late position when unopened. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots.

Folded to me on the button with 4♦8♣ — a weak offsuit hand, but I have position and both blinds to act. Opening here to steal is standard; I want fold equity and to avoid a cheap multiway pot where this hand performs poorly. I’ll raise to 300 (3x the BB) to apply pressure, price out some marginal defenders, and take the initiative postflop. Plan: if called, play cautiously with position and c-bet when appropriate; if I face a sizeable 3-bet, I’ll fold this hand as it has poor equity vs a 3-bet range.

Facing a button open to 300 from the small blind, we’re out of position for the entire hand and will often go multiway if we just call (BB gets a great price). 7h5c offsuit has very poor playability, is easily dominated when we make a pair, and has no useful blockers to justify a light 3-bet bluff. Best EV is to let this go and preserve stack for better defend/3-bet candidates.

I hold Pocket Nines (9s 9d) in the Big Blind facing a Button raise from Minni to 300. Minni has been opening frequently (Hands #1, #2, #3, #14) and has shown a tendency to fold to 3-bets (Hand #14). Pocket Nines is a premium hand in a BvB/BvBTN configuration and is well ahead of Minni's wide opening range. While calling is an option to keep the pot small, 3-betting is superior here to punish the wide open, deny equity to random overcards, and potentially take down the pot pre-flop. Being out of position, I should use a larger 3-bet size, typically 3.5x-4x the open. I will raise to 1100 to apply pressure and extract value.

I opened to 300 on the button with 4♦8♣ to steal the blinds, but Pro (BB) responded with a sizable 3‑bet to 1,100. Calling would cost me 800 more into a 1,450 pot, which requires roughly 36% equity (800/(1,450+800)) to justify — far more than 4♦8♣ has versus a typical BB 3‑bet range. A 4‑bet to the minimum (1,900) would be an overcommitment with a very weak hand. I’ll respect the 3‑bet, preserve my deep stack early in the tournament, and fold to this aggression.

I am UTG with Qs Ts, which is a strong suited broadway hand. In a 6-max game, this hand is a standard open-raise from any position. Limping is generally discouraged as it allows players behind to see a cheap flop or put pressure on us. I will raise to 300 (3x the big blind), which has been my standard opening size in previous hands. This allows me to take the initiative and potentially win the blinds or play a controlled pot in position against some opponents, though I will be out of position for the rest of the hand.

65 offsuit is a speculative hand at best, but offsuit and low-rated, it plays poorly against an UTG open-raise range which includes strong pairs, broadway hands, and high aces. Position is middle, so out of position postflop. Pot odds to call 300 into 450 are about 1.5:1, but with poor playability, low equity, and limited implied odds due to hand strength, folding preserves stack for better opportunities in a deep-stack tournament.

Call 300. UTG opened to 300 from an early position so his range is relatively strong and A♣4♦ is vulnerable to domination — I don't want to 3-bet as a bluff and risk a 4‑bet or isolating a stronger hand. The call is small relative to my ~12.7k stack (about 2–3%), and from the cutoff I'll have position on most players who enter the pot, so it's correct to realize equity and see a flop. Plan: fold to significant preflop 3‑bet; postflop, proceed only with a made ace, good draws, or a favorable board texture and otherwise control pot size or fold to heavy pressure.

Facing an UTG open (to 300) and a cutoff flat, 86o is too weak to continue even on the button: we’re dominated often, have poor playability multiway, and the blinds still have good squeeze incentives. Calling needs very favorable postflop conditions and implied odds that offsuit 86 doesn’t reliably realize versus these ranges. No profitable squeeze option with this hand, so we preserve stack and wait for better spots.

I am in the Small Blind with Js9h (J9s). Facing an UTG raise to 300 and a call from the Cutoff, the pot odds are compelling. The pot is currently 750, and it costs me 250 more to call, offering me 3:1 (25%) odds. J9s is a hand that plays well in multi-way pots due to its flush and straight potential. While being out of position against an UTG opener and a caller is a disadvantage, the low price to see a flop and the deep stacks (84 BB effective) justify a call to realize my equity and seek implied odds. A squeeze would be too risky against an UTG range.
72o is the worst hand in poker. Even getting 5:1 pot odds to call 200 into a 1000 pot, this hand has almost no equity and will be difficult to play postflop multiway. Easy fold.

I am in the Small Blind with 9hJh on a Qh3sQc board. This gives me a Jack-high flush draw on a paired board. It is a multi-way pot with the UTG aggressor (Flash) and the Cutoff (Minni). Paired boards are generally better for the pre-flop raiser's range or the caller's broadway range. Leading out (donk betting) here is not optimal as it folds out bluffs and isolates me against trips or strong overpairs. My flush draw has decent equity, but I am out of position. The standard line is to check to the aggressor. If there is a bet, I can evaluate based on the sizing and the players involved, likely continuing with a call given the draw equity.

I have flopped trips with a Ten kicker on a very dry board (Qh 3s Qc). Being the pre-flop aggressor, a continuation bet is expected here. I want to get value from pocket pairs, any 3 that might be in my opponents' ranges, or even Ace-high hands that might call one street. I will bet 400 into the 1000 pot, which is a standard sizing for this texture, keeping my range wide and inducing calls from weaker hands.

The flop (Qh 3s Qc) misses my Ac4d — I have no pair and no real draw. Flash opened UTG preflop so his range is fairly strong, but his flop sizing is very small (400 into the ~1400 pot), which is consistent with a wide c-bet range (some Qx/value hands but also many missed A/K/x and bluffs). Calling 400 is cheap: I only need roughly 18–22% equity to justify it and calling keeps the pot under control while preserving most of my 12.4k stack. Raising here as a bluff is risky because UTG’s range contains many queens and sets and I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position. If I call and face a raise I will fold; if action checks or is small on the turn I’ll re-evaluate (bet or check for pot control, value bet if I pair my Ace). Given the price and table dynamics, a call is the lowest-variance, +EV play to see one more card.

I am in the Small Blind with a Jack-high flush draw (9hJh) on a paired board of Qh-3s-Qc. The UTG player (Flash) bet 400 into a pot of 1000, and the Cutoff (Minni) called. The pot is now 1800, and it costs me 400 to call, offering me 4.5:1 pot odds (requiring ~18% equity). While the paired board introduces the risk of full houses (rendering my flush dead), my flush draw is likely live against a range consisting of trips (Qx), overpairs, or underpairs. With approximately 19% equity to hit the flush on the turn and the potential for implied odds if I hit against a Queen, calling is the correct mathematical play. Raising would be ineffective as I have little fold equity against a bet and a call on this texture.

I am out of position in a multi-way pot with a Jack-high flush draw on a paired board (Qh-3s-Qc-5s). The turn card (5s) does not improve my hand but introduces a second flush draw (spades). The standard play with a draw out of position is to check to the pre-flop aggressor (Flash) who c-bet the flop. Leading out (donk betting) has no merit here as it folds out bluffs and isolates me against strong hands like trips. My goal is to realize my equity (approx. 18-20%) as cheaply as possible or evaluate a bet if one is made. Therefore, checking is the correct action.

I have trip Queens with a Ten kicker on a very dry board (Qh 3s Qc 5s). Given that I have the Queen of spades and the board contains the Queen of hearts and Queen of clubs, there is only one Queen remaining in the deck (Qd). This makes it highly unlikely that either of my two opponents also has a Queen. My goal is to get value from middle pocket pairs (like 66-JJ) or potentially a spade draw that picked up equity on the turn. Since Pro has checked to me, I will continue to lead out. A bet of 1000 into the 2200 pot is a strong value sizing that can still be called by pairs or hands that suspect I am continuation-betting with a wide range on a paired board. Checking might induce a bluff, but with two opponents, it's more reliable to build the pot ourselves when we hold a near-nut hand.

I called the flop to realize equity cheaply, but this turn bet is too strong for my holding. The pot is 3200 and I must call 1000 to continue — I’d need ≈1000/(3200+1000)=≈23.8% equity to break even. With A♣4♦ I only have about 6 clean outs to improve on the river (3 Aces + 3 Fours) → ~6/46 ≈ 13% equity, and even those pairs will often still be behind Flash’s UTG range (Qx, sets, strong Ax). Flash opened UTG and has shown continuous aggression (c-bet on the paired Q-Q-3 flop then another sizable bet on the turn), so his line is weighted toward value; the frequency of bluffs that would justify a call here is low. Raising as a bluff out of position with no fold equity or equity to improve is a poor choice. Save chips for a better spot and fold.

I am facing a turn bet of 1000 into a pot of 2200 on a board of Qh-3s-Qc-5s. I hold 9hJh, giving me a Jack-high flush draw. The pot odds are roughly 24% (1000 to call into a total pot of 4200), but my direct equity to hit the flush on the river is only about 19.6% (9 outs / 46 cards). While I have some implied odds, the board is paired, which significantly discounts the value of my flush draw as the opponent (UTG opener) could already have a full house or draw to one, leaving me drawing dead or second-best. Being out of position against a range that includes strong trips and overpairs, and not receiving the direct price to call, the disciplined play is to fold.

UTG position in a 6-handed game with K7o (offsuit king with weak kicker). This hand is not in standard opening ranges from early position due to poor postflop playability, vulnerability to domination by better kings, and low equity multiway. Deep stacks (111BB) don't change this; folding preserves stack for better spots. No opponent tendencies from history suggest otherwise for this weak hand.

8♠3♣ is one of the weakest starting hands — very poor equity and frequently dominated. I'm in middle position with several players to act behind me (Cutoff, Button, SB, BB), so calling 100 to see a flop puts me out of position in likely multiway pots or invites a defend/3-bet. Raising to 200 as a steal with this hand is unnecessarily risky and low EV against players who defend or squeeze. Preserve my stack and fold.

With two folds ahead, this is a profitable late-position open. J9o has decent connectivity and plays well in position, and we can win the blinds often. I’ll use a small-but-standard CO open to keep risk down versus potentially active blinds and a capable Button, while still applying pressure and building initiative. If faced with a sizable 3-bet, we can comfortably fold this offsuit hand.

I am on the Button with 6c 2s, facing a raise to 250 from the Cutoff (FiveTwo). My hand is extremely weak (6-high, unsuited, disconnected) and is near the very bottom of any starting hand range. Despite having position, there is no justification for calling or raising with this holding against a standard open. The pot odds do not justify playing a hand with such poor equity and playability. Folding is the only correct decision.

I have 3h8h in the small blind facing a cutoff raise to 250. This is a weak suited hand that doesn't play well out of position. 83 suited is below my calling range against a cutoff open - it lacks good high card value and the straight potential is limited. Folding is the disciplined play here.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Five offsuit. FiveTwo, the current chip leader, has raised to 250 from the Cutoff. With the pot at 400 and needing only 150 more to call, I am getting approximately 2.66-to-1 odds. A5o is a standard defending hand in the Big Blind against a late-position open. While not strong enough for a value 3-bet, it has sufficient equity against a wide Cutoff opening range to justify a call and see a flop.

On a paired flop of 6h-6s-7c, the standard procedure from the Big Blind is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. Although this board texture can favor the Big Blind's range (which contains more 6x combinations like 86s or 76s), Ace-high remains a strong enough hand to check-call against a standard continuation bet, but not strong enough to lead out with. I will check and see how FiveTwo responds, keeping the pot manageable with my marginal showdown value.

Heads-up as the preflop raiser with position on a low, paired board (6-6-7). This texture is somewhat sticky for the BB, but we still benefit from range/initiative (overpairs and strong broadways) and can fold out a chunk of his air/overcards with a small c-bet while keeping the pot controlled. Jh9c has a little backdoor straight potential (T/8 runouts), and if we get check-raised we can comfortably fold. I’ll use a small sizing to deny equity and maintain pressure without bloating the pot versus trips/7x.

On a paired board of 6h-6s-7c, my Ace-high (Ah5s) remains a relatively strong hand with significant showdown value. FiveTwo's small continuation bet of 200 into a 550 pot (roughly 36%) is a common sizing with both strong hands and air. Given the pot odds of 3.75-to-1, folding is too conservative as I likely still have the best hand or at least enough equity against his range. Raising would likely force out hands I currently beat and keep me in against hands that have me crushed (like 7x or pocket pairs). Therefore, calling is the most profitable line to see a turn and evaluate based on further action.

The turn brings the Jack of Spades, which is an overcard to the 7 but does not significantly improve my hand or drastically change the board texture for my current holding. As the defender in the Big Blind, I will continue to check to the pre-flop aggressor. Leading here would rarely be for value and would likely be turned into a bluff-catcher or forced to fold against a raise. Checking allows me to see how FiveTwo reacts and preserves the opportunity to reach showdown cheaply if he checks back.

Turn brings the Js giving us top pair with Jh9c on a paired (6-6-7-J) board. BB’s flop check-call range contains plenty of 7x, pocket pairs (88-TT), some Jx/overcards, and occasional floats that we can charge. We should bet for value and protection while keeping the pot controlled against the portion that has 6x (which can trap) and being ready to fold versus a big check-raise. A medium sizing targets calls from worse and denies equity without committing us.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Five offsuit. The pre-flop action was a standard defense against a Cutoff raise. On the flop of 6h-6s-7c, I called a small continuation bet with Ace-high, which had reasonable equity and showdown value on such a dry, paired board. However, the Jack of Spades on the turn is a significant scare card. It fits well into the Cutoff's opening range (containing hands like AJ, KJ, QJ, and JTs) and gives him a second barrel opportunity. FiveTwo is the chip leader and has shown a willingness to be aggressive. By betting 500 into 950, he is putting pressure on my marginal holdings. My Ace-high now loses to any pair, any 7, any 6, and any Jack. While I am getting nearly 3-to-1 odds, calling here would likely lead to a difficult river decision where I would be forced to fold to almost any further aggression. Given that I have no draw and only three likely outs (the remaining Aces) which might not even be good if he has a 6 or a 7, folding is the most prudent and disciplined play to preserve my stack.

I’m UTG with 5♠7♥ offsuit — a weak, unconnected, unsuited hand with five players to act behind. Although stacks are deep, this hand plays terribly out of position and will be dominated or crushed by typical calling/3‑betting ranges (Elon and FiveTwo both have deep stacks and can 3‑bet or call). The cost to see the flop is 100 or a standard open to 200; neither is sensible here. Fold and wait for a better spot in position or with a playable hand.

Unopened pot in middle position with Q4o is too weak and dominated by the ranges that will continue (especially CO/BTN and the blinds). Limping invites isolation and forces us to play a marginal offsuit queen out of position on later streets with poor playability. Best EV is to preserve chips and wait for a better opening spot.

I am in the Cutoff with 5c 4s, a very weak offsuit connector. With three players yet to act, including the Button and the Blinds, the likelihood of stealing the blinds is not high enough to justify opening with such low equity. The standard play is to fold.

On the button with 92o - one of the worst hands in poker. While button is a great stealing position with 3 folds in front, 92o is below my opening range even here. Elon has shown willingness to 3-bet (hands 4, 5, 10), so I risk getting raised if I try to steal. Not worth it with this trash hand.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Six suited. Everyone has folded to me, leaving only the Big Blind (Elon) to act. In a blind-versus-blind situation, J6s is a reasonable hand to open for a raise to try and steal the blinds or build a pot with a hand that has some post-flop potential (suitedness and high-card value). I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the Big Blind.

Preflop in the big blind facing a standard small blind raise to 300 (200 to call into 400). My hand is T6o, which is a weak offsuit connector with poor playability out of position. Against a typical small blind opening range (wide, around 40-50% of hands), T6o has roughly 32-35% equity heads-up but struggles postflop OOP due to reverse implied odds and difficulty realizing equity. Pot odds are about 2:1 (needing ~33% equity), but folding preserves stack for better spots in this multi-way tournament (hand 22/150, stacks deep ~100BB). No history suggests Flash is folding to 3-bets often enough to bluff, and value 3-betting range doesn't include this. Standard fold to avoid spew.

We’re 6-handed UTG with AKo suited (AdKd), a premium that wants to build the pot and deny equity to the blinds. Opening is mandatory; limping wastes value and invites multiway play. A 2.5x open is a solid size in this lineup (others have been using 250–300), keeps our range protected, and sets up clean 3-bet/4-bet decisions versus aggressive players on the Button/CO.

I am holding 3d 2h (3-2 offsuit) in Middle Position facing a raise from UTG (FiveTwo) to 250. This is statistically the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Facing an UTG open, which typically indicates a strong range, I have negligible equity. The pot odds do not justify playing this hand under any circumstances. The standard and only correct play is to fold immediately to conserve chips.
K4o is too weak to call or 3-bet against a UTG raise. UTG ranges are typically strong, and K4o has poor playability post-flop. Easy fold.

I am on the button with Jack-Trey suited. FiveTwo has opened from under-the-gun for 250. While J3s is a suited hand, it is very weak and easily dominated by an early position raising range. Given the raise comes from UTG, I expect a range consisting of strong pairs, big aces, and high broadway cards. Calling here would put me in a difficult spot post-flop, and I don't want to bloat a pot with a marginal hand against a potentially strong range. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here.

Facing an UTG raise to 250 (2.5BB) from FiveTwo, who has shown aggression but UTG suggests a strong range. My hand is 4s9s, suited connectors but low and gappy, with poor playability out of position in the SB. Pot odds to call 200 into 400 are about 3:1, but implied odds are limited OOP against a strong range. Folding preserves stack for better spots; no reason to speculate here with 100BB effective stacks.

Facing an early-position open to 250 from FiveTwo and I'm in the big blind with 8♥T♣ offsuit. Calling 150 into a 400 pot requires ~27% equity to break even; out-of-position against an EP opening range my hand is frequently dominated and has poor playability. A small 3-bet bluff is possible in some spots, but versus an UTG opener it's a marginal, higher-variance line I don't need to take this stage of the tournament. I'll fold and preserve stack for better spots in position or with stronger holdings.

I am in UTG position with 3d 4c, which is a very weak unsuited hand. With 77.5 big blinds, there is absolutely no reason to play this hand from early position. The correct play is to fold immediately.

Ks9s is a decent suited hand in middle position. With 80 BBs effective, this is a standard open-raise hand. Good playability with flush potential and some straight possibilities. Opening to 2.5x (250) is standard.

Claude raised from Middle Position to 250. I am in the Cutoff with Queen-Eight suited. While this is a decent suited hand, it is marginal against an opening raise from a player in a middle position. Calling risks being squeezed by the Button or the blinds, or being dominated by Claude's opening range (which likely includes stronger Queens and pocket pairs). Given the current tournament stage and the fact that I want to preserve my stack for more advantageous spots, folding is a disciplined and safe play here.

Preflop on the button with 2c4d, a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability, low equity against a middle position raise from Claude, who has shown aggression in prior hands. Pot odds of calling 250 into 400 require too much equity (~38%), but 24o has minimal showdown value and weak postflop potential even with position. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots in this multi-table dynamic.

Fold. In the small blind with 8s4h — a very weak, unsuited, disconnected hand that performs terribly out of position. Claude opened to 250 from middle, which is a reasonably strong opening range here, and calling 200 into the 400 pot would require ~25% equity (200 to win ~600). 8-4o will be dominated vs that range and loses a lot of equity multiway if the BB defends or 3-bets. With a healthy stack (~118 BB) there’s no reason to invest chips in a marginal, OOP spot — preserve the stack and wait for a better opportunity.

Claude opens to a small 2.5x and we’re getting a good price from the big blind (150 to win 400). J9o has enough equity and playability to defend here, especially closing the action preflop. A 3-bet would bloat the pot with a dominated, offsuit hand versus a likely tighter MP range, so calling is the higher-EV, lower-variance line.

Out of position as the BB defender on a low, connected flop (2-6-5 rainbow), this board generally hits my range better than Claude’s MP opening range. With J9o I mostly have two overcards plus a backdoor club draw, but no real made hand or strong draw worth building the pot immediately. Checking keeps my range protected (I can have sets/straights here), induces c-bets from overcards, and lets me evaluate based on sizing—continuing only versus smaller bets where my overcards/backdoors have a reasonable price.

I completely missed this 2-6-5 rainbow board with Ks9s - just backdoor spade draw. However, as the preflop aggressor, this dry low board is good for a continuation bet. FiveTwo defended from BB and likely missed too. A small c-bet around 1/3 pot should take this down frequently.

Claude’s flop c-bet is very small (175 into 725), giving us an excellent price. With J9o we have two overcards plus a backdoor club draw (Jc) and some backdoor straight potential, and this low/connected board also favors the BB defend range enough that we can profitably float versus this sizing. Raising would be higher variance and J9o isn’t an ideal check-raise candidate; we can realize equity and reassess on the turn, continuing on good cards or versus continued small sizing and folding to larger pressure on bricks.

We improve to top pair on the turn, but with a weak kicker (J9) and out of position versus Claude’s MP range that still contains overpairs (QQ+), better Jx (AJ/KJ/QJ), and some slow-played strong hands, betting ourselves risks getting raised off our equity or bloating a pot where we’re often not ahead of his continuing range.

Checking keeps the pot controlled, allows Claude to continue bluffing with his overcards that took the small flop stab, and lets us respond based on sizing (comfortably calling reasonable bets, but avoiding getting stacked versus heavy pressure). We can also value/protect on many rivers if he checks back.

Turn brought a Jack which is a reasonable card for my range as preflop aggressor. However, I have complete air - just K-high with a very weak backdoor spade draw (need runner-runner). FiveTwo called my flop c-bet, showing some interest in the pot. Double-barreling here as a pure bluff into a calling station is risky. Better to check back, take a free card, and potentially hit a K, 9, or running spades on the river. Pot control with no made hand or strong draw.

River goes check-to-me after Claude’s tiny flop c-bet and turn check-back. With J9 we have top pair but a weak kicker; still, this is very likely best versus his give-up range (Ax/Kx overcards, some Tx, and pocket pairs 77–99 that took one stab and then shut down). Checking will often just end the hand with him checking back, while a small block/value bet can get called by worse (Tx, 77–99, some A-high sticky hands) and also sets a price so we can comfortably fold to a raise. I’ll bet small for thin value and to avoid facing a tougher-sized decision.

I have K-high on 2s6d5cJdTc. FiveTwo called preflop, called my flop c-bet, checked turn, and now leads 300 (1/3 pot) on river. This line suggests they have showdown value - likely hit a pair somewhere (J, T, or had a small pair). With just K-high, I only beat pure bluffs. The small river bet sizing often indicates thin value rather than a bluff. I need 25% equity to call but K-high has near-zero showdown value here. Easy fold.

68 suited is a speculative hand that plays poorly from UTG in a 6-handed game. While it has some playability with straight and flush potential, opening from first position with this hand is -EV. Too many players left to act behind me, and I'll often face 3-bets or be out of position postflop. Standard fold.

I am in a middle position with Queen-Four offsuit. This is a very weak starting hand that does not play well in multi-way pots or against any significant resistance. Since Claude has already folded and I have five other players behind me, including those in the blinds, it is most prudent to fold this hand and wait for a more favorable opportunity.

9c3s is a complete trash hand with no connectivity, suitedness, or high card strength. In cutoff position with folds from UTG and middle position, I have a stealing opportunity in theory, but this hand has zero post-flop playability, especially multiway with aggressive opponents like Minni on the button and blinds likely to defend. Folding preserves my deep 10950 stack (109BB effective) for premium spots to maximize long-term EV in this 150-hand tournament.

I’m on the button with Jc4c — suited junk but a reasonable candidate for a button steal. Only the SB (FiveTwo) and BB (Pro) remain to act; with 118 BB effective I can open a wide range to apply pressure and take the blinds. I’ll raise to 300 (≈3x BB) to maximize fold equity while keeping the size practical. Postflop I’ll use position to c-bet heads-up on dry boards and play cautiously if it’s multiway. If I face a big 3-bet I’ll fold this hand; I’ll only continue versus small, single-opponent 3-bets with good pot odds.

Button open from Minni is likely wide, but Q7o is a dominated, low-realization hand from the small blind with the big blind still to act. Calling invites tough OOP multiway spots and poor postflop playability; 3-bet bluff candidates are better chosen with stronger blockers (Axs/Kx) or suited hands. Best EV is to surrender the small blind and wait for a better defend/3-bet spot.

I am in the Big Blind with Ks 5h. Minni has raised to 300 (3bb) from the Button. While I am getting decent pot odds (calling 200 to win 650), K5o is on the very bottom edge of a defending range against a Button open, especially against a larger 3x sizing. Being out of position with a weak offsuit King makes it difficult to realize equity and prone to domination or reverse implied odds if I hit top pair. Given the larger raise size compared to a standard min-raise, folding is the prudent play to preserve chips.

I am holding Ten-Three offsuit in the Under The Gun (UTG) position. This is one of the weakest starting hands in poker, particularly from the earliest position at a six-handed table. With five players still to act behind me, including several with larger stacks, there is no strategic reason to enter the pot with this hand. Standard professional play is to fold and wait for a more favorable opportunity.

4h5s is a suited connector with excellent playability, potential for straights and flush draws. Deep effective stacks (around 75-130BB) justify speculative hands from middle position after UTG fold. This acts as an opening raise. Opponents show loose tendencies from history (frequent limps/calls), so a min-raise to 200 builds the pot modestly while taking initiative, folding out junk behind, and allowing postflop flexibility. Folding too nitty early in tournament; calling (limp) invites multiway pots without initiative.

Elon opened to 200 from MP and I’m in the cutoff with 6♠7♥ offsuit. This is a marginal, non‑suited connector facing an early position open with the button and blinds left to act. Calling 200 into a 350 pot requires ~36% equity to break even (200/(350+200)); against a typical MP opening range — and with the real possibility of a multiway pot if the button or blinds come along — my equity and implied odds are poor. A tiny raise to 300 won’t credibly fold many better hands and a larger 3‑bet is too loose with this holding. I’ll preserve my ~120bb stack and wait for a better spot. Fold.

Elon’s 2x open from middle position is likely fairly wide, and we have the button with position and a hand that can realize equity well enough in a smaller pot. K8o isn’t strong enough to 3-bet for value, and as a bluff 3-bet it performs poorly when called (dominated king highs, awkward postflop), so I prefer keeping ranges wide and leveraging position via a flat. Blinds haven’t shown a strong squeeze tendency, so calling is higher EV than turning this into a marginal bluff 3-bet.

I hold 8h 3s in the Small Blind, which is a very weak off-suit hand. Facing a raise from Elon and a call from FiveTwo, I am out of position against two opponents. Although the price to call is cheap (150 chips into a pot of 550), the hand has very poor playability and equity. Playing trash hands from the worst position is a long-term losing strategy. I will fold.

I have KK in the BB - a premium hand. Elon min-raised to 200 and FiveTwo called. This is an obvious 3-bet for value. I want to build the pot while still getting action. A raise to around 800 is a good size - roughly 3.5-4x the original raise with a caller in the pot.

Facing a 3-bet to 800 from BB Claude after my MP min-open to 200 and button FiveTwo's flat call. 4h5s is a speculative suited connector with good playability in position multiway, but this spot is poor: out of position postflop if I call (button and BB behind), facing a BB 3-bet range that includes strong hands and bluffs but crushes my equity (low cards, vulnerable to domination). Pot odds ~3:1 (600 to 1850), but implied odds diminished multiway vs aggro BB and loose button caller. Folding preserves deep stack (107BB) for better spots; shoving too aggressive (~13BB, over 10% stack), raising risks escalation with weak hand. History shows Claude 3-bets light sometimes but still profitable fold.

Claude’s cold 3-bet from the BB versus an MP open and my flat is typically a fairly tight, value-heavy range (lots of TT+/AQ+). Calling creates a low SPR (~3) 3-bet pot where K8s realizes poorly: we’ll miss often, face frequent cbets, and when we make a king we’re dominated too often by better Kx. The pot odds (600 to win 1250) aren’t enough given reverse implied odds and the tight range. 4-bet bluffing is also unattractive with weak blockers and poor playability when jammed on. Best is to conserve chips and fold.

Pocket Queens (QQ) is a premium hand, one of the strongest starting hands in No-Limit Hold'em. As UTG in a 6-handed game with 107BB effective stack, standard play is to raise for value, to build the pot, and to isolate against weaker ranges from later positions. Opponents have shown aggression in previous hands (e.g., frequent 2.5-3x raises like 250-300), so raising to 3x the big blind (300) is appropriate sizing: large enough to thin the field but not so big as to commit too deeply preflop. Limping or calling is too passive and invites multiway pots; folding is incorrect.

Pocket jacks are a strong, value-heavy hand facing an UTG open to 300 in a 6-handed game. Calling 300 keeps the pot multiway and allows players behind to come along, which hurts JJ equity against overcards. I want to isolate Elon and build a pot while still keeping postflop maneuverability with deep stacks (~120bb). A 3-bet to 1,000 total is large enough to take down the pot preflop or go heads-up, but not so large that I overcommit or immediately force a shove decision against a polarized 4-bet. Plan: fold to a very large 4-bet/shove (polarized range), but continue postflop against a call — bet for value and protection on favorable boards, and proceed carefully on heavy overcard flops.

K4o is a dominated, low-playability hand versus an UTG open and a strong-looking MP 3-bet. Calling 1000 invites reverse implied odds multiway/against tight ranges, and turning it into a 4-bet bluff is too thin here (poor equity when called, likely strong continue ranges). Preserve stack and wait for better 3-bet/4-bet candidates.

I hold 6h 8s on the button. The action started with an UTG raise from Elon to 300, followed by a 3-bet from Minni in middle position to 1000. My hand is an unsuited one-gapper with very low equity against strong opening and 3-betting ranges. Calling a large 3-bet cold with this hand is extremely -EV, and bluffing is ill-advised against strength from early positions. The only standard play is to fold.

Holding 88 facing a UTG open and a 3-bet to 1000 from Minni. Pocket 8s is a decent hand for set mining. I'm getting about 2.5:1 on a call (950 to win ~2400). Being OOP is suboptimal, but with 80BB effective stacks, implied odds are reasonable if I hit a set. Folding is too tight with this holding, and 4-betting would commit too many chips with a hand that doesn't play well against their 3-bet calling/shoving ranges. Calling to set mine is the play.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten of Clubs and Four of Hearts, which is an extremely weak hand. There has been an UTG raise to 300 from Elon, followed by a 3-bet to 1000 from Minni and a cold-call from Claude in the Small Blind. Given the significant strength shown by three opponents and the fact that my hand has almost no playability or equity against their likely ranges (which probably consist of high pairs and strong broadway cards), there is no reason to put any more chips into this pot. I will fold.

QQ remains one of the best hands in poker, crushing typical 3-bet ranges from Minni (who has shown frequent aggression, e.g., light 3-bets in hands like #7, #14) and cold-calling ranges from Claude OOP in SB (which can include speculative hands but QQ dominates). Deep stacks (~100BB) make 4-betting ideal for value, building the pot, isolating against the field, and applying fold equity preflop. Calling 700 leads to a bloated multiway pot OOP with postflop SPR too low for QQ's equity realization. Folding is a massive mistake with this premium. Standard sizing is ~3x the 3-bet to 3000 total, pressuring Minni's range while leaving room for further action. This maximizes EV over tournament.

I 3-bet to isolate with JJ and Elon’s 4-bet to 3,000 is big but not an all-in or an absolute polarization shove. UTG 4-bet range is weighted to AA/KK/QQ/AK — JJ is strong enough to continue but I don’t want to commit my whole stack here. Calling 2,000 more (3000 total) gives ~28% immediate pot odds (2000 into a ~5100 pot → need ~28% equity) and preserves postflop maneuverability. Claude may fold to the 4-bet, leaving me heads-up (good), or he may call and make the pot multiway (in which case I’ll play more cautiously). Plan: call to see a flop; on low/J-good flops bet for value/protection, on heavy overcard flops check and surrender to large aggression, and fold to a shove unless I improve. A 5-bet shove here risks flipping with AA/KK and is unnecessarily commit-heavy given stack depths and reads, so I’ll call 3000 total.

Facing a 4-bet from Elon to 3000 and a flat from Minni. I've already invested 1000 and need 2000 more to continue. Pot is 7100, giving me 3.5:1 pot odds. With 88, I'm almost certainly behind both players' ranges here - likely facing bigger pairs or AK type hands. My set mining odds are ~12%, but the implied odds are massive - if I hit a set, I'm likely to stack at least one opponent given the pot size and their commitment level. The effective SPR post-flop would be tiny if I call, making set realization very profitable. Given the pot size and implied odds when I hit, this is a profitable set mine spot despite being behind most of the time.

I'm in a 4-bet pot with 88 on K92 rainbow. I completely whiffed - no set. In a 4-bet pot, Elon's range is heavily weighted toward AA, KK, QQ, and AK. Minni flatted the 4-bet cold, suggesting a similar strong range. On this K-high board, any overpair beats me, and AK just hit top pair. I have almost no equity here unless I catch an 8. With no money invested this street and being first to act, I should check and fold to any significant bet. There's no value in betting and I'd only get called/raised by better hands.

Pocket Queens remains very strong on this extremely dry flop (9c Kd 2h): rainbow board with no flush draws, minimal straight draws (gutshot to T only), and low coordination. My UTG 4-bet range (QQ+, AK) crushes typical calling ranges of Minni (aggressive 3-bettor who flat-called here, likely AK/AQ/KQ/KK/AA with some suited broadways) and especially Claude's OOP cold-call range from SB (trappy/speculative like suited connectors/AJs but dominated here). The King-high board hits some of their value but I beat all AxKx, QJ+, lower pairs, and draws. C-betting as preflop aggressor builds the pot for value against worse (AK often calls 1/2 pot), protects against runner-runner draws (e.g., clubs, runner-runner straight), and denies equity to floats/bluffs. Checking invites cheap turn cards and multiway pot where QQ realizes equity worse. Sizing 1/2 pot (4500) is standard: large enough for value/thin value/protection without overbetting into nuts (KK/AA/set rare and may raise). Pot odds poor for calls with marginal hands. Deep effective stacks (~8BB pot-to-stack) allow flexibility for turns/rivers. History shows opponents call stations (e.g., Claude calling big bets), so extract max value.

I 3-bet preflop with JJ and called Elon's 4-bet to see a flop, but K♦9♣2♥ is a bad texture for my hand: the king is an overcard and squarely favors Elon's very tight UTG 4‑bet/continuation range (AA/KK/QQ/AK). Elon leads 4,500 into a 13,600 pot — calling requires roughly ~25% equity, which JJ does not have vs that polarized range (likely single‑digit to low‑teens equity). If Claude comes along it only gets worse (multiway). Raising would be overly committal and unlikely to fold out the hands I lose to. I conserve chips for a better spot and fold here.

In a 4-bet pot with 88 on K92 rainbow, I completely missed my set. Elon's 4-bet range is heavily weighted toward AA, KK, QQ, AK. He just bet 4500 into 9100 (about half pot). I need to call 4500 to win 18100, giving me ~4:1 odds (need ~20% equity). With my underpair, I have only 2 outs (~8% by river) against his overpairs. Even against AK specifically I'm only slightly ahead. Given his range is mostly overpairs that crush me, this is a clear fold. I can't profitably continue here.

UTG in a 6-handed game with 6♣4♣ — a weak, low suited connector. Out of position with five players to act, this hand is often dominated and performs poorly multiway. Calling 100 or opening to 200 commits chips with very little equity and opens me up to 3-bets from big stacks (e.g. Elon, FiveTwo, Pro). With ~90 BB effective I can wait for much stronger opening spots in position. Opt to fold and preserve the stack for better opportunities.

We’re effectively first-in from MP (no raise yet) with J7o, a dominated, low-playability hand. With four players behind (including aggressive/3-bet capable stacks) and no antes, the immediate steal incentive is small while the downside (getting called/3-bet and playing marginal spots) is meaningful. Best EV is to preserve stack and wait for better open candidates.

I am in the Cutoff with AcTs (ATo). The action has folded to me. ATo is a strong enough hand to open-raise from late position to steal the blinds or play a pot in position. My stack is healthy (76 BB). The Button has 50 BB, and the blinds are deep. Raising to 2.5x (250) is a standard sizing to put pressure on the remaining players without over-committing.

I have 97o on the button facing a min-raise from Pro in the cutoff. This is a marginal hand but I have position. Given my position advantage, I can call to see a flop with this connected hand that can make straights. Folding is also reasonable but I'll take the flop in position.

I am in the Small Blind with 2h 5c, which is a very weak hand. A player in the cutoff (Pro) has raised to 2.5x, and the button (Claude) has called. Calling from the small blind with such a poor hand out of position is a recipe for losing chips, and there is no merit to a squeeze bluff here given my stack size and the hand strength. Folding is the only sensible play.

43o is an extremely weak hand with low card value, no flush potential, and limited straight potential. Pro raised from cutoff (likely wide but still decent), Claude called from button (could be speculative). Facing a potential multiway pot, my equity is poor against their combined ranges. Pot odds are ~4.3:1 (150 to call into 650), requiring ~19% equity, but 43o likely has <15% vs raiser+caller. Deep stack but no reason to speculate with trash. Standard BB defense doesn't include this. Fold to preserve stack.

I am playing out of position with Ace-high (ATo) on a dry Q-5-4 rainbow board. While I have the range advantage as the pre-flop raiser, the Queen connects well with the Button's calling range (KQ, QJ, QTs). C-betting would likely only fold out hands that have very little equity against me anyway, while better hands (pairs, Qx) will call, leaving me in a difficult spot on later streets. Checking allows me to exercise pot control, potentially realize my equity for free if the opponent checks back, or easily fold if faced with aggression without committing more chips.

I have 97o on a Q54 rainbow board. I completely missed - no pair, just a weak gutshot (need a 6 for a straight). Pro was the preflop raiser and checked to me. While I could stab here as a bluff, Pro could easily have a Q or be trapping. With minimal equity and the original raiser showing interest by just checking (possibly slow-playing), I'll take the free card to try to improve on the turn.

I hold Ace-Ten offsuit (Ace-high) on a board of 5d-Qh-4s-7c. The pot is 650. I checked the flop and my opponent (Claude) checked back. Claude's check-back on the flop suggests he likely does not have a strong Queen or better, and his range is capped to weak Queens, middle/low pairs, or air. On the turn, the 7c is a relatively blank card, though it brings some straight connectivity. Betting here turns my hand into a bluff, as worse hands (King-high, Jack-high) will fold, and better hands (any pair) will likely call given Claude's tendency to call down with pairs (observed in Hand #8). My Ace-high has showdown value against his missed broadway hands. Therefore, checking is the superior option to control the pot size and try to reach showdown or improve on the river without bloating the pot with a marginal hand.

I turned a pair of 7s on a Q547 board. Pro has checked twice now, showing weakness. With middle pair and position, I should bet for value and protection. A bet of around half-pot will extract value from weaker pairs and draws while defining my hand.

I hold Ace-Ten offsuit and have checked twice (flop and turn). The board is Q-5-4-7. After checking back the flop, Claude bets half-pot on the turn. His range likely consists of marginal value hands like a weak Queen, a pair of 7s/5s/4s, or pocket pairs (66-JJ) that are protecting against overcards. While he could be stabbing with a bluff, I only have Ace-high with no significant draw (only 6 outs to a pair on the river). The price of 325 chips requires ~25% equity, which I likely do not have against his value range, and playing Ace-high out of position on the river is difficult. Preserving my stack of ~73 BB is the disciplined choice.

UTG 6-max with no antes, opening ranges should be fairly tight because we’ll face multiple players behind and a lot of 3-betting/defending. K9o is too dominated (runs into KTx/KJ+/A9+/pairs) and doesn’t realize equity well out of position, so the EV of opening/limping is poor compared to simply preserving chips for better spots.

I am in middle position with Queen-Three offsuit (Qh 3d), which is a very weak hand. With four players left to act behind me, including the short stack on the cutoff and the chip leader in the small blind, opening with this hand is not profitable. My stack is healthy at ~73 big blinds, so there is no pressure to play marginal holdings. The standard play is to fold.

K7o in cutoff with two folds ahead. This is a standard open-raise spot - K7o plays well from late position with only 3 players to act. I'll make a standard 2.5x raise to steal blinds or play in position post-flop.

I am on the button with 6s 5s, a strong suited connector that plays very well in position. Claude raised to 2.5x from the cutoff. This is a standard opening range. While I could 3-bet as a bluff, calling allows me to see a flop with a hand that has great implied odds and gives me the advantage of acting last on every subsequent street. Folding would be too conservative for this hand in this position.

Preflop in SB with 5c7h offsuit, a weak speculative hand at best. Claude raised from cutoff (250), Flash called from button, building a pot to 650. To call 200 OOP gives about 3.25:1 odds, but this hand has poor playability, low equity against a likely decent range from raiser and caller. No strong draws or pair potential justifies it OOP. Deep stack (167x BB), but folding trash preserves chips for premium spots. Standard fold.

I'm on the big blind with Ac 2s facing a cutoff open to 250 and the button calling. Pot is 650 and I need 150 more to call (250 total). A2o is marginal multiway and I'm out of position against both opponents, so a squeeze/3-bet bluff is unattractive — they can call or shove and I risk playing a bloated pot OOP with a weak hand. The call is cheap: 150 into 650 (~19% break-even equity) and with deep stacks I can realize equity or fold to aggression postflop. Plan: call to see a flop, continue only with an ace, strong draws, or favorable texture; otherwise check-fold to significant bets. Action selected to preserve chips and keep options postflop.

I have A♣2♠ on J♠–7♣–8♥: I missed the flop but hold the nut ace. This is a three-way pot (CO opened, BTN called) and I'm out of position vs both; I have no pair and no realistic draw, so leading OOP with a bare ace has poor fold equity and invites raises or calls from hands that already beat me (Jx, 8x, 7x, T9/9T draws, etc.). I’ll check to control the pot and realize equity rather than bloating the pot unnecessarily. Plan: call small continuation bets (roughly ≤1/3 pot ≈ 250–300) to see a turn, fold to large bets/raises if I don’t improve, and only become more aggressive if I hit an ace or pick up a strong draw on later streets.

I flopped middle pair (7s) with king kicker on Js-7c-8h. Board is somewhat connected with straight draws possible. Minni checked to me. I should make a small c-bet to get value from worse hands and protect against overcards. A 1/3 pot bet around 250-300 is appropriate.

I have flopped an open-ended straight draw (needing a 9 or a 4) on a board of Js-7c-8h. Claude, the pre-flop aggressor, has followed up with a continuation bet of 275 into a pot of 800. My 6s-5s also provides a backdoor flush draw. Calling is the most profitable play here; the pot odds are excellent (275 to win 1075), and I am in position, allowing me to see how the turn develops and potentially extract more value if I hit my straight or take the pot away if Claude checks the turn. Raising is an alternative but is less necessary given I have a clear draw and the button advantage.

Call 275. I'm out of position in a three‑way pot with A♣2♠ on J♠–7♣–8♥ and I missed the flop, but the c-bet of 275 is small (cheap to call). My preflop plan was to call small continuation bets (~≤1/3 pot) to see a turn — this fits that plan. Calling preserves chips, keeps the pot manageable OOP, and gives me a chance to improve to an ace or pick up a better read on turn; I’ll fold to significant turn aggression unless I hit.

Check. I'm in a three‑way pot out of position with A♣2♠ on J♠–7♣–8♥–4♠. I have the nut ace but no pair and no realistic draw, so leading OOP against two callers has poor fold equity and will frequently get called or raised by hands that already beat me (Jx, 8x, pairs, made straights). Pot control is the priority (pot ~1625, my stack 8450). I’ll check to see the river for a cheap card and only continue if I pair an ace or face a very small bet; I’ll fold to significant turn/river aggression unless I improve.

Turn brings 4s which is a blank. I have middle pair with K kicker. Two opponents called my flop bet, suggesting at least one likely has Jx or a draw. With a medium-strength hand in a multiway pot, checking back for pot control is optimal. I can call a reasonable river bet or value bet if checked to again on a safe river.

I have turned a straight (4-5-6-7-8). While T9 is a possible higher straight (7-8-9-10-J), my hand is exceptionally strong given the action. Both Minni and Claude checked to me, indicating they likely don't have the current nuts. I also have a flush draw for additional equity if I am somehow behind or if the river changes the dynamic. I will bet 1000 into the 1625 pot to build value against Jack-x hands, sets, or two-pair combinations that might be inclined to call.

I checked the turn with A♣2♠ as planned and said I'd fold to significant turn aggression. Flash's 1,000‑chip bet into ~2,625 is exactly that: calling 1,000 to win ~3,625 requires ~27.6% equity, but I have only Ace‑high with ~3 outs to pair the ace (~6.5%). I'm out of position and this is a multiway pot against ranges that include Jx, 8x, 7x, two‑pair and sets that already beat me. A raise (min to 2,000) would be an expensive bluff with poor fold equity OOP and would risk a large portion of my 8,450 stack. Preserving chips and waiting for a better spot is the +EV play here, so I will fold.

Flash called my flop c-bet and now leads 1000 when I checked the turn showing weakness. This is a strong line - likely Jx for value, a straight draw (T9 makes a straight on a 6), or the flush draw with two spades now. My middle pair K-kicker is in bad shape against his range here. I'm only beating draws and bluffs, and getting 2.6:1 pot odds needing about 28% equity. Against a likely Jx-heavy range, I'm drawing thin. Folding is correct with this marginal holding.

I am in UTG position with 9h 4h. This is a weak suited gapper hand. With 73.5 big blinds, I have a healthy stack and no reason to get involved with speculative trash from early position. The standard play for this hand range in UTG is a clear fold to preserve chips for better opportunities.
4s 3c is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability. From middle position with players yet to act behind me, this is an easy fold. No reason to invest chips with such a marginal hand.

I have been dealt pocket Queens, which is a premium hand. Since I am in the Cutoff and the action has folded to me, I will make a standard value raise to build the pot and protect my hand against the remaining players.

On the button with K7s suited against a cutoff raise to 300 into a 450 pot. Excellent position, suited king with good playability, blockers to KK. Deep stacks allow for postflop maneuvering. Flash's range from CO is wide, history shows aggression but vulnerabilities. 3-betting light here applies pressure, isolates vs blinds who might fold, and takes advantage of position. Sizing to 900 (3x) is standard for value/bluff mix. Pot odds great if called, but fold equity high.

Flash opened to 300 and Elon 3‑bet to 900. I’m in the small blind with T9s — a suited connector with good playability and equity vs a 3‑bet range. Pot is 1,350 and I must put in 850 more (900 total), so calling would cost 850 to win ~2,200 immediately (break‑even equity ≈ 28%). T9s usually has roughly that equity or better versus a reasonably wide 3‑bet range and has strong implied odds if I connect. I’m out of position, so I don’t want to inflate the pot with a light raise; I’ll call to see a flop, proceed cautiously post‑flop, fold to a large 4‑bet, and look to realize equity or take a free card on later streets if appropriate.

We’re in the BB with AQs facing a CO open, BTN 3-bet, and a cold-call from the SB. Flatting invites Flash to come along and leaves us playing a bloated multiway 3-bet pot out of position, which is a tough, low-realization line even with a strong hand.

AQs is strong enough to 4-bet for value/protection here, and the pot already has a lot of dead money (open + 3-bet + cold-call + blinds). A 4-bet also pressures the SB’s capped cold-call range and often folds out the original raiser, letting us isolate the button 3-bettor with initiative.

I’ll use a larger OOP size due to the cold-caller: 3300 total. Plan: comfortably continue versus calls, but versus a tight 5-bet/jam from Elon (or a strong SB shove) we can fold, since those ranges skew heavily to AK/QQ+ and we’re dominated too often.

I have pocket Queens, a premium hand. The pre-flop action is very heavy: I opened, the Button 3-bet, the SB cold-called, and the Big Blind cold 4-bet to 3,300. While a cold 4-bet usually represents extreme strength (JJ+, AK), folding QQ with 88 big blinds is too tight, especially since the pot is already quite large. Calling 3,000 more would commit nearly 40% of my stack, leaving me in a difficult spot post-flop. Moving all-in is the most effective play here. It maximizes my equity, prevents the players behind me from calling profitably, and puts pressure on hands like AK or JJ that might be in the Big Blind's range. Given FiveTwo's demonstrated aggression in earlier hands, I will commit my stack.

Facing massive action preflop: Flash's loose CO open followed by my light 3-bet, Minni flat, FiveTwo large 4-bet from BB (3300 into modest pot), and Flash's huge 5-bet to 8800 (nearly shoving with 300 behind). This sequence screams strength from both FiveTwo and Flash, likely QQ+, AK+ for Flash given the sizing and history of aggression but not bluffing this big. K7 suited has ~20-30% equity vs such ranges heads-up, worse multiway with Minni likely to shove 7550 and FiveTwo having skin left. Calling 7900 into 13900 gets ~1.8:1 but pot becomes 21800+ with possible side pot, and postflop SPR low (~1 with my remaining 7900), no implied odds for draws. Raising commits my last 7900+ into monsters. Preserve stack; fold and wait for premium spots. History shows these players value bet thin but this is polar.

Action escalated far beyond my plan: Flash 5‑bet to 8,800 (leaving ~300) over FiveTwo’s 3,300 and my earlier call. I would have to put in my last 7,550 chips to continue. That call would need ≈35% equity (7,550 / (13,900+7,550) ≈ 35%). T9s is playable versus a single 3‑bet, but facing a 4‑bet and a 5‑bet shoved/near‑shove the ranges are heavily AA/KK/QQ/AK‑type. My realistic equity multiway against those ranges is well below the break‑even threshold (roughly in the mid‑teens to low‑20s percent), and I’d be committing my tournament life out of position with marginal ROI. Preserve chips and wait for a better spot — fold.

We’re facing Flash’s 5-bet to 8800 after a CO open, BTN 3-bet, SB cold call, and our large OOP 4-bet. His sizing leaves him essentially all-in, which is typically an extremely value-heavy range in this multi-action configuration (mostly AA/KK/AK, sometimes QQ). 

Pot odds: we must call 5500 to win 13900+5500 = 19400, needing ~28% equity. With AhQs (offsuit), our equity versus a tight 5-bet range like {AA, KK, AK} is around ~24% (even with the ace blocker), and it doesn’t improve enough unless he’s capable of 5-betting hands like JJ/TT or bluffing here—both unlikely given the huge commit size into a cold-call/4-bet spot.

Calling would put most of our remaining stack at risk in a dominated/low-equity spot; folding preserves a playable stack and avoids a large negative-EV gamble.
7s4h is a trash hand in UTG position. No playability - offsuit, not connected, low cards. Easy fold.

I am in the Hijack position with Q6o. This is a weak hand and does not meet the standard opening requirements for this position in a 6-max game. Given that I have a healthy stack and several aggressive players yet to act (Elon, Minni, FiveTwo), there is no reason to play this hand and risk chips unnecessarily. I will fold.

Preflop, unopened pot (folds from UTG and MP), in cutoff position with K7d suited. This is a strong raising hand from late position: king-high suited with good playability, straight and flush potential. Standard to raise for value, initiative, and fold equity against blinds. Players behind have shown aggression but fold frequently to raises. Raise to 3x BB (300) to build pot while keeping it affordable. Deep stacks support postflop play.

Facing an open raise to 300 from the cutoff with the button, pot 450. My hand 4♦7♥ is a weak, unsuited connector with poor playability and is heavily dominated by a standard opening range. Calling 300 (about 3 BB) requires roughly 40% equity to break even preflop; 4-7o vs a raiser’s range is well below that and will be difficult to play profitably even in position (likely multiway with SB/BB defending). A 3-bet bluff here would need better blockers and hand quality — 4-7o isn’t a good candidate. I have ~75BB and can wait for a much better spot. Fold and preserve chips.

We’re in the small blind facing a cutoff open to 300 with 6h5d. Defending by calling is unattractive because we’ll be out of position for the entire hand, and the big blind can overcall/squeeze, putting us in tough spots with a weak offsuit connector that realizes equity poorly OOP. A bluff 3-bet would need a very high fold frequency (risking a lot to win 450) and this hand doesn’t play well when called. Best EV is to release and preserve the stack for better defend/3-bet candidates.

I am in the Big Blind with Td2d facing a standard raise from the Cutoff (Elon). The pot odds are approximately 31% (calling 200 to win a total pot of 650). While T2s has roughly 32-33% raw equity against a wide Cutoff opening range, realizing this equity out of position is extremely difficult due to the weak kickers and lack of connectivity. Calling would likely lead to difficult post-flop spots where I am dominated or forced to fold. The hand is too weak to defend profitably or to turn into a 3-bet bluff. Folding is the disciplined choice.

I am UTG with King-Eight offsuit (Kd 8s). In a 6-max format, this hand is below the standard opening range for the earliest position. Opening here would likely lead to difficult post-flop situations out of position against multiple opponents. Given my healthy stack and the tournament's early stage, I will stick to a disciplined opening range and fold this hand.

Preflop, unopened pot after UTG fold. My hand is K6o, offsuit king with a weak kicker, poor playability postflop. In middle position with 3 players to act behind (Cutoff, Button, blinds), including aggressive opponents like Minni and FiveTwo. Deep stacks (159BB), but standard play is to fold marginal offsuit kings here to avoid difficult spots OOP. No need to limp or raise a speculative hand. Folding preserves stack for better opportunities.

Fold. 3♥9♠ is a weak, unsuited rag with very little equity against callers or 3‑bettors and is easily dominated. In 6‑handed play the cutoff should be opening much stronger hands — here I’d risk playing out of position versus the button and run into aggression from the blinds (Claude/Pro) who have shown willingness to fight back. Calling 100 to see a flop gives poor pot odds for the hand’s equity; preserving my ~75BB stack for a better spot (or a legitimate steal with a stronger hand) is the optimal play.

Folds to me on the Button, so this is a prime steal spot. A9o is well within a Button opening range, and the As gives useful blocker effects versus strong ace holdings while retaining decent playability in position. I’ll apply pressure to the blinds with a standard ~2.5bb open rather than min-raising to reduce their immediate price and realize more fold equity.

I am in the Small Blind with Jd 3d facing a raise to 250 from the Button (FiveTwo). The pot odds are roughly 33% (calling 200 to win a total pot of 600 if BB folds), but I will be playing out of position against an aggressor with a very weak suited hand. J3s has poor playability due to the large gap and weak kicker. Additionally, calling invites the Big Blind to squeeze or call, putting me in a sandwich or multi-way pot out of position with a marginal holding. Standard play is to fold this hand.

Facing a standard button min-raise to 250 with Q5o in the BB. I'm getting 2.67:1 pot odds (150 to win 400). Q5o is marginal but has enough playability to defend against a likely wide button opening range. Calling is standard here.
Missed the flop completely with Q5o on Ks3c4c. No pair, no real draws (backdoor gutshot at best, no clubs). Standard check and evaluate - will likely fold to a c-bet given I have no equity.

Claude’s check in the BB on K-3-4 two-tone is a spot where my Button opening range retains a range advantage and can profitably c-bet small at high frequency. With A9o I have little immediate showdown value and I want to fold out his complete whiffs and some weak underpairs/ace-highs, while also denying equity to hands like 56/A5 and random overcards with a club. The As is a useful blocker versus strong Ax continues. I’ll use a small sizing to keep risk low and maintain fold equity; if check-raised big I can comfortably fold.

I have Q5o on Ks3c4c. No pair, no flush draw (no clubs). I do have a gutshot straight draw (need a 2 or 6 for a straight). Getting 3.75:1 odds (200 to win 750), but gutshot is only ~8% equity. OOP with no real showdown value and limited implied odds. The small c-bet is tempting to float, but being OOP makes it difficult to realize bluff equity. Fold is correct.

UTG with A5o offsuit is a standard fold in a 6-handed tournament. This hand has poor playability, gets dominated by better aces, and has weak post-flop potential without suited or connected strength. Deep stacks (160BB) allow waiting for premium hands from early position to maximize EV over 150 hands. No opponent tendencies from history justify limping or raising this trash hand.

I have K♦J♠ in middle position with a ~75bb stack and no open raise yet (pot 150, to call 100). KJo is a strong, standard open from MP — good heads-up equity and playability post‑flop. I want a clean open size that preserves fold equity and discourages multiway calls, so I’ll raise to 300 (3×BB), which also matches my prior raising pattern at this table. Plan: if I get one caller I’ll play as the aggressor (c-bet favorable K/J/A flops, pot-control on coordinated boards). If I face a sizable 3‑bet (large sizing or ~3× my open), KJo is usually a fold; I may consider calling vs a small, clearly light 3‑bet depending on opponent. Overall, raise to 300 to build pots in position and apply pressure.

62s is too weak to defend versus a 3x open, especially with Button/SB/BB still to act and limited playability when we flop marginal pairs/draws. A bluff 3-bet here is unnecessary risk given Minni’s opening frequency but willingness to continue at times. Preserve stack and wait for better spot/hand.

I hold 2s 4s on the Button facing a raise to 300 (3BB) from Minni in Middle Position. While suited connectors/gappers have playability, 4-high is extremely weak and easily dominated. The price to call (300 into a pot of 450) offers poor immediate odds (40%) for a hand that often misses or makes second-best hands. Additionally, calling invites squeezes from the blinds. Against a standard MP opening range, this hand is a clear fold.

T8o in the small blind facing a raise from Minni. This is a marginal hand out of position - not strong enough to 3-bet and calling OOP with T8o is -EV against a middle position raise. Flash still to act behind me in the BB. Easy fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 9h 4c, a very weak starting hand. Minni has opened for 300 from middle position. While I am getting decent pot odds (200 to call for a 450 pot), 94o performs poorly against even a wide opening range and is difficult to play out of position post-flop. Given that I have a healthy stack of 14,100, there is no reason to defend with such a marginal hand.

UTG with Q♠3♦ offsuit is a very weak, poorly coordinated hand and is routinely dominated by better Qx and broadway holdings. From first position in a 6-handed game I should be selective — opening or even limping with this hand invites 3-bets and multiway pots where I’ll be out of position and have little playability. With a healthy 77bb stack there’s no need to gamble — preserve chips and wait for a proper late-position or blind-steal spot with a stronger hand. Fold preflop.

7♥4♦ is a very weak offsuit hand with poor high-card strength, low connectivity, and bad playability (often dominated when it makes a pair). From middle position in 6-max, it’s not in a profitable opening range, and limping invites isolation without realizing equity well. Best is to preserve stack and wait for better spots.

I hold Q6 offsuit in the Cutoff. While the Cutoff is a good position to open wide to steal the blinds, Q6o is generally too weak, especially with two large stacks (Flash and Elon) in the blinds who can apply pressure. There is no strategic merit in entering the pot with this hand given the stack dynamics and hand strength. Folding is the standard play.

On the button with 65o after three folds. Good spot for a steal attempt with a connected hand. Both blinds have big stacks but that shouldn't deter a standard button raise. Min raise to 200 to steal cheaply.

I have been dealt pocket Jacks in the Small Blind. This is a premium hand. The Button (Claude) opened for 250. Given the strength of my hand and my position, a 3-bet is the standard play here to extract value from the Button's wide opening range and to discourage the Big Blind (Elon) from entering the pot with a speculative hand. I will raise to 850 to take control of the hand and build the pot while I likely have the best hand.

K6o is a marginal hand with poor post-flop playability out of position in the big blind facing a button raise and small blind 3-bet. Flash's SB 3-bet squeeze after Claude's BTN open is strong, likely premium hands or suited aces/broadways. Claude can have a wide range but may fold or continue selectively. Pot odds to call 750 into 1200 (~2.6:1) require ~28% equity, but vs polarized 3-bet range plus potential multiway pot, equity is lower (~20-25%). Raising with K6o lacks value or fold equity against aggro opponents with deep stacks. Folding preserves stack for better spots, as 158BB deep with 150-hand tournament ahead.

Flash 3-bet me from the SB after my button steal attempt with 65o. This is a clear fold - 65o has very poor equity against a 3-bet range, and I'm getting 2:1 which requires ~33% equity I definitely don't have. No need to defend here.

6-handed but still UTG; 8h5h is too weak and dominated to open into a table that has shown a lot of 3-betting and aggression (Flash/Elon/Minni). Limping creates multiway, low SPR spots where we realize equity poorly and can’t profitably continue versus isolation. Best EV is to preserve stack and wait for stronger opens from early position.

I am in Middle Position (Hijack) with KdTh. In a 6-handed game, this hand is on the borderline of an opening range but leans towards a fold, especially with aggressive big stacks (Flash and Elon) remaining behind me who have shown a willingness to 3-bet.
