Playing KTo out of position against deep stacks or facing a re-raise puts me in a difficult spot. Folding is the prudent play to preserve chips for better spots.
K9o in the cutoff with two folds before me. This is a standard open-raise spot from late position. The hand plays well heads-up or in a small pot. Opening to 2.5x (250) is standard.

I am on the button with Qh3h. Claude has opened for 250 from the cutoff. While I have position and the hand is suited, Q3s is too weak to calling a raise profitably, especially since I've recently been aggressive against Claude's opens. Claude has shown a tendency to fold to 3-bets, but Q3s isn't the best hand to use for a 3-bet bluff here, and calling would likely lead to a difficult post-flop situation against the blinds or the original raiser. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play.

Preflop in the small blind with QcTs offsuit facing a cutoff raise to 250 after two folds. Claude, the raiser, has a short stack of 4125 (~41BB) and is in a stealing spot, but QTo is too weak to defend out of position against a raiser's range. My stack is deep at 15800 (~158BB), and there are players behind including the big blind. Folding preserves stack and avoids difficult post-flop spots OOP with a marginal hand that plays poorly against likely calling ranges.

Claude opened to 250 from the cutoff. I’m on the BB with T8s and stacks ~76bb (me) vs ~41bb (Claude). The pot is 400 and I need 150 to call; break-even equity is only 150/(400+150) ≈ 27%, and T8s has decent equity and good post‑flop playability (backdoor flush/straight potential, can hit top pair and two‑pairs). A tiny 3‑bet to 400 is an option but I’d be out of position and the min 3‑bet is unlikely to fold out enough of Claude’s range; I prefer to defend and see a flop where I can realize equity and control the pot. Call to 250 total and reevaluate on the flop (fold to any large 4‑bet).

I hold Td8s and the flop Jd-7d-9c gives me a made straight (7-8-9-10-J). I’m heads-up vs Claude (cutoff raiser) and acting last — this is very likely the best hand right now but can be vulnerable to a full house if the board pairs. I want to extract value from worse hands (Jx, 9x, 7x, weaker pairs and some draws) while denying cheap cards that could turn into straights/full houses later. A half‑pot to slightly over half‑pot bet keeps worse hands and floaters in, gives Claude incorrect odds to chase backdoor possibilities, and leaves me room to call or raise if he turns this into a big pot. Betting 300 (pot ≈550) is the right balance: sizable enough for value/protection but small enough to keep worse hands calling. If Claude raises, I’ll re-evaluate facing a raise (likely to call or shove depending on size), but for now I bet for value.

I flopped middle pair with top kicker (K9) on Jd7d9c. Minni's donk bet of 300 into 550 is relatively small. I have a solid hand with showdown value but the board has a diamond flush draw and straight possibilities. Calling keeps the pot manageable and lets me use my position to control the pot on later streets. Raising would likely only get action from better hands.

I have the made straight (7-8-9-10-J) on J‑9‑7‑K with T8s. Very few hands currently beat me — mainly Q10 and Q8 combinations that make a higher straight on this K turn. Claude’s line (CO open, then check‑call my 300 flop bet) looks like a fairly wide, medium‑strength calling range: Jx, 9x, Kx, medium pairs and some draws. Those hands will happily call a turn bet and I should extract value while charging any draws and denying a free Queen on the river. I’ll bet for value but keep the sizing manageable in case Claude check‑raises (a large check‑raise would often indicate one of the rarer higher straights or a set). Betting ~2/3 pot (~750) is the right balance: it gets called by worse hands (Jx/9x/Kx/pairs), charges draws, and leaves room to fold/call/re‑evaluate against a large raise. Action: bet 750.

The King on the turn improved my hand from middle pair to two pair (Kings and Nines). This is a very strong hand. Minni continues betting 750 into 1150. With the diamond flush draw still out there, I should raise for value and protection. If I just call, I risk a diamond completing on the river. Raising to ~1800 gets value from Jx, draws, and worse hands while protecting against the flush draw.

I have the made straight (7-8-9-10-J) on J-9-7-K. Claude’s check-call on the flop then a big turn raise is consistent with a value-heavy range (Kx, KJ, Jx, some sets/two-pair lines and the occasional Q10 that now makes a higher straight). Practically speaking, very few hands currently beat me — the main real fear is Q10 combos that make the 9–K straight; many of the other hands that would raise (Kx, Jx, medium pairs, even sets) are actually behind my straight. The pot is 3700 and I must put in 1050 to call, so break-even equity is only ~1050/(3700+1050) ≈ 22%. My hand is well ahead of most of Claude’s plausible range and calling is cheap value: it keeps worse Kx/Jx/pairs in to pay me off and avoids bloating the pot (and possibly isolating me) against the small number of combos that beat me. Raising would risk committing more chips and mostly get called by the very hands that beat me (or fold out the weaker hands I want to extract value from). I’ll call and re-evaluate on the river if he jams or barrels again.

I have the made straight (7-8-9-10-J) and the river 5 doesn't change that — the only realistic hands that beat me are Q10 combos that make the Broadway straight. Claude’s line (CO open, check-call flop, large turn check‑raise, then checking the river) is polar but is actually more consistent with Kx, two‑pair/set showdown lines or pot control — if he held Q10 he often bets for value on the river instead of checking. With Claude short‑stacked there’s limited fold equity and a river bet risks either folding out the very hands I want value from (Kx/Jx/pairs) or inducing a shove from the tiny number of combos that beat me. Checking here maximizes my showdown equity against his most likely range, avoids bloating the pot unnecessarily, and keeps worse hands in to pay me off. I’ll check to take it to showdown.

I have two pair (Kings and Nines) on a Jd7d9cKc5s board. The flush draw bricked. Minni check-called my turn raise and now checked the river. This indicates a medium-strength hand like Jx, a 9, or possibly a busted draw. My two pair is very strong here and I should value bet. With 2025 left in my stack and 4750 in the pot, I'll bet around 1600 to extract value from weaker hands like top pair or worse two pair.

I have the nut (7-8-9-10-J) straight with T♦8♠. The pot is 6,350 and Claude bet 1,600; calling requires ~20% equity to break even (1,600 / 7,950 ≈ 20%). Practically nothing in Claude’s likely range (CO open, check-call flop, big turn raise, small river bet) beats me except Q10 combos that make the higher Broadway straight. Claude is short-stacked and his smallish river stab is either a thin value bet or a blocker/protection move — a raise here would often fold out the Kx/Jx/pair hands I want value from or simply induce a jam from the few hands that do beat me. Given the price, the hand strength, and the desire to maximize showdown value while avoiding getting isolated against the very few beaters, I will call the 1,600 to take it to showdown.

I am in UTG position with Q6o (Six of Diamonds, Queen of Hearts). This is a very weak offsuit hand that is dominated by many calling ranges and has poor playability post-flop, especially out of position. With a short stack (Claude) left to act who might shove light, and generally deep stacks elsewhere, there is no incentive to open this hand. The standard play is to fold immediately.

With only 4.25 BB and AJo, this is a mandatory all-in spot. AJo is a premium hand for short-stack shove ranges. I need to push for fold equity and chip up potential. No point in limping or min-raising when I'd be committing most of my stack anyway.

I am holding 4d 2c, which is an extremely weak hand. Claude has opened with a raise from middle position. Given the high probability of being dominated and the lack of playability for this hand post-flop, folding is the only sensible move.

67o is a marginal offsuit hand with one gap and no suited connectivity, offering poor playability and equity against a middle position raising range after UTG fold. Claude's raise to 425 (4.25BB) suggests a strong-to-medium strength range. On the button with deep stacks, position helps, but speculative calls here lead to difficult postflop spots, especially if facing resistance from blinds. Pot odds (575:425 ~26% equity needed) are insufficient given estimated <30% equity vs range. Standard fold to preserve stack in tournament setting (hand 36/150).

I have 9♠2♦ in the small blind facing Claude’s raise to 425 (pot 575, 375 to call). This is a garbage, unsuited, unconnected hand that plays very poorly out of position, and the big blind still acts behind me. Calling 375 to see a flop with 92o is a losing and unnecessary gamble; a 3-bet would be reckless. With a healthy stack (11,650) I should conserve chips and wait for a better spot. Fold.

Claude opens very large (4.25bb) from middle position, which usually tightens his range and reduces the profitability of light 3-bets. With 33 in the big blind, folding is too tight given the price and our ability to realize strong implied odds when we flop a set. Calling keeps the pot manageable OOP, preserves our range, and gives us clear postflop plans (check most flops; continue strongly on set boards, proceed cautiously on overcard-heavy textures).

I have 8d2d in UTG with only 900 chips (9 BB). This is an extremely weak hand - 82 suited is near the bottom of all starting hands. Even though I'm short-stacked and approaching push/fold territory, 82s from UTG is not a hand I should be shoving. The hand has almost no playability and terrible equity against calling ranges. Easy fold.

I am in the Hijack position with J6o. This is a very weak starting hand and does not meet the requirements for an opening raise from this position. With several players still to act behind me, including large stacks, folding is the standard and most prudent play to preserve my chips.

Preflop in cutoff with Qh7s offsuit, which is a weak hand outside standard opening ranges. UTG and MP have folded, but Q7o lacks playability, blockers, or strength postflop. Folding preserves stack in a multiway pot potential with aggressive opponents behind. Deep stack allows waiting for better spots to maximize EV over tournament.

Blinds 50/100 — pot 150, to call 100, min-raise to 200. On the button with 9♦5♦: a weak but playable suited hand with backdoor flush/straight potential. Against only the blinds left, I prefer to apply pressure and try to steal the blinds rather than limp and invite multiway action. A raise to 300 (3xBB) gives good fold equity while still keeping pots manageable with deep stacks. Plan: if both fold I take the pot; if called I play postflop in position — c-bet on favorable/dry flops, check and realize equity or pot-control on wet boards, and fold to large aggression if I miss. If I face a large 3-bet (e.g. pot-sized/very aggressive sizing) I’ll fold this hand. Raise to 300 to attempt a steal and preserve positional advantage.

Minni is opening from the Button and has shown a high-activity/aggressive profile. From the small blind, flat-calling A6o is uncomfortable (domination risk, poor realization OOP) and also leaves Pro in the BB a good spot to squeeze. With the ace blocker, this is a solid light 3-bet candidate: we can win the pot preflop often, reduce BB’s squeeze frequency, and have a clear plan to fold versus a 4-bet. I’ll use a standard SB 3-bet sizing versus a 3x open.

I am in the Big Blind with Ad 3c. Minni opened from the Button to 300, and FiveTwo 3-bet from the Small Blind to 1000. Facing a 3-bet cold, A3o is too weak to continue. The SB's 3-bet range against the Button typically includes strong Ax hands that dominate me (AK, AQ, AJ) and pairs. Cold calling would put me out of position against the 3-bettor and potentially sandwiched if the original raiser calls or 4-bets. The risk is high and the playability of A3o is low in this spot. The correct play is to fold.

Call 1000. I opened to 300 from the button and FiveTwo 3-bet to 1000 — a standard-sized 3-bet but not pot-sized. 9♦5♦ is speculative and plays very well in position: backdoor flush and straight equity plus post‑flop maneuverability. Calling 700 more (1000 total committed) buys me a cheap look — pot will be ~2100 and effective SPR ≈ 3.5–4, which is playable for sets, two‑pair, and strong draws. I won’t 4-bet with this weak holding (poor blockers, not enough fold equity vs a likely strong 3‑bet range). Folding surrenders too much equity/position for a small price. Plan: see the flop in position, c-bet or float on favorable/dry flops, realize equity or fold to heavy aggression on misses, and proceed aggressively only when I hit or get appropriate odds to continue.

We’re in a SB vs BTN 3-bet pot and I retain a strong range advantage here (overpairs, AK/AQ, etc.) on a paired TTx board. This texture is excellent for a small range c-bet: it puts immediate pressure on Minni’s overcard floats and small pairs (22–77), and denies equity to hands like A-high/KQ/QJ that may have peeled preflop. With As6h I have no made hand and no club equity, so I prefer a low-risk, high-frequency stab rather than checking and letting Minni realize equity or take the betting lead. I’ll use a small sizing to keep my range protected and make their continues indifferent.

I opened from the button and called a standard 3-bet preflop with 9♦5♦ to see a flop in position. The flop T Tc 8c completely misses my hand — no pair, no club draw — but the bet is very small (550 into a 2650 pot). I have 6 clean pairing outs (three 9s + three 5s) and about a 24% chance to hit one of them by the river. Calling 550 gets me ~17% pot‑odds (550 / (2650+550) ≈ 17%), so a call is +EV purely on raw pot odds vs. my two‑card outs, and I keep positional leverage. FiveTwo’s tiny c‑bet sizing looks like a probe/continuation bet that can be floated; raising as a bluff is thin (I have no meaningful blockers and risk a 4‑bet or being called by better hands), and folding surrenders fold equity and the +EV chance cheaply. Plan: call now to see the turn, attempt to float and apply pressure if Villain shows weakness (checks), fold to meaningful turn aggression, and if I pair or pick up meaningful equity I can start betting for value or showdown.

Turn Jc is a bad barrel card for this specific combo: it adds a third club (giving BTN many floats additional equity) and also improves a chunk of her flop-calling range (KJ/QJ/JTs/J9s, plus hands with a club). With As6h we have no club, no straight draw, and essentially no profitable way to continue versus a bet, so firing again is likely just burning chips against a player who has shown a willingness to continue. Best is to check, preserve stack, and only realize equity when she checks back (we can still win vs some A-high/underpairs).

I have 9♦5♦ on a board 8♣ T♣ T♦ J♣ — no pair, no club draw, only 6 outs (three 9s and three 5s). After the turn there are 46 unknown cards, so my chance to improve on the river is only ~13%. The opponent 3‑bet preflop and led small on the flop, then checked the turn — that line is ambiguous but leans toward a fairly strong or pot‑control range. Betting here as a bluff would sometimes win the pot, but vs a preflop 3‑bettor who has shown aggression it will be called too often and is low EV. Checking preserves my stack and gives me a free river card with ~13% equity; if the villain checks the river I can reach showdown cheaply, if they bet small I can call by pot odds in spots or fold to large river aggression. Therefore I check and see the river.

River brings the 4th club (and pairs us with a weak 6), which is a very poor card to turn this hand into a bluff: I hold no club blockers, and Minni’s flop-call range contains a lot of Tx (now full houses), Ax/Kx/Qx with a club (now flushes), and other club floats that improved. Betting mostly gets called/raised by better and folds out the hands we beat. Best is to take the showdown line and realize our small equity, then reassess if she bets.

Check. I missed the river with 9♦5♦ — there are four clubs on board (8♣ T♣ J♣ 6♣) so any club in FiveTwo's 3‑bet/c‑bet range now beats me. Villain's line (3‑bet preflop, tiny flop bet, check turn) is consistent with medium/strong hands or a made/turned club and is unlikely to fold to a river bluff, especially since I have no club blockers or showdown value. Betting as a bluff is thin and high variance; checking gives me the cheapest route to a showdown or lets him bluff if he chooses. Plan: check and fold to any river bet — preserve stack and wait for a better spot.

I am in the UTG position with 7d 4s, which is one of the weakest hands in the game. With five players still to act behind me and no significant reason to enter the pot with such poor equity, the standard and most optimal play is to fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Preflop in middle position with 9h3s offsuit, a complete trash hand with no high card value, connectivity, or suited potential. Standard fold to avoid spew. Deep stacks, no need to play marginal hands out of position. Pot is small blinds only, no value in limping or raising this.

I’m in cutoff with A♥T♠ — a strong opener with good playability and positional advantage. The table’s standard open size has been ~3x, and with three players to act (Button, SB, BB) I want to build the pot and apply fold equity while keeping a reasonable price for callers. Claude (BB) is very short (~8bb) and may shove, which I’ll re-evaluate if it happens, but versus the Button and SB I prefer to be the aggressor and take the initiative. Raise to a standard 300 keeps my range balanced, pressures the blinds, and preserves postflop maneuverability in position.

Pocket kings on the button versus a cutoff 3bb open is a clear value 3-bet. Minni has shown an active opening tendency, so we want to build a pot and get called by worse (AQ, AK, QQ-JJ, some suited broadways) while also denying equity and discouraging the blinds from coming along. With BB extremely short (800), a normal in-position sizing also leaves room for BB to jam and for us to comfortably continue.

I am in the Small Blind with 8c5c, facing a raise to 300 from the Cutoff and a 3-bet to 1000 from the Button. My hand is a weak suited gapper which performs poorly against strong ranges, especially out of position. The 3-bet represents significant strength or aggression, and I have no invested interest in the pot beyond the small blind. Calling 950 chips (approx 13.5% of my stack) with this hand in the worst position is highly unprofitable. This is a trivial fold.

Facing a 3-bet with 93o is an easy fold. This is one of the worst hands in poker, and calling off my entire 800 stack against FiveTwo's 3-bet would be a disaster. Even with pot odds of ~1.8:1, 93o has terrible equity against a 3-betting range.

I opened CO with A♥T♠ and the BTN 3-bet to 1,000 (I must call 700). The 3-bet sizing is modest and FiveTwo has shown aggression — likely a fairly wide range. ATo has good equity vs a wide 3-betting range and my Ace is a useful blocker to premium holdings. I don’t want to inflate a pot out of position with a marginal hand by making a small 4-bet (min-4bet to 1,700 is too little to achieve fold equity and a large 4-bet would commit too much). I’ll call 700 (1,000 total) to keep the pot controllable and realize equity postflop while gathering info on the BTN’s tendencies.

Check. I have A♥T♠ on 2♠-7♠-9♣ — only Ace-high with a single spade (no made pair and only a runner-runner flush possibility). I’m out of position to a BTN who 3‑bet to 1,000 and is likely to continue with a wide but sticky range, so a flop c-bet here has limited fold equity and often gets called by pairs/draws. Pot control is the priority: I have ~6 clean outs (3 Aces + 3 Tens) to improve and blocking the A helps a little, but my showdown value is weak. Plan: check to keep the pot manageable, call small continuation bets to realize equity, fold to a large raise or polarizing shove, and re-evaluate on the turn — if Villain checks I can take a free card and elect to barre on very favorable turn cards (A/T or safe/rainbow turns).

Heads-up in position after 3-betting preflop, this flop (2s-7s-9c) is very good for our range and KK is an overpair that wants value and protection. Minni can have many worse continues (9x, 77/99/22 sometimes, 88-QQ, A9s, 76s, spade draws like AsXs/KsQs, and straight draws like 86s). We should c-bet small to keep those hands in, deny equity to spade/straight draws, and avoid bloating the pot unnecessarily against the top of her range. A ~1/3 pot sizing accomplishes that well and sets up clean turn decisions.

I call 700 (total committed 1,700). Rationale: I’m OOP with A♥T♠ on 2♠-7♠-9♣ — Ace-high with only backdoor (runner-runner) spade potential and ~6 clean outs (3 Aces + 3 Tens). BTN (FiveTwo) 3‑bet pre and now chose a very small flop bet (700 into 2,850 ≈ 25%), which is consistent with a wide, often sticky/structural c-bet range. Calling keeps the pot controlled, realizes my equity, and preserves options on the turn. Immediate pot odds (700 into ~3,550) are ~20% and my two‑card equity to make a pair by river is ~24%, so a call is justified even before considering fold equity on later streets and the fact that I block some strong Ace combos. Raising OOP with only Ace-high would bloat the pot and play poorly against a 3‑bettor’s continuing range; folding surrenders good equity for a very small bet. Plan: call to see the turn, fold to a polarizing shove if I haven’t improved (unless turn action/sizing strongly indicates a bluff), and press when I pair my Ace/T or pick up a clear draw.

Board: 2♠-7♠-9♣-3♦. I have A♥T♠ — no pair, only two clean categories of outs (3 Aces + 3 Tens = 6 outs) and only one card to come (6/46 ≈ 13% to improve to a pair). The BTN 3‑bet pre and barreled a small flop bet, then checked the turn — that check looks like weakness or pot control from a fairly sticky/wide 3‑bet range. Betting OOP here would bloat a pot I’m unlikely to win at showdown and won’t get folds from many of the villain’s continuing hands (pairs, some combos with equity). I planned to check the turn to see a free card and that remains the best line: keep the pot manageable, realize my ~13% equity to improve on the river, and only commit more chips if I pair or the opponent gives a clear bluffing line. Plan going forward: check the river if villain checks back; call small river bets to realize equity; fold to large river shoves unless I improve or have a very strong read to continue.

Minni check-calling flop after opening and calling the 3-bet leaves her with a lot of 9x, 77/99/22 sometimes, 88-QQ, and spade draws. The 3♦ doesn’t meaningfully change relative hand strength, and checking back gives a free river to flush/straight draws while missing value from worse pairs. I want a sizing that (1) still gets calls from 9x/88-TT and many spade draws, (2) charges the turn draws appropriately with one card to come, and (3) sets up a river SPR where we can comfortably go for value on blanks. A ~50% pot barrel accomplishes this well.

Call 1700. Board is 2♠-7♠-9♣-3♦ and I hold A♥T♠. FiveTwo 3‑bet pre and has shown aggression with a very small flop stab and now a moderate turn bet (1700 into 5250). Pot odds to call are ~24.5% (1700/(5250+1700)); my raw chance to pair by the river is ~13% (6 outs). Pure drawing odds are short, but villain’s line is wide and contains many single-pair combos and bluffs — plus my Ace blocks some strong A combos and has showdown value. Raising OOP with only Ace‑high would bloat the pot and is negative EV versus a sticky 3‑bettor; folding surrenders too much equity and the pot. Calling keeps the pot controlled, realizes my equity, and preserves options on the river: fold to a large river shove if I miss, call/raise for value if I pair the A or T (or if river action indicates a bluff).

I improved to top pair on the river (A♥T♠ vs board 2♠-7♠-9♣-3♦-A♣). FiveTwo’s line — 3-bet pre, a small flop stab, a turn bet and then checking the Ace on the river — looks like a wide, sticky range containing missed spade draws, single pairs (9s/7s/2s) and some stronger Ax/sets. I want to extract thin value from hands I beat (weaker single-pair hands and missed draws) while keeping the pot manageable against hands that dominate me (sets, two-pair, better Aces). I’ll make a small value bet (~1/3 pot) to charge misses and weaker pairs; if Villain raises big I’ll give credit and fold, if called I go to showdown.

River A♣ is a bad card for KK, but Minni’s line (check/call flop + turn, then small-ish lead) is consistent with two buckets: (1) value hands that improved to an Ace (AQ/AJ/AT, A♠X♠) or slowplayed sets, and (2) blocker/bluff leads with hands that hate checking (QQ–TT, 9x) or missed spade/straight draws trying to fold out our one-pair. 

We’re getting strong pot odds: calling 2300 to win ~11550 total means we need only ~20% equity. Given Minni can arrive here with plenty of non-A hands (QQ–TT, some 9x, missed spades) and may choose this sizing as a blocker/bluff, folding is too tight. Raising is poor: worse hands rarely call, and better hands (Ax/sets) will. So we bluff-catch and take the price.

UTG position in 6-handed play with 6c3c, a weak suited connector with low cards. This hand has poor playability, low equity multiway, and is easily dominated. Standard fold from early position to preserve stack for better opportunities. No limping or raising viable here given position and hand strength.

I have Q♠T♦ in middle position — a playable, flexible hand that plays well postflop and is a reasonable open here. Pot is 150, to call 100 (BB=100) and min-raise is 200; I want to take the initiative and narrow the field, so I’ll open to a standard 3x-ish sizing. Raising to 300 applies pressure to the blinds/button, gives fold equity, and keeps pot control with deep stacks (I have ~159 BB). Plan: c‑bet on favorable flops; fold to heavy, non‑committal 3‑bets that clearly represent stronger ranges. I’ll treat any very small all‑in shoves from the short SB (Claude ~7.5 BB) differently — we can call those for pot odds — otherwise avoid getting overly committed with QTo vs a strong 3‑bet.

Minni’s 3x open from middle position will be fairly strong/linear, and 34o has very poor equity and playability even in position. Calling also risks getting squeezed by the button or facing a short-stack SB jam that we can’t profitably continue against. With no good 3-bet bluff properties (offsuit, low connectivity, reverse implied odds), the best stack-preserving decision is to fold.

I am on the Button with JTo facing a raise to 300 (3bb) from Minni in Middle Position. Minni has a deep stack and an active image. My hand, JTo, is often dominated by an MP opening range (which includes AJ, KJ, QJ, AT, KT, and pairs). Additionally, the Small Blind (Claude) is extremely short-stacked (7.5bb) and likely to shove any playable hand, which would complicate the spot if I flat call. Calling would also invite the Big Blind to come along, leading to a multi-way pot with a marginal offsuit hand. 3-betting is too loose here. Folding is the most prudent long-term play.

With only 750 chips (7.5 BB), I'm in push/fold territory. Q3o is a weak hand against a middle position open from Minni who has been active. Calling is weak - I'd be out of position with a marginal hand. Shoving Q3o as a resteal isn't profitable against an MP raiser. Better to fold and wait for a stronger spot to commit my short stack.

I am in the Big Blind holding 7-high suited (7h 2h). Minni raised to 300 from Middle position. While I am getting approximately 2.25:1 odds to call, 72s is one of the weakest hands in the game and realizes equity very poorly out of position. Given that I have a healthy stack and there are 110 hands remaining, there is no need to defend with such a weak hand against a standard open. Folding preserves my stack for more favorable spots.

UTG in a 6-max game with K6o off-suit — a weak, easily-dominated hand with very poor postflop playability out of position. Calling 100 to limp invites multiway pots and puts me in bad spots; raising would be reasonable with a much stronger UTG opening range, but K6o is too marginal to open and can be crushed by callers or exploited by 3-bets. The button is a short stack (750) who could shove over a raise, creating an uncomfortable decision with a weak kicker. Preserve the deep stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

Minni has folded UTG and action is on me in middle position. Th8h is a reasonable open in 6-max, especially with position on the blinds. I don’t want to limp and invite squeezes from the deep SB/BB. Also, Claude on the button is very short (7.5bb), so a small open lets me comfortably call off if he jams due to strong pot odds, while still being able to fold versus larger 3-bets from Pro/Flash/Elon.

I hold 3c 7c in the Cutoff. This is a weak suited three-gapper. An opponent in Middle Position has opened to 2.5BB. My hand has very low equity against a standard opening range and suffers from reverse implied odds (dominated flushes/straights). Additionally, the Button is very short-stacked (7.5BB) and might shove over a call, and the blinds are deep and could squeeze. There is no strategic merit in entering this pot with such a weak holding. Folding is the correct decision.
With only 750 chips (7.5BB), I'm in push/fold territory. 57o is a weak hand - not strong enough to call or shove with here against an open raise. I should preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

I am in the Small Blind holding 8s 2c, which is a very weak hand. FiveTwo has raised to 250 from middle position. Given my poor hand strength and the fact that I would be out of position for the rest of the hand, there is no strategic reason to call or raise. I will fold.

9d5h is a trash hand: offsuit, gapped, low cards with minimal straight or flush potential. Facing an early/mid position raise from FiveTwo (5700 stack), their range is strong (top ~15-20%: pairs, broadways, suited connectors/Ax). Pot odds are 150 to call into 400 (~27% equity needed), but my equity vs their range is ~25-30% at best, and postflop playability is poor OOP. No strong reads from history justify a hero call or squeeze. Folding preserves stack for better spots in this multi-table tournament simulation.

6-handed UTG, Q3o is far too weak to open-raise and plays poorly postflop (dominated by all better queens, no suitedness, weak connectivity). Limping invites multiway pots and reverse implied odds. With 61bb we don’t need to take marginal spots; we preserve stack and wait for better open/3-bet candidates.

I am in middle position with 4s 3h, which is an extremely weak hand (unsuited connectors with low rank). With action folding to me and several players left to act, including a short stack in the Cutoff who might shove, there is no strategic justification for entering the pot. The blinds are small relative to my stack, so preserving chips is standard here. I will fold.

With pocket 7s in the cutoff and only 7.5 BBs, I'm in push/fold territory. Pocket 7s is a strong hand to shove with - it's ahead of most calling ranges except higher pairs. Two players already folded. Shoving all-in maximizes fold equity and protects against being outflopped.

I am on the Button holding Ten-Four offsuit. Claude has opened with a significant raise to 750 (7.5x the big blind) from the Cutoff. Ten-Four offsuit is a very weak hand that does not play well post-flop, especially against a large raise. There is no reason to defend or bluff in this spot given the hand strength. I will fold.

AQo is an extremely strong hand facing a cutoff all-in shove of 750 (about 7.5BB) after two folds preflop. Claude's shoving range from cutoff is wide, including many Ax weaker than AQo, broadway hands, suited connectors, and pairs. AQo has excellent equity against this range (likely 60%+ heads-up). Pot odds are favorable: calling 700 more (total 750 committed) into a 900 pot gives 900:700 or roughly 43% equity needed, but we have far more. BB (Minni) still to act with deep stack, but even if she calls with worse (KQ, AJ, pairs), we remain ahead multiway, and postflop we'll have good playability OOP. Raising to isolate risks folding out BB's worse hands while bloating pot OOP vs her strong range. Flat call maximizes EV by keeping pot small and inducing action from BB with marginal holdings. Long-term stack maximization favors calling premium hands vs small all-ins.

I have 6♥9♣ — a weak, unsuited hand, but the cutoff (Claude) is effectively all-in for 750 and Elon (SB) has called. The pot is 1600 and I only need to add 650 more (total commit 750) — about 29% equity required. Against a short-stack shove and a likely wide SB call this is a reasonable defend from the big blind. I won’t isolate by raising to 1400 with a poor hand out of position; that would risk more chips without hand-quality or fold equity. Call 750 total to see a flop and proceed cautiously: fold to heavy action on dry boards, continue with pairs, strong draws or improvement.

Flop completely misses my AQo; ace-high has minimal showdown value against Claude's wide cutoff shoving range (pairs, broadways, suited connectors, Ax) and Minni's calling range from BB (premiums, suited broadways, medium pairs). Board 7c9s5h is low, rainbow, uncoordinated, favoring their holdings. No draws on my end (backdoor spade at best). OOP first to act multiway with dead money (Claude all-in), checking is mandatory: controls pot size, sees turn cheaply or responds to action without risking more chips unnecessarily. Bluff-betting air multiway OOP is -EV; preserves deep stack for better spots in long tournament.

I have top pair (pair of 9s) with a weak 6 kicker on a fairly dry 7c-9s-5h board. Pot is 2250 and SB (Elon) checked to me, so I should try to get value from worse pairs (7x,5x, weaker 9x) and charge the few straight outs (an 8) while denying free cards. A half‑pot c-bet keeps the pot manageable out of position but still achieves protection and fold equity. If Elon flats I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn; if he check‑raises big I’ll heavily discount my kicker and likely fold to avoid getting stacked by a stronger 9x/overpair/sets.

Preflop AQo flatted a wide short-stack shove to induce action and keep pot small OOP multiway. Flop 7c9s5h completely misses, leaving ace-high with no pair, draw, or strong showdown value against Claude's wide CO shoving range (many pairs 5-9, suited connectors that connect) or Minni's tighter BB calling range (premiums, broadways, medium pairs that hit this low board). Board rainbow but connected enough for Minni's donk-bet (1100 into 2250) to be polarized toward value (pairs+, sets, two-pair) or strong semi-bluffs (68, T8 draws). Pot odds 3350:1100 require ~25% equity, which ace-high might achieve raw multiway (~30-40% vs Claude, less vs Minni), but OOP position kills playability: check-fold turn likely vs continued aggression, no implied odds with air vs deep BB stack. Bluff-raising air -EV multiway. Folding preserves 14.95BB deep stack for superior spots in 150-hand tournament, consistent with preflop strategy of value preservation. Long-term EV-maximizing play.

I hold 2s 3c (3-2 offsuit) in UTG position. This is arguably the worst starting hand in poker. Being the first to act with 5 players behind me, there is absolutely no justification for playing this hand. I am deep enough (70.5 BB) that I don't need to make desperate moves. The correct strategy is to fold immediately.

A3o from middle position with 22.5 BB stack. Marginal hand but playable with position on blinds. Standard 2.5x open to 250 to steal blinds. Will fold to a 3-bet given my stack size and marginal holding.

Qd7c is a weak off-suit hand that does not play well against an open raise from middle position. Claude is playing a short stack (20BB), and his raise to 2.5BB indicates strength or a commitment to the pot. Calling or raising with such a marginal hand is negative expected value, especially with three active players behind me.

Preflop on the button with T8o facing a middle position raise to 250 into a 400 pot (pot odds ~1.6:1, requiring ~38% equity). T8o is a speculative hand with straight potential that plays well postflop in position. Claude has shown aggression from middle with a short stack (~2000), but his range is likely wide based on history (e.g., raises with marginal hands like in hands 24, 28). SB Minni and BB FiveTwo may defend, but position allows controlling the pot and realizing equity. Folding is too tight multiway potential; 3-betting risks committing vs shorter stack without premium. Calling balances range and exploits position for implied odds if we hit.

Claude opened to 250 and Elon called on the button — this is a three-way pot and I'm out of position to both. JhTd is playable but marginal: decent equity and good postflop playability, but not strong enough to 3-bet light. A min‑raise to 400 will just bloat a multiway pot OOP and a larger isolation 3‑bet would be too loose here. I’ll invest the 200 more (250 total committed) to see a flop and proceed postflop with top pair, two pair, or strong draws; otherwise I’ll exercise pot control and fold to heavy aggression.

We’re getting a great price (150 to win 850) in a multiway pot with a pocket pair, and our main goal is to realize implied odds by flopping a set versus several ranges that can pay off. Squeezing 4-4 out of position risks creating an awkward spot versus Claude’s short-stack jam and bloats the pot without strong playability. Best is to take the cheap flop and proceed straightforwardly postflop.

I completely missed this flop. Board 3♦ Q♥ Q♠ and I hold J♥ T♦ — no pair, only a backdoor heart (needs both turn and river) and no meaningful straight draw. This is a multiway pot (Claude opened and got callers) and I’m out of position acting first, so bluffing has very low expected value here. My preflop plan was to continue only with top pair or strong draws; I don’t have either. I’ll check to control the pot and realize equity. If anyone fires a sizeable probe bet (especially >~1/3–1/2 pot) or Claude shows aggression, I’ll fold — I won’t bloat the pot OOP with a marginal hand.

On Q-Q-3 rainbow our 4-4 is just an underpair with some tiny backdoor/full-house equity (need a 4 or running 3s). In a 4-way pot, leading is unlikely to fold out enough better hands (any Q, any pair 55+, and even A-highs may peel) and it risks getting raised by trips/boats, especially with Claude short. Best is to keep the pot small, check, and play reactively versus sizing (mostly continuing only versus small bets).

I flopped bottom pair with A3o on a Qh Qs 3d board. As the preflop raiser, I should c-bet this paired board - it's a good texture for continuation betting since it likely missed the callers' ranges. A small bet around 1/3 pot (300-350) applies pressure while keeping my commitment low with only 2000 behind. If I get raised, I can fold. Checking invites aggression and gives up initiative.

Facing a flop bet of 325 into 1325 (pot odds ~4:1) on Qs Qh 3d with T8o, which connects with nothing - no pair, no flush draw, no straight draw (gutshot at best but unlikely). Preflop call was speculative for multiway implied odds in position, but this board misses completely and favors Claude's MP raising range (Qx, overpairs, sets, etc.). Minni and FiveTwo checked, suggesting weakness, but Claude's bet after two checks shows strength. Equity too low multiway (<15% vs villain ranges), no fold equity for raise, and short stack Claude unlikely to bluff light here. Deep stack allows folding air without issue to preserve chips for better spots. Standard fold.

I missed this flop (JhTd on 3♦ Q♥ Q♠): no pair, only a backdoor heart and no straight draw. This is a multiway pot and I'm out of position, so I don't want to bloat the pot with a bluff or a raise. Claude's bet is small (325 into a 1325 pot, ≈25%), below my threshold for folding to heavy aggression, so it's cheap to see a turn. I will call to realize equity and re-evaluate on the turn — if Claude barrels large or shoves (he's short-stacked) I'll fold; if I pair up or pick up a real draw I'll proceed more aggressively.

Claude’s 325 into 1650 is a very small c-bet on a paired board, and Minni’s flat doesn’t narrow to only trips (she can easily have pocket pairs, floats, or some weak Qx). We’re getting excellent immediate odds (325 to win ~1650+), and while 4-4 is usually behind, we still realize equity via (1) spiking a 4 by the river (~8.4%) and potentially stacking Claude’s short remainder, and (2) occasional check-through / showdown wins when both are light. Raising is terrible (we isolate ourselves versus Qx and better pairs and burn chips), and folding is a bit too tight given the price and implied odds vs Claude’s capped stack. Plan: call once, then mostly fold to meaningful turn pressure unless we improve or action checks through.

I hold J♥T♦ on 3♦ Q♥ Q♠ 6♦ — I completely missed. There are only 6 clean outs (3 Js + 3 Ts) — ~13% to pair on the river — and no meaningful draws. This is a three‑way pot and I’m out of position, so bluffing the turn has very low EV and I should avoid bloating the pot. Claude bet small on the flop (could be a thin c‑bet or Qx) but he’s short‑stacked and could shove later; I’ll check to control the pot and realize my equity. Plan: check now; call a small, cheap turn bet to see the river, but fold to large overbets/shoves without improvement.

We have an underpair (44) on a very static paired board (QQ36) in a 3-way pot. Donk-leading doesn’t credibly fold out enough better hands (any Qx and most pocket pairs that called flop) and risks getting jammed on by Claude’s short stack, forcing us to burn chips with poor equity and dominated “outs” (a river 4 often still loses to higher pocket pairs). Our best line is to keep the pot small, realize whatever showdown equity we have versus missed overcards, and respond only to very small sizing; otherwise we can comfortably fold to real turn pressure.

I have bottom pair (3s) with Ace kicker on Qh Qs 3d 6d. Both opponents called my flop bet and checked turn again. My hand is marginal - just bottom pair. With 1675 behind into a 1975 pot, betting would commit me. The 6d turn adds diamond draw possibilities (I have Ad for backdoor equity). Checking controls pot size with a weak made hand. If I bet and get check-raised, I'm in a terrible spot. Check behind, potentially catch an Ace on river, and get to showdown cheaply.

I have J♥T♦ on 3♦ Q♥ Q♠ 6♦ 9♦ — no pair and only four diamonds (no flush). I completely missed; my hand has essentially no showdown value and I’m out of position in a three‑way pot. Claude’s small flop bet then turn check looks like pot‑control with a real Q or a marginal c‑bet; FiveTwo has shown passive calls. Bluffing here has very low EV — getting two opponents to fold is unlikely and I don’t want to bloat the pot when behind. I’ll check and fold to any meaningful river bet.

River is Q-Q-3-6-9 with the diamond flush coming in. With 4♠4♦ (actually 4h4d) we have a very weak showdown hand: we only beat pure overcard floats, and those are less common in a 3-way line where both players called flop and then checked turn. Bluffing is unattractive multiway: Minni can easily have any pocket pair 55+ or a Qx, and Claude’s short stack is price-incentivized to look us up with almost any pair. The diamond river may make some medium pairs uncomfortable, but it also gives opponents more reasons to call (made flushes / improved hands), and our line (check/call flop, check turn) doesn’t credibly represent a strong value hand. Best is to take the free showdown and avoid lighting chips on fire.

River completed a diamond flush draw but I hold the Ad as a blocker. Both opponents have checked all streets after calling my flop c-bet, showing extreme weakness. My bottom pair with Ace kicker is marginal - better hands call any bet, worse hands fold. With only 1675 behind into a 1975 pot, check behind to realize showdown value. My hand might beat missed draws and Ace-high.

QJo UTG with 16.75 BB stack. Decent hand that plays well as a raise. With my short stack, I should be looking to open-raise standard hands to build pots and steal blinds. QJo is playable from UTG at this stack depth. Standard 2.5x open to 250.

I have Ace-King offsuit (AKo), which is a premium starting hand. The UTG player, Claude, has raised to 250 but is very short-stacked with only 1,425 total. My goal is to isolate Claude and maximize the value of my hand while discouraging the players behind me (Elon, Minni, FiveTwo, and Pro) from entering the pot with speculative hands. A 3-bet to 750 chips is a standard sizing that puts Claude in a position where he is effectively committed to the pot given his remaining stack, while providing me with control over the betting lead.

T♥ J♥ is a strong speculative hand with straight and flush potential, excellent playability in position from the cutoff. Facing an UTG open from short-stacked Claude (14BB) and a 3-bet from Flash, folding is too tight given pot odds of ~1.5:1 (750 into 1150). A 3-bet risks bloating the pot multiway or facing a shove from Claude while Flash shows strength; calling keeps the pot smaller, realizes implied odds if I hit draws, and maintains position on button Minni and blinds. Opponent tendencies from history show aggressive play but frequent multiway pots where speculative hands perform well. Plan to play postflop aggressively if I connect.

I’m on the button with T6o facing an UTG open and a 3-bet to 750 from Flash with a caller (Elon). Pot is 1900 and it costs 750 to call (pot would be 2650), so I need ~28.3% equity to break even. T6o is heavily dominated by the 3-bettor’s range and by the caller’s range, and in a multiway pot my realized equity and fold equity are poor. Raising (to 1250) would be reckless with this hand and would commit chips with little chance to win the pot uncontested. I’ll preserve my healthy stack and fold preflop.

We’re in the small blind with Q2o facing an UTG open, a 3-bet, and a cold call. Even getting ~2.7:1 to call, this hand is dominated, plays terribly multiway, and we’ll be out of position with the BB still to act (risking a squeeze). A 4-bet/squeeze is not credible with this holding and would burn chips. Best EV is to fold and preserve stack.

I hold [4h Qc] in the Big Blind. The action began with a UTG raise from a short stack (Claude), followed by a 3-bet to 750 from Flash in Middle Position, and a cold call from Elon in the Cutoff. This is a multi-way 3-bet pot. My hand, Queen-Four offsuit, is extremely weak and plays poorly multi-way, especially out of position. I am facing a bet of 650 more into a pot of 1900. My equity against a 3-betting range and a cold-calling range is very low, and I could still be squeezed if the initial raiser (Claude) decides to shove over the top. There is no strategic merit to entering this pot with trash cards. Folding is the only correct option.

Facing a 3-bet from Flash and cold-call from Elon with QJo. My stack is only 1425 with 250 already in. If I call 500, I'll have 925 left - awkward SPR. With ~14BB effective, this is a shove-or-fold spot. Pot is 1900, so I'm getting decent odds. QJo has reasonable equity multiway (~30-35%) and some fold equity. Flatting OOP with this stack depth is problematic. Given pot odds and stack dynamics, shoving is better than folding or calling.

Claude has gone all-in for a total of 1,425 chips. Having already committed 750 chips with AKo, I only need to call an additional 675 chips to see a flop. AKo is a premium hand that is well ahead of a short-stack's desperate shove range. Elon, who cold-called my initial 3-bet, is still in the hand and will likely call the 675 as well. Since I am out of position to Elon, calling is the most prudent move. It allows me to realize my equity against Claude's all-in while keeping the pot size manageable against Elon, with the option to evaluate the flop before committing more chips to a side pot. Folding is out of the question given the pot odds and hand strength.

Pot odds are outstanding: calling 675 into a 3750 pot (~5.5:1), providing massive implied odds for my suited connectors T♥J♥ which have excellent straight and flush potential. Already invested 750 preflop, and folding now throws away equity in a huge pot. Claude is short-stacked (250 behind) and likely committed with his 3-bet shove range; Flash's flat call suggests strength but not enough to fold out our speculative hand. Calling keeps the pot multiway where suited connectors thrive, maintains flexibility postflop in good position (Cutoff with Button folded), and realizes equity against shorter stacks. Raising risks isolating against Flash's strong range without fold equity from Claude, bloating the pot OOP; better to call and play postflop aggressively if we connect. Opponent history shows frequent multiway pots where speculative hands perform well.

I have QJo on an 884 board with only 250 chips left (~2.5BB). I completely whiffed this flop - just two overcards. With the pot at 4425, my remaining stack is insignificant. Betting 250 into 4425 accomplishes nothing - I can't fold anyone out. Best to check and see a free card. If someone bets, I'm essentially priced in anyway with 6 outs to overcards.

The flop of 8s-4c-8h is very dry and paired, which doesn't particularly help my AKo. Claude is already all-in, so my main concern is the side pot against Elon. Elon cold-called a 3-bet pre-flop, suggesting a range of medium pocket pairs or high-card hands like AQ or AJ. Since I am first to act between the remaining active players, checking is the most prudent move. This allows me to see how Elon reacts and prevents me from bloating a side pot with only Ace-high. If it goes check-check, I get a free look at the turn card to potentially improve or reach a showdown for the main pot.

Flop board 8s-4c-8h pairs the 8s, favoring pocket pairs, 8x, or sets. My ThJh has no pair, no straight draw (gutshot unlikely), and only a weak backdoor heart flush draw since one heart is on board. Pure air with overcards but no showdown value against Claude's 4-bet shoving range (QQ+, AK, 8x) or Flash's 3-betting/calling range (similar strong hands). Both opponents checked, suggesting possible traps, weak hands, or waiting to realize equity, but pot is massive (4425) with Claude short (250 behind, committed). Betting risks getting raised by monsters or called by worse without fold equity; too thin at <20% equity. Checking preserves stack, takes free turn card in position, allows pot control OOP from Claude/Flash, and realizes any runner-runner equity (improbable but pot-committed-ish). History shows speculative hands play well multiway but fold when miss; don't bluff here vs strong ranges. Plan: check, re-evaluate turn.

I have QJo on 884A board with only 250 chips left. The Ace on the turn hurts me - anyone with an Ace now has me crushed. My Q and J are unlikely to be good even if they hit. With 250 into a 4425 pot, betting has zero fold equity. Check and pray for a miracle Q or J on the river, though even then I could lose to a better hand or trips.

On the turn, the Ace of Hearts (Ah) gives me top pair with the best possible kicker (AKo). The board is paired (8s-8h), making it unlikely that either of my opponents has an 8 given the aggressive pre-flop action. Claude is already all-in for 1,425 chips. My focus is on the side pot with Elon. Elon cold-called a 3-bet and a 4-bet pre-flop, which suggests a range of medium pocket pairs or hands like AQ and AJ. By checking here, I'm employing a pot-control strategy and inducing Elon to potentially bluff with his pocket pairs or value-own himself if he also hit the Ace with a weaker kicker. Since I am out of position, checking allows me to evaluate Elon's aggression before committing more to a side pot.

Board: 8s-4c-8h-Ah. Paired eights with an ace turn card, very draw-heavy and favoring strong made hands like 88 (trips), Ax, pocket pairs for two pair/full house potential, or sets. My ThJh missed entirely: no pair, no straight draw (T-J gutshot blocked by A/8/4), weak backdoor heart draw (board has two hearts, but irrelevant for strong draw). Equity ~10-15% max against Claude's short-stack 4-bet shove range (QQ+, AK, 88+) and Flash's 3-bet/call range (similar premiums, bluffs unlikely here). Pot 4425, Claude 250 behind (committed), both opponents checked turn (possible traps with Ax/sets or weakness, but cautious given preflop action). Betting air risks spew: no fold equity multiway (Claude calls any bet, Flash with strong range), bloating pot OOP for river. Checking takes free river card in position (last to act effectively), controls pot, preserves 13k stack for better spots, realizes minuscule runner-runner outs (e.g., Q for broadway). History shows speculative hands fold when bricked vs aggression; no reason to bluff into premium ranges here. Plan: check, fold most rivers unless miracle, exploit future spots.

I have QJo on 8846A board - complete whiff with just queen high. With only 250 chips (2.5BB) left and a 4425 pot, betting has zero fold equity. Both opponents checked through, so checking gives me a free showdown. My Q-high is unlikely good but it costs nothing to see.

On the river, the board is 8s-4c-8h-Ah-6d. I hold AKo, giving me top pair with the best possible kicker on a paired board. Claude, the short-stack who 4-bet pre-flop, has checked. He has only 250 chips remaining. Elon, who cold-called the pre-flop action and has checked every street post-flop, is also in the hand. Since there is currently no side pot between Elon and myself, and my primary goal is to win the substantial main pot of 4,425 chips, checking is the most strategic play. Checking through ensures that I reach a showdown to realize my equity against Claude's range without risking a confrontation with Elon that could force me off the best hand or lose additional chips to a potential slow-play (though an 8 is unlikely given the pre-flop action). TPTK is very likely the best hand here, and checking allows for a safe showdown.

River board 8s-4c-8h-Ah-6d completes no flush, adds a 5-7 straight possibility but pairs the eights with an ace, favoring Ax, 88, sets, or two-pair combos from aggressive preflop ranges. ThJh missed completely: jack-high no pair, no straight (gutshot blocked), no flush draw realized. Pure bluff catcher at best, but <10% equity vs Claude's committed short-stack 4-bet range (88+, ATs+, AQo+, bluffs rare) and Flash's 3-bet/call range (similar premiums). Pot 4425, both opponents checked (possible weakness or traps, but preflop action too strong for frequent air). Bluffing lacks fold equity: Claude calls 250 all-in with any Ax/8x/pair, Flash with medium strength. Value betting impossible. Checking realizes any tiny showdown equity (overcards), controls pot OOP effectively, preserves 13k stack (95BB+ deep) for superior spots in tournament with 6 players remaining. History confirms speculative suited connectors fold bricks multiway vs premium action; exploit passive checks in future but fold here.

I am UTG with Ten-Nine offsuit. This is a marginal hand and is generally a fold in a 6-max game from early position. Although Claude is very short-stacked in the Big Blind and will likely be all-in, T9o does not play well out of position against the other deep-stacked players who have yet to act. Standard strategy dictates a fold here to maintain my healthy stack and wait for better spots.

93o is a trash hand: offsuit, gapped, poor playability preflop and postflop. In middle position with multiple players behind, including deep stacks like Minni and Flash (who folded UTG but still deep), and short BB, no reason to play this. Deep stack (13275), pot small (150), blinds low (50/100), fold to wait for premium hands and build stack long-term over 150 hands.

UTG and MP folded; I'm in the cutoff with 5♥7♥, stack 14,775 and blinds 50/100 (pot 150). 5♥7♥ is a speculative suited connector that plays well in position — good backdoor flush/straight potential and a reasonable open from late position to take the initiative. BB (Claude) is effectively a short stack (150) and will usually fold or shove; Button (FiveTwo) and SB (Pro) can defend but I have positional advantage. I want fold equity and to avoid a cheap multiway flop, so I’ll open-raise to 300 (3x BB). If I get a large 3-bet I’ll fold (hand is marginal); if called I’ll play the flop aggressively from position and leverage the backdoor draws and initiative.

Minni is opening to 3x from the cutoff and Q7o is a dominated, low-playability hand versus her continuing range (AQ/KQ/QJ, better queens, broadways). Calling is marginal at best given the size and the likelihood we end up in awkward postflop spots, and 3-bet bluffing with Q7o is too loose with our current image and poor realization when called. With plenty of stack depth, we can pass and wait for better button spots.

I am in the Small Blind with Pocket Fives [5d 5c]. The action folded to Minni in the Cutoff who raises to 300 (3BB). Minni has been very active and opening wide throughout the tournament. I have a stack of 6900 (69 BB), which gives me sufficient implied odds to set-mine against a deep-stacked aggressive opponent, even out of position. The Big Blind is extremely short-stacked (1.5 BB) and will likely be all-in, but this does not significantly deter my plan to see a flop. Folding is too tight against this specific opponent's frequency, and 3-betting bloats the pot with a hand that plays poorly post-flop without a set. Therefore, calling to try and hit a set is the best option.

I have pocket 6s with only 150 chips left - essentially committed. The pot is 700 and I need to call 150, giving me great odds of nearly 5:1. Pocket pairs play well multiway and I could hit a set. With such a short stack, this is an easy all-in call.

I hold Pocket Fives on a Q-J-4 rainbow flop. This board texture heavily favors the pre-flop raiser (Minni), connecting well with broadway cards (AQ, KQ, QJ, AJ, KJ) that are well within their range. As the Small Blind, I am out of position. The Big Blind (Claude) is already all-in, so there is no betting leverage to be gained against him, and bluffing Minni into a fold on this texture is highly unlikely and dangerous given my weak underpair. My hand has very little equity to improve. The standard play is to check to the aggressor. If Minni bets, I will fold, as I am likely behind and drawing thin. My goal is to see a cheap showdown if the action checks through, but I will not commit more chips.

I hold 5♥7♥ on Jc‑4h‑Qs: no pair, only backdoor heart and runner‑runner straight possibilities — essentially a marginal hand. I opened preflop so a c‑bet is credible, but SB (Pro) is a sticky caller and Claude is effectively all‑in (can’t fold), so my fold equity is very low and a bet will often be called by Q/J/pairs/overcards. Building a big pot with almost no equity is a losing line. I’ll pot‑control and see a free turn: check to keep the pot small, realize my tiny equity, and only start betting/raising if I improve (heart, pair, or a very favorable turn) or if Pro shows clear weakness on a later street.

The turn is the 3c, making the board Jc 4h Qs 3c. I hold pocket fives (5d 5c). The active opponent, Minni, checked back the flop, which often signals a capped range (weak showdown value like middle pairs or weak top pairs) or a complete miss. The Big Blind (Claude) is already all-in, so there is no side pot currently. Leading out here would effectively turn my hand into a bluff, and it is unlikely to fold out better hands like 66-TT or a weak Jack. Checking allows me to potentially see a cheap showdown against the all-in player and keeps the pot small against Minni. If Minni bets, I will likely have to fold given my weak holdings.

Board Jc‑4h‑Qs‑3c; I have 5h7h — no pair and only tiny backdoor heart/runner‑runner straight outs. Pot is 850, my stack is deep relative to this pot and BB (Claude) is effectively all‑in, so my fold equity is essentially zero and SB (Pro) has been a sticky caller. Betting here would build a pot I have almost no chance to win unless I improve, and a small bet will usually be called by hands that already beat me (Qs/Jx/pairs/overcards). I will pot‑control and see the river for free to realize my tiny equity and preserve my stack. If I hit a pair or a heart/straight on the river I can take a more aggressive line; otherwise I’ll check‑fold to large river aggression.

I hold a pair of Fives on a Q-J-4-3-9 board. The action has checked through on the flop and turn, suggesting the cutoff (Minni) is weak or potting control with marginal showdown value. The Big Blind is all-in, so I am competing for the main pot and a negligible side pot. Betting here has no value as worse hands (A-high, K-high) will fold and better hands (any pair of 6s+, or a matched board card) will call. Checking gives me the best chance to win at showdown against missed broadway cards or lower pocket pairs without risking more chips. If Minni bets, I will fold.

I missed — 5♥7♥ has no pair and only a tiny backdoor/runner-runner possibility on Jc‑4h‑Qs‑3c‑9s. Pot is 850 and Claude is effectively all‑in, so I have virtually no fold equity versus the remaining callers (Pro is a known sticky caller). Betting here will be called by hands that already beat me (Qx, Jx, pairs) and will rarely fold better hands, so the expected return on a bluff is poor. I’ll pot‑control and check to the showdown; if faced with significant river aggression I will fold rather than commit chips with no real equity.

UTG position with 6h Jc offsuit. This is a speculative, weak hand with poor playability, dominated potential, and low equity against typical raising ranges. Standard play is to fold preflop to avoid tough postflop decisions and preserve stack. No history or dynamics justify entering the pot here.

I have 8♦6♣ in middle position — a weak, unsuited, disconnected hand with poor playability out of position. There has been no raise (pot 150) but I still have button, small and big blind to act after me; both the button and big blind have healthy stacks and will defend or 3‑bet frequently. Limping or opening OOP with 86o invites multiway action and gets frequently dominated, so it's not a profitable spot. Preserve my stack and wait for a better hand/position, so fold.

We’re first in from the cutoff after two folds, so this is a classic steal spot, but Tc3h is simply too far down the hand ladder (dominated when called, poor playability, and no suit). With a very short SB (700) who can jam over an open and a capable BB who can defend/3-bet, the EV of opening this specific offsuit combo is not worth it. We can pass and preserve our 74bb stack for better cutoff opens.

I am on the Button with K7o, which is a marginal hand. The action has folded to me. Normally, this is a standard steal opportunity. However, the Small Blind (Claude) is extremely short-stacked with only 700 chips (7 BB). If I raise, he is priced into shoving with a very wide range, and I would be mathematically committed to calling off a portion of my stack with a hand that is often dominated (by Ax, better Kx) or flipping. Additionally, the Big Blind (Flash) has a massive chip lead (172 BB) and could easily defend or 3-bet, putting me in a difficult spot post-flop or forcing a fold. Given the high risk of a shove from the SB and the threat of the BB, coupled with the poor playability of unsuited K7, the disciplined professional play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I have pocket 5s in SB with only 700 chips (7 BBs) - a short stack. Everyone folded to me, so it's heads-up vs BB. With this stack size, pocket pairs are a clear shove. Going all-in for maximum fold equity and value.

I am in the Big Blind with pocket Tens (Th, Ts), which is an extremely strong starting hand, especially in a heads-up situation against the Small Blind. Claude has raised to 700 and has only 50 chips remaining in their stack. My goal is to maximize value and potentially eliminate the opponent if they are short-stacked. Given that Claude is essentially all-in, I will raise to 750 to commit the rest of their chips now and see all five cards.
I'm getting 29:1 pot odds to call 50 into 1450. With pocket 5s, even against a premium hand I have ~18% equity. This is a trivial call - I'm already essentially all-in.

5-handed UTG is still the worst position postflop, and 98o is a marginal offsuit connector with weak high-card value and poor playability when we get 3-bet or called by strong/broadway-heavy ranges. Given the table’s tendency to raise/3-bet and defend blinds, opening this hand will often create tough, negative-EV spots out of position and can lead to reverse implied-odds situations when we make dominated pairs/straights. I’ll take the disciplined fold and wait for a better open.

I am holding 2h 5c in UTG+1 (effectively the Cutoff in a 5-handed game). This is one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em, with no connectivity and no suited potential. Facing a fold from UTG, there is no strategic justification to enter the pot with this hand given my stack depth of 67.5 BBs. The correct play is to fold immediately.

I am on the button with 6s 3s. While this is a suited hand, it is extremely weak and bottom-of-the-range for an open. Given that both Elon (SB) and Minni (BB) have shown significant activity and aggression in previous hands, they are likely to defend their blinds or 3-bet, making a steal attempt with such a low-equity hand unprofitable. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Unopened pot folded to small blind with deep stacks (132BB effective). Standard aggressive strategy in SB vs BB is to raise a wide range (~60-70% of hands) to apply pressure and steal the dead money (150 in pot). Q2o is a playable steal hand: Queen is a decent high card blocker, sufficient equity (~40%+) versus typical BB defending range, and post-flop playability OOP heads-up. Minni has defended BB before but folds often enough for +EV steal. Raising to 3BB (300 total) balances value and bluffs while maintaining fold equity. Avoid limping OOP with marginal hand; folding too tight misses steal EV in tournament.

I call to 300. Facing a 3BB open from the small blind, T5h is worth defending from the big blind — it's suited with backdoor heart potential and reasonable showdown/playability. The pot is 400 and calling 200 more (300 total committed) gives ~3:1 pot odds, and with deep stacks (~143 BB) I can realize equity and play postflop without bloating the pot. I prefer a flat call to keep weaker hands in and to see a flop; plan is to continue with top pair, two pair, or strong draws, and to fold or control the pot on dry boards where I'm missed or face heavy aggression.

Flopped bottom pair (2s) with Queen kicker plus strong Qd flush draw on a Td 2d 6h board. This is a monster hand with ~35-50% equity vs typical BB calling ranges (Ax, mid pairs, suited connectors, diamond draws). Board is drawy (flush draw, gutshots like J9), so betting protects my hand, denies opponent equity, and extracts value from worse made hands (6x, T9s) or weaker draws. As preflop raiser OOP, continuation bet is standard to represent strength and maintain initiative. Minni's BB calling range is wide but not crushing; we've seen her defend wide previously. Pot 600, betting 400 (2/3 pot) builds pot for when we hit flush/straights while allowing multi-street play with deep stacks (~130BB). Checking risks giving free cards to overcards/draws or facing a check-raise. No reason to slowplay OOP.

Heads-up vs Elon (SB) who opened and made a small flop stab (400 into a 1,000 pot). I have T5 hearts — top pair (ten) with a weak kicker but a backdoor heart draw. His small c‑bet represents a wide range (missed overcards, draws, small pairs, and some tens). Raising is an option to charge floats/draws, but a small raise here will bloat the pot with a mediocre kicker and can get me 3‑bet or committed on later streets unnecessarily. Calling 400 keeps worse hands and bluffs in, gets correct pot odds to see a turn, and lets me re‑evaluate on the next card. Plan: call now; on a safe turn (heart, 5, T) extract value; if a threatening card or heavy aggression appears, fold/control the pot accordingly.

On the turn, the board is 6h Td 2d Ah. I hold Qd 2c for bottom pair (twos) with a queen kicker and no relevant draws (only three diamonds total, no flush draw). Preflop, I raised wide from SB and Minni defended her BB. I c-bet flop and she called, which caps her range somewhat but still includes Ax (especially diamond flush draws like AdXd), Tx, 6x, sets, two-pair combos, and some straight draws/gutshots. The Ah turn is a strong card that hits her likely calling range hard (top pair Ax, runner-runner flush draws improve to heart flush draws, possible straight with KT). My bottom pair is highly vulnerable OOP with no showdown value against improved hands and little protection from further draws (remaining diamonds/hearts). Stacks are deep (~130BB effective), so no need to commit with a marginal hand. Betting risks getting raised by Ax or better and folding loses value; thin value betting gets called by better. Checking allows pot control, realizes equity vs bluffs/missed draws, and can induce bluffs on river or check back showdown if she is weak. If she bets large, likely folding; small bet can call to realize equity or control pot. This balances my wide SB range and exploits Minni's history of calling stations but folding to aggression when beat.

Check. I have Th5h — I was frozen to call the flop and that got me top pair with a very weak kicker. The turn Ace is a dangerous overcard that turns a marginal, kicker‑dependent hand into a likely second‑best holding vs many parts of Elon's range (he opened and c‑bet the flop). Elon’s check on the turn is ambiguous (either a missed continuation, pot‑control with an Ace, or a trap), so betting here would bloat the pot and commit me with a weak kicker while getting called or check‑raised by better hands (Ax, sets). Checking controls the pot, keeps bluffs in, and lets me see the river for free. Plan: evaluate the river — if I hit a heart/5/T I can extract thin value; if it’s a blank and Elon fires a large barrel I’ll give up to avoid losing a big chunk with a marginal hand. Pot ~1,400, stack ~13,775 — no reason to inflate the pot now with T5h.

River Qh pairs my Qd, upgrading me to two pair (queens and deuces) on a 6h Td 2d Ah Qh board. No straights possible (no combinations like TJQKA or T9876 complete without missing cards). Possible heart flushes (3h on board) or diamond flushes beat me, but my preflop raise + flop c-bet range (wide SB) includes strong hands like sets, two pairs, Ax. Minni's BB defense and flop call range: Ax (AdXd draws, AHx), Tx, 6x, pairs/draws. She checked turn after scary Ah (hits Ax), suggesting not super strong, but could have top pair Ax, T6/T2 two pair (worse), or missed draws planning to bluff/check. OOP on river, but two pair has high showdown value and gets called by worse (Ax weak kicker, Tx/6x, AQo would have bet turn probably). Pot 1400, deep stacks (~125BB), bet 3/4 pot (1050) for value/extraction from 1-2 streets' worth while denying bluffs and building pot. Avoids check-back losing to Ax or facing bet; balanced as value-heavy from my range after c-bet/call/check/check. Minni calls light historically (e.g., Hands 8,29,35). Fold equity low needed since strong.

The river Qh completes a heart flush for me — Th5h makes A‑Q‑T‑6‑5 hearts. This is a very strong hand but not the nut: only hands that beat me are two‑heart hole combos for Elon that produce a higher flush (any combo including a K or J heart, etc.), which are relatively rare. He opened preflop, c‑bet the flop, checked the Ace on the turn, and now fires a medium river bet (1,050). The pot is 2,450 and calling 1,050 makes the final pot ~3,500, so I need ~30% equity to justify a call. The probability Elon actually has a two‑heart combo that beats my flush is well below that threshold, so this is +EV to call for value. I will not raise — a raise bloats the pot and risks getting called (or check‑raised) by the few higher flush combos; folding is conceding a large equity advantage. Call 1,050.

I am in UTG position holding 6c 4s, which is a very weak off-suit hand (6-high). With four players remaining to act behind me, including several large stacks, opening this hand is extremely -EV. I have a healthy stack of 67.5 BBs and should preserve it for better spots. The correct play is a straightforward fold.

Queen-Ten offsuit is a marginal opening hand in early position, even in a five-handed game. Given that there are aggressive players behind me and a relatively short stack in the big blind who may look for spots to shove, folding is a disciplined choice to maintain my chip lead and avoid unnecessary variance with a hand that plays poorly against 3-bets.

Folded to me on the button with KQs offsuit, a strong hand in this spot with position on both blinds. Standard play is to raise for value and to steal the dead money in the pot (150). Minni and FiveTwo have shown aggression but also folding tendencies. Raising to 3x the BB (300) builds the pot while keeping fold equity and allowing postflop control. Deep stacks support this sizing.

Elon opened to 300 from the button (standard 3x). I have A♠4♣ in the small blind — an ace blocker and some showdown value but a weak kicker out of position. With my 16175 stack (deep) this hand is not strong enough to 3-bet for value OOP against a button raiser; a light 3-bet would bloat the pot and put me in tough spots versus calls or 4-bets. Best line is to defend by calling the extra 250 (300 total) to keep the pot manageable, see the flop, and play postflop based on texture. Plan: on an A-high flop play for value/protection (lead or donk-bet when appropriate); with backdoor equity check-call small bets; on dry misses fold to large pressure. If faced with a sizable preflop 3-bet I’ll fold this hand. Calling preserves SPR and allows exploitation postflop.

Button opens to 3bb and SB flats, giving me 700 in the middle and only 200 more to continue. I’m getting ~3.5:1 immediate odds, so I don’t need much equity to defend. T5o is weak and plays poorly out of position, so I’m not interested in squeezing; but folding is too tight given the price and the likelihood both opponents are wide. I’ll take the cheap defend and play fit-or-fold postflop, avoiding big pots when I make marginal pairs.

Called preflop with A♠4♣ to keep the pot small and preserve SPR. Flop 3♥2♦3♦ is a paired board and I’ve missed — I only have Ace-high and an Ace blocker. Out of position vs the button, I want to control the pot size and keep options open, so checking is best. I have some showdown value so I can call small continuation bets to see a turn; if an Ace or a harmless card comes I can apply pressure. I’ll call modest bets (probe bets up to roughly ~1/2 pot) and fold to large pressure that strongly represents sets/full houses/quads. If checked around I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and look to extract value if an Ace appears.

Multiway on a low paired flop (3-2-3) our hand is complete air with no meaningful backdoors. While BB can have more trips, leading as a bluff here is high-variance and likely to get called/float-raised, especially with two opponents. Best is to check, keep the pot small, and continue only if we get an unusually cheap price later.

Preflop raise with KQo from button is standard for value and position. Flop 3h-2d-3d is dry and paired, favoring my range as the preflop raiser. No piece of the board, but both opponents check, indicating weakness. Their calling ranges preflop include broadways, suited connectors, Ax – many miss this flop. Diamond flush draw is possible but board is uncoordinated otherwise. C-bet for value against weaker pairs/A-high and to deny equity to draws/gutshots. Sizing 600 (2/3 pot) balances protection, fold equity, and pot building without overcommitting on a missed flop. Position allows control postflop. Opponents' histories show Minni calls light but folds to aggression; FiveTwo similar. Deep stacks support this.

Call 600. I flat-called preflop to keep the pot small with A♠4♣; the flop 3♥-2♦-3♦ misses me but Elon’s bet is small (600 into a 1500 pot ≈ 40%). With an Ace blocker and some showdown value, plus the pot odds (600 to win ~2700 → ~22% break-even), this is defendable vs a wide button c-bet — and the BB has also called, so his bet range is likely still fairly wide. Plan: see a turn card. If I pair an Ace or 4 I can apply pressure/get value; if the turn is a blank and Elon applies significant pressure (large bet/raise) I’ll fold since the board strongly favors sets/full houses. Calling controls the pot size and keeps options open post‑flop.

We’re facing a large c-bet (600) into a multiway pot on 3-2-3 with T5o and essentially no equity realization: no flush/backdoor flush, no meaningful straight backdoors, and our “outs” to a 5 or T make very marginal pairs that are often dominated by any pocket pair, any 3x/2x, and even many A-high floats that can apply pressure. With Minni already calling, the line is heavily weighted toward at least one player having real showdown value, and we need ~22% equity to call (600 to win ~2700), which we won’t have here. Preserve stack and avoid spewing in a low-EV spot.

I hold A♠4♣. After calling Elon’s small flop raise on 3♥-2♦-3♦, the Q♣ on the turn is a blank for me — I still have only Ace-high. Elon’s line (bet on the flop, now checking the turn) looks like pot-control with Qx/top pair, a missed draw, or a trap with a set. I don’t have fold equity and betting here would be a thin bluff that will often get called by better hands or raised by strong hands; raising would be reckless with no equity. The correct, low-variance line is to control the pot and gather information: check and see how Elon acts on the river. Plan: fold to significant turn aggression; call very small bets only if pot odds and blocker reasoning justify; if I pair an Ace on the river I can look for value. Action: check to keep the pot manageable.

Turn board pairs the Queens, giving me top pair top kicker (KQ) with the Qc. This is a strong holding on a dry paired board (3h2d3dQc) where Minni's SB calling range vs my button raise includes sets (unlikely given call sizing), two-pair combos like 32, diamond flush draws, weaker pairs, or stubborn Ax/high cards. She checked flop (weakness), called my c-bet (possible draw or pair), and checks turn again (no strong hand likely improved much; Q might help some but her check suggests no Q). Position allows me to bet for thin value against worse pairs/draws/A-high while protecting against gutshots/river draws. Pot 2100, bet 1400 (~2/3 pot) sizes well for value, denies equity, and builds pot with deep stacks (my 10625, her 15325). History shows Minni calls light but vulnerable to aggression postflop. Checking back risks giving free cards to draws or bluffs.

I hold A♠4♣ out of position on a paired 3-2-3 board with the Q♣ turn — still only Ace-high. Pot = 3500; Elon bets 1400; calling requires ~28.6% equity (1400/(3500+1400)=1400/4900).
