My clean outs are limited (three Aces + three 4s = up to 6 outs) and many of those do not fare well against the hands that keep betting (sets, two‑pair, or Qx). Elon’s line (button open, small c‑bet 600, turn bet 1400) is very consistent with value (Qx, 2x/3x, sets) and his history shows frequent multi‑street aggression. I have virtually no fold equity and no good play to extract value; raising would be reckless and calling is a negative‑expectation call given my equity vs his continuing range. Preserve chips and choose better spots — fold.

I am holding Ad Kc, which is a premium starting hand and one of the strongest in 5-handed play. Being Under the Gun (UTG), I need to set the tone of the hand and build a pot. Raising is the standard play here to extract value and thin the field. A 3x big blind raise to 300 is consistent with standard opening sizes and appropriate for my stack size.

Facing a UTG raise to 300 from Flash, who has shown aggression in previous hands but an UTG open indicates a strong range (likely top 15-20% of hands). My 7s6h is a weak offsuit connector with poor high-card strength and limited playability out of position. Calling would commit 300 into a 750 pot (after call), but postflop I'll be OOP against a strong range, with dominated equity and reverse implied odds if I hit. Raising is off-table given the strength disparity and multiway potential. Folding preserves stack for better spots; speculative hands like this are for late position or blind defense. Deep stacks don't change this fundamental EV-negative decision.

Flash opened UTG to 300 in a 5-handed pot. I’m on the button with Q♦J♣ and ~153bb effective. QJo has good playability in position — can make top pair, second pair with decent kickers, and broadway straights. 3-betting is an option, but I’d prefer to 3-bet with stronger broadways or suited connectors; a 3-bet here would bloat the pot unnecessarily and could invite uncomfortable 4-bets. Calling 300 keeps the pot manageable while preserving positional advantage. Plan: fold to a large 4‑bet, otherwise see the flop and either take the initiative with c-bets on favorable textures or control the pot and realize equity on dryer boards.

We’re in the small blind facing an UTG open (300) and a button flat, so ranges are already fairly strong and we’d be out of position postflop against at least one player. 43o has terrible equity realization, no high-card value, and very poor playability multiway. A squeeze is also unattractive: we have no blockers, little fold equity versus two players, and we’d be risking chips with a hand that can’t continue vs a 4-bet or sticky callers. Best EV is to preserve the stack and fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 7h5h, facing a raise to 300 (3BB) from UTG and a call from the Button. The Small Blind has folded. The pot currently contains 750 chips (300+300+50+100), and I need to call 200 more to see the flop. This gives me pot odds of 200 to win 950, or roughly 4.75:1 (approx 21% equity required). My hand, a suited one-gapper, has decent playability and flush/straight potential. Given my stack depth of ~66BB and the excellent price, defending the Big Blind is the standard profitable play here, despite being out of position against two players.

I have 7h5h on a dry board of 2h 2d Qs. I completely missed the flop, holding only 7-high with a backdoor flush draw. Out of position against two opponents (the UTG raiser and the BTN caller), my hand has very little equity. The board texture favors the pre-flop aggressor's range (Queens, broadways, pairs). Leading out (donk betting) would likely be burning chips. The standard and correct play is to check and fold to any significant aggression.

I am the pre-flop aggressor with Ad Kc. The flop is 2h 2d Qs, which is a very dry, paired board. While I missed the flop, this board texture heavily favors my perceived range as the UTG raiser, as I am more likely to have overpairs or a strong Queen (like AQ) than my opponents. Pro has checked to me. A continuation bet here is standard to take down the pot immediately or to thin the field. I'll bet roughly 42% of the pot (400) to maintain initiative and force hands like smaller pairs or weaker high-card combinations to make a decision. If I am called, I still have two overcards to improve on the turn.

I have top pair (Qs) with a decent J kicker on a dry board of 2h 2d Qs; only hands that realistically beat me are sets (22) or better queens (Qx with A/K), while I’m ahead of most of Flash’s c-bet range (AQ/KQ/QJ/KT/Ax/Kx and many bluffs). Flash’s bet is small (~400 into 1350), which often represents a wide or probing range, so I want to take the initiative in position and extract value while protecting my hand. I’ll make a pot-pressure min-raise to 800 to charge worse Qx and weaker one-pair hands and to fold out some equity (and gather information). Plan: if I face a large, polarized 3-bet I’ll give up (most large 3-bets here represent sets/very strong hands); if called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and continue extracting or pot-control depending on the card and opponent’s action.

I hold 7h5h on a board of 2h 2d Qs. I have missed the flop entirely, holding only 7-high with a weak backdoor flush draw. The action on the flop has been heavy: the UTG player bet 400, and the Button raised to 800. This raise typically signifies strength (likely trips or a strong Queen), and I am caught in the middle out of position with no immediate equity. There is no justification to continue. I must fold immediately.

The board is extremely dry (2h 2d Qs). My continuation bet of 400 was met with a minimum raise to 800 by Minni. This 'min-click' often represents a range of a marginal Queen, a pocket pair trying to see where it stands, or a pure bluff on a board that is difficult to hit. Given that I only need to call 400 more into a pot of 1,750 (providing pot odds of roughly 4.4 to 1), I have the correct price to see a turn card. I still have two overcards (Ace and King) which could give me the best hand on the turn. Folding AK here to a minimum raise on such a dry board would be too exploitable.

On the turn, the Jack of Diamonds (Jd) is an overcard that doesn't improve my Ace-high. After calling Minni's minimum raise on the flop, I need to proceed with caution. Minni's line (min-raising the flop and then acting on the turn) suggests either a Queen, a slow-played Deuce, or a semi-bluff. As the out-of-position player, checking is the standard and most prudent play here. I want to see if Minni follows through with another bet. If she checks behind, I might still have the best hand or can potentially see a free river card. If she bets, I will have to evaluate the sizing, but I am leaning towards a fold as my Ace-high has limited showdown value against a range that raised the flop.

I have Q♦J♣ on 2♥2♦Q♠J♦ — two pair (Q+J). This is a very strong showdown hand but is only behind sets (22, JJ, QQ). Flash (UTG) opened and made a small flop c‑bet (400) that I min‑raised to 800; he called. His turn check looks like pot‑control with Qx or a float, or a trap with a set. There are far more Qx/Ax/KQ combos in his calling range than sets, so a value bet is +EV. I want to charge worse one‑pair hands (Qx, AJ/AQ, some KQ) while keeping the pot controllable vs a potential set. Bet 1,600 (~62% of the 2,550 pot) — large enough to get value and fold out bluffs, but small enough that a large polarized check‑raise (pot‑sized or shove) is a clear fold. If he check‑raises big I’ll concede (he’s very likely a set); if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate the river and extract further on blanks while being cautious if the board pairs or gives full‑house possibilities.

On the turn, the Jack of Diamonds (Jd) is an overcard that doesn't improve my Ad Kc. Minni has shown significant aggression by min-raising my flop continuation bet and now firing a 63% pot bet (1600 into 2550) on the turn. While the flop min-raise could have been a probe or a bluff, the follow-through on the turn suggests a strong hand, likely a Queen (AQ, KQ) or possibly a slow-played Deuce. Even if I call and catch an Ace or King on the river, I might still be behind a hand like QJ or a set of deuces. Calling here with only Ace-high out of position against a showing of strength is unprofitable in the long run. I will fold and preserve my stack.

Pocket tens is a premium hand from UTG in a 5-handed game. It plays well multiway but raising thins the field and builds the pot for value. Standard opening size of 3x the big blind (300) is appropriate given the deep stacks and player tendencies from history showing aggressive play from several opponents like Flash and Minni. This size denies equity to weaker hands and sets up post-flop play.

Fold. Q♥4♥ from UTG+1 is a very weak, often-dominated holding facing an UTG open to 300. Elon’s UTG raise represents a strong range and we’d be out of position postflop, often facing continuation bets or multiway action that crushes our equity. The hand only has backdoor potential and a poor kicker — not worth committing 300 into a 450 pot when we can wait for a better spot or position. No reason to 3-bet here.

We’re on the button with AcAs facing an UTG open to 300 at 50/100. Against an UTG range, AA is far ahead and wants to build a pot for stacks while denying equity to the blinds (especially with a big-stack BB who could overcall/squeeze if we flat). A 3-bet also sets up a comfortable SPR to get the money in across most flops.

Sizing: raise to 1100 (about 3.7x the open) to charge dominated hands, reduce multiway likelihood, and make future stack-off lines straightforward with our ~71bb stack.

I am in the Small Blind with Ad 3d. The action started with Elon (UTG) raising to 300, followed by a 3-bet from FiveTwo (Button) to 1100. Facing an UTG open and a Button 3-bet, my hand (A3s) is too marginal to call out of position. Cold-calling would leave me vulnerable to a squeeze or 4-bet from the original raiser, and I would be playing a bloated pot out of position likely dominated by better Aces or pairs. 4-bet jamming is too risky given the UTG opener is still in the hand. The prudent play is to fold and preserve my stack.

I am holding QJs in the Big Blind. Elon opened from UTG, which typically indicates a strong range. FiveTwo then 3-bet from the Button. While QJs is a strong starting hand, I am out of position against both players. Furthermore, if I call, I still have to worry about Elon potentially 4-betting or shoving. Given FiveTwo's history of frequent 3-betting, he might be wide, but the presence of the UTG raiser makes this a risky spot to commit 1,000 chips (10 BB) with a hand that is easily dominated by AK, AQ, or big pairs. I will preserve my stack and find a better opportunity to engage.

Pocket Tens remains very strong facing a button 3-bet over my UTG open. FiveTwo's position suggests a polarized range: value like QQ+/AK or bluffs/light calls like suited connectors, Ax, lower pairs. TT has excellent equity against the bluffing portion and is often ahead of their calling range. Folding is too tight; flat-calling is playable but leaves me OOP multiway if blinds wake up, and pot grows awkwardly. 4-betting isolates the aggressive button, applies pressure on their wide range, builds a pot with a premium hand, and denies equity to speculative hands. Sizing to ~2.7x their 1100 (3000 total) is standard: large enough to fold out junk but not overcommitting. My deep stack (124BB) and their 60BB support this; history shows FiveTwo folds to heavy aggression (e.g., Hand 2, 11, 21). Post-flop OOP is fine with TT's playability if called.

We have AcAs versus an UTG 4-bet to 3000 after our 3-bet. AA is miles ahead of Elon's value range (KK/QQ/AK) and also wants to keep any light 4-bets/bluffs in. With stacks, calling leaves us ~3000 behind into a ~6150 pot (very low SPR), so we’re effectively committed anyway and can comfortably get the rest in on most flops (call his jam or jam when checked to). Shoving now risks folding out the exact bluffs/marginal hands we dominate without gaining much strategically given the SPR.

Pocket Tens remains an overpair on a highly coordinated flop of 3s7s5s, which is extremely wet with a flush draw, open-enders (e.g., 4-6, 2-4, 6-8), and wheel draw potential. As the preflop 4-bettor OOP heads-up against FiveTwo's shorter stack (41BB left), their calling range after a UTG open and button 3-bet is strong and polarized: primarily QQ+/AK/AQs+ (value) with some bluffs like Ax suited or broadway suited connectors, but light calls OOP post-4-bet are rare deepstacked. This board hits their suited speculative range hard, especially spades. TT blocks sets poorly (only 77/55/33 possible, unlikely), has no draw, and is vulnerable to any spade. Equity vs. their value range is poor (behind QQ+/AKo often), decent vs. draws (~55-65% crush), but they likely don't 4-bet/call draws pre. C-betting risks getting shoved on by made hands/draws/semis, forcing tough calls with 4100 behind into 10k+ pots where I'm often behind. Folding to a bet is fine (-EV but controlled), but betting commits too much for protection/value on a board they crush parts of. Checking induces bluffs/checkbacks from AKo/missed broadways, controls pot, and allows folding to aggression without spew. Tournament stage (hand 49/150) favors survival; FiveTwo folds to pressure historically (e.g., Hands 2,11,21,32), but here they invested heavily pre—check preserves stack for better spots.

Flop comes monotone spades and with As in hand we already have the nut flush (only 4s6s makes a straight flush that beats us). After the 4-bet pre, SPR is tiny (we have 4100 into 6150), and Elon checking caps him a bit / invites a stab. Best play is to jam: we get maximum value from any hand containing a spade (KK/QQ/AK with a spade), from overpairs that may feel priced-in, and we deny equity to hands that might peel one card and potentially boat up on runouts. With the As blocker, he cannot have the nuts, so his check-call range is wider against an all-in.

Preflop, TT is a strong UTG open and 4-bet over FiveTwo's button 3-bet, as it has excellent equity against their polarized range and deep stacks allow it. However, the monotone spade flop (3s7s5s) is disastrous: extremely draw-heavy with flush draws, open-enders (4-6, 2-4-6, 6-8), and wheel potential, hitting FiveTwo's suited speculative range (suited connectors, Ax suited spades) from their 3-bet/call spot hard. TT is an overpair with no spade blockers, blocking few sets (only 55/77 possible in range, 33 unlikely), and vulnerable to any turn spade or completed straight. FiveTwo's check-raise to 4100 (40% pot) into the preflop aggressor OOP suggests strength: value like sets/two-pair, strong flush draws with overcards (AQs, KJs, etc.), or nutted floats. Their range rarely includes naked overpairs or weak draws here after heavy preflop investment. Equity vs. value <50%, vs. draws okay but they bet for value/protection. Calling commits ~42% of remaining stack into a pot where I'm often behind and drawing thin-ish; shoving risks 56BB left into bigger pot dominated. Folding is -EV in isolation but +EV long-term: preserves 97BB stack for hand 49/150 in multiway tournament, exploits future spots vs. aggressive opponents like FiveTwo (folds to pressure in Hands 2,11,21,32). Survival > variance here; better spots await.

UTG with 9♥2♥ is one of the weakest starting hands — very poor playability and highly likely to be dominated. We're opening into three players to act (and several are aggressive based on recent hands), with a to-call of 100 into a 150 pot; calling or raising from early position would put us out of position postflop and expose a big portion of our 16,775 stack to 3-bets. This is not a profitable spot to invest chips. Preserve the stack and wait for a much stronger hand or a later position spot. Fold.

5-handed and first in after Minni folds, KJo is comfortably within an opening range. Limping invites the blinds to realize equity cheaply and gives up initiative; raising lets us pick up blinds/antes immediately and play in position versus the blinds when called. I’ll use a modest open size to keep risk down while still applying pressure and building a pot with a hand that flops decent top-pair and straight potential.

I hold Ts7c on the Button, facing a 2.5BB raise from FiveTwo in UTG+1. Ten-Seven offsuit is a very weak hand with poor playability and is dominated by most of the opener's range. Despite having position, the hand is not strong enough to call or 3-bet bluff profitably, especially with a healthy stack of 64BB that I should preserve for better opportunities. The correct play is to fold.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Four suited. FiveTwo has raised from UTG+1 to 250. While K4s is a suited hand with some post-flop potential, it is very weak against a range that typically raises from early position. Calling would put me out of position for the rest of the hand against FiveTwo, who has been active and aggressive lately. Given the strength of the opener's position and my poor relative position, folding is the most disciplined and stack-preserving play here.

J3 offsuit is a trash hand with poor playability and low equity against an early position raising range from FiveTwo, who has shown aggression but still likely holds better cards here. Facing a raise to 250 (150 to call into 400 pot), the pot odds aren't sufficient given the out-of-position postflop play. Standard fold to preserve stack for premium spots in this deepstack tournament phase.

5-handed UTG (effectively HJ), 87o is at the very bottom of an opening range and performs poorly vs the fairly aggressive preflop tendencies shown (frequent opens/3-bets). If we open and get 3-bet we’re forced to fold; if we get called we’re often out of position with no high-card value. With 100bb+ there’s no need to take thin, high-variance spots here—preserve stack for better steal/3-bet opportunities.

I hold 4h 5d in UTG+1. This is an off-suit connector but a very weak one. With three players yet to act behind me and a relatively short stack compared to the field (64 BBs), playing this hand out of position is negative EV. The standard play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with pocket threes. In a 5-handed game, pocket pairs are definitely strong enough to open for a raise when folded to on the button. This puts pressure on the blinds and allows me to take control of the pot. A standard 3x open (300 chips) is consistent with my previous betting patterns and the general tournament flow.

Preflop from SB OOP facing a button raise to 300 (to call 250 into 450 pot). T9o is a marginal hand with some playability but offsuit, no suited connectors premium. Button ranges are wide, but postflop OOP is tough, especially vs aggressive Flash who has shown raise-fold equity in history (e.g., Hand 22, 45). BB Minni may defend wide, complicating multiway pots. Stack deep at 9575, early tournament (51/150), better to fold and preserve for premium spots rather than risk 250 OOP with ~30-40% equity realization needs. No strong blockers or 3-bet value. Standard fold.

I have A♠4♠ in the big blind facing a standard button open to 300. With deep stacks and a wide BTN opening this is a hand worth defending—I have an Ace blocker and nut-flush/backdoor equity. I'm out of position, so I don't want to inflate the pot with a marginal 3-bet; calling preserves my range and lets me realize equity postflop. Plan: call and evaluate the flop—continue aggressively on Ace or spade-heavy flops, otherwise proceed cautiously and fold to large pressure when not improving.

I have A♠4♠ on A♣6♥5♠ — top pair with a weak kicker and backdoor spade/backdoor nut-flush potential. The BTN opened wide preflop and is often aggressive; this flop is exactly the type I want to continue on to get value and protect. Checking gives the button a free opportunity to apply pressure or realize equity with many floats and draws. I will make a value/protection bet of about half the pot to charge worse A‑x, medium pairs and turn draws while keeping the pot manageable out of position. If he raises big I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold to extreme action vs strong line, call or shove depending on sizing and reads).

I raised pre-flop with pocket threes and the Big Blind (Minni) defended. The flop comes Ace-high (Ac-6h-5s), which is generally a good board for the pre-flop aggressor. However, Minni has led out with a 'donk bet' of 350 into the 650 pot. This lead often indicates a marginal hand like a pair of sixes, a small Ace, or a straight draw (like 74 or 87). While I am in position, my pocket threes are currently an underpair to the 6 and the Ace, and I have very little potential to improve other than hitting one of the two remaining threes. Calling here likely leads to facing more pressure on the turn, and raising to represent the Ace is risky against a player who led into me on this specific texture. Given the stack sizes and the early stage of the tournament, I will opt for a disciplined fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in UTG position with Jd 4h, which is an extremely weak hand (unsuited connector with a large gap and low high card value). With 64 BBs, there is no need to get involved with trash hands from early position. The standard and correct play is to fold immediately.

I am holding 72o in early position (UTG+1) in a 5-handed game. This is the statistically worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em and should never be played from this position. Folding is the only viable strategic option.

Preflop on the button with Td5s offsuit, a weak, unconnected hand with poor playability and dominated by many calling ranges. Two folds ahead, but blinds (Minni and FiveTwo) are deep-stacked and have shown aggression in prior hands, likely to defend or 3-bet wide. Standard play is to fold trash like this from late position rather than limp or raise into aggressive blinds. Preserves stack for better spots.

Heads-up vs the big blind with As-Jh — a strong, highly playable hand in this spot. I’m the small blind (button postflop) so I will have positional advantage after the flop. Min-raise to 200 is allowed but too small — it gives the BB good odds to see a flop and invites lots of cheap multiway play (not relevant here) or a light 3-bet. With deep stacks and only one opponent left, standard play is to open to ~2.5–3x the BB to take the initiative, deny equity to weaker hands, and build a pot with a hand that plays well postflop. I’ll raise to 300 (3x BB total). If called I’ll continue with positional advantage; if I face a large 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and villain tendencies.

Minni is opening from the small blind after folds (effectively a button-vs-BB spot), so her range is wide and we should defend aggressively. Ah2h is a great 3-bet candidate: it blocks strong Ax (reducing the chance she has AA/AK/AQ), has good playability (nut-flush potential and wheel draws), and benefits from taking initiative rather than flat-calling out of position and letting her realize equity with position. I’ll use my consistent sizing versus her SB opens to pressure her wide range and win the pot often preflop; if she 4-bets, this remains an easy fold.

I have A♠J♥ — a strong, playable hand and I’ll have position postflop. FiveTwo 3-bet to 1000 after my 300 opener. With deep stacks and position, the correct default is to call rather than 4-bet: AJo is frequently dominated by the stronger parts of a 3-bet range and plays well seeing a flop in position. Calling 700 into a 1300 pot gives ~35% pot odds and preserves maneuverability postflop. Plan: call to see the flop; if I hit an ace/jack or a strong draw I will apply pressure, otherwise proceed cautiously and fold to extreme aggression if the board is unfavorable.

I have A♠J♥ on 2♣-8♥-6♥: I missed the flop but have two overcards and a backdoor heart (J♥). I’m on the button (postflop positional advantage) and Villain (FiveTwo) 3‑bet preflop, so their range is weighted to strong hands. This is a fairly dry board and I don’t want to inflate the pot with a marginal hand out of position. Checking preserves stack, keeps my options open, and lets me call a reasonable c‑bet to realize ~25% equity to pair by the river. Plan: check — if they fire a small/standard c‑bet I’ll call and use position on the turn to continue when I improve or to control pot size; if they check back I’ll look to take the initiative on the turn with appropriate cards (or showdown a paired card). I’ll fold to large, polarizing aggression if I don’t improve.

We’re heads-up in a 3-bet pot with initiative and Minni has checked to us on a fairly dynamic board (2c-8h-6h). With Ah2h we have bottom pair plus the nut-flush draw (and some wheel/runout equity), so we’re in great shape to bet: we deny equity from her overcards, get value/protection versus weaker pairs and draws, and start building a pot for when we make the nut flush or improve. A smaller c-bet also keeps our range wide and lets us comfortably continue if she check-raises.

I have A♠J♥ on 2♣-8♥-6♥: two overcards and a backdoor heart. Villain (BB, preflop 3‑bettor) made a small probe bet of 700 into a 2700 pot. Calling is the right play: I get ~20.6% pot odds (700/(2700+700)) and my chance to hit at least an A or J by the river is ~24–26% plus a small backdoor flush/backdoor equity. I have positional advantage, so calling preserves maneuverability — I can fold to large turn aggression if I miss, or apply pressure / value-bet when I pair up or pick up hearts. Raising here would bloat the pot with only overcards and invite a big shove or re-raise from a 3‑bet caller; folding is too tight given my equity and position. Plan: call 700, re-evaluate on the turn — continue when I improve or when villain shows weakness.

I hold A♠J♥ on 2♣-8♥-6♥-K♦ — still no pair, just two overcards and no completed heart draw. Villain (FiveTwo) 3‑betted preflop and barreled the flop, so their range is weighted to strong hands and also includes draws; the K on the turn helps a lot of their continuing range (AK, Kx). There is currently no bet to call and I have position, so the prudent line is to check and keep the pot under control rather than bluff‑bet into a likely stronger or calling range. Checking preserves fold equity on the river, lets me realize my equity, and keeps options open to either call a reasonable river bet, fold to big turn aggression, or apply pressure only if I improve or detect clear weakness. I will check and re‑evaluate on the river.

Turn brings the Kd, which is a very good card for our 3-bet range advantage (we credibly have AK/KQ/overpairs) and a pressure point for Minni’s flop-calling range (a lot of 8x/6x, medium pairs, and draws). With Ah2h we also have strong real equity: nut-flush draw plus a pair, so betting accomplishes (1) fold equity versus her unpaired hands and some weaker pairs, (2) denial versus overcards, and (3) pot-building for when we hit the nut flush. I’ll use a medium sizing that keeps her in with worse/draws but still applies meaningful pressure, and it also preserves our ability to fold if she check-raises all-in (where our equity is usually insufficient).

Fold. FiveTwo 3‑betted preflop and barreled the flop; the turn K significantly helps his continuing range (AK, Kx, big pairs). He bet 1,900 into a 5,300 pot — I must discount my chance to win unless I improve. Pot odds to call = 1,900 / (5,300+1,900) ≈ 26.4%. My realistic outs on the river are the three remaining Aces and three Jacks = 6 outs → 6/46 ≈ 13.0%, well below the required equity. The backdoor heart route is dead after the K♦ turn, and many of my outs are “dirty” vs likely hands. Raising as a bluff is unappealing: villain’s line is value‑heavy and will often call or shove, putting me in a dominated, low‑equity spot. With >15k chips and better spots ahead, preserving the stack is the correct +EV choice.

I am dealt Seven and Eight of Diamonds (78s) in the Under the Gun (UTG) position. In a 5-handed game, this is equivalent to the Hijack or Cutoff in a full-ring game, making it a viable opening hand to maintain a balanced and aggressive range. Opening here allows me to take control of the pot and provides excellent post-flop playability and board coverage. I will stick to my standard open-raising size of 300 chips (3x the big blind) to keep my play consistent with previous hands where I've held stronger values.

Flash raised to 300 from UTG, which is a strong early position and suggests a strong range (pairs, broadway cards, suited connectors, etc.). My hand 9h4d is offsuit, disconnected, low cards with minimal playability or equity against that range. Even out of position or multiway, it has no value for calling or raising. Position is UTG+1, still early. Stacks are deep, but folding trash preserves stack for better spots. Long-term tournament strategy prioritizes discipline over speculative plays.

Button with A♣K♦ vs an UTG open to 300 — this is a clear 3‑bet spot. I want value and fold equity, plus to avoid a multiway pot where AK loses equity. A raise to 1000 (~3.3x the open) pressures weaker aces and broadways, discourages speculative calls from the blinds, and keeps the shortish BB (Pro, 6.3k) from automatically committing. If I get called I’ll use position to c‑bet favorable flops; if I face a large 4‑bet I’ll re‑evaluate (fold to extreme sizing, call or shove against a short‑stack jam depending on exact size). Overall 3‑betting maximizes my EV here.

We’re in the small blind facing an UTG open and a button 3-bet to 10bb. 9♣5♦ is far too weak to defend out of position in a bloated pot, with high risk of the opener continuing and us getting squeezed/forced into tough postflop spots with dominated equity. No blockers, poor playability, and negative EV versus these ranges, so we simply cut losses.

I am holding Ten of Hearts and Jack of Spades in the Big Blind. The action involves an UTG raise from Flash followed by a 3-bet to 1000 from Minni on the Button. JTo is a marginal hand that plays very poorly against a UTG opening range and a Button 3-betting range, as I am likely dominated by hands like AJ, AT, KJ, QJ, and overpairs. Additionally, I am out of position and the original raiser (Flash) is still left to act behind me, creating a risk of a 4-bet. Investing nearly 15% of my stack to see a flop in this spot is not profitable. The disciplined play is to fold.

I opened with 78s from UTG, which is a standard aggressive play in a 5-handed game. However, Minni has 3-bet to 1000 from the Button. While 78s has good playability, calling a 3-bet out of position against a player who has shown consistent aggression (as seen in Hand #3, Hand #8, Hand #13, etc.) is generally a losing play over the long term, especially when the 3-bet size is large. Folding here preserves my stack and avoids a difficult post-flop situation where I'm likely behind and out of position.

UTG with T7s suited in a 5-handed game. While suited connectors play well multiway, T7s is gapped and marginal from early position. Facing aggressive opponents like Minni, FiveTwo, and Flash who frequently raise preflop, opening or limping risks difficult postflop spots or domination. Deep stack (95BB), better to fold and wait for stronger holdings to build stack long-term over 150 hands. Standard tight UTG range excludes this hand.

UTG+1 with 5♥4♦ offsuit is a very weak, easily-dominated hand. Calling 100 invites multiway pots out of position and frequently runs into raises/3-bets from aggressive players behind; open-raising to try and steal is also a poor risk/reward from this early seat. I prefer to preserve a large stack and wait for a better spot in position. Fold.

Folded to me on the button in a 5-handed game, so this is a prime steal spot. Even though 4♣3♥ is weak and plays poorly postflop, a minimum open keeps the risk low while still pressuring the blinds to fold and letting me realize position when called. If either blind 3-bets, I can comfortably fold and only lose the small open size.

I am in the Small Blind holding Kc 3c facing a min-raise from the Button. While the price to call is cheap (getting 2.33:1 immediately), playing K3s out of position is notoriously difficult. The hand suffers from reverse implied odds (often dominated by better Kings) and low playability when missing the flush draw. Additionally, the Big Blind is still to act and has a large stack, risking a squeeze or a multi-way pot where I am sandwiched. Conserving chips for better positional spots is the standard strategy here.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Eight offsuit. The player on the Button (FiveTwo) has made a minimum raise to 200. With the Small Blind folding, I only need to call 100 more to see a flop. Ad8h is well ahead of a typical Button opening range, and the pot odds of 3.5-to-1 make this an easy call to defend my blind. While I could 3-bet, calling allows me to keep the pot smaller while out of position and see how the flop connects.

On this King-high, Queen-high flop, my Ace-high has some showdown value but very little connectivity to the board. FiveTwo raised from the Button, so their range contains many Kings, Queens, and draws. Since I am out of position and the board is quite coordinated with potential straight and flush draws (hearts), I will check to see how the pre-flop aggressor reacts. Checking is the standard play for the Big Blind in this spot to keep the pot small with a marginal hand.

Heads-up in position after a button min-open, this KQ7 two-tone flop is generally better for my range than the BB’s defending range, and Flash’s check caps him somewhat (lots of whiffs and marginal pairs). Even though 4♣3♥ has very little immediate equity, a small c-bet leverages fold equity at low risk and keeps my line consistent with strong broadways/overpairs. If I get check-raised, I can comfortably fold; if called, I can often shut down unless I pick up equity (heart/A/J/T) or good runouts to continue barreling selectively.

On a board of Ks-Qh-7h, my Ace-Eight offsuit has very little equity. This flop connects strongly with a Button opening range, which contains many Kings, Queens, and straight draws like JT. FiveTwo's continuation bet of 1/3 pot is small, giving me 4-to-1 odds to call, but without any real draws (no hearts, no straight potential) and being out of position, it is difficult to realize any value. I have no 'backdoor' equity and even hitting an Ace on the turn doesn't guarantee I have the best hand given the coordination of the board. I will fold and preserve my stack.

7♠3♠ UTG is a very weak hand. Out of position, calling 100 (or limping/raising) will likely put me in multiway pots with very poor equity and many dominated situations. With deep stacks I can wait for a much better spot; opponents have shown frequent preflop aggression so this hand is not worth investing in. Standard, low-variance play here is to fold and preserve the stack for later hands.

We’re UTG+1 in a 5-handed game with 2c4s, a hand with very poor equity, terrible playability, and reverse implied odds when we make weak pairs. Limping invites multiway pots from position disadvantage, and raising as a steal from this seat is too ambitious with four players behind (including blinds who can defend aggressively). Best is to preserve stack and wait for a better spot.

I am on the Button with J6o. The action has folded to me. While the Button is a good position to steal the blinds, J6o is below the standard opening threshold, even for a wide stealing range. The Small Blind (Flash) has a large stack and can easily defend or 3-bet, and the Big Blind (Elon) is also deep enough to defend. My stack is 62.5 BB, so there is no desperation to play weak hands. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve chips for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with Qd 5c. The action has folded to me. While I only need to call 50 more to see a flop, Q5 offsuit is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position. Elon in the Big Blind has shown aggression in previous hands. Folding is the most disciplined play here to avoid a potentially difficult post-flop situation with a sub-par hand.

5-handed UTG still has the worst postflop position versus Pro and especially Flash on the button. Jh8s is an offsuit, domination-prone hand (AJ/KJ/QJ/JT, A8/K8) with weak realization when we get called and poor defense versus 3-bets, and my recent image has been fairly active so I’m more likely to face pressure. With no need to force thin EV spots at 123bb, the highest-EV/lowest-variance line is to just let this go.

I am in UTG+1 with [8d, 2d], a very weak suited hand. With the UTG player folding, I still have three opponents left to act behind me. This hand is too weak to open-raise profitably from early position given the gap and low rank. Preserving my 62.5 BB stack is more important than speculative play with junk cards.

I am on the button with 3h 5c, which is an extremely weak hand. Although I have position, there are still two players to act behind me. Given the strength of my hand and the fact that I want to preserve my stack for more profitable opportunities in this 150-hand tournament, folding is the most prudent play.

Preflop in the small blind with Qh9d facing an unopened pot after folds from UTG, UTG+1, and Button. Excellent pot odds to complete the small blind (3:1 by adding 50 to a 150 pot). Q9o is a strong speculative hand with good playability, decent high-card strength, and equity realization against the BB's wide defending range. Limping (completing) allows us to see a flop cheaply out of position, where we can play postflop effectively with our stack depth. Raising would be too thin for a steal given Minni's history of wide calls and aggression, and we prefer realizing equity rather than polarizing here. Deep stacks support this play to maximize stack growth over many hands.

We are heads-up with only the SB (Elon) left to act. K♣Q♠ is a strong heads-up hand and plays well both for value and as a blocker to many SB holdings. Stacks are deep (~158bb for me, ~95bb for Elon), so I want to take the initiative preflop and isolate rather than check and be forced to play a marginal pot out of position. A standard, non-committal raise to 300 (3x BB) applies pressure, gains fold equity, and keeps the pot a manageable size if called. Plan: take the flop with initiative; if Elon 3-bets large I can fold and preserve my deep stack, but I can continue postflop if he just calls. Raise to 300.

Facing an isolation raise from Minni in the BB after completing as SB with Qh9d. Pot offers 400:200 (3:1 odds, requiring ~25% equity), and Q9o realizes 40-50% equity against a typical loose BB iso-range (pairs, suited broadways, Ax, connectors) based on Minni's aggressive history of frequent preflop raises and wide defending/calling. Deep effective stacks (~95BB) favor realizing equity multiway or heads-up postflop rather than folding good odds. Hand has decent playability: straight potential (J10x, T8x), Q-high blocks her QQ/AQ, and flush draw blocker with Qh. OOP challenges mitigated by pot odds and long-term stack maximization in tournament. Raising too thin (Q9o not strong enough for 3bet bluff/value), folding too nitty given history and odds. Call to continue realizing equity.

Flop as first to act OOP heads-up with top pair (9s) and queen kicker on 4c5d9c board. Strong hand that improved from preflop speculative call realizing equity as planned. Board has club flush draw and gutshot straight draws (36x, 67x for wheel-ish), but no immediate strong draws. Minni's BB iso range after SB limp includes wide pairs (88-JJ, 66-77), broadway, suited aces, suited connectors; she often calls postflop aggressively per history. Deep stacks (~90BB effective) favor betting for thin value from worse pairs (e.g., 88-66, Ax high, flush draws), protection, and denying equity. Check risks giving free cards to draws or inducement but OOP vulnerable to aggression. Bet 2/3 pot (400) balances value/protection without overbetting; charges draws while keeping fold equity low against calling station tendencies. Continues stack maximization by extracting value long-term.

We were the preflop aggressor with K♣Q♠ and Elon (SB) called. Flop 4♣ 5♦ 9♣: we have two overcards plus a small backdoor club possibility, but no pair and we’re out of position. Elon’s 400 into the ~600 pot (pot now 1,000) requires 400 to call — about 28.6% equity to justify a call on pure pot odds. Our chance to pair K or Q by river is ~24% and the backdoor club-runner adds ~4% — roughly borderline on raw odds, and importantly Elon's c-bet range contains a lot of missed hands (A‑x, broadways, weak draws) against which we have decent equity and can still realize equity or apply pressure on the turn. Raising out of position with only overcards is thin: it bloats the pot and exposes us to a strong 3‑bet or difficult turns. With deep stacks, the prudent line is to call, keep the pot manageable, see a turn and re-evaluate (bet for value if we pair or apply pressure selectively if the turn favors our range). Therefore: call 400.

Turn board 3c completes the club flush draw on a flop that already had flush and straight draw potential (4c-5d-9c). I hold top pair (9s) with Q kicker, no club or straight draw, OOP heads-up vs Minni's BB iso-call range after my flop lead. Her range: medium pairs (88-JJ, overpairs less likely given call), club flush draws (AcXc, KcXc, suited connectors like 67c/86c), straight draws (67, 26-67 wheel potential), Ax suited clubs. She peeled flop OOP, so weighted to draws/pairs that hit or semi-bluff. Deep stacks (~90BB eff), but board texture worsens my equity realization: flushes beat me, runner-runner straights possible, but I block QQ/AQ/99 weakly. Leading risks folding out worse Ax/8x pairs/draws while getting raised by made flushes/improved draws; thin value from overplayed pairs unlikely given her aggression history (wide calls but also pots pots when strong). Checking best: pot control with vulnerable top pair, induces bluffs from missed draws (her iso often bluffy), allows hero call river if small bet vs stationy tendencies, or fold to big bets. Maximizes EV long-term by avoiding spew, realizing equity selectively. Bet sizing if any (none here) would be 600-800 but check superior OOP.

Elon c‑bet the flop then checked the turn — a sign of weakness or pot control rather than a turn nut (if he had two clubs he’d likely bet for value). With K♣Q♠ on 4♣-5♦-9♣-3♣ I have a strong semi‑bluff: the nut 4‑to‑the‑flush (9 clubs left → ~19.6% to hit the river) plus overcards and additional pair outs (roughly 14 distinct outs total → ≈30% to improve to at least a pair or the flush). The pot is 1,400. Betting here applies pressure to missed hands (A‑x, broadways, some small pairs) while building the pot when I hit. I’ll make a half‑pot bet (~700) — large enough to get folds and to be +EV even when called (approx. 0.30*(1,400+2*700) - 700 ≈ +140 chips), but small enough to keep stacks manageable if called. Plan: if called, re‑evaluate the river — value‑bet big when I pair or make the flush, and use pot control / selective bluffing on blanks.

Turn check was for pot control with vulnerable top pair (9s) queen kicker OOP on flush-completing 3c after Minni called flop lead on 4c5d9c. Facing her 700 (1/3 pot) bet into 2100 pot offers ~4:1 odds (700 to win 2800, needing ~25% equity). Minni's BB iso-call range post-flop bet includes medium pairs (88-JJ, 66-44), club draws (AcXc/KcXc/ suited connectors like 67cc/86cc), gutshots (67/26 wheel), Ax high; turn bet polarized to made flushes (XcXc with 4c/9c/3c blocker), improved two-pair/straights (unlikely), or bluffs from missed draws/brick pairs turning into thin value/semi-bluffs per her aggressive history (frequent wide peels, turn aggression like hand #38/52). We realize 35-50%+ equity vs bluff-heavy/value mix (blocks QQ/AQ/99 weakly, no club), deep stacks (~85BB eff post-call) favor calling to realize equity OOP, control pot, and decide river (fold to big bets, hero vs small). Raising thin/risky OOP (gets snapped by nuts, folds worse); folding -EV given odds/history/stack preservation tournament play. Call maximizes EV long-term by balancing caution with speculative realization from tournament mid-stage position.

River OOP heads-up pot: 2800 with TPTK (9d pair, Qh kicker) on 4c5d9c3c2h board. Board scary: club flush draw completed turn (4c9c3c), straight possible with 6x (23456). Turn check-call pot control vs Minni's polarized turn bet (flushes, improved hands like sets/trips/straights, bluffs from missed draws/Ax per aggressive iso-call history). River 2h bricks flush, completes some gutshots (6x straight now live), low straight blocker but no major change. Equity: ahead worse pairs (Ax,77-44,76s missed), behind flushes/6x/straights/sets; ~45-55% vs her range weighted to value+bluffs from history (wide peels, turn aggression Hand38/52, river value Hand46). Deep stacks (~80BB), mid-tourney (56/150): bet risks raise-fold OOP (thin value from Ax/draws, folds better); check maximizes EV - induces bluffs/value bets from Ax/medium pairs/missed draws (her stationy tendencies), pot control showdown/value extraction long-term, balances check-call range. No strong blockers to nuts (no club/6), but Qh9d blocks QQ/AQ/99 weakly. Check optimal for stack preservation/growth over volume hands.

We missed. Holding KcQs I never paired and the river 2h is a blank for our hand — we finished with only K-high and no flush. Opponent’s line (call preflop, c‑bet flop, check/call my turn semi‑bluff, check river) looks like a lot of showdown‑worthy hands: top/mid pairs, some two‑pairs/sets, occasional turned/slow‑played straights (A‑x now has the wheel) or completed club flushes if he began with two clubs. A river bluff would need him to fold a fairly large portion of that calling range to be +EV (required fold% = bet/(pot+bet) — e.g. a 1,000 chip bet into 2,800 needs ~26% folds); given he’s been willing to call flop and turn, I expect his fold frequency to be well below that. I also don’t have good blockers to nutted holdings (he can have A or two clubs), and stacks are deep enough that losing a big river bluff is costly. Therefore I take the low‑variance line: check and go to showdown — we save chips when behind and only lose the pot when he actually shows a made hand.

I am in UTG with J9o. In a 5-handed game, this position is still early. J9o is a weak off-suit hand that is dominated by many hands in the calling or 3-betting ranges of the four players behind me (like AJ, KJ, QJ, AT+). With a stack of 62.5 BB, I have plenty of time to wait for better spots. Opening here would be too loose and likely -EV in the long run due to positional disadvantage and hand weakness.

I am holding Ace-Ten offsuit in UTG+1 (effectively middle position in this 5-handed game). ATo is a strong opening hand, and since the first player has already folded, I have a good opportunity to take control of the pot. Raising to 3x the big blind (300) is a standard opening size that helps define the range of my opponents and discourages light callers from the blinds.

64d is a speculative suited connector, but facing an early position raise from Flash, who has shown aggression in previous hands. My position on the button is advantageous, but the hand is too weak against a strong EP range (likely pairs, broadways, suited aces). Equity is marginal (~30-35% vs typical range), and realization is poor if called by blinds or multiway. Pot odds are 1.5:1 direct (300 to 450), but implied odds don't justify due to domination risks and reverse implied odds with low cards. Folding preserves stack for better spots in a deep tournament (110bb effective). No strong reason to 3-bet bluff or call given history of Flash continuing post-flop.

Facing an early-position raise to 300 from Flash while I’m in the small blind with 8♠2♠. This is a weak, poorly performing hand out of position that is often dominated and has limited postflop playability. To call I must put in 250 into a 450 pot, so I’d need ~36% equity (250 / (450+250)) against the raiser — unlikely versus a UTG+1 opening range, and the big blind still acts which can further complicate the hand. A squeeze/raise here would be a thin bluff against two deep, competent stacks and can easily get me into a tough spot. The 50 posted is sunk, so best to preserve chips for better spots — fold.

Flash is opening from CO after UTG folds, so his range should be fairly wide. In the big blind we’re getting a good price (200 to win 450) and A3o has decent playability (top-pair potential plus wheel/straight possibilities). A light 3-bet risks getting played back at by an aggressive opener and would bloat the pot out of position with a marginal hand, so the best EV line is to defend and realize equity.

We defended BB versus a wide CO open, and this flop (KQ6) is much better for the preflop raiser’s range than ours. With Ah3d we’re essentially on ace-high with only thin showdown value plus a backdoor nut-flush possibility, so we don’t want to bloat the pot out of position or start leading into the range advantage. Best is to check our range, see whether Flash c-bets and what sizing, then respond accordingly (often folding to larger pressure, occasionally continuing versus small bets).

On a board of Qh-Ks-6c, I have a significant range advantage as the pre-flop aggressor. This board texture heavily favors my perceived range, which includes hands like AA, KK, QQ, AK, and KQ. Although I only have Ace-high with a gutshot straight draw (needing a Jack), a standard continuation bet of roughly half the pot (300) is likely to force a fold from many hands in FiveTwo's big blind defending range that missed this flop, such as middle pairs or lower cards. If called, I still have the possibility of hitting a Jack on the turn to make the nut straight.

Flash’s CO/UTG+1 open is fairly wide and this KQ6 flop heavily favors his range, so we don’t want to bluff-raise with a hand that has almost no immediate equity. However, his 300 c-bet into ~650 is a small sizing, giving us good pot odds (300 to win ~950 now, and ~1250 after we call), and Ah3d has a little future play: an overcard that can improve to top pair on an A turn, plus a backdoor nut-flush draw with Ah on Qh. Calling once keeps his range wide and prevents him from profitably auto-c-betting every time; we can re-evaluate on the turn and generally fold to bigger pressure on bricks.

We improve to top pair, but it’s a very marginal top pair (A3) on a board where Flash’s preflop range contains many better Ax (AK/AQ/AJ/AT) plus two-pair/sets. If we lead, we mostly get called by better hands and fold out a lot of the bluffs and weaker holdings we want to keep in (missed floats, some Qx), and we also open ourselves up to an unpleasant raise.

Checking keeps the pot controlled out of position, allows Flash to continue bluffing/barreling his air and draws (including club draws), and lets us make a cleaner decision based on his sizing. If he checks back, we can consider a thin value/blocker bet on many rivers, but for now we don’t need to build a big pot with a weak kicker.

On the turn, the Ace of Clubs is a very strong card for my range as the pre-flop aggressor. It pairs my Ace, giving me top pair with a Ten kicker. While the board is quite coordinated and a hand like Jack-Ten has now completed a straight, I still have significant value against FiveTwo's likely holdings such as a King, a Queen, or even a lower Ace. Additionally, I still have a gutshot draw to the nut Broadway straight if a Jack appears on the river. Betting 750 (60% of the pot) allows me to extract value from weaker made hands and protect against draws, while keeping the pot manageable.

We pick up top pair, but with a weak kicker (A3) on a board where Flash can value-bet a lot of better Ax (AK/AQ/AJ/AT) and strong hands (KQ/sets). That said, his flop c-bet was small and this turn barrel (750 into 1250) still gives us solid pot odds (~27% required), and his range here should also contain plenty of non-made hands that continue (club draws, JT/T9 type floats, some Kx/Qx turning into bluffs/value). Raising is bad because we mostly get action from better hands and fold out the bluffs we want to keep in. Best line is to call and bluff-catch intelligently on the river based on sizing/runout.

River is a relative brick: it doesn’t complete a flush (only 3 clubs) and mainly just adds some two-pair possibilities for 86/88, while our hand remains a weak top pair (A3) on a board where Flash’s value range contains many better Ax (AJ+), AK/AQ, and strong hands like KQ/sets. Leading here is thin-to-bad: worse hands (most Kx/Qx) often fold, while better hands call/raise, and we also cut off his opportunity to bluff missed floats. Best is to check and play a bluff-catch decision versus his sizing.

On the river, the board has become quite coordinated with the Eight of Clubs completing a potential flush and the presence of King, Queen, and Ace creating possibilities for straights (JT) and two-pair combinations. While I have top pair with my Ace, my kicker (Ten) is mediocre. FiveTwo has checked to me, and while I might have the best hand against a King or Queen, betting is unlikely to get called by worse hands and could potentially face a check-raise from a flush, straight, or better Ace. Checking back allows me to take my showdown value and see if my top pair is good without risking more chips.
6h 3c is an extremely weak hand and playing it from UTG in a 5-handed game is unprofitable. Folding to preserve stack.

86o is a trash hand from UTG+1 position in a 5-handed game. No strong playability, offsuit, poor high card strength. Standard fold to avoid bloating the pot OOP with a weak range. Preserve stack for premium hands.

On the button with Ad2d (A2 suited) in a 5-handed game this is a clear open: strong suited ace with position against only the blinds. I want to take the initiative, get fold equity, and avoid a limp-heavy multiway pot. A standard 3x BB sizing (300 total) is appropriate — it’s large enough to apply pressure to the small blind and big blind but keeps postflop play comfortable with deep stacks (~144 BB). Plan: fold to an over-aggressive shove from a tight player, call or 4-bet selectively vs small 3-bets depending on opponent and sizing, and if called play aggressively postflop (C-bet dry boards, pot-control or check/call with backdoor draws and A-high).

Button opens to 3x and appears fairly active, so their range is wide. From the small blind, flatting 87s often creates tough multiway, out-of-position spots (and gives the BB a great price to come along). 8d7d has good playability and can realize equity well as a 3-bet candidate: we generate fold equity preflop, isolate the opener, and still have suited-connector equity when called (with many flops we can c-bet or barrel on favorable runouts). Sizing up to ~3.5–4x discourages the BB from overcalling and sets up manageable SPR postflop.

I am in the Big Blind with Tc 2h, a very weak hand. The action involves a Button raise to 300 followed by a Small Blind 3-bet to 1100. Facing a 3-bet with T2 offsuit is an immediate fold. I have no invested interest beyond the blind, and the hand has very poor playability and equity against the ranges of a Button opener and Small Blind 3-better. Preserving my stack of 61.5 BBs is the priority.

I opened to 300 on the button with A♦2♦ and the SB 3-bet to 1100. Heads-up and in position with a suited Ace, calling is the best line: A2s has excellent post‑flop playability (nut‑flush potential, ace blocker) and I can exploit position against what looks like a fairly wide/aggressive SB 3‑bet range. A tiny 4‑bet to 1900 has little fold equity and commits me OOP with a hand that wants to see flops; I don’t want to bloat the pot preflop with a marginal hand. I’ll call 800 more (1100 total), plan to c‑bet favorable flops, float/control the pot on drawy boards, and fold to overbearing action or a polarized shove unless I have a specific read that FiveTwo is wide enough to call.

This A-K-4 flop heavily favors the preflop 3-bettor: we have a big nut/range advantage (AA/KK/AK/AQ) and can profitably c-bet at high frequency with a small sizing. With 8d7d we have no made hand, but we can still leverage fold equity vs hands like QJ/JT/medium pairs and deny equity, while keeping our risk low and setting up occasional turn barrels on favorable runouts (diamonds/5/6).

I have top pair with the ace (Ad2d) on A♣K♣4♥ (A♦ on board) — strong but with a very weak kicker and vulnerable to AK/KK/44. FiveTwo 3‑bet preflop so his range contains a lot of strong hands (AK, AQ, broadway suited Aces, medium pairs) but his flop bet is very small (650 into a 2,950 pot) — a probe that can be many bluffs or thin value hands. Calling is the best line: I keep worse Ax and Kx hands and bluffs in his range, control the pot with position, and get very good pot odds to see another card (call 650 to win ~3,600 → ~15% equity required). Raising here risks folding out worse hands and overcommitting against the exact hands that beat me (AK, two pair), and opens me up to a larger shove. Plan: call the 650, re‑evaluate on the turn — extract value or pot‑control depending on villain’s action.

After our small flop c-bet gets called, the turn Td is not a great barreling card for this specific holding: we still have essentially no equity (no flush draw, no meaningful straight draw), and Td actually connects with/can improve a chunk of Minni’s continuing range (JT/TT, and QJ can even arrive as a made straight). With SPR getting low, firing again risks spewing into hands that won’t fold, while checking keeps our range protected (we can still have AK/AQ/sets) and lets us realize whatever tiny chance we have to win at showdown or choose better bluff rivers if checked through.

I have A♦2♦ — top pair but the worst kicker on A♥ K♣ 4♥ T♦. FiveTwo 3‑bet preflop (wide/aggro range) and made a very small flop probe (650) then checked the turn. That line looks like weakness or pot control rather than a massive value hand, but his preflop aggression means his range still contains AK/A10 and some sets/pairs that beat me. With position and a marginal kicker I want to control the pot and keep his weaker A‑hands and Kx in to extract value later rather than folding them out or getting check‑raised/raised off my hand. There are no meaningful draws to deny, so betting now gains little and risks committing me against the exact hands that beat me. I’ll check and re‑evaluate on the river — call small/medium river bets for value, fold to a large shove unless the line is clearly a bluff.

River comes AAKT4 and we have pure air (8-high) with no meaningful blockers to Minni’s likely calling range (Ax, Kx two-pair, occasional slowplayed QJ straight/boats). After she called the flop c-bet and then checked back turn, a lot of her range is either strong enough to bluff-catch (especially any Kx now making AA+KK two-pair) or has an Ace and will never fold. Trying to bluff here is likely to get looked up too often, and our hand doesn’t block her continues or unblock folds in a useful way. Best is to give up, keep our range protected with the turn check line, and be ready to fold if she bets.

The river (Ac) improved my Ad2d from a marginal top‑pair into trips (A♦ + A♥ + A♣). FiveTwo’s line — an SB 3‑bet preflop, a tiny 650 flop probe, then checks on turn and river — looks capped and call‑heavy (many weaker Ax, Kx, and medium pairs) rather than a polar value range full of quads/full houses. The hands that beat me (AK, KK, TT, 44, quads) exist but are a minority and would often have bet differently earlier. I want to extract value from worse A‑hands and K‑hands while keeping the pot from getting excessively large versus the few full‑house combos. Make a small/medium value bet (~1/3 pot) to get called by worse Ax/Kx but limit my downside against a raise; if I face a large river raise I’ll re‑evaluate (likely fold to a polarizing shove).

We have 8-high with zero showdown value and no relevant blockers (we don’t block Ax or Kx, and we don’t block the main value hands that take this line). Minni called the flop in a 3-bet pot on A-K-4, checked back turn, then fired a small river bet after the Ace pairs. This is very consistent with thin/value/block betting (any Ax, and plenty of Kx now making Aces+Kings), and it’s hard to find enough natural bluffs that would bet here often enough. We’re getting 1200 to win 6000 (need ~20% bluffs), which is unlikely. Raising as a bluff is also poor because her continuing range contains lots of Ax that won’t fold. Best play is to let it go and preserve stack.

UTG position with Q2o, a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability, low equity, and dominated by any raising range. Standard play is to fold to avoid building the pot out of position with trash. Deep stacks don't change this; preserve chips for better spots to maximize long-term EV.

Fold. 5♠2♥ is the worst offsuit combination — extremely weak and likely dominated. I'm UTG+1 with three players to act behind (button, SB, BB), so this will often be a multiway pot where my hand has negligible postflop playability. Calling 100 into a 150 pot requires ~40% equity, far more than 5‑2 offsuit has versus reasonable opening ranges; raising is worse (out of position and open to 3‑bets). Preserve chips for better spots in position.

On the button 5-handed we can open quite wide, but 4-2 offsuit is at the very bottom of playable holdings and performs poorly when either blind defends (dominated, low playability, tough to realize equity). With Flash in the BB appearing capable of defending/3-betting and Pro in the SB able to wake up with a squeeze, the steal isn’t worth the frequency of bad postflop spots. I’ll pass and preserve stack for higher-EV opens.

I am in the Small Blind with J8o, a below-average hand. The action has folded to me. My opponent in the Big Blind (Flash) is the chip leader with a deep stack (172 BBs), which allows them to apply significant pressure. Playing a marginal offsuit hand out of position against a deep-stacked opponent is difficult and likely negative EV. While completing the bet is cheap, it often leads to bleeding chips post-flop or facing a pre-flop raise. Folding is the disciplined and standard decision here to preserve my stack for better spots.

J♦T♦ in a 5‑hand game is a strong, playable UTG open — good straight/flush potential and reasonable post‑flop playability. With a 16,275 stack I want to take the initiative, get fold equity and avoid a limp that invites multiway pots. Standard ~3x BB sizing (300) is appropriate: big enough to discourage callers and consistent with my table image, while keeping plenty of chips for post‑flop play. Plan: open to 300; if I face a large 3‑bet I’ll usually fold out of position, but may call a small 3‑bet to see a flop given the hand’s playability.

We’re facing an UTG 3bb open from Minni with 7♦3♥ offsuit, out of position with three players still to act behind. This hand has very poor equity, is frequently dominated when it makes a pair, and doesn’t have strong suited/connected playability to justify a call. A 3-bet bluff is also low quality versus an UTG range and with shallow-to-medium stacks where we don’t want to burn chips. Best decision is to preserve stack and fold.

I am holding 6h 4d on the Button. This is a weak off-suit hand with very little playability. Minni has raised to 300 from UTG, which indicates a strong range (likely premiums, strong broadways, or pairs). Despite having the positional advantage, my hand equity is far too low to profitably call an early position open. Calling would be a losing play in the long run, and raising is reckless bluffing against a strong range. The correct play is to fold and preserve my stack of 61 BBs for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with A7o. Minni has opened from UTG (effectively the Hijack in a 5-handed game) to 300. While Ace-high has some value in shorthanded play, A7o is a weak hand to play out of position against an UTG opener who has shown significant aggression in previous hands. Calling would put me in a difficult spot post-flop, and 3-betting as a bluff against a stack of Minni's size is risky given her tendency to play pots. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

ATs is a premium hand in the BB facing a single UTG raiser after folds. Excellent equity, suited, blocker to AA, plays well postflop. Calling is too passive OOP, risking multiway if others wake up, but with folds already it's heads-up likely. 3-bet sizing to 3x the raise (900 total) for value against Minni's wide UTG range, builds pot, denies equity to weaker broadways/pairs, and applies pressure given Minni's aggressive history but occasional folds to aggression (e.g., Hand 7, 19). Pot odds great even if called. Avoids check-fold disaster.

Elon’s 3‑bet to 900 is a standard, relatively small 3‑bet vs my 300 open. J♦T♦ is a very playable hand — suited connector with good straight/flush potential and good playability in position. Calling 600 more (900 total) keeps the pot reasonable, lets me use position post‑flop to realize equity and apply pressure when appropriate. 4‑betting here as a bluff is too thin against a 3‑bet range and would commit more chips unnecessarily; I will reserve folding to any large 4‑bet and proceed cautiously post‑flop (c‑bet or float depending on texture).

Flop Ad Ah 3c is an absolute dream board for ATs: I hold As Ts for top set (AAA) with a spade flush draw backdoor. Monster hand that crushes Minni's UTG 3bet-call range, which includes AK/AQ/AJ/pairs like KK-QQ/JJ-TT, sets unlikely except AA (but board paired and she has one ace max now). OOP first to act heads-up in 1850 pot. Value bet essential to build pot and charge draws/bluffs (pocket pairs, gutshots). Check risks free card to runner-runner straights/flushes or induces unnecessary showdown. Bet 2/3 pot (~1200) extracts max value from worse pairs/top pairs while pot-controlling somewhat given stacks (mine 10k). Minni's history shows she calls stations but folds to pressure sometimes (e.g., Hand 7 pre, Hand 47 turn); this size balances value/protection. Avoid overbet as board dry, no need to rep crazy strength yet.

Elon 3‑bet preflop and now fires a smallish c‑bet (1,200 into a 3,050 pot). I have J♦T♦ on A♦ A♥ 3♣ — no pair, only backdoor diamond/straight possibilities. To call 1,200 I need ≈28% equity (1,200 to win ~4,250). My realistic equity is well under that: backdoor flush by river ≈4.5%, chance to pair a J or T by river ≈24% but many of those pairings are still behind Elon's likely range (A‑x with a diamond, sets, or big pocket pairs). Raising as a bluff is too thin — Elon 3‑bet pre‑flop so his range is weighted to hands that will continue and can punish a raise. Given poor equity, limited fold equity, and tournament stack considerations, the +EV play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

5-handed UTG we can open fairly wide, but 7♦5♥ offsuit is near the bottom of a reasonable opening range: poor playability, frequently dominated when we make a pair, and we’ll often face 3-bets from position (Button/SB) and be forced to continue out of position. With 92bb stacks there’s no urgency to gamble—better to preserve stack for higher-EV opens/spots.

I am in the Cutoff (UTG+1 in 5-handed play) with J9s. While this is a reasonable hand to open from late position, I am the shortest stack at the table with 61 BBs, facing three aggressive opponents with deep stacks behind me (Button, SB, BB). The risk of being 3-bet or floating out of position against the large stack on the button makes this a marginal spot. Opening suited one-gappers is at the bottom of my range here, and given the table dynamics, a disciplined fold is preferable to preserve chips for better opportunities.

I am on the button with 8d 7h, a marginal off-suit connector. While this is sometimes an opening hand in a steal position, both Elon (SB) and Minni (BB) have shown significant aggression and willingness to defend their blinds in recent hands. Specifically, Elon recently 3-bet and Minni frequently calls or raises pre-flop. With a healthy stack of 17,350, there is no need to risk chips on a sub-par hand that plays poorly against 3-bets or when out of position post-flop if the blinds are sticky. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Preflop in the small blind with 7s4h offsuit, a very weak hand with poor connectivity and no suited value. Folded to me vs BB (Minni, who defends aggressively based on history). Limping offers good implied odds but poor postflop position and low equity against BB's wide defending range (~35-40% equity vs calling range). Raising risks 3-bet or difficult postflop spots with a trash hand. Folding the dead money (50 chips) is -EV short-term but preserves stack for better spots in a deep-stack tournament (119BB effective). Optimal to fold bottom-of-range hands like 74o in this dynamic.

I am in UTG position with Jc 2h, which is an extremely weak hand (J2 offsuit). My stack is 6100 (61 BBs), which is comfortable but not deep enough to splash around with trash hands out of position. With four players left to act behind me, the probability of someone waking up with a better hand is high, and this hand has very poor playability post-flop. The standard and optimal play here is to simply fold.

I am holding King-Jack offsuit (Kh Jd) in UTG+1 in a 5-handed game. This is a strong starting hand in this position. Based on my previous play style in earlier hands (e.g., Hand #53 and #57), I have consistently used an opening size of 300. With 5 players remaining, opening this hand allows me to take control of the pot and leverage my position as the table chip leader. I will raise to 300.

87h suited connectors from the button against an early position raise from Flash. Excellent position to realize equity post-flop, especially multi-way with blinds to act. Deep stacks provide strong implied odds for hitting sets, straights, flushes, or two-pair. Pot odds are favorable: calling 300 into 450 current pot (1.5:1 direct, better implied/multiway). Folding too tight; 3-betting light risks variance without much fold equity given Flash's aggressive history. Call to play in position.

Facing an early-position open to 300 (Flash) with the Button calling, I’m in the small blind out of position and would have to call 250 to see a multiway flop. Q6o (Qh6s) is a weak, easily-dominated hand with poor post‑flop playability and little equity in a 3‑way pot. A 3‑bet bluff here would be better with suited connectors or blockers; Q6o isn’t a candidate. Pot odds and position don’t justify calling or isolating — fold and preserve my deep stack for a better spot.

We’re getting a very good price (200 to win 750) and 6-5 has decent postflop playability via straights/two-pairs, especially with deep stacks. A squeeze/3-bet bluff is unlikely to generate enough fold equity versus an opener plus a caller, and we’d be out of position in a bloated pot with a dominated offsuit connector. Best is to defend and see a flop.

We flop bottom two pair on a very dynamic, multiway board (8s-5d-6d) with lots of bad turn cards (any 7/4/9 for straights, any diamond, many overcards that can kill action or create tougher runouts). Checking risks giving a free card to two overcards + diamond draw / open-enders, and we also want to start building a pot versus overpairs, 8x, pair+draw hands, and diamond draws.

Leading is best here: it denies equity, gets value from a wide continuing range, and simplifies the hand OOP. I’ll use a solid value/protection size around 2/3 pot to charge draws and avoid giving good odds.

I am holding King-Jack offsuit (Kh Jd) on a board of 8s-5d-6d. This flop is very coordinated and favors the range of the Big Blind (FiveTwo), who just 'donk-bet' 650 into a pot of 950. My hand has no direct straight or flush draws, and while I have two overcards, they are vulnerable on this texture. Furthermore, Elon is still left to act behind me on the button. Calling here would mean investing 650 chips with only King-high and no real equity, likely facing further aggression on later streets. Given the board texture and the lead from the BB, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my chip lead.

Flop of 8s5d6d hits my 87hh perfectly: top pair (eights) with a weak kicker but crucially an open-ended straight draw (4 or 9 makes the nuts), plus backdoor flush draw. This is a monster hand on a highly coordinated, draw-heavy board (diamond flush draw, straight draws like 47, 79, 24, 37, etc.). FiveTwo leads as BB after calling pre-flop multiway, representing a wide range: draws, weak pairs, floats. My equity is massive (~50%+ vs his range). Calling is fine but allows him to realize equity cheaply and check-fold later streets. Raising builds the pot for value from worse (pairs, weaker draws), charges draws heavily, and protects against free cards. Position advantage post-flop. Deep stacks (my 11k, his 8k), so sizing to ~3x his bet: raising to 2000 total commits (650 to call + 1350 raise) into 1600 pot, offering poor odds for calls while leaving room for further action. Folds from Flash simplify to heads-up.

We have bottom two pair on a very dynamic board (8s-6d-5d). Against Elon’s flop raise after flatting pre, his range is often a mix of strong value (sets like 88/66/55, occasionally 74 for the straight, some better two-pairs) and a lot of high-equity hands (diamond flush draws, pair+draw, open-enders like 97/43, etc.).

With pot odds (1350 to win 4950), calling needs ~21% equity and we comfortably have that versus the overall range. Raising here tends to bloat the pot and effectively commits us, which is great vs draws but bad when he’s value-heavy; plus a raise may fold out weaker 8x/pair hands that we want to keep in. By calling, we keep his bluffs/draws in and preserve flexibility on turns while still setting up a low SPR where we can continue aggressively on safer runouts.

Plan: call now, then on many non-diamond, non-straightening turns we can consider leading/committing to deny equity; on very bad turns (diamond/7/4/9) we proceed more cautiously.

Turn brings Qs: it’s not a diamond, so most flush draws still need to pay, but it does add an overcard that can (a) give Elon a pair with some of his flop semi-bluffs (Q♦X♦ type hands) and (b) make the river more dangerous if we give a free card. With SPR ~1.4 (6900 into 4950), we want to keep charging the large portion of his range that is draws / pair+draw / overpairs / 8x, while still getting called by worse and denying equity.

I prefer leading rather than checking: checking lets him realize equity for free with some hands and can put us in a brutal spot versus an in-position shove sizing. A ~1/2 pot bet sets up a clean river shove and keeps in more of his drawing region than an immediate jam would.

Plan: bet 2500; if he jams, we’ll usually be priced in to continue given the draw-heavy portion of his raising range and our boat outs versus made straights.

Turn board Qs is a reasonable card that doesn't drastically change the board texture but adds a spade flush draw possibility and potential top pair for some hands. I have top pair eights with a weak seven kicker and still hold the open-ended straight draw (4 or 9 for nuts), giving me strong equity (~35-45% against a typical value+draw calling range). FiveTwo's line is suspicious: BB caller preflop multiway, small flop lead into a draw-heavy board, then calling my large raise (which he might perceive as very strong), and now turn donk of 2500 (1/3 pot) into a 7450 pot with only 4400 behind (leaving ~1900). This sizes poorly for bluffs and smells like value: sets (55/66), two-pair combos (Q6s/Q5s/85s/65s), or Qx top pair. However, he could have missed draws turning to bluff or weaker hands continuing. Direct pot odds are excellent: 2500 to call into 7450 (3:1), and implied odds strong with my draw, position, and his short-ish stack (I cover him deeply at 9625). Raising min to 5000 risks folding out worse while getting called by better, bloating pot OOP; shoving overkill. Folding too tight pre-river with this equity and position. Call to realize equity, control pot size, and play river perfectly in position. Long-term EV positive given tournament dynamics.

River is the 3s, completing the backdoor spade flush while not pairing the board. With 6c5s we have bottom two pair, which is strong at showdown but close to the bottom of our value range after this line (lead flop, call raise, bet turn). If we bet/shove 4400 into 9950, most worse hands (one-pair Qx/8x, some 6x) will often find folds on this scary runout, while all better hands (sets, higher two pair, straights, and the occasional 2-spade backdoor flush) happily call—so we risk value-owning ourselves and folding out the hands we actually beat.

Checking maximizes EV by (1) realizing our showdown value when he checks back with missed diamonds / marginal made hands, and (2) keeping his missed diamond draws in position to bluff into us. With the pot so large relative to stacks, we can then decide whether to bluff-catch based on sizing, but the best default is to check this river rather than bet thinly into a polarized continuing range.

River check from FiveTwo after leading flop and turn on a highly coordinated board with flush completed (spades). My 87h gives top pair eights (weak kicker) which blocks some of his two-pair combos (87 unlikely). His range for calling flop raise + turn donk: polarized to sets (55/66/88), two-pair (56/65/85), combo draws that hit Q (Qx suited), overpairs, or stubborn draws. However, check-back suggests not the nuts (sets/flush would often bet for value/protection), more likely medium strength like Qx (top pair), weaker pairs, or missed draws turning passive. Pure air bluffs unlikely to check after investing. Top pair eights good often enough vs this checking range (beats QJ-QT, overpairs JJ-99, weaker 8x/A-high). Pot 9950, he has 4400 behind, I cover with 7125. Value bet 3000 (~30% pot) targets Qx top pair, Ax high, JTs etc. that call but lose; pot odds for him ~20% equity needed (3000/16950), which worse hands have but fold bluffs. Fold equity from missed spade/diamond draws. Position perfect. Checking realizes equity but misses dead money value; overbet shove too thin/thick; smaller bet too station-callable by better. Tournament mid-stack building: extract value aggressively when ahead.

We’re facing a smallish river bet (3000 into 12950) after a line where Elon can arrive with a lot of missed diamond draws/pair+draws that floated the turn with implied odds, and this spade-completing river is a very natural spot for him to bluff (it’s credible and he needs very little fold equity). Our hand (two pair, 6s and 5s) blocks key value like 66/55 and doesn’t block his missed diamonds, and we only need ~19% equity to call. Raising would only get called by better and fold out bluffs, so the best play is to bluff-catch.

I am holding Jack-Ten offsuit in the Under The Gun (UTG) position in a 5-handed game. While this is at the bottom of a standard opening range, the hand has decent playability. I will make a standard opening raise of 300 (3x the big blind) to take control of the pot early. If I face significant aggression from the deeper stacks (FiveTwo or Minni), I can comfortably fold, but I want to maintain an active and aggressive image at this stage of the tournament.

Pocket nines is a strong premium hand, well ahead of Flash's UTG opening range (which is typically tight).
