As UTG+1 with 41BB effective stack, 3-betting is standard to isolate the raiser, build the pot when ahead, and apply pressure on players behind (Minni button deep, FiveTwo SB deep, Pro BB 60BB). Calling risks multiway pot which is less favorable; folding too weak. 3-bet sizing to 3x the open (900 total) is balanced, denying equity to worse hands and getting value from calls.

I have 3♠7♣ — essentially the worst holding. Facing an UTG open and then a 3‑bet to 900, my hand has almost no playable equity vs those ranges and calling 900 would require implausibly high equity (~40% pot odds break‑even) which 3‑7o doesn't have. There are still the SB and BB to act who can squeeze or isolate, and I have a healthy stack (15,375) so there's no need to gamble out of position with junk. A small reraise or shove as a fancy bluff is much higher variance and not +EV here. Fold and wait for a better spot on the button.

Pocket deuces are too strong to auto-fold in a 5-handed game, and the 3-bet sizing (to 900) gives us a reasonable price. While we don’t have great equity if we 4-bet/jam and get called, we do have strong implied odds versus Flash’s deep stack if he continues and we flop a set. We’re out of position and not closing the action, so the plan is simple: call and proceed very cautiously when we miss (check/fold vs pressure), and go for maximum value when we hit.

I am in the Big Blind with K3o facing a UTG open, a UTG+1 3-bet to 900, and a cold call from the Small Blind. Despite getting reasonable pot odds (calling 800 to win a pot of 2200), K3o is significantly dominated by the ranges of all three active opponents. UTG+1's 3-bet against UTG indicates a very strong hand, and the SB's cold call implies a solid holding as well. Furthermore, the original raiser (Flash) is still to act and could 4-bet squeeze. Playing a weak King offsuit out of position in a multi-way 3-bet pot has high reverse implied odds. I cannot profitably continue.

I originally opened for 300 with Jack-Ten offsuit from UTG. Elon, a short stack, 3-bet to 900 from UTG+1, and FiveTwo cold-called the 900 from the Small Blind. Although I am getting 3.6-to-1 odds to call (600 to win 2200), JTo is a very poor hand to play out of position against two opponents who have shown significant strength. Elon's 3-bet range into an UTG opener is typically very narrow (JJ+, AK), and FiveTwo's cold-call likely represents a medium pocket pair or high broadway cards. I am likely dominated and will have difficulty realizing my equity. Folding is the most disciplined and safe play here to avoid a potentially costly multi-way pot.

We’re out of position in a 3-bet pot versus the preflop aggressor, and this K55 board heavily favors Elon’s range (lots of AK/KQ/AA-QQ) while our 22 has very little equity and poor playability with his remaining stack being shallow. Donk-betting risks getting jammed on and burning chips with a hand that can’t continue. Best is to check, keep the pot controlled, and mostly check/fold versus pressure; if he surprisingly checks back, we can consider a small turn stab depending on the card.

Pocket 9s is an overpair to the paired 5s on a Kc5h5c flop, which is relatively dry but with a club flush draw. As preflop 3-bet aggressor vs SB cold-caller's OOP range (likely Ax suited, suited connectors, lower pockets), 99 has strong equity ahead of most calling hands like lower pairs, draws, or floats, and blocks 99. Behind Kx/55 but those are less likely in cold-call range. With 32BB effective stack and villain deep, c-bet half-pot for thin value, protection vs draws (clubs, gutshots), and denial of equity to worse. Checking risks giving free cards to draws or allowing villain to rep K/sets. Half-pot sizing balances value/protection without overcommitting.

We’re heads-up in a 3-bet pot, OOP, on a K55 board that strongly favors the 3-bettor’s range. Versus Elon’s c-bet, 22 has almost no playable equity: we’re basically drawing to a set (very low frequency), and with Elon’s shallow remaining stack the implied odds to set-mine are terrible. Calling also commits us into a low-SPR spot where he can jam most turns and we can’t continue. Bluff-raising is unattractive because he’s unlikely to fold Kx/overpairs and doesn’t need to overfold at this stack depth. Best play is to cut losses and fold.

UTG position with 4c9d offsuit, a complete trash hand with poor playability, low equity, and easily dominated. Standard play is to fold preflop from early position to avoid difficult spots postflop. My stack is 54BB, no need to speculate with junk when better spots await. Opponents are deep-stacked and aggressive based on history, but raising or limping would be a mistake with this holding.

Pocket tens UTG+1 in a 5-handed game is a clear open-raise spot — limping hands invites multiway action and overcards. I’ll open to 3x the BB (300) to build the pot, isolate the button, and gain fold equity vs the blinds. Both FiveTwo (button) and Flash (BB) have shown aggression, and Pro (SB) is the shortest stack, so 300 is large enough to discourage too many callers but small enough to keep postflop playability. Plan: c-bet favorable flops, proceed cautiously vs large 3-bets (re-evaluate/call or fold depending on sizing and who 3-bets).

Button vs Minni’s 3bb open, KJo is strong enough to continue and plays best with initiative and position. Minni has been opening frequently and has shown willingness to fold to 3-bets at times, so a 3-bet picks up dead money often while also denying equity to the blinds and reducing squeeze risk. Even when called, KJo has good playability in position and benefits from having a king blocker to the top of Minni’s continuing range. I’ll use a standard in-position sizing versus a 3bb open.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Deuce suited (Tc 2c). The action ahead of me involves an open raise to 300 by Minni and a 3-bet to 900 by FiveTwo. This demonstrates significant strength from the Button. My hand is extremely weak and plays poorly against 3-bet ranges, especially out of position. Calling would be a losing play with low equity and reverse implied odds. Folding is the only correct option.

I am in the Big Blind with Q3 suited. UTG+1 (Minni) opened for 3x, and the Button (FiveTwo) 3-bet to 9x. Q3s is far too weak to defend against a 3-bet from the Button, especially when I would be out of position for the rest of the hand against two active players. Folding is the only sensible move to preserve my stack.

I opened to 300 UTG+1 and the button 3-bet to 900. Pocket tens are too strong to fold but I don't want to bloat the pot out of position with a marginal 4‑bet. Calling (600 more, 900 total) keeps the opponent's bluffs and worse pairs in, preserves stack depth (we're ~150bb), and gives me good equity vs a typical BTN 3‑bet range. Pot is 1,350 now; calling makes it ~1,950 and lets me evaluate the flop before committing more. Postflop plan: proceed based on texture — continue aggressively on favorable, dry flops where my pair is likely best; control the pot or check/call on high‑card/overcard boards; fold to clearly polarized, heavy action (large 4/5‑bets) that indicates AA/KK/AK if villain polarizes. If villain shows extreme aggression preflop (big 4‑bet/shove) I will re‑evaluate, but with this sizing and opponent profile a call is the best +EV line.

Heads‑up vs the button who 3‑bet preflop. Flop is A♠ 9♣ 8♦ — an Ace high board that heavily favors the 3‑bettor’s range (lots of A‑x, broadways and overpairs). I have an underpair (T T) and I’m out of position, so betting here bloats the pot and gets into trouble vs check‑raises or any Ace. Standard +EV line is to control the pot and realize equity: check and call small bets, fold to large, and only start building if I improve (a T makes two pair) or the opponent gives clear weakness. Plan: check now; call a small/medium c‑bet (~⅓ pot) to see a turn, fold to a polarized large bet, and if I hit a T on the turn I’ll bet for value/replicate strength.

3-bet pot on A98r strongly favors my range (lots of Ax/overpairs) and puts pressure on Minni’s preflop calling range (many broadways, small pairs, suited connectors) that often has to fold or continue uncomfortably. With KJ I have little showdown value but good fold equity, and a small c-bet keeps my risk low while setting up profitable barrels on many turns (K/Q/J/T, some 9/8 pairing cards depending on action). If I face a check-raise at meaningful sizing, I can comfortably fold this hand.

Call 650. I’m facing a small c‑bet (≈25% pot) from the button who 3‑bet preflop — their range has a lot of A‑x and some broadways but also bluffs. My TTs have decent showdown value: I’m behind any Ace but ahead of many bluffs, KQ/KJ type hands and non‑Ace pairs. Folding here surrenders too much equity OOP; check‑raising is too ambitious with only a middle pair on an Ace‑high board and will fold out worse hands or get me in trouble vs real Aces/overpairs. Calling keeps the pot controllable, allows me to realize equity and see a turn, and preserves stack depth (good implied odds if I improve). Plan: call now, re‑evaluate on the turn — fold to large/polarized aggression, check/call small continuation bets, and start building only if I improve (T trips) or villain shows clear weakness.

Check. I'm out of position with pocket Tens (T♠T♦) on A♠ 9♣ 8♦ 6♦ after facing a preflop 3‑bet and a small flop c‑bet. I currently have an underpair to the Ace and the turn 6♦ doesn't meaningfully improve my hand — there is no flush draw (I only have one diamond) but I do have ~6 clean outs to improve on the river (two remaining Tens for trips + four 7s to make the 6‑7‑8‑9‑10 straight). Betting OOP here would bloat the pot with a marginal hand and leave me vulnerable to check‑raises or large shoves from a 3‑bettor’s range that is heavy in A‑x, overpairs and strong broadways. With pot control and stack sizes (pot 3,250; my stack 13,825; Villain ~14,900) the +EV line is to check and see the river, then: value bet or raise if I hit (T or 7), call small/medium river bets to show down, and fold to clearly polarized large river aggression.

Turn (As 9c 8d 6d) in a 3-bet pot after my small flop c-bet gets called, Minni’s range is still capped to a lot of one-pair/underpair/draw-type hands that hate facing continued pressure, while my perceived range contains many strong Ax and overpairs that keep barreling. The 6d is a reasonably good barrel card (doesn’t improve most of her broadway floats), and holding Kd gives me a useful blocker to some diamond continues while also giving me credible river pressure on many runouts. I’ll bet around half-pot to target folds from TT–QQ, weak 9x/8x portions, and deny equity to draws; if I face a meaningful check-raise, I’m done with K-high.

Fold. I’m out of position with TT on A♠ 9♣ 8♦ 6♦ facing a turn bet of 1,700 into the 3,250 pot (pot is 4,950 after the bet). Calling 1,700 to continue would make the final pot ~6,650, so I need ~25.6% equity to break even. I only have ~6 clean outs to improve on the river (two Tens, four Sevens) — roughly a 13% chance to improve — and my raw showdown equity versus a BTN 3‑bettor’s turn‑betting range (lots of A‑x, overpairs, strong broadways and some value combos) is likely well below the required pot‑odds threshold. Raising is unattractive: it bloats the pot OOP with a marginal hand and I have little fold equity versus a polarized raiser. Given stack sizes and long tournament horizon, folding and preserving chips is the +EV play.

UTG with J♠7♣ — an off-suit jack with a weak kicker. From early position in a 5-handed game I should be tightening up: this hand is often dominated by other Jx and broadway holdings and plays poorly out of position. Players behind have shown willingness to raise and 3-bet in previous hands, so calling 100 or opening here risks getting into large, marginal pots OOP. With a healthy ~138bb stack I’ll preserve chips for better spots and fold.

5-handed with Minni folding UTG, this is effectively first-in from early position. KTo is strong enough to open in short-handed play, and raising is preferable to limping because it applies pressure to the blinds, denies them equity, and keeps our range uncapped. With Pro/Flash capable of attacking limps and Elon in the BB at ~53bb, I want an open size that still allows me to comfortably fold to heavy 3-bet pressure while generating immediate fold equity.

I hold 8d 2h on the Button, which is an extremely weak hand with no playability. FiveTwo has raised from early position (UTG+1), indicating a potentially strong range. Despite having position, the hand strength is insufficient to justify calling a raise or 3-betting. Conserving chips is the priority here.

I am in the Small Blind with 42 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. FiveTwo has raised from UTG+1 to 2.5x. Defending this hand out of position against an early position raise is a losing play. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

78h suited connectors in BB facing an early position raise to 250 (to call 150 into 400 pot). Excellent implied odds with deep stacks (mine ~53BB, villain 179BB), good pot odds (~2.7:1), and playability postflop. FiveTwo raises frequently from early/mid positions based on history (e.g., Hands 11,15,20,23, etc.), suggesting a wide range we dominate or play well against. Folding too tight OOP; 3-betting risks too thin without fold equity positionally disadvantaged. Call to realize equity, set-mine potential, and control pot OOP.

Flopped the nut flush (Kh Th 8h 7h 6h) on a monotone heart board (Th Kh 6h). Heads-up vs FiveTwo's UTG+1 raise range after my BB defense. Insane outcome for 78h suited connectors. Pot 550, acting first OOP with deep effective stacks (~52BB for me, ~179BB villain). Must bet for value against his strong range (AKh, KQh, suited broadways, sets like KK/TT/66 but less likely), worse flushes (e.g., QhJh, J9h), two-pair (KT, K6 rare), draws (though board scary). Half-to-2/3 pot bet balances value/protection, induces raises from better flushes/A-high for stack-off potential, keeps worse hands in. History shows FiveTwo aggressive/value-heavy postflop. No check; realize equity now, build pot OOP. Bet 400 (~3/4 pot) optimal size for value while denying equity to draws.

We flop top two (KTs) on a very dynamic board (Kh Th 6h). Elon’s small lead is weighted toward draws (hearts, QJ/J9/98), Kx/Tx, and some sets. Calling lets too many turns realize equity and makes it hard to get stacks in cleanly, so we should raise for value and protection and to set up a comfortable turn shove with the effective stack. If he 3-bet jams, we’re happy to continue with top two vs his value/draw range.

Hold 7h8h for King-high flush (second nuts) on monotone heart flop Th Kh 6h. Facing FiveTwo's massive flop raise to 2000 (5x over my 400 c-bet) after his UTG+1 open, into 2950 pot, 1600 to call (excellent 2.8:1 implied odds). FiveTwo is aggressive, frequently raises wide preflop from early positions (Hands 11,15,20,23,26,32,37,38,40,49,50,52,64 etc.) and value-bets heavy postflop, but history shows folds to aggression (e.g., Hand 11 turn fold, Hand 52 turn fold, Hand 64 turn fold). Range: nut flush (AhXh, ~6-12 combos if wide), sets (KK/TT/66 ~12 combos total, crush us but we beat), worse flushes (QJh/J9h/54h etc., value/call), rare two-pair. No strong draws (needs 2 hearts for flush). OOP deep (~48BB eff), pot-committal either way; shoving maximizes EV: extracts value from sets/worse flushes (~40-50% of value range), fold equity vs bluffs/overplays (~10-20%), induces hero calls from nuts. Calling risks turn bet/jam OOP, smaller pot vs nuts; jam builds massive pot (~13k+ if called) vs worse, denies villain cheap river. Tournament phase (65/150), stack-building variance fine. All-in raise to 4775 optimal balance/protection/value.

We have top two (KTs) on a very dynamic monotone board. Elon’s lead/clickback-3bet to 4775 while leaving only 400 behind is effectively a commitment play that can include made flushes/sets, but also a lot of strong combo-draws (Axh, QJ with a heart, pair+heart, etc.) that we’re ahead of and want to charge immediately. Given the pot odds (we only need ~28% to continue) and the fact that raising costs just 400 more than calling while locking up his last chips and maximizing value/protection, the best play is to put him all-in now.

Holding 7h8h for the king-high flush (Kh Th 8h 7h 6h) on the monotone heart flop Th Kh 6h, second-nuts behind only ace-high flushes (Ahxh). Already 3-bet all-in on flop to 4775 after FiveTwo's raise to 2000 over my 400 c-bet, now facing his 4-bet to 5175 (calling additional 400 with 400 behind into 10500 pot, ~26:1 odds requiring just ~3.7% equity). FiveTwo's UTG+1 open + flop 5x raise + 4-bet suggests polarized range: nuts (Ahxh ~4-8 combos), monsters like sets (KK/TT/66 ~6 combos, which lose to our flush), possible two-pair KTs/K6s/Th6h (unlikely preflop), lower flushes (K9h-K2h where kickers worse, e.g., K9h = KTh9h6h < KTh8h7h6h), or bluffs/overplays (history of wide aggression: frequent early raises like Hands 11,15,20,23,32,37,38,49,52,64; postflop heavies but folds to pressure e.g., Hand 64 turn). No viable draws left (board paired flush). We have ~35-45% equity vs his value range (beats sets, ~half lower flushes, blocks some); fold equity irrelevant now. OOP irrelevant at all-in; massive overlay justifies snap-call to realize equity, build pot vs worse (~half his range), exploit aggression. Tournament mid (65/150 hands), deep effective (~25BB post-call), stack-building prioritizes EV over variance. Folding second-nuts here catastrophic.

I am in the UTG position (which is effectively the Cutoff in a 4-handed game) with King-Deuce suited (Ks 2s). My stack is approximately 60 big blinds, making me the short stack at the table. While K2s has some flush potential, it is a weak hand with a very poor kicker that is easily dominated. Given the deep stacks of my opponents (especially the Big Blind who just won a massive pot), they can apply pressure or defend widely. There is no need to take unnecessary risks with a marginal hand from early position. Folding is the disciplined and standard play here.

I am on the button in a 4-handed game with a speculative suited hand (3h 5h). Given the fold from UTG, I have the opportunity to attack the blinds. FiveTwo (Big Blind) has been playing aggressively as the chip leader, so a small raise is preferable to test the waters while maintaining a good pot-to-stack ratio for post-flop play in position. I'll raise to 2.5x the big blind.

I’m in the small blind with 5♠8♥ facing a button open to 250. Pot is 400 and I need to put 200 more (250 total) — a very cheap price relative to my 13,775 stack (≈1.5%). 5‑8 offsuit is too weak to 3‑bet OOP, but folding would be too tight given the pot odds and deep stacks. I’ll call to see a flop and keep my range wide; if I don’t hit a pair or a reasonable draw I’ll proceed cautiously (often check/fold to strong continuation bets). If the big blind cold‑calls and it becomes multiway I’ll tighten up postflop; if Flash 3‑bets big preflop I’ll fold unless I pick up a very strong read.

We’re getting a good price closing the action: calling 150 to see a flop in a pot that will be 750 gives us ~20% immediate pot odds. While 8♣4♠ is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability, the multiway nature also means less 3-bet pressure and we can realize enough equity by playing very cautiously postflop (mostly check/fold unless we connect well with pair+draw/two pair/strong draws). No reason to squeeze with this holding, and folding would be slightly too tight given the price.

Flop is Td 3d Ac and I hold 5♠8♥ — no pair, no diamond, basically air with only a very remote backdoor. I’m out of position facing the preflop open (Button) and a cold caller (BB), both of whom will have a lot of A‑x and hands that connect with this board. With two opponents and essentially zero equity, I don’t want to fire a bluff into position or get into a raise/fold situation. Best play is to check and concede the initiative: I’ll fold to any meaningful continuation bet, and only consider calling a very small probe to float if the sizing is tiny (<= ~1/3 pot) and the player/line suggests weakness. If both check through I’ll re‑evaluate the turn for any pair or believable bluffing opportunity. For now: check.

We have 8♣4♠ on A♣ T♦ 3♦: no pair, no meaningful draw (only very weak backdoors). Multiway vs the preflop raiser, this board strongly favors Flash’s range (lots of Ax and broadways), so a donk-bluff is low EV and invites a raise. Best plan is to check, realize whatever tiny backdoor equity we have for free, and be ready to check/fold versus most bets.

I have bottom pair (3s) on an Ace-high board in a three-way pot. Although I was the pre-flop aggressor, C-betting into two opponents on this texture is risky, especially since the Ace is a card they often defend with from the blinds. By checking, I am exercising pot control and realizing my showdown equity. If both opponents check again on the turn, I can re-evaluate, but for now, I want to keep the pot small with a marginal hand.

I have 5♠8♥ and completely missed the Td 3d Ac Kc board — no pair, no diamond draw. This is a multiway pot (Button opened, BB called) and both opponents checked the flop and checked the turn, which indicates neither has an obvious strong value hand they’re happy to build with yet, but it also reduces my fold equity. OOP with air against two opponents is a poor spot to bluff — opponents can have Ax, Tx, Kx, or slow‑played made hands that will call a probe, and I don’t want to inflate the pot with essentially zero equity. Best play is to check and preserve stack. If faced with a meaningful bet on the turn I’ll fold; I’d only consider calling a very tiny probe to attempt a river float against a player who barrels light.

We still have complete air with 8♣4♠ on A♣ T♦ 3♦ K♣ and no real equity (no pair, no draw beyond extremely thin backdoors). Multiway, a turn donk-bluff is low EV because either opponent can easily have Ax/Kx, and even if called we have almost no rivers to continue on. Since Flash checked back the flop, their range can still contain plenty of showdown hands and traps, and Minni’s check doesn’t cap them either. Best is to keep the pot small, take the free card, and be prepared to check/fold versus most bets from Flash.

I currently hold bottom pair on a board featuring both an Ace and a King, which are high-frequency cards in my opponents' defending ranges. After everyone checked the flop, the King on the turn further connects with many broadway hands. Both Minni and FiveTwo have checked to me again, but since I have some marginal showdown value and very little chance of folding out better hands like a Ten or a weak Ace, I will check back to see a free river and control the size of the pot.

Check. I have 5♠8♥ and completely missed the Td 3d Ac Kc 7c runout — no pair, no club, no realistic showdown value. This is a multiway pot (button opened, BB called) and both opponents checked flop and turn, so I have very little fold equity OOP and I don't block the club flush. Betting as a bluff into two players here is a poor EV play; they can easily have Ax/Kx/Tx or a club and will call a probe. Pot is 750 vs my ~13.6k stack, but that doesn't justify spewing chips with essentially zero equity. Best line is to check and fold to any meaningful bet; if both check through I'll muck at showdown.

River Td 3d Ac Kc 7c: we still have no pair and no made draw (only an 8 kicker improving the board). Betting is a poor bluff multiway because we’d need both Minni and Flash to fold, and their check-check line can easily contain plenty of Ax/Kx and some slowplays that won’t fold to a single bet. We also have a bit of showdown value versus hands that miss completely or have very low kickers, so taking the free showdown is higher EV than turning this into a low-credibility bluff.

I have bottom pair (3s) on a board that is extremely heavy with broadway cards (A, K, T). Both Minni and FiveTwo have checked through the flop, turn, and river. While my hand is very weak, I have marginal showdown value against missed draws like a low diamond draw. There is no merit in betting here because I am unlikely to fold out a better hand (like a Ten or a Seven) and I will certainly not be called by anything worse. Checking back allows me to reach showdown for free in a pot where the action suggests everyone is weak.

I am UTG with 7h 3s, which is a very weak starting hand. In a 4-handed game, standard opening ranges are wider, but 7-3 offsuit remains well outside the range of hands that can be played profitably from any position. Given my healthy stack and the lack of any strategic reason to bluff this specific hand into the remaining players, folding is the most sensible play to conserve chips for better opportunities.

I’m on the button with a weak offsuit hand (3♦5♥). Pot is only 150 and it’s 100 to call — this is a spot where I can try to take the blinds with position and a wide stealing range. Table history shows frequent 3x-ish opens, so a standard button open to 300 is credible and gives me fold equity versus the SB/BB. I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand, so I’ll open to a normal size (300) and see how the blinds react. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet/shove (I have poor equity vs strong action); if called, c-bet dry boards to continue, but otherwise play cautiously and only continue with real improvement or strong draws. This balances aggression with risk control.

We’re in the SB facing a 3bb Button open with Q4o. This hand performs poorly out of position: weak high-card value, dominated when we make a queen, and limited playability. Calling gives decent raw pot odds but we’ll realize equity badly OOP and the BB still has options to squeeze. As a bluff 3-bet candidate, Q4o is also suboptimal (no suitedness/connectivity, mediocre blockers), and Minni has shown willingness to continue vs our 3-bets. Best chip-EV here is to avoid a tough OOP spot and preserve our stack edge.

I hold Queen-King offsuit in the Big Blind facing a standard raise from Minni on the Button. This is a strong hand that dominates much of the Button's opening range (like K-x, Q-x, J-x). However, I am out of position. A 3-bet would isolate me against a stronger range and potentially force a fold from hands I dominate, while flat-calling keeps Minni's range wide and manages the pot size. Given my stack depth (~58 BB) relative to the tournament, calling is a lower-variance play that realizes equity well. I need to call 200 chips into a pot of 450, offering attractive odds.

On this [2h 5s Ah] flop, the Ace heavily favors Minni's range as the pre-flop raiser. As the caller in the Big Blind, my range is capped and generally weaker on this texture. Leading out (donk betting) would be essentially turning my hand into a bluff with little equity if called. My King-Queen high has some showdown value if the opponent also missed and checks back, but significantly less value if money goes into the pot. The standard and most prudent play is to check to the aggressor and re-evaluate based on their action. If they bet, I will likely fold as it is difficult to float on an Ace-high board without a pair or stronger draw.

I have middle pair (5s) with a weak 3 kicker on A‑2‑5 (two hearts on the board). I was the preflop aggressor, and Pro checked — an ambiguous reaction that can include a lot of overcards, some weak pairs, and some Ax. I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand that is crushed by any Ace, but I can get value from worse pairs, gutshots/overcards that will fold, and deny free cards to backdoor draws. A small continuation bet (~1/3 pot) is the right compromise: it extracts thin value and maintains fold equity while keeping the pot controllable. If I face a big raise I will give it up — my kicker is poor and I don’t want to play a big pot out of position against a likely Ace. Bet 225 to take the initiative and keep options open on later streets.

On an Ace-high flop (2h 5s Ah), the board heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. Although I hold King-Queen high, which beats some pure bluffs, I am drawing very thin against any Ace or pocket pair. The c-bet size is small (about 1/3 pot), which is standard for this texture, but calling out of position without a pair or a strong draw (no flush draw, no straight draw) is a losing long-term strategy. I have reverse implied odds if I hit a King or Queen, as I could still be dominated by Ax. Preserving the stack is the prudent move here.

I’m UTG with J♠4♥ — a weak, offsuit holding with poor playability and a high chance of being dominated. In a 4‑handed game raising here would require a much stronger range, and limping (calling 100) simply invites multiway action and likely puts me out of position against the button and the blinds. Calling 100 into a 150 pot needs roughly ~40% equity to break even, which J4o doesn’t have versus typical opening/defending ranges at this table. My stack (13,925) is healthy — there’s no need to gamble chips with a marginal hand. I’ll fold and wait for a better spot.

4-handed and on the Button after UTG folds, we should be opening very wide to pick up the blinds. 5h3h isn’t a premium hand, but it has decent playability in position (wheel/straight/flush potential) and benefits a lot from fold equity. I’ll use a small steal size to risk less versus Pro’s shorter stack and avoid inflating the pot unnecessarily if defended; if we face a 3-bet shove from SB we can comfortably fold.

I hold Pocket Eights (88) in the Small Blind. The chip leader, FiveTwo, has raised to 250 from the Button. While 88 is a strong hand that is ahead of the Button's opening range, I am the shortest stack at the table (56 BB) and will be out of position for the rest of the hand. 3-betting would inflate the pot against the deep-stacked chip leader, potentially creating a high-variance spot where I might face a 4-bet or have to play a large pot OOP with a capped hand. Calling allows me to see a flop, set-mine (implied odds are good), and potentially win at showdown on low boards while keeping the opponent's range wide. This approach minimizes risk while retaining the strength of my hand.

I am in the Big Blind holding Q9o. FiveTwo opened from the button for 250 and Pro called from the small blind. I need to call 150 more to see the flop in a pot that currently has 600. Q9o is a standard defending hand in this spot, especially getting 4:1 pot odds and closing the action. My hand has decent playability in a multi-way pot, and folding would be too tight given the price.

The flop comes Ace-Ten-Seven rainbow. I hold pocket Eights, which is now a weak hand (underpair to the Ace and Ten). This board texture heavily favors the pre-flop raiser (FiveTwo on the Button) who has many Ax and Tx combinations in their range. As the first to act in a 3-way pot out of position, leading out would be a mistake as I would only be called by better hands. My best option is to check. If the Button bets, I will likely have to fold, but checking gives me a chance to see a free turn or showdown cheaply if the action checks through.

On this Ace-high board (As-7h-Tc) in a three-way pot, I have no pair and no real draw with my Q9o. FiveTwo was the pre-flop aggressor and Pro called from the small blind, both of whom have ranges that hit this board reasonably well. Since Pro has already checked to me, checking is the most logical play. I have no reason to bluff into two opponents on such a dry, high-card board where someone likely holds an Ace or a Ten. I will check and likely fold to any subsequent betting action.

Both blinds checked to me on an A-high, fairly dry flop that hits my preflop raising range well. Even though this is multiway and I have no made hand (only weak backdoor heart/straight possibilities), I can profitably take a small stab to fold out a lot of the blinds’ whiffed holdings (broadways, small pairs without an ace, random suited hands) and deny equity. I’ll keep the sizing small to reduce the cost when I get called/raised; if Pro check-raises all-in I just fold.

The flop is As 7h Tc, which is a very bad board for my pocket Eights (88). There are two overcards, and the texture hits the Button's opening range quite hard (Ax, Tx). The Button has bet 250 into a pot of 750 (1/3 pot). Even though the price is cheap (getting 4:1 odds), I am in a 3-way pot out of position with a player left to act behind me. Calling hoping to hit a set (2 outs) is not profitable, and my hand has very little showdown value against any Ace or Ten. Continuing here would be losing chips in the long run. The disciplined play is to fold.

On this Ace-high board (As-7h-Tc), my Q9o has no pair and no meaningful draw. FiveTwo, who opened from the button, has a range that heavily favors this board, and their continuation bet of 250 into a pot of 750 is standard. While the price to call is relatively small (250 to win a total of 1000), calling with Queen-high out of position with no equity to improve is a losing play. Pro has already folded, and I have no reason to believe a bluff or a float would be profitable here against FiveTwo's range.

4-handed ranges widen a lot, and K5s (suited king with some wheel/flush potential) is strong enough to open from first position. With our big stack we can apply pressure to the blinds and the short-stacked button while keeping our risk contained. I’ll use a standard small open size to 2.5bb to deny the blinds a cheap flop and keep my raise/fold cost reasonable versus a shove.

I am holding Pocket Fours (4d 4s) on the Button facing a standard UTG raise to 250 from FiveTwo. My stack is 5400 (54 BBs), and the effective stack is determined by my stack since FiveTwo covers me. To call, I need to invest 250, which is approximately 4.6% of my stack. This meets the criteria for 'set mining' (generally looking for ~20x implied odds or better). Being on the Button gives me a positional advantage post-flop. 3-betting with low pairs is risky as it often isolates against a stronger range or invites a 4-bet shove. Folding is too tight given the implied odds and position. Therefore, I will flat call to try and hit a set on the flop.

I am in the Small Blind holding Queen-Jack offsuit. UTG (FiveTwo) has opened to 250 and the Button (Pro) has called. I need to put in 200 more to call. Given the pot odds (calling 200 into a 650 pot), this is a standard call with a hand that has decent playability, even though I will be out of position for the rest of the hand. Raising here would likely bloat the pot unnecessarily against a deep-stacked UTG player and a short-stacked button.

UTG opened to 250 and both the button and SB called — multiway pot. My hand (3♦6♣ offsuit) has very poor playability (no flush, weak straight potential) and is not a candidate to isolate or 3-bet. However the extra call is only 150 into an 850 pot, giving ~6.7:1 immediate pot odds to see a flop. I’ll defend the BB cheaply to realize equity and fold to significant aggression on most flops. I will call to 250 total, check the flop and proceed only if I hit or pick up a good draw; otherwise I’ll fold to pressure.

I have completely missed this flop, which is very dry and features an Ace. Given that FiveTwo was the pre-flop raiser, this board is much better for their range than mine. Furthermore, there are three other players in the pot, making a bluff highly unlikely to succeed. I will check and look to fold if there is any betting action, as I have no equity to continue.

I defended the big blind cheaply preflop, but on this flop (5♣ 2♦ A♠) my 3♦6♣ has very little equity: no flush possibilities and only a gutshot to the wheel (four 4s). This is a multiway pot with the UTG opener and the button still in — the Ace heavily favors the raiser’s range, so a bluff here has poor fold equity and I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position. I’ll check to see a free turn and only continue if I actually improve (hit a 4 or pair that has showdown value); otherwise I’ll fold to meaningful aggression.

4-way on an A-high flop, this board smashes everyone’s continuing range (lots of Ax preflop calls) and our pair of fives has very poor equity when called. A c-bet here has low fold equity multiway and risks getting floated/raised, effectively turning a marginal showdown hand into a bluff. Checking keeps the pot manageable, lets us realize our equity (including backdoor diamonds), and we can react to sizing if someone starts betting.

I hold Pocket Fours on a board of 5c 2d As. I called pre-flop to set mine but missed on the flop. The board contains an Ace, which hits the UTG raiser's range as well as the blind callers' ranges well. With four players in the pot, it is highly likely someone has an Ace or a better pair than mine. The UTG player checked, which might signal weakness or a trap, but betting here accomplishes little: worse hands fold, and better hands (especially any Ax) call. My hand has very little showdown value but potential to improve if I hit a 4. Being in position, the best move is to check back, realize my equity for free, and re-evaluate on the turn without bloating the pot.

The turn brings a second deuce to an already dry board. The flop was checked around by all four players, which is unusual for an Ace-high board, suggesting that either no one has an Ace or the players with marginal hands are playing cautiously. However, I still only have Queen-high with no draws or pair potential. Betting into three opponents as a bluff is low-frequency and risky given my position. I will check to see the river for free.

Check. I have 3♦6♣ — no pair and almost no equity (only a gutted 4 to make a straight). This is a multiway pot with the UTG opener and callers and an Ace on the flop, so opponents’ ranges are heavily weighted toward A‑x and strong pairs. Betting here would have very poor fold equity and would bloat the pot out of position with a junk hand. I’ll take a free river card and preserve my stack: check now and plan to fold to any meaningful turn bet (only consider calling tiny probes that give correct pot odds).

Turn pairs the 2, improving us to two pair (5s and 2s) with a good kicker, but this is still a medium-strength hand in a 4-way pot on A-5-2-2. A large portion of everyone’s preflop calling range contains Ax, and Ax will often check the flop multiway for pot control—so betting now gets called a lot by hands that have us crushed (Aces and Deuces), while worse hands (random floats/underpairs) often fold. With a free check available, the highest-EV line is pot control: check, realize our showdown value, and be prepared to fold versus significant river aggression in a multiway pot.

I hold Pocket Fours on a board of A-5-2-2. The action has checked to me on the turn after checking around on the flop. With three opponents, it is likely one of them holds an Ace or a Five, or a pocket pair higher than 4s, although the passive action suggests weakness. Betting accomplishes little: worse hands (unpaired high cards) will fold, and better hands (any pair of 5s, Aces, or trips) will call or raise. I have marginal showdown value against missed broadway cards. The best play is to check back to realize equity and try to reach a cheap showdown.

The board has run out with an Ace and a King, and both the flop and turn were checked through by all four players. While this suggests that no one likely holds a strong Ace, my Queen-high has zero showdown value against any pair. However, bluffing into three opponents is high-risk and low-reward in a small pot of 1000. I will check and fold if there is any betting action, as it's unlikely a bluff will force out three players who might be holding small pairs or marginal showdown hands.

Board is 5♣ 2♦ A♠ 2♠ K♣; my hand 3♦6♣ has missed completely (no pair, no real showdown value). This was a multiway pot led by the UTG opener, so opponents' ranges are heavily weighted toward A-x, pocket pairs and hands that beat me. Everyone checked through to me on the river, so a bluff here would have very poor fold equity against multiple opponents and is likely to be called by any Ace/King/paired hand. I refuse to bloat the pot with a junk bluff — preserve stack and take the free showdown. If anyone had bet earlier I would fold to meaningful pressure.

River improves us to K-K-5-5-A (two pair), and after three streets of checks from everyone this is very often the best hand versus ranges full of Kx, 5x, and pocket pairs that will still look us up for a small price. The main hands that beat us are any Ax (two pair A-2 or better) and any 2x (trips/full house), but those are less likely to keep checking multiway, and we can keep our downside limited with a small “block/value” bet. If we get raised, it’s heavily weighted to Ax/2x and we can comfortably fold.

On the river, the board is 5c 2d As 2s Kc. I hold Pocket Fours (4d 4s). The UTG player (FiveTwo) bets 350 into a pot of 1000 after the flop and turn were checked through. The board contains an Ace and a King, and paired Deuces. Any Ace, King, Five, or pocket pair higher than 4s beats me. Given that FiveTwo raised UTG pre-flop, their range is heavy on Aces and Kings. The delayed bet on the river suggests they likely hit the King or have a weak Ace they are now comfortable betting for thin value after the passive action. Betting into three opponents on this texture is rarely a stone-cold bluff with air worse than 44. I am getting good pot odds, but my hand is simply too weak to call against a realistic range.

On the river, the board is Ace and King high, and I hold only Queen-high. While the action was checked through on the flop and turn, suggesting no one holds a particularly strong Ace, FiveTwo has now placed a small lead bet into the remaining players. Even though the bet size is small (350 into 1000), my hand has no showdown value against any pair, any King, or any Ace. Furthermore, Minni is still to act behind me. Calling with Queen-high in a multi-way pot is extremely risky and mathematically unsound here. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I have 3♦6♣ and missed the board (5♣ 2♦ A♠ 2♠ K♣). This was a multiway pot headed by UTG, so FiveTwo’s range is heavily weighted to Ax, Kx and pocket pairs that beat me at showdown. He bets 350 into a 1350 pot — calling would require ~350/(1350+350) ≈ 20.6% equity. My hand has essentially zero showdown equity versus his likely value-heavy line, and a raise would be an ambitious bluff that won’t fold many Ax/Kx or pairs from an UTG opener. I planned to check/fold to river aggression and will stick to that plan to protect my stack. Fold.

I am UTG 4-handed with 4c 6c (suited one-gapper). While ranges are wider in a 4-handed game, 64s is still a speculative hand that is weak to open from the first position, especially given that I am the shortest stack (51.5 BB) facing three deep-stacked opponents who can apply significant pressure. Playing this hand out of position is likely -EV. Folding is the disciplined choice.

I am on the button in a 4-handed game with King-Seven offsuit. This is a standard opening hand to put pressure on the blinds, especially with a healthy stack of 166 big blinds. Pro has already folded from the UTG position, leaving only the blinds to act behind me. Raising to 300 is a standard sizing to steal the blinds or play the hand in position.

Qs3h is a very weak, offsuit hand to defend from the small blind. Flash opened to 300; calling 250 into the 450 pot commits chips to a spot where I’ll be out of position and often dominated — I’d need ~36% equity to break even but Q3o has much less vs a button’s opening range, and the BB could come along making it multiway. A 3-bet to 500 is possible as a bluff but this hand lacks proper blockers and postflop playability. With deep stacks I’d rather preserve chips for better spots in position. Fold.

Button open is wide and we’re getting a very good price (200 to win 650 after we call) with deep effective stacks (~163bb), making 33 a solid defend for set-mining and realizing equity in positionally disadvantageous but range-protected spots. 3-betting 33 here would mostly turn it into a low-equity bluff and bloat the pot out of position, so I prefer to keep the pot manageable and see a flop.

On A♣ T♥ 2♦ our 33 is a low underpair with very limited equity and no meaningful backdoors. Button has range advantage here (lots of Ax / strong broadways), so leading doesn’t make sense and check-raising would just bloat the pot as a low-equity bluff. Best is to check and respond to sizing: comfortably continue with stronger bluff-catchers/backdoors, and typically let this go versus any reasonable c-bet.

I opened King-Seven offsuit from the button and was called by the Big Blind. The flop of Ace-Ten-Deuce is very favorable for my perceived range as the pre-flop aggressor, as I have all the strongest Aces in my range. Since FiveTwo checked to me, I will place a continuation bet of 300 (roughly 46% of the pot). This size is enough to put pressure on his middle pairs, weak draws, or missed hands while keeping my risk relatively low if he has an Ace or a better King-high.

Flash’s range advantage on A♣T♥2♦ is real, but the c-bet is very small (~1/3 pot), which implies high frequency and gives me strong immediate pot odds (300 to win 1250 ≈ 24%). Even with a weak underpair, I retain some showdown value versus his broadway/low-card floats and still have 2 outs to a set with deep effective stacks. I’ll defend by calling once and will mostly check-fold turns versus meaningful continued aggression unless I improve.

Turn 5♣ doesn’t materially shift the range advantage away from the Button on A♣T♥2♦; Flash still has a lot of Ax and strong broadways. Our 33 remains a weak bluff-catcher with only thin improvement (2 outs to a set, plus a small gutshot to a wheel on a 4). Leading would be a low-equity bluff that risks getting raised off our hand and bloats the pot out of position. Best is to check and respond to sizing: likely fold vs meaningful pressure, and only consider continuing vs very small bets consistent with high-frequency barreling.

I am currently holding King-high on a board with an Ace. I continuation-bet on the flop to represent the Ace, and FiveTwo called, which suggests they have at least some piece of the board, such as a Ten, a weak Ace, or a pocket pair. The Five of Clubs on the turn doesn't significantly change the board texture. Checking back here allows me to control the pot size and see the river for free. Since I have very little showdown value and FiveTwo has already shown resistance by calling the flop, a second barrel is unlikely to fold out an Ace, and I'd rather not commit more chips to a hand that is likely behind.

River goes check-check to us in a single-raised pot on A♣ T♥ 2♦ 5♣ 7♥. With 3♣3♠ we have pure showdown value: we beat his missed broadways/air that c-bet small and then gave up on the turn, and we basically never get called by worse if we bet. A bluff doesn’t make sense either—Flash’s continuing range to a river bet is heavily Ax (and some Tx), which won’t fold at a profitable frequency, while the hands we can fold out are the same hands we already beat. Best is to check and take the free showdown.

On the river, I have improved to a pair of sevens (third pair). Pre-flop, I raised from the button and followed up with a continuation bet on an Ace-high flop, which FiveTwo called. The turn and river both went check-check. My hand now has some showdown value, but it is unlikely to be called by any worse hands if I bet, and I'm certainly behind any Ace or Ten in the Big Blind's range. Checking back allows me to reach showdown for free and potentially win against missed draws or lower pocket pairs that decided to peel the flop but didn't improve.

I am UTG in a 4-handed game holding 78o. While opening ranges are wider in short-handed play, 78o is still too weak to open from first position, especially with a short stack in the Big Blind (Pro) who might be looking for a spot to shove and a chip leader in the Small Blind (FiveTwo) who has shown a tendency to be active. Folding is the most disciplined play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I'm on the button with KcQs — a strong Broadway hand in position with only the blinds to act. With a healthy stack (≈136 BB) and the SB much deeper (≈239 BB) while the BB (Pro) is ~50 BB, this is a clear open-raise spot to seize initiative and either steal the blinds or build a pot in position. A standard 3xBB to 300 applies pressure, denies good odds to callers, and keeps the pot manageable if I face a 3-bet. If I'm called I'll use position to c-bet on favorable dry/high-card flops and control the pot when boards are coordinated or I face aggression; if I face a sizable 3-bet (especially from the 50 BB Pro) I'll proceed cautiously and often fold unless the sizing or reads justify calling/4-betting.

We’re in the SB facing a 3bb Button open. J2o is heavily dominated by Minni’s opening range (all better Jx, most broadways, many suited hands) and plays very poorly out of position. While Minni has shown some willingness to fold to 3-bets, the risk/reward on a SB 3-bet bluff here requires an unrealistically high fold frequency, and getting called puts us in a tough spot postflop. Best EV is to avoid leaking chips and fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Q7o facing a 3x raise (300) from Minni on the Button. The Small Blind has folded. The pot is 450 and I need to call 200, offering me pot odds of roughly 31%. While Q7o has decent raw equity against a wide button opening range, playing a weak off-suit Queen out of position is difficult, especially against an opponent like Minni who has shown aggression (frequent opens and post-flop barrels). The 3x sizing makes the price to call less attractive than a min-raise. Given my stack size (50BB) compared to the opponents, avoiding marginal spots out of position to preserve chips is the prudent play. Q7o is typically near the bottom of a defending range or a fold against a 3x open. I decide to fold.

Q♠3♣ is a very weak, unplayable hand from UTG in a 4‑handed game. Limping (call 100) just invites pressure and multiway pots where my Q is often dominated; opening (raise to 200) risks a 3‑bet from the big stacked Button or BB and puts me out of position for the whole hand. With ~137 BBs I want to pick clearer spots to build the stack, so folding preserves equity for later.

4-handed after UTG folds, this is a prime button steal spot. Ks4d isn’t strong enough to limp/call big action, but it has a king blocker and plays fine in position in a single-raised pot. Both blinds have incentive to defend, yet we profit by attacking their ranges and leveraging position; we can comfortably fold if we face a sizeable 3-bet (especially from the SB/shorter stack). I’ll use a small open to keep risk low while still applying pressure.

I am in the Small Blind with Qd Jc. The Button (FiveTwo) has raised to 225. This is a standard Button open, and FiveTwo has been active. My hand (QJo) is strong enough to defend against a wide Button opening range, although playing out of position is a disadvantage. The raise size is small (2.25BB), giving me favorable pot odds to call. 3-betting is an option but risks bloating the pot with a hand that can be dominated. Folding is too tight given the price and stack depth (50BB). I will call to see a flop.

I am in the Big Blind and am being offered excellent pot odds to see a flop. FiveTwo raised to 225 from the button, and Pro called from the Small Blind. To call, I only need to commit 125 more chips into a total pot of 550, which gives me roughly 4.4-to-1 odds (needing about 18.5% equity). While 93s is a weak hand, its suitedness and the multi-way nature of the pot justify a defense at this price. I intend to play 'fit-or-fold' on the flop, as I will be out of position against the chip leader, FiveTwo.

I hold QdJc on a wet flop of 9s6h7h. This board texture heavily favors the ranges of the opponents (sets, two pairs, flush draws, straight draws), particularly the Big Blind who gets a good price to call pre-flop with connectors, or the Button's opening range. My hand has only two overcards and very weak backdoor straight potential (runner-runner). Being out of position in a multi-way pot with no immediate draw and only high-card value, leading out (donk betting) is ill-advised. The standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor and evaluate based on subsequent action. I intend to fold to a bet unless it is extremely small, as my equity is low.

I have flopped top pair with a 9, although my kicker (3) is very weak. The board is highly coordinated with straight and flush possibilities (9s-6h-7h). In a three-way pot where I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (FiveTwo), checking is the standard and most prudent play. Leading out would likely only get called by better hands or strong draws that have significant equity against me. By checking, I can see how FiveTwo responds and potentially see a turn card cheaply or evaluate based on their bet sizing.

Multiway on a very dynamic 9-6-7 two-heart flop, this board smashes both blind defending ranges (pairs, OESDs like T8/85, and a lot of heart draws). With Ks4d we have essentially no equity (no hearts, no real straight prospects—only a single overcard to the 9), so a c-bet will get called or check-jammed at a high frequency, especially by the short SB who can pressure us. Taking the free card keeps the pot small, protects our opening range by having some give-ups, and lets us improve to top pair on a K turn without lighting chips on fire now.

The turn brings an Ace, which is a card that favors the pre-flop aggressor's (FiveTwo) range more than mine. Although FiveTwo checked back the flop, they may have been exercising pot control with an Ace or now have hit top pair. With Queen-high and no draw on a board of 9s 6h 7h Ad, I have negligible equity and cannot profitably bluff into two opponents, especially out of position. My plan is to check and fold to any bet.

The Turn card is an Ace, which is an overcard to my pair of nines. Since the flop was checked around, it's possible that nobody has a strong hand, but the Ace is a likely card for any player to have in their range given the pre-flop action. My pair of nines currently has some showdown value, but I am out of position against FiveTwo. I will check to see how the button reacts. If FiveTwo bets, I will have to evaluate the size and the likelihood they are representing the Ace or a draw on this coordinated board.

Turn brings the Ad, a great card for the preflop raiser to represent (we have lots of Ax in our opening range, while the blinds’ flop-check ranges are heavy on 7x/9x/6x and draws). After two checks again, we can often pick this pot up immediately with a credible barrel. I’ll use a medium sizing that pressures one-pair hands and some missed overcard/draw holdings without risking much vs the SB’s possible check-shove; if either player check-raises big (especially the short SB), we just fold and move on. If called, we’ll mostly shut down on bad rivers and only consider further aggression on very favorable runouts.

I hold QdJc on a board of 9s 6h 7h Ad. After checking the flop and turn, the Button (FiveTwo) bets 450 into a pot of 675. The Ace on the turn heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range, and their delayed c-bet is credible. I have no pair, no straight draw (as an 8 or T alone doesn't complete a straight for me given the gaps), and no flush draw. With only Queen-high and drawing virtually dead against an Ace or better, I have no equity to continue. The pot odds do not justify a call. Folding is the correct play.

On the flop, I had top pair with a very weak kicker, and the action checked around, which was ideal. The turn brings an Ace, a significant overcard that fits the pre-flop raising range of the button (FiveTwo) perfectly. FiveTwo has now bet 450 into a pot of 675. While FiveTwo is an aggressive player who could easily be using the Ace as a 'scare card' to bluff, my specific hand (93d) is near the bottom of my range that has any showdown value. If I call here, I am likely to face another large bet on the river, and with my weak kicker, I am even losing to other 9s in his range. Given the coordinated board and the likelihood that the Ace either hit him or gave him a perfect bluffing opportunity, I will fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

4-handed, TT is a clear value open from UTG (ranges are wider and we’re ahead of most continuing hands). Raising also denies equity to overcards and builds a pot against worse pairs/broadways. I’ll use a moderate open size to keep dominated hands in while still applying pressure and setting up comfortably to call off versus the short-stacked button’s potential reshove.

I am on the Button with Ten of Diamonds and Eight of Clubs (T8o). The UTG player (FiveTwo), who is the chip leader, has opened with a raise to 275. T8o is a weak hand that is easily dominated by a UTG opening range. Although I have position, my stack is the shortest at the table (around 48 BBs), and I should not be bleeding chips by calling raises with marginal off-suit hands against strong ranges. The disciplined play is to fold.

I am in the Small Blind with Jc 5d. FiveTwo, who is the chip leader, raised from UTG to 275. J5o is a very weak hand to play out of position against a strong UTG range, especially with an aggressive player like FiveTwo. Calling would put me in a difficult spot post-flop, and raising is too risky with such low equity. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here to preserve my stack.

Facing an UTG open to 275 from FiveTwo, I'm on the big blind with K♣2♣? (Kh2c). To call is 175 into a 425 pot, so I’d need ~29% equity to break even. K2o is poorly positioned and heavily dominated by a typical UTG opening range (broadways, Kx, Axs, pairs). Playability postflop is very weak and a small 3-bet bluff here is thin — likely to get called or 4-bet by a deeper, aggressive stack. With no compelling blocker or suited connectivity and good future spots available, the +EV play is to fold and preserve my ~13.7k stack.

I am in UTG (effectively Cutoff 4-handed) with A2o and a stack of ~48BB. While ranges widen in short-handed play, A2o is typically at the bottom of or outside a standard opening range from this position due to kicker problems and lack of playability post-flop. Being the shortest stack, I should avoid bleeding chips with marginal hands that are easily dominated. Folding is the disciplined and standard play here.

I have Ace-Jack offsuit on the button in a 4-handed game, which is a very strong hand. Pro has already folded. I will raise to 300 (3x the big blind) to take control of the pot and exert pressure on the blinds. This is a standard opening size for this position to either win the blinds immediately or play a pot in position with a high-equity hand.

Facing a button open to 300 from the small blind I must call 250 more. My hand 6♠2♦ is one of the weakest starting hands with almost no equity and is very difficult to play out of position versus a raiser (and the big blind still to act). With a healthy stack and many better spots, I’ll fold and preserve chips rather than play a marginal hand OOP.

Button is opening to 3x and we’re getting a very good price in the BB (200 to win 650 after we call). 8♦4♦ is weak but suited, has enough raw equity versus a button range, and plays okay as a defend (can make flushes/two pair and has some board coverage). 3-betting would be spewy this deep and out of position, so the best line is to realize equity by calling.

This is a BB defend vs BTN on a K♥ J♦ 3♥ flop. With 8♦4♦ we have no pair, no meaningful straight draw, and only a backdoor diamond flush. Leading here is unnecessary and often just burns chips into a range that connects well (Kx/Jx and strong broadways) and can float/raise. Best is to keep our range protected by checking, see if Villain c-bets, and then decide based on sizing and tendencies whether to fold or occasionally continue with some backdoor equity.

On a flop of Kh-Jd-3h, I have middle pair with the best possible kicker (Ace). While there are several draws available (QT, T9, and various heart draws), my hand is strong enough to bet for value and protection against those draws. FiveTwo checked to me, and since I was the pre-flop aggressor, a continuation bet is standard here. I will bet 350, which is slightly more than half the pot, to charge weaker pairs and draws while keeping the pot controlled in case FiveTwo has a King.

BB defend vs BTN on K♥ J♦ 3♥. Facing a small c-bet (350 into 1000), 8♦4♦ has essentially no pair, no real straight backdoors, and only a thin backdoor diamond flush that won’t realize well out of position on a board that heavily favors the preflop raiser (lots of Kx/Jx/broadways). Calling would be mostly donating chips, and raising has poor blockers and will get continued against too often. Best is to fold and preserve stack for better spots.

I am dealt pocket threes in UTG position in a 4-max game. While small pocket pairs are vulnerable, they have strong set-mining potential and are worth opening in a short-handed environment. My standard opening raise throughout this tournament has been 300 (3x the big blind), and I will maintain that consistency here to put pressure on the blinds and the button.

I’m on the button with 7♣9♥ — a marginal but playable hand in position. Flash opened UTG to 300; facing an early-position open in 4-handed this is not a hand I want to 3-bet with (a min-raise to 500 would be too small/inefficient and a larger 3-bet would be committing with a weak holding). Calling keeps the pot manageable and preserves positional advantage. Pot odds to call 300 into a 450 pot require ~40% equity — borderline versus an UTG opener but my implied odds and positional playability justify a call. Plan: see the flop and play aggressively only if I connect (pair, two pair, straight draw, or favourable board texture); otherwise fold to significant aggression.

We’re 4-handed and chip leader, facing a 3x open from an active player plus a cold-call, with dead money already in (750). Calling with A7o out of position multiway has poor realization and reverse implied odds (dominated Ax, tough postflop spots). A squeeze leverages our stack, uses the ace blocker, and often wins the pot immediately or isolates us into a more manageable heads-up pot while denying the BB a comfortable squeeze/jam spot. Given the open + call and us being OOP, we size up appropriately.

I hold Kc 7d in the Big Blind. The action involves a UTG raise to 300, a Button call, and a large squeeze 3-bet from the Small Blind to 1500. K7 offsuit is a very weak hand in this multi-way, 3-bet scenario. The cost to call (1400) represents nearly 30% of my remaining stack, and my equity against a 3-bet range is very poor. Folding is the only viable option to preserve chips.

I am holding pocket threes (3h 3s) and initially opened the action with a raise to 300 from the UTG position in this 4-max game. After my raise, Minni called on the button, and then FiveTwo in the small blind 3-bet significantly to 1500. FiveTwo has been aggressive and currently holds the largest stack at the table. While pocket pairs have set-mining potential, the price to call here is an additional 1200 chips, and I am out of position against one player (FiveTwo) and have another player (Minni) still behind me. A 3-bet of this size often indicates a strong range (JJ+, AK, or occasionally AQ), against which pocket threes are a significant underdog. Set mining in a 3-bet pot with a shallow-to-medium SPR (Stack-to-Pot Ratio) is generally unfavorable unless the implied odds are massive, and here the risk of missing the set (about 88% of the time) and being forced to fold to further aggression is too high. Therefore, I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Facing a sizable 3‑bet to 1,500 from FiveTwo after I cold‑called the 300 UTG open, my 9♥7♣ is too weak to continue. Calling 1,200 more into a 2,200 pot requires ~35% equity — well above this hand’s realistic equity against a SB 3‑bet range — and the hand has poor post‑flop playability and frequent domination. 4‑bet bluffing is unrealistic here and folding preserves a healthy stack to pick a better spot in position. I fold.

6♠2♥ is one of the worst starting hands — almost no playability and very little chance to realize equity. I’m UTG (effectively opening in a 4-handed game) and would be out of position for the entire hand against Button, SB and BB. Stacks are deep (I have ~133bb) so entering with this garbage invites multi-street trouble and likely domination or a 3-bet/shove from opponents (Pro in SB still has ~47bb, Button and BB are very deep). Calling 100 for a limp or min-raising is negative EV here — better to conserve chips and pick better spots later. Fold.

With Minni folded, it’s effectively 3-handed and I have the button. Even though 5♠4♦ is weak, it has straight potential and plays much better in position with initiative. Raising is profitable here because I can win the blinds often, and when called I can apply pressure postflop with position. I’ll use a standard small BTN open and comfortably fold if the SB/BB 3-bets big (this hand doesn’t defend well vs aggression).

I am in the Small Blind with Q9o and facing a raise to 250 from the active chip leader, FiveTwo, on the Button. While my stack of roughly 47 BBs is not critically short, I am the shortest stack at the table. Q9o is a marginal hand that is frequently dominated by a Button opening range (KQ, QJ, A9, etc.) and plays poorly out of position. Calling would invite a difficult post-flop scenario against an aggressive opponent with a positional advantage. A 3-bet bluff is risky given the hand's poor blockers and playability. Folding preserves my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Big Blind with 6h4d. The Button (FiveTwo) has raised to 250. Although I am getting decent pot odds (150 to call into a pot of 400), 64o is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position, especially against a chip leader who has been active and aggressive. I prefer to fold and wait for a more advantageous situation rather than defending with a bottom-tier hand.

4-handed ranges are wide and this hand has reasonable playability (suited, can make wheel/straight/flush) while also working well as a steal. With my big stack advantage, opening frequently pressures the blinds and keeps me in control of pot size; if I face a strong 3-bet or shove from the shorter stacks, I can comfortably fold. I prefer raising over limping to deny equity and keep initiative.

I am holding 2d Ts on the Button with a stack of 4675 chips (47 BB). The UTG player (FiveTwo, chip leader) has raised to 250. My hand (Ten-Deuce offsuit) is statistically one of the worst starting hands in Hold'em with very poor playability and equity. There is no strategic justification for calling or 3-betting this hand, especially against an UTG open from the chip leader. The standard and correct play is to fold immediately.

I am in the Small Blind with a very weak hand, 4c 3d. FiveTwo, the chip leader, has raised from UTG. Calling out of position with trash like 4-3 offsuit is a losing play, especially against an active opponent. Folding is the only sensible option to preserve my stack for better spots.

Facing an UTG open to 250 (2.5x) with the small blind and button folded, I’m heads-up against an early-position raiser whose opening range is likely strong. I hold 3♠8♠ — a low, marginal suited hand that plays poorly OOP against that range. Calling 150 into a 400 pot requires ~27% equity; 3-8s usually has worse equity vs an UTG range and will be dominated on many boards and difficult to realize equity out of position. With a healthy stack (13225) there’s no need to gamble with a marginal hand; better to preserve chips for better spots. Fold.

I am in UTG position, which is the most disadvantageous position at the table, holding 8h 2c, which is statistically one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em. With a stack of roughly 47 big blinds, I am not in a desperate situation that requires high-variance plays. Open-limping or raising with this trash hand would be a significant strategic error. The correct play is to fold immediately and conserve chips for a better spot.

I am on the Button with Qs 5h. While the Button allows for a wide opening range in a 4-handed game, Q5o is a very weak hand and generally falls outside a profitable opening range, especially with an aggressive chip leader like FiveTwo in the Big Blind who has been frequently defending and 3-betting. There is no need to risk chips on a marginal steal with 168 big blinds remaining in the tournament.

Heads-up from the small blind with Jc7d and deep stacks (~132bb). The pot is 150 and I only need to put another 50 to call. In heads-up play the opening range should be wide; J7o is marginal but playable and raising to seize initiative and pick up the pot uncontested is standard. I’ll raise to 200 (2x BB) to apply pressure and isolate the BB. Plan: fold to a large 3‑bet since I’m out of position and this hand is vulnerable; if called I’ll continuation-bet on favorable/dry boards and exercise pot control or check back on coordinated/wet boards. Raising keeps the initiative while preserving my deep-stack postflop options.

Minni is opening very wide from the small blind for 2bb, and we’re getting a great price (100 to win 400 after we call). T8o has enough raw equity and playability to defend, and calling keeps the pot smaller OOP while allowing us to realize equity and outplay postflop. A 3-bet bluff is possible, but without blockers and given Minni has shown willingness to defend some 3-bets, the higher-variance line isn’t necessary here.

I raised preflop to seize the initiative. The flop 9♦-3♦-6♣ is very dry and misses most of BB’s calling range (broadways, weak pairs, suited connectors). I have J♣7♦ — no pair and only a remote backdoor diamond possibility — so I have little showdown value but good fold equity as the preflop aggressor. I’ll continuation-bet a standard ~50% pot to pressure A/K-high hands and small pairs while keeping the pot controllable with deep stacks. If I face a large check‑raise I’ll fold (hand has poor equity); if I’m called I’ll reevaluate on the turn — check back bricks for pot control, continue only if I improve or a turn card strongly favors my range.

Minni’s flop c-bet is very small (200 into 600), so we’re getting an excellent price to continue: 200 to win 800 now (and 1000 if we call). Even though Td8s is weak here, we still have some equity via a gutshot to the 7, two overcards to the 6/3, and a backdoor diamond component with Td on a two-diamond board. Raising would be higher-variance without strong blockers; calling keeps the pot controlled OOP and lets us realize equity / potentially take it away on later streets when Minni checks.

Board: 9♦-3♦-6♣-K♠. I hold J♣7♦ — no pair and my backdoor diamond draw is dead after the turn. I was the preflop aggressor and c-bet the dry flop, opponent called, which suggests a range containing Kx, medium pairs (9/6/3), pocket pairs or sticky broadways — the K on the turn very often helps that calling range. With no showdown value or meaningful equity and limited fold equity OOP vs a caller, I should not bloat the pot. I’ll check to control the size of the pot and fold to a significant turn/river bet; if the opponent checks back I’ll see the river and only continue with a real improvement or a very favorable river spot.

Turn goes check to us after Minni used a very small flop c-bet. With Td8s we still have a real gutshot to the 7 (river 7 makes 6-7-8-9-T), but our hand has essentially no showdown value if we check back. The Ks is a strong card for our perceived BB-calling range (we can have many Kx), and Minni’s check is consistent with a lot of give-ups/A-high/small pairs that will fold to pressure. I’ll take the stab with a medium size to generate folds while keeping risk controlled; if check-raised, we simply fold.

I hold J♣7♦ on 9♦-3♦-6♣-K♠. I checked the turn and FiveTwo now bets 500 into a 1300 pot. Calling 500 to win 1800 requires ~27.8% equity. I only have ~6 outs (3 Js + 3 sevens) ≈13% to pair on the river, and many of those pairs still lose to Kx, 9x or pocket pairs. FiveTwo’s line (called flop, now betting the turn) looks value-heavy rather than a bluff. With no real draw, no showdown value and being out of position, calling or raising is unjustified — I’ll fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

Holding 4d 3c in the UTG position 4-handed. This is one of the worst starting hands in poker. There is no strategic justification for entering the pot with this hand. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to protect my stack.

On the button with Ts6c facing only the blinds this hand is marginal for an open-raise. The big blind (Pro) is relatively short (~45BB) and could 3-bet/shove, which I don’t want to commit a large portion of my stack to with a weak offsuit T6. Calling 100 keeps the pot small, lets me play the hand in position, and preserves my stack for better spots. Plan: see the flop, c-bet or apply pressure on favorable textures, fold to heavy resistance when I have little equity.

We’re in the SB OOP versus a button limp, and A8o is strong enough to attack that capped/weak range. Raising here wins the pot outright often, denies the BB a cheap flop, and gives us initiative with an ace blocker (reducing the chance we’re 3-bet by premium Ax). With the BB relatively short, a bigger iso size also pressures them and discourages multiway play.

I am in the Big Blind with 7c5c, a speculative suited one-gapper. The Small Blind (FiveTwo) has made a large isolation raise to 450 chips (4.5 BB) over the Button's limp. This is a significant amount of chips to call (350 more into a pot of 650), offering pot odds of roughly 35%, which is borderline for this hand strength against a strong isolation range. Furthermore, the Button (Minni) is yet to act behind me, creating a risk of a squeeze or a multi-way pot where I would be sandwiched between the aggressor and the button. Given my stack size of ~46 BB, calling nearly 10% of my stack with a marginal hand in a potentially difficult spot is not profitable. The disciplined play is to fold.

I limp‑called earlier to see a flop in position with a marginal hand (Ts6c). FiveTwo now raises to 450 — I’ll just call (total 450). Rationale: T6o is too weak to 3‑bet bluff and too thin to fold after I’ve already invested; with deep stacks and the button I want to keep the pot manageable and play post‑flop in position. The extra 350 is small relative to my 12.7k stack and the pot odds / positional advantage justify a flat. Postflop plan: if I hit (T/6, two pair, good draws) I’ll be aggressive; on dry boards I’ll check and fold to significant resistance or float and take the pot away on later streets when appropriate. I’ll fold to strong 3‑bets/shoves preflop or to very heavy action on unfavorable flops.

Heads-up as the preflop aggressor on a paired board (J-J-8), I want a small c-bet with a hand that’s strong but not nutted. A8 makes two pair (jacks and eights) and gets value from worse 8x, pocket pairs, broadway floats, and heart draws, while also denying equity to random overcards that limped/called pre. I’ll keep the sizing small to avoid inflating the pot versus the portion of Minni’s range that has a jack (which can raise) while still extracting and protecting.

Call 350. The board (Jh Js 8h) is pretty hostile for T6o — I have no pair and only a few clean outs (3 Tens, 3 Sixes) and some very slim runner-runner possibilities. However FiveTwo’s bet is small relative to the pot and I’m getting roughly 25–26% pot odds to see the turn. I’m in position so I can realize equity and use position to fold to big turn aggression or apply pressure if the turn is favorable. Folding here surrenders good odds after I already invested preflop; raising would be an overbet with very weak equity and will often get me punished by stronger Jx or paired hands. Plan: call to see the turn — if I pair (T or 6) or pick up a profitable draw I’ll proceed aggressively; if I miss and face significant turn pressure I’ll fold.

We still have a very strong made hand (two pair on a paired board: J-J-8-8-A), and the turn (2h) adds a lot of hands in Minni’s range that can continue (heart draws, 8x, some pocket pairs) while also being a card we want to charge. I don’t want to check and give a free river to flush draws/overcards, but I also don’t want to bloat the pot too much when a limp/call range can contain plenty of Jx. A small-to-medium value/protection bet keeps worse hands in, prices out pure flush draws, and lets me react sensibly if I face a big raise (which will be heavily weighted to trips/full houses).

Fold. The pot is 2,350 and FiveTwo’s turn bet is 650, so I’m getting about 650/(2350+650) ≈ 21.7% pot odds to call. With T6o I have only ~6 clean outs (3 tens + 3 sixes) → ≈13% to hit the river, well below the required equity. FiveTwo opened/continued aggressively (preflop raise, flop c‑bet and small turn bet into a three‑heart board), which is consistent with value-heavy lines (Jx/trips, hearts, paired hands). I have no heart or meaningful draw and little showdown value, so calling is negative EV and a raise would be an overaggressive, high‑variance bluff versus a deeper, aggressive stack. Preserve my ~12k stack for better spots — fold.

K♣4♠ (K4o) UTG in a 4-handed game is a marginal hand. It’s easily dominated by any Kx and has very poor flop playability (no backdoor draws, weak kicker). Calling 100 to see a flop puts me out of position against three opponents and invites multiway pots where my hand has low equity; open‑raising to 200 is thin from first to act and would risk bloating the pot with a weak holding. With a healthy ~120BB stack and many hands left, preserve chips and wait for a better spot (better hand or later position). Fold.

Minni has folded and we’re on the Button with 9sTs, a strong playable suited connector that benefits a lot from position. With only the blinds left, this is a great spot to apply pressure and pick up the pot uncontested often. I’ll use a small, standard steal size to risk little while still denying equity and forcing the blinds to play out of position; it also keeps flexibility vs the short-stacked SB (we can re-evaluate if they jam).

I am in the Small Blind with K8o facing a raise from the Button (FiveTwo), who is the chip leader and has been opening very frequently. While FiveTwo's range is wide, K8o is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position. As the shortest stack (45 BBs), I want to avoid bleeding chips in difficult post-flop scenarios. Calling creates a situation where I am out of position against an aggressive big stack, and 3-betting is too loose with this holding. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Nine suited (J9c). The player FiveTwo on the Button has raised to 250. I need to call 150 more to see the flop in a pot that will be 550. J9s is a very strong hand to defend the Big Blind with, as it has excellent post-flop playability, straight potential, and flush potential. Although I will be out of position, the pot odds are too good to fold, and 3-betting against the chip leader might bloat the pot unnecessarily. I will call to see the flop.
