The flop is Ace-Ace-Seven rainbow. On such a static, paired board, I am out of position as the Big Blind against the pre-flop aggressor (FiveTwo). My hand, Jack-Nine of clubs, has very little showdown value currently but does have a backdoor flush draw. The standard play in this situation is to check to the raiser to see their action. Betting into them on an Ace-high paired board rarely accomplishes much unless I am turning my hand into a pure bluff, which isn't necessary yet. I will check.

Paired A-A-x flop heavily favors the preflop raiser; BB has a lot of unpaired broadways/small pairs that struggle to continue versus even a small c-bet. With T9s we don’t have showdown value but we can profitably fold out his air and deny equity, while keeping the bet size small enough to comfortably fold if he check-raises representing an Ace.

On this Ace-Ace-Seven rainbow board, FiveTwo has made a small continuation bet of 175 into a pot of 550. This is a common sizing for a range bet on a dry, paired board. My hand, Jack-Nine of clubs, has no direct connection to the board but does possess a backdoor flush draw (with the Jack of clubs) and two overcards to the Seven. Given the pot odds (175 to call into 725), I only need about 19.4% equity to make a profitable call. Folding J-high here against a small bet would be over-folding, as I may still have the best hand if FiveTwo is betting with air, and I have potential to improve on several turn cards. I will call to see the turn.

On the turn card 6s, which is a total brick, I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, FiveTwo. My hand, Jack-Nine of clubs, has failed to improve and I no longer have the backdoor flush draw potential since only one club is on the board. My goal now is to reach showdown as cheaply as possible if FiveTwo is bluffing, or to fold if they continue with significant aggression. Leading out into the chip leader on this board doesn't make sense as a bluff because it's difficult to represent a hand that would have called the flop but now wants to lead the turn. I will check to see how FiveTwo responds.

We have a clear range advantage on A-A-x as the preflop raiser, and Flash’s flop check-call range contains a lot of hands like 7x, pocket pairs (88–TT), and broadway floats that are uncomfortable facing multiple streets. The 6s is a good card to continue pressuring: it doesn’t meaningfully weaken our perceived strong Ax/overpair range, while it adds a little equity for us (gutshot to an 8 for a straight). A larger turn barrel should fold out most non-Ax holdings and sets up a credible river follow-through; if we get check-raised, we can comfortably fold this air hand.

Sizing: ~2/3 pot to maximize fold equity versus 7x/pairs without overcommitting.

On the turn card 6s, the board remains very dry (Ac-Ah-7d-6s). I am currently holding Jack-high with no draws remaining, as the backdoor flush draw from the flop has evaporated. My opponent, FiveTwo, has increased their betting sizing significantly, from a small 1/3 pot bet on the flop to a 2/3 pot bet on the turn. While it is possible FiveTwo is bluffing, calling 600 with no made hand and no equity to improve significantly on the river is an unprofitable play. I have no pair and no real draw; even hitting a Jack or a Nine on the river may not be enough to win the pot if the opponent has an Ace or a pocket pair. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

4-handed we can open wider, but 9♠6♦ offsuit is near the bottom of any UTG opening range and plays very poorly when called (dominated by 9x/6x, hard to realize equity multiway). Limping also invites the Button to isolate and puts us in awkward spots against a short stack reshove. With a comfortable chip lead, I don’t need to take thin, low-EV spots—so I’ll just release it and preserve stack for better steal/value opportunities.

4-handed on the Button with A6s is a strong candidate for opening the pot. With 45 big blinds, I am deep enough to play a standard raising strategy rather than shoving. A raise to 2.5x (250) puts pressure on the blinds (Flash and Minni) while giving me the advantage of position post-flop if called. Folding is too tight given the hand strength and position.

In Hand #81, Pro (Button) has raised to 250. I am in the Small Blind with Ten of Spades and Eight of Diamonds (T8o). While the pot odds to call are 2:1, T8o is a weak hand to play out of position, especially against the Button's range which often leads to difficult post-flop scenarios. Additionally, Pro is the shortest stack at the table, making them more likely to commit chips if they hit anything. Calling also invites Minni in the Big Blind to either squeeze or see a flop cheaply with a wide range. To maintain my stack and wait for better spots, folding is the most disciplined action.

Pro (button) opened to 250. I’m in the big blind with 9♣8♥ and deep stacks (~119 BB). Calling costs me 150 more into a 400 pot — pot odds ≈27% — and 9-8 offsuit is marginal but has decent postflop playability vs a button open. I won’t 3-bet (min-raise to 400 would commit too many chips with little fold equity and bad reverse implied odds). Plan: call to see a flop, continue with pairs or strong draws, and fold to heavy aggression on dry, unconnected boards.

I completely missed this dry 5♣‑2♥‑3♠ flop with 9♣8♥ — no pair, no meaningful straight/flush draw (only remote backdoors). Pot is 550 and Pro opened a wide button range, so a small c‑bet might take it down, but if called I have very little equity and with deep stacks I don’t want to invest more. Plan: control the pot and gather info. Check here; fold to medium/large pressure (≈>1/2 pot ~275) and only entertain a call to a very small probe (≈≤1/3 pot) to realize equity. If Pro checks back I’ll see a free turn and re-evaluate.

I hold Ah6h on a dry, low board of 5c 2h 3s. I have Ace-high with a gutshot straight draw (needs a 4). This board texture misses a lot of broadway hands in the Big Blind's defending range, giving me some showdown value. While a continuation bet is standard to deny equity to overcards, checking back is a strong option here for pot control and range protection. Checking allows me to realize my equity (hitting an A, 6, or 4 on the turn) without getting blown off my hand by a check-raise, and it disguises my strength to potentially induce bluffs or call down later streets on favorable runouts. Given my stack depth (~42 BBs) and the dry board, I elect to take the free card.

Board is 5♣-2♥-3♠-Q♠ and I hold 9♣8♥ — I completely missed. Pro (BTN) opened preflop and checked the dry flop, so their range is wide and they’re likely pot-controlling or checking back marginal hands. I have only ~6 outs (~13%) to make a pair and no backdoor flush/straight, so bluffing the turn is unnecessary and risky. Best play is to check and concede unless Pro gives a very cheap probe: I’ll consider calling very small bets (≈≤1/3 pot ≈ ≤180–200) to realize equity but will fold to medium/large pressure (>≈1/2 pot ≈>275). Checking preserves my stack and keeps options open for the river.

On the turn, the board is 5c 2h 3s Qs. I hold Ah 6h, giving me Ace-high and a gutshot straight draw (requiring a 4). After checking back the flop, the turn Queen is a card that arguably favors my perceived range as the pre-flop aggressor, but it also connects with the Big Blind's broadway defenses. Minni checks to me again. My Ace-high has reasonable showdown value against missed draws or lower high-card hands. Betting turns my hand into a bluff and might fold out hands I beat while getting called by pairs. Checking back allows me to realize my equity (hitting an A, 6, or 4 on the river) for free and keeps the pot small with a marginal made hand. I intend to take the free card to the river.

Board: 5♣‑2♥‑3♠‑Q♠‑J♣. I hold 9♣8♥ — no pair, no meaningful showdown equity or completed draw. Pro opened preflop and then played totally passive (checked flop and turn), which is consistent with a wide range that includes many hands that beat me (Qx, Jx, pocket pairs) and some missed broadways that will still call a small bluff. Pot is only 550 and villain has plenty behind, so my fold equity on a river bluff is thin and I’ll get called too often. I’ll check and show down (or fold to any bet) — only consider calling an extremely tiny probe (≤ ~1/3 pot) but otherwise concede the pot.

I hold Ah6h on a board of 5c 2h 3s Qs Jc. The action has checked through to the river. Minni's line of checking three streets suggests a weak hand, likely a small pair trying to reach showdown cheaply, or a completely missed draw/high-card hand. My Ace-high has showdown value against their missed draws and lower high-card holdings. Betting is unlikely to get value from worse hands (which would fold) and turning my hand into a bluff to fold out a weak pair (like 2x or 3x) is risky given the action and stack depth. Taking the free showdown is the highest EV play to realize my equity and preserve chips.

I am in UTG position (effectively Cutoff in 4-handed play) with 48BB. K5s is a marginal hand that falls at the bottom of a standard 4-handed opening range. However, the table dynamics suggest caution: the Big Blind (FiveTwo) is the chip leader with over 260BB and has shown aggressive post-flop tendencies. The Button (Flash) is also deep-stacked. Opening with a weak suited King invites 3-bets or difficult post-flop spots where I am often dominated or out-kicked, especially against aggressive big stacks who can apply maximum pressure. To minimize variance and preserve my stack for hands with better playability or equity, I will fold.

I am on the button with Ace-Seven offsuit. In a 4-handed game, this is a standard hand to open for a raise. I have the best position for the rest of the hand and a healthy stack of 16,400 chips. By raising, I can either take down the blinds immediately or play a pot in position against the small or big blind. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate here.

Fold. In the small blind with 5♥3♥ facing a standard button open to 300, this is a marginal hand with poor playability out of position. Calling 250 to play OOP against the button (and likely the big blind too) would commit chips with low equity and frequent domination; a light 3-bet is possible but 5-3 suited is too weak to use as a credible isolate bluff. Stack is healthy (≈117bb) so preserve chips and wait for a better spot in position.

Button open is wide 4-handed, so ATo is comfortably within our BB defend range getting a good price (200 to win 450). I prefer calling rather than 3-betting: we’re deep and out of position, ATo can be dominated by better aces if we bloat the pot, and Flash has shown willingness to apply pressure (including 4-betting), which makes a marginal 3-bet more volatile. Calling keeps ranges wide and lets us realize equity with a hand that can make top pair/top kicker often enough.

Heads-up BB vs BTN on Q♠8♠6♥, this texture hits my defending range reasonably well but A♣T♦ has very little connectivity here: no pair, no spade/backdoor flush, and only weak runner-runner straight possibilities. Leading would turn a hand with modest showdown value into a low-equity bluff without good blockers (I don’t block Qx or the spade continues). Best is to check and let Flash c-bet his range; I can consider continuing only versus small sizing or favorable turns, and comfortably fold versus larger pressure.

As the pre-flop aggressor, I'm making a standard continuation bet on this flop. The board features a Queen, which is generally more favorable for my range than the defender's. I also hold an overcard (the Ace) and a backdoor spade flush draw with the 7 of spades. A bet of 350 (slightly over half the pot) puts pressure on the Big Blind's middle-card connectors and small pairs, allowing me to potentially take down the pot right now or set up a barrel on favorable turn cards.

Flash’s BTN range is wide 4-handed and his flop c-bet sizing is very small (350 into 1000), giving me strong immediate odds: 350 to win 1350 (~20%). Even though A♣T♦ has no spade/backdoor flush and poor connectivity, it still has two overcards to 8/6 and enough equity versus his high-frequency c-bet/bluff range to continue once. I don’t like check-raising: we lack key spade/Q blockers and would be turning a marginal hand into a low-quality bluff OOP. Plan: call and proceed cautiously—fold to significant turn pressure on bad cards (especially spades/connected cards), and consider continuing on A/T turns or versus further small sizing.

Turn J♣ gives us a gutshot to Broadway (K) but we still have no pair, no spade, and our A-high has very thin showdown value on Q♠8♠6♥J♣. Donk-betting here would be a low-quality bluff (we don’t block Qx or spade continues) and risks getting raised off our equity. Best is to check and let Flash continue with his wide button range; we can then respond based on sizing (continue only versus small barrels with our gutshot/overcard, otherwise fold).

On the turn, the Jack of Clubs is a card that interacts well with the Big Blind's calling range (cards like JT, QJ, or straight draws). Having been called on the flop, my Ace-high has limited fold equity against a player like FiveTwo who has shown stickiness in previous hands. By checking back, I realize my equity, control the size of the pot, and can potentially reach showdown or catch an Ace on the river. Betting again risks a check-raise on a board that now contains several completed draws and pairs that won't fold to a second barrel.

River pairs the Q, and with A♣T♦ we have essentially zero showdown value (we only beat pure air that checked back turn). Flash’s line (small flop c-bet, then check back turn) heavily caps him to a lot of middling hands (8x/6x, small pairs, some Jx, and give-ups), while our BB defending range contains plenty of strong Qx and boats that can credibly bet big for value on this runout. A large polar river bet should fold out most of his non-queen bluff-catchers; and we even block some QTo/AQo combos with Td/Ac, slightly reducing his strongest calls. If he raises, we trivially fold.

On the river, the board pairs the Queen (Qs 8s 6h Jc Qh). FiveTwo, after calling my pre-flop raise and flop continuation bet, checks the turn when a Jack appears and then leads out for 1200 on the river, which is nearly a pot-sized bet. My check on the turn likely signaled a lack of a top-pair hand to my opponent. While many draws (straight draws like T9, 97, and the spade flush draw) missed, FiveTwo's large sizing is polarizing. My Ace-high (Ah 7s) has very little showdown value against his range, which includes pairs of 8s, 6s, or Jacks that might have been played passively, as well as any Queen. Even if FiveTwo is bluffing with missed draws, a large portion of those draws might still include hands that beat me or will not fold to a raise. Given the tournament stage and the risk-to-reward ratio, making a hero call with Ace-high here is too risky against an aggressive opponent like FiveTwo.

In a 4-handed game, being UTG is essentially the Hijack position. While Jh 3h is suited, it is a very weak Jack and lacks straight connectivity. Given the stack sizes, particularly Pro's short stack of 4,725 which might look to shove over an open, and FiveTwo's aggressive tendencies, J3s is not a hand I want to play out of position or face a 3-bet with. Folding is the most disciplined play to maintain my stack for better opportunities.

I'm on the button with J♣2♠ — a weak, non‑suited hand, but in position against only the blinds so there's decent steal equity. The small blind (FiveTwo) is deep and often defends wide/aggressively; the big blind (Pro) is shorter and will either fold, call or shove occasionally. I want a credible open that applies pressure and reduces limp‑calls, but I don't want to commit too much with a marginal hand. Raising to a standard 3x the big blind (to 300) gives solid fold equity and keeps post‑flop stacks playable. Plan: fold to a large 3‑bet/shove; if called, proceed in position — continuation bet dry flops, control the pot on coordinated/interactive boards, and fold to strong resistance unless I pick up meaningful equity.

We’re in the SB versus a 3x Button open and will be out of position for the entire hand. 84o has poor playability (dominated when we make a pair, few strong draws) and no useful blockers to make an appealing 3-bet bluff. Calling also invites the BB (shorter stack) to realize equity or squeeze. Given the price and positional disadvantage, this is a clean fold.

I am in the Big Blind with T6o facing a 3x raise (300) from Minni on the Button. The pot odds are 200 to call into a pot of 450, requiring about 30.7% equity. Against a standard wide Button opening range, T6o has roughly 34% raw equity. However, being out of position with an unsuited, gapped hand makes it difficult to realize that equity. Factoring in realizable equity, T6o falls below the required threshold to profitably call a 3x raise. My stack is healthy (47 BBs), so there is no need to defend with such a marginal hand.

UTG in a 4‑handed game with A♣T♠ is a standard open — strong hand and position requires taking the initiative. With deep stacks (~119BB) I want to build the pot and get position vs the blinds/button; 3x the BB keeps sizing in line with the table and gives fold equity. The small blind (Pro) is relatively short (~47BB) and may shove or call, but ATo is ahead of a lot of his shove range. Plan: raise now to 300; if I face a 3‑bet I'll re‑evaluate vs size and opponent (call smaller 3‑bets, 4‑bet/press vs exploitable ranges, fold to polarizing action). Postflop I'll c‑bet favorable flops and proceed cautiously on highly coordinated boards.

4-handed, Minni’s UTG open is relatively wide and we’re on the button with a suited wheel Ace. A♠3♠ is a strong candidate to 3-bet: it blocks Ax continues (reducing 4-bet frequency), has good playability in position (nut-flush potential, wheel/straight draws), and benefits from fold equity while denying the blinds a cheap defend/squeeze spot. I’ll 3-bet to a size that pressures the opener but still keeps my risk controlled versus a short-stack jam behind. Plan: fold to a 4-bet; versus a shove from Pro, generally fold unless I get unusually good price/read.

I hold Pocket Jacks (Jc Jd) in the Small Blind, a premium hand in a 4-handed game. The action involves an UTG open from Minni and a Button 3-bet from the aggressive chip leader, FiveTwo. With a stack of 4,675 chips (approx. 47 big blinds), I am the shortest stack at the table. Flat-calling the 3-bet is risky as it invites a squeeze from the UTG player and leaves me playing a large pot out of position against the chip leader with a hand that is vulnerable to overcards. A standard non-all-in 4-bet would commit about half my stack, creating an awkward stack-to-pot ratio post-flop. Given FiveTwo's history of aggressive play and wide ranges, a cold 4-bet all-in maximizes fold equity, punishes light 3-bets, and ensures I fully realize my equity if called. This move denies equity to overcards and allows me to potentially pick up the 1,400 dead chips (a ~30% stack increase) uncontested.

My hand is Queen-Six offsuit, which is a weak hand. There has been significant pre-flop action: an open from UTG, a three-bet from the Button, and an effective all-in four-bet from the Small Blind. Given the strength shown by multiple opponents and the fact that I am out of position with a marginal hand, the best course of action is to fold and preserve my stack.

I opened UTG with A♣T♠ to 300, got a 3‑bet to 950 from the button and then a very large 4‑bet to 4,675. The pot is 6,025 and it costs me 4,375 to call (≈38% of my stack). Calling would require ~42% equity to break even (4375 / (6025+4375) ≈ 0.42). Against this polarizing 4‑bet (likely QQ+/AK and a capped 3‑bet range behind), ATo has well under that equity — and this is a multi‑way spot where ATo is often dominated. A shove or a 5‑bet raise is unlikely to fold out enough better hands and is unnecessarily risky; calling is too loose given the price and ranges. Fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

We 3-bet A♠3♠ as a blocker/playability bluff in position, planning to release versus strong 4-bet action. Pro’s 4-bet to 4675 is large relative to our 950 and creates a low SPR if we call, which reduces our ability to realize equity with a suited wheel ace. Against a typical value-heavy 4-bet range (QQ+/AK, sometimes JJ/AQ), A3s doesn’t have the ~38% equity our pot odds require, and 5-bet jamming would be high-variance with poor call equity. Best stack-preservation and EV is to fold and keep our 26.7k intact for better spots.

4-handed we can open wider, but J7o is still a dominated, low-playability hand from first position. With stacks deep versus blinds and plenty of incentive for SB/BB to 3-bet or defend, opening creates a lot of awkward postflop and preflop (vs 3-bets) spots for little EV. I’ll pass and preserve my edge for better opens.

I am on the Button with Ac 6s in a 4-handed game. UTG has folded. A6o is a reasonable hand to open-raise from the Button to steal the blinds or play in position, as it falls within a standard stealing range for this position. My stack is roughly 60 BB, allowing for normal maneuverability. I will raise to 2.5x BB (250 chips) to put pressure on the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind holding 9-8 offsuit. The Button (Pro) has opened with a min-raise to 250. While 9-8 offsuit has some connectivity, it is a weak hand to play out of position, especially against a Button opening range that can be quite wide but still dominates my hand. Calling here would put me in a difficult spot post-flop, acting first on every street with a marginal hand. Additionally, the Big Blind (Minni) still has the opportunity to squeeze or defend, which further reduces the attractiveness of a call. Given the tournament stage and my healthy stack, there is no need to commit chips in this disadvantageous position.

Facing a button open to 250; pot is 400 and it costs me 150 to call (so I'd be committing 250 total preflop). I have K♦2♦ — a suited King that’s marginal but playable in the big blind heads-up. The call gives ~27% break-even equity (150/(400+150)) and K2s has reasonable equity and playability against a typical BTN opening range (backdoor flush + occasional K pair). A tiny 3-bet to 400 is just a thin bluff out of position and worsens postflop decisions, while folding surrenders good pot odds. Plan: call to see a flop, play aggressively if I hit a K or a strong diamond draw, otherwise control the pot and fold to heavy turn aggression. Therefore I will call.

I have K♦2♦ on Q♣5♦Q♠. I missed — no pair and only a very weak backdoor (runner‑runner) diamond possibility (~4%). The preflop raiser’s range contains lots of Qx, pocket pairs and better hands that beat me; betting out of position with a bluff here is thin and will put me in tough spots if called or check‑raised. I’m not priced to bluff and have very little equity, so I’ll check and keep the pot under control. Plan: check and fold to meaningful turn/river aggression; only continue if the button checks behind or the turn brings a direct improvement (K or a diamond that gives a real draw).

I hold Ac6s on a paired board of Qc 5d Qs. I raised pre-flop from the Button and the Big Blind called. The opponent checks to me. On this dry, paired board, Ace-high has decent showdown value against a wide blind defense range. Betting unlikely gets value from worse hands and folds out hands I likely already beat (trash). If I bet and get raised, I have to fold decent equity. Checking back allows me to pot control, realize my equity, and see a turn card for free, keeping my range disguised. I plan to re-evaluate on the turn.

Board is Qc 5d Qs Jc; I hold Kd2d and missed. The turn killed my backdoor (with one card left I cannot make a 5‑card diamond flush), so I have essentially no real equity — just K‑high. The preflop raiser’s range contains lots of Qx, Jx and pocket pairs that already beat me or improved on the J. Leading or barreling as a bluff here has very little fold equity and will put me in tough spots if called. With a 550 pot and no outs, priority is pot control and preserving my stack. I’ll check and fold to any meaningful turn aggression; if the button checks back I’ll reevaluate the river (possibly a small bluff or a showdown/river‑call only on very favorable cards).

I hold Ace-high (A6) on a Q-Q-5-J board. I raised pre-flop and checked back the flop. The opponent has checked to me again on the turn. My hand has some showdown value, but not enough to value bet, as worse hands (like King-high) likely fold and better hands (any pair) call. Betting turns my hand into a bluff with little equity if called. Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, potentially winning at showdown or improving on the river, while keeping the pot manageable.

Board: Qc 5d Qs Jc As. I hold K♦2♦ — no pair, no meaningful draw and no river improvement. Pro (preflop raiser) checked the flop and turn, so his checked-down line suggests a range that includes Qx, Jx, Ax and pocket pairs — many hands that beat me or will call a bluff. Betting here would be a thin bluff with very little fold equity and no value to extract. Best play is pot control: check and concede to any river aggression (fold if he leads), since calling a bet with K‑high is a losing spot. Action: check to the end.

I hold Ac6s on a final board of Qc-5d-Qs-Jc-As. My best five-card hand is Two Pair, Aces and Queens, with a Jack kicker (A-A-Q-Q-J). My 6 kicker does not play. Any opponent holding an Ace (A2 through AT) will have the exact same hand, resulting in a chop. I only lose to AK (which likely would have 3-bet pre-flop), AJ (Full House), or Qx/55/JJ (stronger hands). Given Minni has checked all three streets, she likely holds a weak hand, missed draw, or weak Ace. Betting is unlikely to get value from worse hands (like Jx or pocket pairs) as the Ace is a scare card, and betting mostly just leads to a chop or being called by better (traps). Checking back secures the showdown to win or chop the pot without risking chips.

I am in UTG position with 9h 2d, which is an extremely weak off-suit hand. With 63.75 BBs, I have plenty of stack depth to wait for better opportunities and no reason to get involved with trash cards from early position. Folding is the only standard play here.

I am on the button with Ace-Nine suited (Ad9d), which is a very strong hand in a 4-handed game. Standard play is to open for a raise to take control of the pot and leverage my positional advantage. A 3x raise is standard for this tournament structure.

Kh-Jc is a playable hand but marginal to 3-bet out of position. Flash opened to 300 from the button and we have deep stacks (~113bb). A small reraise to the minimum (500) gives little fold equity vs an aggressive button and can get us 4-bet or played poorly OOP. Calling 250 more (300 total) costs only 2.5bb and gives reasonable pot odds (need ~36% equity) to see a flop, preserves stack flexibility, and lets us play postflop (c-bet/fold or value-bet) depending on texture. I’ll call and evaluate the flop — play straightforwardly: value top-pairs, proceed cautiously on tough boards, fold to big 4-bets/pressure without strong improvement.

We’re getting an excellent price in the big blind: 200 more to win a 700 pot (~29% needed). Even multiway, A6 has enough equity to continue, and calling keeps the pot manageable OOP with a hand that can be dominated if we bloat it via a squeeze. Minni’s flat also caps her a bit, so we can realize equity and navigate postflop (especially on A-high/6-high or favorable low boards) without risking a large 3-bet pot with a marginal offsuit ace.

I missed this flop with K♥J♣ on 5♣-4♥-6♦. The board is low and connected (4-5-6) so only two-card combos have flopped straights; single overcards (like mine) have very little fold equity multiway. I’m out of position to both the button (opener) and the BB, stacks are deep (~110bb) and the pot is only 900 — a c-bet OOP with just two overcards is low EV. I’ll check to control the pot and see how the other two act. If checked to the turn I can continue to realize equity cheaply; if I face a small bet I’ll consider calling to see a turn, but I’ll fold to large, committed aggression since I have no pair and limited redraws.

Flop is very dynamic and we’re multiway OOP. With A6 we have top pair plus an open-ended straight draw (3 or 7), so we’re far ahead of lots of overcards and can get value from worse pairs (5x, worse 6x) and many draws (87/73/32, etc.). If we check, the button may check back too often and we give a free card on a board where turn cards change everything. Leading around 2/3 pot charges draws, denies equity, and starts building a pot where we can comfortably continue versus raises given our strong equity.

I raised pre-flop with Ace-Nine suited from the button and got two callers. The flop (5c-4h-6d) is very low and connected, which heavily favors the ranges of the blinds (Minni and FiveTwo). FiveTwo has led out for 600 into a 900 pot (effectively a 2/3 pot bet) into two opponents. This is a strong lead that suggests either a made hand (a set, two pair, or a straight) or a very strong draw (like 78 or 37). My Ace-high has very little equity on this board, and I have no direct draws. Floating or raising here against a lead on such a coordinated board is unnecessary and risky given my comfortable stack. The most disciplined play is to fold.

I checked the flop OOP with K♥J♣ on a low, connected 5♣-4♥-6♦ board. FiveTwo’s 600 into the ~900 pot is a modest bet (pot ~1500 after the bet), so calling 600 requires ~40% equity — expensive but reasonable given the small sizing and that I have live cards to realize equity. My hand has poor immediate showdown value and little fold equity OOP, so a raise would be a high-variance bluff that’s likely to get me 4-bet or committed out of position. I’ll call to see a turn and re-evaluate: fold to heavy turn aggression if I don’t improve, and if I pair a K or J I’ll look to extract value.

The turn (5♣-4♥-6♦-4♣) pairs the board and I still have no pair with K♥J♣. I checked the flop and called FiveTwo’s 600; his flop raise indicates a range with a lot of real hands (5x/6x/sets/strong pairs) and he barrels often. Leading out OOP with only two overcards is low EV — I have very little fold equity and would be committing with a weak hand. I’ll check to control the pot (pot ≈ 2100, my stack 10,425). Plan: if FiveTwo fires a large bet I’ll fold without improvement; if he makes a small probe I may call for pot odds to see the river; if checked to the river I’ll re-evaluate and only invest further if I pair or get a favourable card/read.

Turn pairs the 4, improving us from one pair to two pair (6s and 4s) with an ace kicker. This is a strong value hand versus Minni’s flop-calling range: lots of 5x (now two pair but still worse than ours), worse 6x, and some sticky pocket pairs / straight floats. We should keep betting to extract value and deny free realization on a board where many rivers change relative hand strength. Sizing ~70% targets calls from those worse made hands while still keeping our range uncapped and allowing us to bet-fold if Minni springs a big check-raise representing trips/full houses (hands containing a 4, especially 45/46).

Pot is 3600 and FiveTwo fired a large turn bet of 1500 after c-betting the flop. Calling 1500 to win 5100 (pot + your call) requires ~29.4% equity. With K♥J♣ I have only ~6 clean outs (3 kings + 3 jacks) to pair by the river (~13% chance), and very little additional showdown value vs a range that lines up for value (5x, 6x, pocket pairs, sets, maybe two-pair). FiveTwo’s line (bet flop, big bet on the paired turn) looks value-heavy; raising OOP as a pure bluff would be high-variance and likely called or 4-bet. I planned to fold to heavy turn aggression without improvement, and the math + opponent line confirm this. Fold and preserve stack for a better spot.

K2o is a very weak hand to open from UTG (first to act) even in a 4-handed game. Given the presence of an aggressive big stack (FiveTwo) and a short stack (Pro) behind me, opening with such a marginal hand is unnecessarily risky. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

QcTc suited on the button — a strong, playable hand (suited broadway, good post‑flop playability and blockers). Only the blinds are in (pot 150, to call 100), so I want to take the initiative and apply pressure. I’ll open-raise to a standard 3x BB (300) to steal the blinds and define the pot size. This sizing is consistent with my opening range, gives fold equity versus the small blind, and keeps stacks manageable against the big blind. Plan: c-bet favorable flops and use position to realise equity; fold (or seriously re-evaluate) to a large 3‑bet/shove depending on sizing and opponent tendencies.

Minni is opening very wide on the button, but 6♦4♠ offsuit is still near the bottom of a reasonable SB defend range versus a 3x open. Calling puts us out of position (often multiway if Pro comes along) with poor playability and frequent domination issues. A light 3-bet also isn’t attractive with this specific hand because if called we’re stuck in a bloated pot OOP with little equity realization, and if Pro back-jams we have an easy fold but burn chips unnecessarily. With a big stack lead, I’d rather pass on this marginal spot and apply pressure later with hands that realize equity better.

I am in the Big Blind with 6h 2c, facing a raise to 300 from the Button (Minni). The Small Blind has folded. I need to call 200 chips into a pot of 450. The pot odds are roughly 2.25:1 (requiring ~30.7% equity to call). My hand, 6-2 offsuit, is one of the weakest starting hands in Hold'em with very poor post-flop playability and low equity against even a wide Button opening range. Despite the reasonable price, playing this hand out of position is expected to be negative EV. Therefore, the correct decision is to fold.

Fold. 7h-2d offsuit is essentially the worst starting hand — no playability and near-zero equity vs any reasonable opening range. I’m first to act (UTG) at a 4-handed table with ~105 BB effective; while ranges widen in short-handed play, this hand still shouldn’t be played out of position. Calling 100 into a 150 pot or raising to 200 will put me in difficult postflop spots against button/SB/BB ranges and is an unnecessary chip investment. Preserve the stack and wait for a real hand or a better spot to apply pressure.

We’re 4-handed on the Button after UTG folds, so we should be opening a wide range and applying pressure to the blinds. AKo is near the top of our range and plays very well in position. Raising builds the pot for value, denies equity to the blinds’ weaker hands, and sets up profitable decisions versus a 3-bet (including comfortably calling off versus the SB’s shorter stack). I’ll use a standard, non-committal 2.5x sizing to keep my range consistent and encourage dominated calls.

I am in the Small Blind with 9h 5d, a very weak offsuit hand. The Button has raised to 250. Although the price to call is relatively small, playing 95o out of position against a Button raiser is a losing play due to poor equity and playability. There is no strategic merit to bluffing here. Folding is the correct standard action.

I am in the big blind holding 3s 2s. The button (FiveTwo) has raised to 250. While the cost to call is only 150 more and I am getting decent pot odds (nearly 3:1), 32s is one of the weakest hands in poker. Playing out of position against the chip leader with such a weak hand is likely to lead to losing more chips on later streets. Given that I have a healthy stack of 15,200 and we are just past the midpoint of the tournament (hand 88 of 150), I should prioritize protecting my stack and wait for a better opportunity.

4-handed, ranges are wide and we have a big stack advantage over the field, so we should be opening aggressively to pick up blinds and put pressure on the shorter stacks. 7c5c is a reasonable open in this spot: it can win immediately, plays well postflop with flush/straight potential, and benefits from having initiative. I’ll use a standard 2.5bb sizing to reduce multiway limps/calls while not overinflating the pot with a marginal holding.

I hold Q5o on the Button facing a 2.5x raise from the UTG player (FiveTwo). Q5o is a very weak hand that is dominated by almost the entire opening range of an UTG player (which typically consists of strong aces, pairs, and broadway hands). Despite having position, the equity of Q5o against a tight UTG range is poor (around 30%), and the pot odds do not justify a call. 3-betting is not a viable bluff option given the hand strength and the stack depths (UTG covers me significantly). The prudent play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind holding Ten of Diamonds, Deuce of Diamonds. FiveTwo, the chip leader, has raised to 250 from the cutoff/UTG position. Ten-Deuce suited is a very weak hand, and calling would put me out of position for the remainder of the hand against an aggressive opponent with a large stack. Given my solid stack size and the stage of the tournament, there is no need to defend with such a marginal hand. Folding is the most disciplined play here.

FiveTwo opened UTG to 250 (pot 400) and it costs me 150 to call. Qh9c is marginal but has decent heads‑up equity and playability, and calling is cheap given the pot odds. I don’t want to take on a bloated multi-street contest out of position with a marginal hand, and a min-raise to 400 is too small to be a real 3‑bet — a bigger 3‑bet would be a bluff with limited upside. Plan: call to see the flop, continue only with strong top pair or good draws, and control the pot / fold to heavy aggression on dry boards.

Flop: 4d 4c Qs. I hold Qh9c — top pair with a medium kicker on a very dry, paired board. FiveTwo opened UTG preflop and has the initiative, but he checked this flop, which can mean weakness or a slowplay. My preflop plan was to control the pot and continue only with strong top pair or good draws; this hand qualifies as a playable, but vulnerable, top pair (dominated by any QQ/sets and beat by better Qx). Betting here would extract thin value from worse Qx and small pairs but also builds a pot OOP that I don’t want to get committed to if I face heavy aggression. Checking keeps the pot manageable, lets me induce bluffs, and allows me to call a smallish bet while folding to a large raise (which likely represents a set or pocket queens). Plan after checking: call reasonable c-bets and re-evaluate on the turn; if checked to me again I can consider a small delayed value bet or a pot‑control check to showdown.

Heads-up as the preflop raiser on a paired low board (4-4-Q), my range retains a clear advantage (lots of Qx/overpairs) and villain’s BB defend contains many unpaired overcards/small pairs that will often fold versus a small c-bet. With 7c5c I have little showdown value but can profitably take a low-risk stab to win the pot now and deny equity. I’ll use a small sizing to keep my bluffs efficient and avoid bloating the pot when I get called; if I face a check-raise I can comfortably fold.

Top pair (Qh9c) on a very dry, paired flop (4d 4c Qs). FiveTwo opened UTG so his range is relatively strong, but his flop bet is tiny (200 into a 750 pot) — likely a probe/c-bet rather than a polarized value shove. Calling is the correct, low-risk line: pot odds (~4.75:1, ~17% equity required) make a 200 call cheap and my hand has good heads‑up showdown value. Raising OOP would bloat the pot and get me in trouble versus better Qx or any 4 in hand (trips/quads); folding gives up too much. Plan: call 200 to control the pot and see the turn; if FiveTwo now fires a large, polarized raise I will strongly consider folding, but if checked to me or faced with a small barrel I can extract or take a controlled line to showdown.

I have Qh9c on 4d 4c Qs Ah — top pair with a medium kicker on a very dry, paired board that just picked up an Ace. Pot is 950 and Villain (FiveTwo, UTG, 27,550) showed up with a tiny flop c-bet (200) which looked more like a probe than a polarized value shove. The turn Ace significantly increases the portion of his range that now beats me (Ax, and of course sets/4x), and betting OOP here would bloats the pot against a range that contains many hands that dominate me. I want to keep the pot small with a marginal top pair and see a cheap river/showdown. Checking controls the pot, keeps weaker Qx and bluffs in his calling/folding range, and avoids getting exploited by a check‑raise or a big lead card bet. Plan: check behind on the turn. If Villain barrels the river with a small/medium bet I will call for value; I’ll fold to a large polarized shove unless I pick up a specific read that he’s bluffing.

Turn is a very good barrel card for the preflop raiser: the Ace heavily favors my range after I c-bet flop (I have lots of Ax that now improves to top pair, plus strong Qx), while BB’s flop-calling range contains many medium pairs (55–JJ) and some floats that are now in a tough spot facing continued aggression. With 7c5c I have essentially no showdown value and no real draw, so my best way to realize EV is to keep applying pressure with a credible second barrel. I’ll use a medium sizing to generate folds without bloating the pot too much versus Qx/4x/Ax. If I face a check-raise, I’ll fold.

Villain (FiveTwo, UTG) has been opening strongly preflop and made a tiny flop c‑bet, then followed up with a medium turn bet (500). The turn Ace added a lot of hands that now beat my Q9 (Ax, better Qx, and any 4x/quads), but his line—small flop c‑bet then a medium turn bet—also fits missed broadway hands (AK/KT type) turning a pair, or a probe-style continuation with thin value. Folding now is too tight given the pot odds; raising OOP with a vulnerable kicker would bloat the pot and get me committed against many hands that dominate me. Math: I must call 500 into a 1450 pot, so I need ~500/(1450+500) ≈ 25.6% equity. Q9 has reasonable showdown equity vs a continuing range and I can fold to extreme river aggression if necessary. Plan: call the 500 to keep the pot controlled, see the river, and then fold to a large polarized shove but call small/medium river bets for value vs bluffs and weaker Ax/Qx.

I have Qh9c on 4d 4c Qs Ah 9d — I made two pair (Queens and Nines). Villain (FiveTwo) opened UTG, fired a tiny flop probe and a medium turn bet that I called, then checked the river. His line looks like a lot of medium/weak Qx, Ax or small pocket pairs — hands I beat — while the few hands that crush me (4x trips/quads, QQ, A9, 99, A4/full houses) are relatively scarce. I want to extract thin value while keeping the pot controlled OOP, so I’ll make a small value bet (~1/3 pot) to get called by worse Qx and thin hands; if he check-raises big I’ll concede (fold) as that line will usually denote the nuts. Betting a modest 650 balances value extraction and pot control here.

We arrived at the river with pure air after double-barreling. Minni’s line (check/call flop, check/call turn on the Ace, then donk 650 into 2600) is heavily weighted toward made hands that want a cheap showdown/value (Ax, Qx, some 9x, and slowplayed 4x). With 7-high we have essentially zero showdown value, and a bluff-raise is unattractive because we don’t block any of the strongest continues (A4/Q4/94/4x) and a competent opponent can hero with Ax/Qx given our aggressive line. Calling also requires them to be bluffing at an implausibly high frequency. Best play is to give up and preserve stack.

I am in UTG (effectively Cutoff in 4-handed play) with J7o. This is a very weak hand that falls well outside of a standard opening range, even in a short-handed game. My stack is the shortest at ~62 BB, so I should avoid bleeding chips with low-equity hands out of position against deeper opponents who can apply pressure. This is a standard fold.

I am on the button with Q7o, which is a marginal hand. While the table is 4-handed and opening from the button is generally profitable, my opponents in the blinds, Minni and FiveTwo, have shown significant aggression and willingness to defend. FiveTwo currently holds the chip lead and has been active in many pots. Given my solid stack of 15,150 and the tournament progress (hand 90 of 150), I prefer to fold this weak offsuit hand and wait for a more favorable situation rather than risk a confrontation out of position or against a wide range from the aggressive big blind.

Jc 2d is a very weak, unsuited hand and I’ll be out of position postflop versus a deep-stacked Big Blind. Although calling 50 is cheap, I’m likely to be dominated and outplayed; raising to 200 with junk is a losing play. With a healthy stack (≈115BB) I’ll conserve chips and avoid marginal spots — fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am UTG in a 4-handed game holding 8s 7h. While opening ranges are wider in short-handed play, 87 offsuit remains a weak hand to open from early position, especially with an aggressive big stack (FiveTwo) and an active button (Minni) yet to act. Given my healthy stack size and the marginal nature of the hand, folding is the most prudent play to wait for better spots.

Button with T6o is marginal but position is powerful — I should be opening to steal the blinds rather than folding. Small blind (FiveTwo) is very deep and will sometimes defend, and Pro (BB) is relatively short and could shove, so I’ll use a standard 2x BB open to 200 to take the initiative while keeping the size modest. Plan: if either opponent responds with a large 3-bet/shove I will fold this weak hand; if called I’ll play post‑flop in position and look to c-bet/fold or float depending on the texture and opponent tendencies.

We’re in the small blind facing a button min-open. Q4o is dominated by a huge chunk of Minni’s continuing range (Qx, broadways, pairs), and flat-calling creates a tough OOP spot while also inviting the big blind to come along, further realizing our equity poorly. As a chip leader I don’t need to defend this wide; and as a 3-bet bluff, Q4o has weak playability when called and doesn’t provide enough high-card/suited connectivity to justify the risk versus a reasonably sticky button. Best is to cut losses and preserve EV for better steal/3-bet spots.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Five offsuit facing a min-raise (200) from the Button (Minni). The pot is 350 and I need to call 100 more, offering me direct pot odds of 4.5 to 1 (approx 22% equity required). Against a wide button opening range, A5o has significantly more equity than 22%. Folding is incorrect due to the price. 3-betting is an option as a bluff with an Ace blocker, but flat-calling is the standard, lower-variance line that guarantees seeing a flop with a hand that plays reasonably well heads-up. I will defend my big blind by calling.

I have bottom pair (Fives) with a strong Ace kicker on a dry, rainbow board (Qh 5d 2c). As the pre-flop caller in the Big Blind, standard strategy is to check to the pre-flop aggressor (Minni on the Button). The board texture connects reasonably well with a Button opening range (Queens, sets), but my hand has sufficient showdown value to catch bluffs. Leading out (donk betting) is not optimal as it folds out worse hands and isolates me against better ones. My plan is to check and call a standard continuation bet.

I opened to 200 preflop and got one caller from the BB. Flop Qh‑5d‑2c is very dry and completely misses my T6o. As the preflop aggressor and in position I have substantial fold equity against the BB’s calling range (many broadways and small pairs miss this board). I’ll make a standard continuation bet to try to take the pot now and charge any floats. Size: ~2/3 pot (300). If he folds I take it down; if he calls I will proceed very cautiously on the turn and fold to significant aggression since I have almost no showdown equity unless I pair.

I hold bottom pair with the best kicker (A5) on a dry Q-5-2 rainbow board. The opponent (Minni) opened from the Button and is making a continuation bet of 300 into a pot of 450 (about 66%). This is a standard spot for a button opener to bet with a very wide range, including top pairs, pocket pairs, and many bluffs (high cards, connectors). I need to call 300 to win a total pot of 1050, requiring roughly 28.5% equity. Against a wide button range, my hand has significantly more equity than this, as I am ahead of all their pure bluffs and dominate other 5x hands. Folding here would be too tight and exploitable. Raising would isolate me against better hands (Qx+) and fold out the bluffs I am beating. Therefore, calling is the most profitable line to keep their bluffing range in the hand.

I am in the Big Blind with a pair of Fives and an Ace kicker, plus a gutshot straight draw (needing a 4) on a board of Qh-5d-2c-3h. I check-called the flop. The turn card (3h) adds a flush draw but doesn't drastically change the board texture. Leading out (donk betting) is not optimal as it folds out bluffs and gets action primarily from better hands (Qx, better pairs). Checking is the standard line to control the pot size, induce further bluffs from the aggressive Button player, or potentially see a free river. I intend to check and evaluate any subsequent bet.

I missed this turn (Th6d on Qh-5d-2c-3h). I was the preflop aggressor and c-bet the dry flop, but the BB called — his calling range includes Qx, small pairs and some floats. The 3h on the turn is a low card that doesn’t improve my hand and doesn’t give any realistic draw I can pursue; it slightly helps hands with a 3 or a heart but mostly keeps villain’s calling range intact. With almost no showdown equity and no good semi‑bluff outs, I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position on a second barrel. I’ll check to control the pot and fold to meaningful aggression — only consider a bluff on the river if he checks back and the river is favorable.

I hold a pair of Fives with an Ace kicker on a Q-5-2-3-8 board. After check-calling the flop and seeing the opponent check back the turn, their range is likely capped or consists of marginal made hands (weak Qx, pocket pairs 66-TT) or air. Betting here is unlikely to get value from worse hands (like A-high or 2x) and will likely only be called by better hands. My hand has sufficient showdown value to beat missed draws and high-card bluffs. Checking allows me to potentially induce a bluff from air hands or see a cheap showdown. I will check.

I have T6o on a Qh‑5d‑2c‑3h‑8c board — essentially ten‑high with no realistic showdown value. I opened preflop and c‑bet the dry flop (300) and he called, then both of us checked the turn and he checked the river. That line is weighted toward Qx and small pocket pairs or cautious calls/float attempts; many of those hands will call a river bluff. Pot is 1,050, so a bluff would need substantial fold equity to be profitable — I don’t have confidence this opponent will fold enough given his passive calling line. Betting risks losing chips to calls from Qx/pairs for very little benefit. I’ll check and take showdown rather than bluffing into a calling range; if he had instead bet I would fold to meaningful aggression with this hand.

UTG in a 4-handed game with 8♣7♥ — a marginal but playable hand in short‑handed, deep‑stack poker (I have ~110BB). Table has shown standard 3x opens to 300, so I’ll open to 300 to take the initiative and steal the blinds rather than limp and give the button free odds. 300 is big enough to generate fold equity vs the blinds/button but small enough that I won’t be overly committed if I face a big 3‑bet. Plan: fold to a large 3‑bet (or shove) from an aggressive deep stack; if I get one or two callers I will proceed post‑flop with pot control and c‑bet in favorable spots (or check and realize equity when out of position). In short — apply pressure preflop with a standard open size, but be prepared to release this hand to heavy aggression.

4-handed, UTG is effectively a CO open and Minni has shown a fairly wide raising frequency and some willingness to fold vs pressure. On the Button we’re getting a good price (300 to win 450) with position and a hand that can make top pair/straight draws often enough to realize equity. I don’t love 3-betting J8o here (no blockers, can get called and dominated), but folding is a bit too tight given the pot odds and positional advantage. I’ll flat and play postflop in position; if the blinds squeeze big we can comfortably let it go.

I am in the Small Blind with Jc 4d, facing a raise to 300 from Minni (UTG) and a call from FiveTwo (Button). My hand is very weak, poorly connected, and I would be playing out of position against two opponents if I called. Even though the pot odds are roughly 3:1, the reverse implied odds and lack of playability make this a clear fold. I will preserve my stack of 6725 chips for a better spot.

I am in the Big Blind with T4s. Minni (UTG) raised to 3x and FiveTwo (Button) called. I am getting 3.75-to-1 odds to call (200 to win 750). While T4s is a weak hand, the pot odds and the fact that I am in the big blind with a suited hand make a call reasonable to see a flop. However, given the tournament context and the strength of a UTG raise, I must be careful post-flop.

I have bottom pair (fours) on a board featuring an Ace and a Queen. In a multi-way pot against an UTG raiser and a button caller, this is a very vulnerable hand. Both opponents have ranges that hit this board frequently with top pair or middle pair. I will check to see how the action unfolds; if there is significant betting, I will likely have to fold, but checking allows me to potentially see a cheap turn or catch a bluff.

I opened UTG to 300 and got two callers; pot is ~950. Flop A♦ Q♦ 4♥ absolutely misses 8♣7♥ — no pair, no realistic flush, only very remote backdoor straight possibilities. I'm out of position to both the button and the big blind in a multiway pot, and both players’ calling ranges include many Ax/Qx and pairs that will continue against a c-bet. Betting here as a bluff has poor fold equity and a high chance of being called by better hands, so I prefer pot control and information: check to see how opponents react. If checked to, I’ll take a free turn to reassess; if faced with a bet I’ll fold to sizable pressure and only consider calling very small bets to realize showdown equity.

We’re 3‑way on an A-Q-x board with J8o and no real equity (no flush draw, no meaningful straight draw, and our pair-outs are often dominated). Even though both opponents checked, multiway checks on this texture are common from the preflop raiser and the BB, and a small stab is likely to get called by any Ace/Queen and some stubborn pocket pairs while exposing us to check-raises. With poor barrel cards and limited turn playability, taking the free card in position is higher EV than forcing a low-equity bluff.

On the turn, the board is Qd-4h-Ac-9c. I have bottom pair (fours). After the flop checked around, it's possible that both opponents missed or are playing marginal hands cautiously. However, an Ace-high board in a multi-way pot is still dangerous. Since I am first to act and my hand has limited showdown value, I will check again to see if I can get to the river cheaply. Betting here likely only gets called by hands that beat me (Queens or Aces that checked the flop), and checking allows me to evaluate based on their actions.

Board: Qd 4h Ac 9c. I hold 8c7h — no pair and no realistic outs to make a meaningful hand by the river. Pot is ~950 and both opponents checked the flop and the turn, so this is a multiway pot out of position against deep stacks. Betting as a bluff here has poor fold equity versus calling ranges that contain Ax/Qx and pairs; it’s expensive to create a big pot with essentially no equity. I stick to my earlier plan to take the free turn and keep the pot under control: check and fold to significant aggression. If checked to on the river I’ll reassess and only attempt a bluff if the card and opponent actions give a credible story or strong blockers; otherwise I’ll check/fold to pressure.

We’re still 3‑way on A♣ Q♦ 4♥ 9♣ with J8o and essentially no showdown value. Our only clean equity is a gutshot to a Ten (4 outs), while pairing J/8 is often dominated and won’t comfortably continue versus pressure. Multiway, the double-check doesn’t create enough fold equity to justify turning this into a bluff on a board where both opponents can easily have Ax/Qx and will continue at least some frequency (and we risk a check-raise). Best play is to take the free river, realize our small equity, and preserve stack; if a good bluff/value spot appears on the river after further checks, we can reassess.

The board is extremely heavy with high cards (A-K-Q). While I have bottom pair (fours), this hand is very weak in a three-way pot. The fact that the action checked through on the flop and turn suggests that no one has a monster, but it is highly probable that someone has a King, a Nine, or a cautious Ace/Queen. There is no value in betting here, as I will only be called by better hands and won't fold out any significant part of my opponents' ranges. I will check to see if I can reach a cheap showdown.

Checked through to the river in a three‑way pot (board Q♦ 4♥ A♣ 9♣ K♦). I hold 8♣7♥ — no pair, no meaningful showdown value and only marginal blockers (one club). Pot is only 950 while stacks are deep (my stack ~10.7k, opponents much deeper), and both opponents have been passive (check/call lines). In a multiway spot like this my bluff has very poor fold equity against calling ranges that include A/Q/K and small pairs that often check back; betting risks chips for little expected gain. I stick to my preflop/turn plan to keep the pot under control and not force a bluff here. Check and take showdown (fold to any river bet).

River goes check/check again on a runout (A♣ Q♦ 4♥ 9♣ K♦) where it’s very hard for both players to have a strong hand and never take a value/protection bet. With J♥8♠ we have essentially no showdown value, so checking is mostly conceding the pot to any pair.

This is a good spot to apply pressure: the K♦ is an extra overcard that makes pocket pairs (55–TT) and many weak Qx/9x hands uncomfortable, while our line can credibly represent slow-played Ax, KQ improving on the river, or even strong AQ/AK that checked back in position multiway.

I’ll use a medium sizing to target folds from those marginal holdings while keeping the risk contained; if we face a raise, we simply fold.

The board is extremely heavy with high cards (Ace, King, Queen, and Nine). My pair of fours is bottom pair and represents the absolute lowest end of a potential showdown range. Although the flop and turn checked through, suggesting no one has a monster like an Ace or a set, the King on the river hits many cards that players would call with pre-flop (like KJ, KT, or K9). FiveTwo's bet of 650 into a 950 pot is a significant lead in a multi-way pot. Even if FiveTwo is bluffing, Minni still has to act behind me. Given the high probability that someone has at least a Nine, a Queen, or a King, my hand has almost no showdown value. I will fold.

I have nothing on the river with 8♣7♥ against Q♦-4♥-A♣-9♣-K♦. FiveTwo’s line (call preflop, check/call flop and turn, then a river raise) looks like slow-played value (A/Q/K or a pair) rather than a frequent bluff. The pot is 1600 and calling 650 requires ~29% equity; I have essentially no realistic outs and few blockers to credibly represent a strong hand. A bluff-raise would be speculative and unlikely to succeed. Preserve my ~10.7k stack and fold to the river raise.

4-handed UTG is effectively a wide-opening spot. K9s has good playability (suited, connected enough) and blocks strong Kx holdings. With a big chip lead, I want to apply pressure to the shorter stacks and pick up blinds/antes-equivalent frequently. I’ll open-raise rather than limp to deny equity and keep initiative, using a modest size to reduce the cost if someone jams over the top.

I am on the Button with J4o, a very weak hand. The UTG player (FiveTwo) has raised to 250. Given the strength of my hand and the position of the raiser, I have no reason to enter this pot. Folding is the standard and correct play.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Six offsuit. The chip leader (FiveTwo) has opened from under-the-gun (which is the cutoff in this 4-max game) with a raise to 250. While Ace-Six is a decent hand in a short-handed game, playing it out of position against an aggressive chip leader is often a recipe for trouble. Calling from the Small Blind is generally a weak play as it invites the Big Blind to squeeze or call, and forces me to play the rest of the hand first-to-act. Given my solid stack of 14,800, there is no need to defend with a marginal hand against the table's most active player. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

FiveTwo opened UTG to 250 and both CO/BTN folded — I’m in the BB with J♦8♠. Calling 150 more (250 total) gives me ~27% breakeven equity (150/(400+150)), and against the table dynamics FiveTwo has been opening fairly wide, so defending the blind is reasonable. I won’t 3‑bet: J8o is a weak hand to iso‑3bet out of position and a small re‑raise to 400 has limited fold equity while committing chips unnecessarily. Plan: call to see a flop; continue aggressively if I pair a jack/eight or hit strong draws, but fold to heavy action on dry boards where I miss to protect my stack.

I missed this flop with J♦8♠ — I have one overcard (J) and only a backdoor diamond that needs both turn and river (~4%); no pair, no meaningful draw. The board is dry and I’m out of position vs FiveTwo (the preflop raiser) who opens fairly wide but will also have many hands that beat J-high. With plenty of chips and a medium pot, I prefer pot control: check and concede the initiative rather than bluff into a likely calling/check-raising range. Plan: check and fold to any sizable bet; I can call a very small probe if the price is right, but otherwise take a free card and re-evaluate on the turn if I pair up or pick up a real draw.

Heads-up as the preflop raiser with position, I can profitably apply a small range c-bet here. Even though 2d-5d-7h hits the BB’s defend range somewhat, Minni still has plenty of total air/overcards that will fold to a modest bet, and I don’t need much fold equity given the pot size. With Kc9c I have two overcards plus a backdoor club flush, so a small bet also sets up some good turn barrels on A/K/Q or club turns while keeping the cost low if I face resistance (easy fold vs a check-raise).

I have J♦8♠ on a dry 2♦-5♦-7♥ flop and I missed – only an overcard (J) and a one-card diamond backdoor (needs runner-runner). FiveTwo (UTG opener) c-bet small (200 into the 550 pre‑bet pot, pot now 750), so the price to call is 200 for a final pot of 950 — about 21% breakeven. Against his fairly wide, small‑betting range my hand has borderline but acceptable equity (jack overcard + the tiny chance to improve), and I’m out of position so I don’t want to inflate the pot with a bluff‑raise that lacks fold equity. I’ll call the 200 to see a turn and only continue if I pair/improve or pick up a real draw; otherwise I’ll fold to significant turn aggression to protect my stack.

I hold J♦8♠ on 2♦-5♦-7♥-3♥ — I missed. The turn kills the backdoor flush (I needed both turn+river diamonds) and I have no pair or meaningful draw, just an overcard J. Pot is 950 and I'm out of position vs the UTG opener (FiveTwo) who opened wide but can still have many hands that beat J-high. I called the small flop c-bet to see a card, but now with no improvement I should avoid inflating the pot with a marginal hand. Checking preserves my stack and gives me the cheapest way to fold to aggression; I'll fold to any significant turn bet and only consider calling a very small probe (roughly <= ~20% pot) for pot odds to see the river. If FiveTwo checks back I’ll re-evaluate on the river and only continue with a real pair or a strong read.

Turn is a poor barrel card for my range: 3h adds a second heart (bringing in more draws) and also completes 64, which is very plausible in BB’s flop-calling range. With Kc9c I’ve lost the backdoor club flush and now have only two overcards with marginal fold equity vs pairs/7x/5x/draws that continue. Betting risks getting check-raised off my equity and bloating a pot where I’ll often face a tough river decision. I’ll take the free card, realize my K/9 outs, and keep my range protected by also checking back some stronger hands in this node.

River pairs the board (7s), I have J♢8♠ with no pair and no realistic showdown value. FiveTwo was the preflop raiser and showed some aggression earlier but checked the turn — his range includes many hands that beat me now (any 7, any pocket pair that makes a full house with the paired board, or two pair), and a bluff bet here would have very poor fold equity. I’m out of position and the pot is only mid‑sized (950). Betting risks getting called by worse hands or crushed by stronger ones; checking preserves my stack and is the standard pot‑control line. I’ll check and fold to any meaningful bet on the river.

River checks to me on 2d-5d-7h-3h-7c after I c-bet small and then checked back turn. With Kc9c I’m basically at K-high and only beat pure misses (some diamond draws/overcards that didn’t pair). Minni’s flop check-call range contains a lot of hands that will not fold to a river stab now that the board paired (any 5x/3x/2x, pocket pairs, and especially 7x that is now trips, plus occasional 64 straight). Given my turn check-back, my river bluff is also less credible and risks getting looked up or check-raised off a dead hand. Best play is to take the free showdown and realize whatever equity I have versus missed draws.

The current hand is #94 of 150 with 4 active players. I am in UTG (effectively the Cutoff) with a stack of 67.25 BBs. My hand is 3d 4d, a very low suited connector. While suited connectors have post-flop potential, 34s is at the very bottom of the range and often dominated. Opening from the first position subjects me to 3-bets from the larger stacks behind me or playing out of position if called by the button. Given the tournament structure and my position as the shortest stack (though still deep), preserving chips for higher expected value spots is prudent. A fold is the standard disciplined play here.

I am on the button with 2s 6h, which is an extremely weak hand. Although I have position, opening with such a marginal hand is generally unprofitable, especially in a four-handed game where the blinds are likely to defend or 3-bet. With a healthy stack and many hands remaining, there's no need to take unnecessary risks with junk cards. I will fold.

I’m in the small blind with Q♦5♣ — a marginal, offsuit queen and out of position vs the big blind. The pot is 150 and I only need to put in 50 more (100 total committed) to see the flop, giving me 4:1 pot odds (need ~20% equity). Raising to 200 would bloat an OOP pot and will be called often by FiveTwo’s wide defending range, so I’d rather see a cheap flop and preserve my stack. I’ll call the 50 and proceed cautiously: fold to heavy aggression if I miss, continue/raise only with a made queen, two pair, better, or strong draw.

Heads-up vs the small blind limp with a big stack edge. Minni’s completing range is very wide and contains many hands that will simply fold to a raise. Even though 7-5 offsuit is weak, it has decent equity realization when we take the initiative and can apply pressure on many flops, especially against a shorter effective stack (10.2k). I’ll punish the limp with a standard isolation size to win preflop often and set up profitable continuation bets.

I’m OOP in the small blind with Q♦5♣ — a marginal but playable hand heads-up. FiveTwo’s raise to 400 after I completed to 100 is aggressive, but calling 300 is only ~3 BB of my ~102 BB stack, so the cost is small relative to stack/deep-stacked dynamics. Folding here is too nitpicky and 3-betting OOP with Q5o is spewy; raising to 700+ would bloat the pot out of position with a weak holding. I’ll call to see a flop and proceed cautiously: fold to strong postflop aggression unless I make top pair (Qx), two pair or better, or pick up a strong draw — continue/raise only with a clear equity or made hand.

I have Q♦5♣ on a 4♥ Q♠ 8♥ flop — top pair but a weak kicker and two hearts on board, so there are meaningful flush draws. Heads-up vs FiveTwo (the preflop raiser) and out of position, I want to both extract value from worse queens/pocket pairs and deny equity to heart draws. Betting takes the initiative and prices draws; a bet ~1/2–2/3 pot is standard here. I’ll bet 500 into the 800 pot to get value and protect. If he folds I win the pot; if called I’ll reevaluate on the turn (continue for value on safe cards, proceed cautiously or check back if a heart/overcard appears); if he raises large, I’ll assume a very strong hand or a committed draw and fold to avoid getting blown off a marginal top pair.

Minni’s small donk-lead (500 into 1300) after limp/calling pre is usually a wide, capped range (many 8x/4x, weak Qx, heart draws, and some stabs) trying to set the price and avoid facing our c-bet. With 7h5s we have a gutshot to the 6 plus a backdoor heart draw, and we have position for the rest of the hand. Raising would bloat the pot versus a shorter stack and gets called too often by any Qx/draw, leaving us in a bad SPR spot. Folding is a bit too tight given the price and the likelihood she checks a lot of turns. Best is to float and apply pressure on turns when she slows down (especially on A/K/J/T/9, hearts, or a 6).

I have top pair (Q) with a weak 5 kicker and I'm out of position vs FiveTwo. Flop I bet to protect and was called — that call represents a range of Qx (some better kickers), medium pocket pairs (88,44), or missed heart draws. The turn (6♣) is a relatively harmless card that didn't complete the hearts, so a lot of the caller’s draw equity has vanished. I want to extract value from worse queens and some pocket pairs, keep the initiative, and charge any lingering backdoor draws. Betting ~50% pot is the right balance here: it gets called by worse Qx and pays off bluffs while still leaving me room to fold to strong aggression. If FiveTwo check-raises or jams, I’ll respect that and fold this marginal top pair. Bet 900 (≈½ the 1800 pot).

Turn completes our hand: with 5s7h on Qs-8h-4h-6c we already have a made straight (4-5-6-7-8). Minni’s second small lead (900 into 2700) is still consistent with a lot of one-pair hands (Qx/8x/6x), two-pair/sets sometimes, and many heart-draws that we want to charge. Calling keeps bluffs in, but it gives a great price to flush draws and introduces bad rivers (any heart) that either beat us or kill our action. Best is to raise for value and protection, using a size that keeps her continuing range wide while setting up an easy river shove with her remaining stack.

I’m facing a large turn check-raise from FiveTwo (they raised to 3200 after my 900 bet). Pot is 5900 and it costs me 2300 to call (~2.56:1 pot odds → need ~28% equity). I have top pair (Q) with a very weak 5 kicker, out of position. FiveTwo’s line — preflop raise, calling the flop bet, then a big turn check‑raise — is heavily weighted toward strong value hands (sets: 88/44, two pair like Q4, big Qx or slowplayed overpairs). There are some possible bluffs (turned heart semi‑bluffs), but the line is more value‑heavy than bluff‑heavy. Against that polarized/value range my actual equity is well below the required 28%, and my weak kicker means I’m getting value‑raised or crushed by almost every hand that continues this way.

Calling or raising here commits a large portion of my stack with a marginal hand; folding preserves chips and position for better spots. Per my earlier plan to respect heavy aggression, I’ll fold this marginal top pair to the large check‑raise.

In a 4-handed game, Ace-Deuce suited is a strong enough hand to open from any position. As the first to act, I will put in a standard raise to 300 to take control of the pot and put pressure on the blinds, especially the short stack (Pro). While the hand has a weak kicker, its nut-flush potential and high-card value make it a standard open in short-handed play.

On the button with A♠K♣ facing an UTG open to 300 (pot 450) — this is a standard 3-bet spot. AKo is a premium hand that plays best heads-up and in position, so I want to isolate the opener, charge speculative hands, and take the initiative. A sizable 3-bet to 1500 (total) will discourage multiway action from the deep SB (FiveTwo) and the BB, build a pot when I’m likely ahead of many of Flash’s opening hands, and still leave me ~7k behind to continue postflop or call a short-stack shove. Calling 300 is too passive and a min-re-raise to 500 is too small here. I will raise to 1500 and re-evaluate if I face a large 4-bet/shove (fold to heavily polarized mega 4-bets from the deep stack; call reasonable shoves from the shorter-stacked BB).

We’re in the small blind facing an UTG open (300) and a large 3-bet from the button to 1500. With 8♦4♥ offsuit, we have very poor playability, are dominated by essentially all continuing ranges, and would be forced to defend out of position versus a strong, condensed range. A squeeze bluff is unattractive because Minni’s sizing and ~70bb stack makes her likely to continue (often by jamming) and Pro still has position behind with a stack that can wake up. Best decision is to preserve chips and fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 7s Ts facing a raise to 300 from UTG and a large 3-bet to 1500 from the Button. The pot is currently 1950. To call, I would need to invest 1400 chips, which is over 20% of my remaining stack (6625). My hand, a suited one-gapper, is far too weak to call a large 3-bet cold, especially out of position against ranges that likely include strong pairs and high cards that dominate me. The implied odds are insufficient given the stack depths and the strength of the action. Folding is the only viable option.

I opened UTG with Ace-Deuce suited, which is a standard play in a 4-handed game. However, Minni's 3-bet to 1,500 from the button is a significant raise (5x my open). At 6,975 chips, Minni is relatively short-stacked, and this sizing often indicates a strong range or a polarized bluff that I don't want to contest out of position. Calling 1,200 more with a weak Ace that is likely dominated (by AK, AQ, AJ) and being out of position for the rest of the hand is a losing proposition. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack advantage.

UTG with K♦Q♦ and a comfortable ~89BB (8925). KQ suited is a clear open in 4‑handed play: strong equity, good blockers to AK/AQ, and very playable postflop. The pot is 150 and the min-raise is 200, but a standard 3x open here gets value, builds initiative, and applies pressure to the bigstack on the button. I’ll raise to 300 (3x) — a size I’ve used previously at this table — which isolates the button more often and keeps postflop SPR reasonable. Postflop plan: continuation bet favorable/dry textures, control the pot on coordinated or overcard boards, and be prepared to fold KQ suited to heavy action/large 4-bets or all-in shoves from the shorter SB (Pro). Versus a standard 3‑bet from FiveTwo or Flash I’ll frequently call and play postflop; versus polarizing 4‑bets I’ll proceed cautiously or fold.

4-handed on the button, Q4s is too dominated to profitably flat a UTG open (we make a lot of second-best top pairs vs AQ/KQ/QJ, etc.), and calling also invites the blinds in. With a big stack and position, this hand performs better as a light 3-bet bluff: we leverage fold equity against Minni’s frequent opens, can take it down pre, and if called we still have initiative and position to apply pressure on many boards.

I am holding Pocket Fives in the Small Blind facing a UTG raise from Minni and a Button 3-bet from the chip leader FiveTwo. To call, I need to put in 900 chips. My current stack is 6,575. The call represents approximately 14% of my stack. For set mining to be profitable, I typically want effective stacks to be 15-20 times the call amount (implied odds), but here I only have about 7x. Additionally, I am out of position against two aggressive players, and the original raiser (Minni) is still left to act and could 4-bet. Calling burns chips in a spot with negative expected value, and 4-bet shoving is too loose against a UTG open and a 3-bet. Folding is the disciplined and correct play.

I am in the Big Blind with 6h 3h, an extremely weak hand. There has been an open raise from Minni (UTG) to 300 and a 3-bet from FiveTwo (Button) to 950. Given the strength shown by both players and my poor relative position, calling 850 chips is not a viable option. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve my stack.

I opened to 300 UTG with K♦Q♦ and the button (FiveTwo) 3-bet to 950. KQs is very playable against a button 3-bet: good equity, suited, and useful blockers. Folding here is too nitty given my stack (~86BB) and how often the button 3-bets; 4-betting to the min (1600) is too polarizing and bloats the pot out of position where I prefer postflop playability. Calling 650 (bringing my total in to 950) keeps SPR around ~4, lets me realize equity, and allows controlled lines: c-bet dry boards, pot-control or check behind on highly coordinated boards, and fold to extreme 5-bet shove/very strong action. I’ll call and play the flop carefully OOP.

Board: A♠ 7♦ 8♥. I hold K♦Q♦ after flat‑calling a button 3‑bet. I have no pair and only a remote backdoor diamond possibility — this is a difficult Ace‑high texture because the 3‑bettor’s range is heavy in A‑x, AK/AQ and strong pairs that dominate me. Betting OOP here is low EV: many hands will call, and a raise would put me in a bloated pot with a marginal holding. I want to keep the pot under control and see a turn to realize equity or pick up a clear improvement. Action: check. Plan after checking: call a small c‑bet (≈≤⅓ pot) to see the turn, fold to large sizing or a check‑raise, and if the button checks back take a free turn and look to barre l only on very favorable cards (K/Q/diamond) or to continue pot control.

3-bet pot on an A-high flop strongly favors my preflop range (lots of AK/AQ/AJ), and Minni’s check contains many hands that will have a tough time continuing (pocket pairs like TT/JJ without a spade/heart, KQ/QJ, etc.). With Qh4h I have very little immediate equity, so I want to leverage fold equity now with a small, range-friendly c-bet size that doesn’t overinvest. If called, I’ll proceed cautiously and mostly shut down on blanks, continuing only on good barreling turns (hearts, some high cards) or if I pick up real equity.

I opened UTG with K♦Q♦ and flat‑called a button 3‑bet to play postflop. Flop A♠ 7♦ 8♥: I have no pair, two overcards to the middle cards and a backdoor diamond possibility (one diamond on board). FiveTwo's c‑bet is small (~1/3 pot pre‑bet), so calling is cheap and keeps the pot controlled while allowing me to realize equity and see a turn. Raising OOP with no pair would be low EV (I’m likely dominated by Ax/strong pairs in his 3‑bet range) and folding to this small size is too nitty given my stack. Plan: call the 650 (total committed becomes 1600), fold to a large check‑raise or very large turn barrel if I miss, and only continue aggressively if I pair a K/Q, pick up a meaningful diamond draw on the turn, or see a cheap card and my opponent shows weakness.
