I’m OOP with K♦Q♦ on A♠–7♦–8♥–2♠ after I called the button’s 3‑bet and small flop c‑bet. I never improved: the turn 2♠ doesn’t give me a pair or a meaningful one‑card diamond draw (I already have both diamond hole cards and only one diamond is on board), and Villain’s 3‑betting range is heavy in A‑x/AK/AQ and strong pairs that dominate me. Betting out OOP with two overcards into a bigger stack is low EV and risks bloating a pot I’m unlikely to win at showdown. I’ll control the pot and keep my options open by checking. If FiveTwo checks back I get a free river to improve; if he fires a small turn bet (roughly ≤1/3–1/2 pot) I’ll usually call to see a river, but I’ll fold to large turn barrels or aggressive shove lines that commit most of my stack. If I pair a K/Q or pick up a real diamond on the river I can apply pressure then.

After my small c-bet gets called on A-8-7, Minni’s range is now much denser in real made hands (a lot of Ax, some 8x, and pairs that felt okay peeling once versus a small size). The 2♠ is a brick for my specific hand (Q♥4♥) — I didn’t pick up equity (no draw), and it doesn’t meaningfully improve my ability to represent new strong value in a way that forces folds from his Ax region. Barreling here would mostly be trying to fold out the pocket-pair/8x portion, but at this SPR he can also respond by jamming with strong hands and I’m forced to abandon the bluff anyway. Checking back keeps the pot manageable with pure air, avoids lighting chips on fire into his continuing range, and preserves the option to take a stab on a better river card if he checks again.

I check. I’m holding K♦Q♦ and missed the river (board: 7♦ A♠ 8♥ 2♠ 6♣). I cold‑called a button 3‑bet and a small flop c‑bet to keep the pot controlled; FiveTwo’s 3‑betting range is heavy in A‑x, AK/AQ and pocket pairs that largely dominate KQ. The turn and river bricks didn’t improve me, so my showdown value is very marginal. Betting here would be a thin bluff with limited fold equity against a big‑stack 3‑bettor who can call lightweight ranges — that’s high variance and low EV. Checking preserves stack, keeps the pot manageable, and lets me take a free showdown for KQ high; if FiveTwo fires a sizable river bet I’ll fold, and I’ll only consider calling a very small probe depending on sizing. Overall, pot control and taking showdown is the best line.

River checks to me in a 3-bet pot and I have zero showdown with Q♥4♥. After Minni check/calls the small flop c-bet, his range still contains lots of Ax (which won’t fold often), but it also contains a meaningful chunk of pairs/8x/broadways that are now in rough shape after checking turn and river. I can credibly represent a checked-back turn with AK/AQ/AJ for pot control and then a thin river value bet, so a small stab should generate enough folds without risking too much versus the Ax portion that continues. I’ll use a modest sizing to maximize fold equity vs marginal hands while keeping my risk low if he calls or check-jams.

I hold K♦Q♦ and never improved on A♠–7♦–8♥–2♠–6♣ after cold‑calling the button 3‑bet and a small flop c‑bet. FiveTwo’s line (3‑bet preflop, small flop bet, check on the turn, small river probe 1,250 into a 4,600 pot ≈27% pot) shows caution and a mixed value/bluff range. Pot odds: calling 1,250 to win ≈5,850 requires about 21.4% equity. My showdown equity is low but not negligible against a wide/button 3‑bet range that can contain missed broadways and thin bluffs — and I explicitly planned to call very small river probes. Raising OOP to the min (2,500) would be a highly polarized, high‑variance move with no pair and is unlikely to be profitable; folding is too nitpicky vs such a small sizing. I’ll call the 1,250 and take the showdown.

4-handed from UTG, A3s is comfortably in an opening range: it has good equity when called, plays well postflop with nut-flush potential, and benefits from the ace blocker reducing the chance we run into premium aces. With the big stack, raising also applies pressure to the medium stacks (SB/BB) and can pick up the blinds/antes frequently while avoiding a multiway limped pot where our ace can be dominated more often. I’ll use a standard small open sizing to keep risk controlled and maintain fold equity.

I am on the Button with 4s 3h, which is a very weak off-suit hand (43o). FiveTwo, the chip leader, has opened from UTG for 250. Given the strength of an UTG opening range compared to my garbage hand, and the fact that I have a healthy stack of roughly 66 big blinds, there is no strategic justification to call or 3-bet. This is a clear fold to preserve chips for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Deuce offsuit (Qd 2h). FiveTwo, the table chip leader, has raised from the cutoff/UTG position. My hand is extremely weak and plays poorly out of position. Given the stack sizes and the opponent's tendency to be active, there is no reason to defend this hand or attempt a bluff. I will fold to preserve my stack.

UTG (FiveTwo) opened to 250; pot is 400 and I must call 150. I hold T♠4♦ — a weak, frequently dominated hand with poor postflop playability out of position. Calling requires ~27% equity to be profitable; versus a typical UTG open range my equity is well below that and I’ll be difficult to play postflop. 3-betting as a bluff is unattractive here (no meaningful blockers, out of position, opponent is deep/aggressive). Preserve the stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

I hold Ace-Queen suited (AhQh) in the UTG position. With 4 players remaining, this is a premium hand that sits near the top of my range. I have a stack of roughly 66 big blinds. Limping is passive and invites multi-way pots which devalues high cards. Folding is incorrect due to hand strength. The standard play is to raise to build the pot and define ranges. Based on table history and my previous standard opens, a raise to 2.5x (250 chips) is appropriate to balance value and risk.

I am on the button with Qc9c, a strong suited connector that plays well in position. The UTG player (Pro) has raised to 250. Pro is the short stack at the table with approximately 63 BBs (6325 chips). With a stack of 14,350, I have plenty of room to navigate. Calling allows me to see a flop in position against the short stack and potentially the blinds, while keeping the pot controlled. Re-raising (3-betting) is also an option to try and isolate the short stack, but with a hand that flops as well as Q9s, I prefer calling to realize my equity and keep the blinds in with worse hands. Given the table dynamics and stack sizes, calling is a solid, high-EV play here.

UTG opened to 250 and the button called — pot is 650 and I have A♠J♠ in the small blind. This is too strong to fold but I don't want to limp/call into a multiway pot OOP. A 3‑bet isolates, gains fold equity, and charges speculative hands; it also puts pressure on the shorter UTG stack. I’ll raise to 1000 total (a meaningful 3‑bet ~4x the open). That size is big enough to take the pot down often and still leaves plenty of chips for post‑flop play if called. If I face a large 4‑bet shove I will fold AJ; if I'm called I proceed post‑flop with initiative but caution OOP. (I’ve posted 50, so this raise adds 950 more.)

We’re in the BB with 78o facing an UTG open, a button cold call, and then a small blind 3-bet to 1000 with action still behind us (UTG/BTN can 4-bet or jam). Although the immediate price (900 to win 1600) looks tempting, our equity realization will be poor: we’ll be out of position versus everyone, the 3-bettor’s range is strong, and with two shorter stacks behind there’s a significant chance we get priced into ugly situations (UTG jam / BTN continue) where 78o has reverse implied odds and no blockers for a profitable squeeze. Without suitedness or key blockers, this is a disciplined fold.

I hold AhQh in UTG (4-handed). After opening to 250, I faced a flat from the Button and a 3-bet squeeze to 1000 from the Small Blind (Minni). Minni has been active and aggressive. Folding AQs here is too weak given the hand strength and position on the 3-bettor post-flop. 4-betting to ~2400 would commit a large portion of my stack and likely isolate me against a range that dominates me (QQ+, AK). Calling allows me to play a pot in position against the SB's potentially wide squeeze range with a hand that flops well. I need to call 750 into a pot of 1600, offering reasonable odds.

I am on the button with Qc9c. The current action is a raise to 250 by Pro (UTG), a call by me, a 3-bet to 1000 by Minni (SB), and a call by Pro. I need to commit 750 more to see the flop. The total pot is 2350, giving me pot odds of roughly 3.1 to 1 (750 to win a total pot of 3100). Given my position on the button and the playability of suited one-gappers like Q9s in multi-way pots, calling is the most profitable decision. I have deep stacks relative to Minni and enough leverage against Pro's shorter stack to realize my equity post-flop. Folding would be too tight given the price and position, and raising into a 3-bet range from the small blind is unnecessarily risky.

Flop: 2♠ J♦ 5♠ — I hold A♠ J♠: top pair with the nut kicker plus a four‑card spade (one card to the nut flush). I 3‑bet preflop and was called by both opponents, so I’m first to act in a multiway 3‑bet pot (pot = 3,100). This is a clear spot to bet for value and protection: I want to charge spade draws (9 outs) and get value from worse Jx, pocket pairs and other hands that will call a reasonable c‑bet. I’ll make a firm c‑bet of 1,500 (≈48% pot) — big enough to deny cheap equity to many draws and extract value, but small enough to keep worse hands in. If I face a big raise I’ll re‑evaluate (raises will usually represent sets/two‑pair or strong combo draws).

I hold AhQh on a board of 2sJd5s. Pre-flop, I called a 3-bet from the Small Blind (Minni) along with the Button (Flash). Minni has led out for 1,500 into a pot of 3,100. This is a significant bet (roughly half pot) in a multi-way 3-bet pot. The board texture favors the aggressor's range (JJ, AA, KK, QQ, AJ) or strong draws (AsKs). My hand is currently just Ace-high with no flush draw (I have hearts, board is spades) and only backdoor straight potential. I am losing to all pocket pairs and any Jack. Additionally, Flash is still left to act behind me, and they cold-called the 3-bet, implying a strong range as well. Calling 1,500 would commit nearly 30% of my remaining stack with a very marginal hand and reverse implied odds. The prudent play is to preserve my remaining ~55 big blinds for a better spot.

I have missed the flop completely with Queen-high and no significant draws. Minni, who 3-bet pre-flop from the Small Blind, has led out with a substantial bet of 1500 on a Jack-high board into two opponents. Given her pre-flop strength and the follow-through on the flop, my Qc9c has almost no equity against her range (which likely includes AJ, KJ, or pocket pairs like AA-QQ). Calling would be a waste of chips, and raising is too risky against a shown strength. I will fold to preserve my stack.

I am holding 7-4 offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand. Even in a 4-handed game, playing this hand from the first position (UTG) is not profitable. Folding is the only sensible move here to preserve my stack for higher-equity situations.

Button with Qd3s — a weak, easily dominated hand. Pot is 150 and it’s 100 to see the flop. Raising to steal is possible, but the BB (Pro) is a ~55BB stack who can realistically 3-bet/shove and the SB (FiveTwo) is deep and likely to call; I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position with a marginal Q‑3o. Calling keeps the pot small, uses my position, and lets me fold to strong action if I miss or continue aggressively if I hit. I’ll take the cheap flop and play post‑flop in position.

Button limp from Minni is a capped range and gives us a profitable spot to attack. Completing with 75o is cheap but plays poorly multiway and out of position; raising lets us win the dead money immediately a good portion of the time and denies equity to both the limper and BB. I’ll use a larger SB isolation size to reduce the chance Pro overcalls and to put real pressure on Minni’s weak limp range.

I am in the Big Blind with 5s Td, a very weak unsuited hand. The Small Blind has raised to 500 (5BB) over a Button limp. The raise size is large, and my hand has poor playability and equity. Even though I would have position on the raiser, the Button is still left to act behind me. Calling 400 chips (4BB) with T5o is not profitable given the pot odds and reverse implied odds. Folding is the clear best option.

Qd3s is a very weak, easily‑dominated hand. FiveTwo’s raise to 500 means I must call 400 into a 700 pot — about 36% equity required — and Q3o has much less equity versus a typical SB open range and will often be second best even when I pair the queen. 3‑betting as a bluff isn’t attractive: the opponent is deep and likely to call or 4‑bet, and I don’t want to bloat a pot out of position with a marginal holding. Preserve my 13,825 stack for better spots and fold.

UTG with K4o (Kh4d) in a 4-handed pot — a weak, often dominated hand with poor post‑flop playability. Calling 100 to play out of position against Button (FiveTwo) and the blinds invites multiway pots where K4o performs badly; open‑raising to the min (200) is a tiny raise that will frequently get 3‑bet or squeezed (Pro is a short stack who could shove, FiveTwo and Flash are active/aggressive). With a healthy 138 BB I don't need to force chips in marginal spots — preserve stack and wait for a better opportunity. Fold.

4-handed on the button after UTG folds, this is a prime steal spot. A6o has decent raw equity, blocks strong ace holdings, and plays well in position versus the blinds’ wide defending ranges. Raising applies pressure to the blinds, wins the pot immediately often, and keeps initiative; limping just invites tougher multiway play. I’ll use a small-but-standard open size that still prints vs folds while not bloating pots unnecessarily.

I am in the Small Blind with [Jd 6c], facing a raise to 250 from FiveTwo on the Button. The blinds are 50/100. My hand is J6 offsuit, which is statistically weak (bottom 20-30% of hands). Playing this hand out of position against the chip leader, who likely has a wide opening range but still dominates J6o, is not profitable. The price to call is 200 into a pot of 400, offering 2:1 odds, but the poor playability and reverse implied odds of J6o make this a clear fold. I will preserve my 54bb stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Big Blind with 8s 6d. FiveTwo, the chip leader, has opened from the button for 250 (a 2.5x raise). While the pot odds are attractive (150 to call into a total pot of 400, requiring about 27% equity), 86o is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position, especially against an aggressive big stack. Since I want to preserve my stack for the final 50 hands and avoid unnecessary complications against the table bully, I will opt to fold this marginal hand.

4-handed UTG plays much wider, and as chip leader I want to keep applying pressure and picking up blinds. K7o is not a premium, but the K blocker plus reasonable high-card equity makes it a profitable open at a small size. I’ll raise rather than limp to deny equity and avoid giving the button a free, comfortable pot. Plan: fold comfortably to significant 3-bet/shove pressure, and play in position versus the blinds when called.

I am on the Button with Ace-Deuce offsuit (A2o). The UTG player (FiveTwo), who is the chip leader, has opened to 250. While having an Ace is decent, A2o is a weak hand to play against an Under-The-Gun raise, as I am often dominated by better Aces and have poor playability post-flop. Calling invites squeezes from the blinds, and 3-betting is risky against the chip leader with a marginal hand. The most disciplined action is to fold and preserve my 54bb stack for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind holding Queen of Clubs and Five of Spades. FiveTwo has raised to 250 from UTG. Given my position, the strength of my hand (Q5 offsuit), and the fact that FiveTwo is the chip leader with a very large stack, I should not be calling or raising here. Q5o is a weak hand to play out of position against a strong opponent who has shown a willingness to be aggressive. I will fold to preserve my stack.

FiveTwo opened to 250 from UTG at a 4‑hand table — that range is likely fairly strong. I’m in the big blind with Q♥3♠, an offsuit queen with a terrible kicker and very poor post‑flop playability (frequently dominated by Qx and hard to realize equity out of position). Calling 150 into a ~400 pot gives only ~27% immediate pot odds, but Q3o’s equity vs an UTG open and the likelihood of facing continuation bets and pressure makes this a losing long‑term defend. A small 3‑bet is a thin, easily exploited bluff against a deep, aggressive opponent and I don’t want to bloat the pot with a dominated hand. With ~137bb effective and plenty of hands still to play, the best EV line is to conserve chips and fold.

I am in UTG position (which is 4-handed, so effectively the cutoff) with Ten-Seven offsuit (Ts 7d). This is a statistically weak hand that does not fall into a standard opening range, even in a short-handed game. My stack is 54 BBs, which is healthy enough that I don't need to force action with marginal holdings. The players behind me have significant stacks and have shown aggression. Opening here would likely lead to playing a weak hand out of position or facing a 3-bet. The disciplined play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with Ace-Eight offsuit (Ah8c) in a four-handed game. With the action folded to me, this is a standard opening range hand. Raising here allows me to take control of the pot and potentially steal the blinds, or play a pot in position against the small or big blind. Given the stack depths and the aggressive nature of FiveTwo (the big blind), a standard 3x raise is appropriate.

I have pocket nines in the small blind facing a 3x button open to 300. This is a strong hand worth playing for value and to seize initiative — Flash's frequent 300 opens look fairly wide and FiveTwo (big blind) will defend a lot, so I want to isolate the button and get heads-up or take the pot preflop. A 3-bet to a size around 3–4x the open gives meaningful fold equity and defines my range; it's a small fraction of my 13,675 stack but large enough to discourage light callers. Plan: raise to 1,200 total. If I get a cold 4-bet to a large size I'll likely fold; if called I'll proceed postflop knowing I have a strong pair with good implied odds.

We’re getting a good price closing the action in the BB versus a BTN open and SB 3-bet, and A3 suited plays well in positionless spots due to nut-flush potential and wheel/straight possibilities. As big stack, I don’t need to take a thin 4-bet bluff line that risks bloating the pot OOP versus a stronger 3-bet range; calling keeps in dominated bluffs from SB and allows us to realize equity. Postflop, we can continue aggressively on wheel/flush/low-board textures and stay cautious on A-high one-pair runouts.

I opened with Ace-Eight offsuit from the button, but the action behind me has become very heavy. Minni 3-bet from the small blind, and FiveTwo cold-called the 3-bet from the big blind. A8o is significantly behind the likely ranges of a 3-better and a cold-caller in this spot. Even with the attractive pot odds to call the additional 900, I will be playing a inflated pot multi-way with a dominated hand. Choosing to fold here preserves my stack for better opportunities where I'm not caught between two showing strength.

I 3-bet preflop to isolate and succeeded — it's now heads-up vs FiveTwo. The flop Tc‑2c‑8d is relatively dry; my pocket 9s are a decent middle pair (vulnerable to Tens and overpairs but ahead of most unpaired hands and weaker pairs). Against FiveTwo's wide calling range I want value and to deny equity to backdoor draws while keeping the pot manageable out of position. A continuation bet of ~55% pot (~1,500) will extract value from worse pairs (8x, 2x, small pockets), charge any clubs/backdoor draws, and fold out many pure floats. If he raises big I will strongly consider folding OOP; if called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and proceed cautiously vs heavy action.

Minni’s 1/3-pot c-bet in a 3-bet pot is very likely a high-frequency range bet (AK/AQ/KQ, medium pairs, some overpairs). We’re getting an excellent price (1500 to win 5700), and Ad3d has enough equity to continue: ace-high will sometimes be best versus unpaired broadways, plus we retain backdoor diamond-flush potential and some wheel runouts on 4/5. A flop raise is unattractive because Minni’s remaining stack is shallow enough to jam over a check-raise and our hand doesn’t have the draw strength to take that variance. Call and reassess—continue on good turns (diamond, A, 4/5, or a turn that slows her down), and be disciplined versus large double barrels on bad cards.

Turn: Tc‑2c‑8d‑3c. I hold 9♠9♦ — a medium pair that was a solid c‑bet on the flop and got called. The turn brings the 3♣, completing three clubs on board; a villain needs two clubs in hand to have a made flush now, but his wide calling range from the big blind includes pocket pairs, tens, one‑card club draws and some two‑club combos. Given I’m out of position, my hand is vulnerable to tens/overpairs/sets and the small chance of a two‑club flush. I want pot control and a cheap showdown rather than bloating the pot with a hand that fares poorly vs heavy action. Action: check. Plan: if he bets a small amount (roughly ≤ ~2,000) I’ll call to realize equity and target river value; if he fires a larger turn bet (> ~2,000–2,500) I’ll fold — too often I’m behind. If he checks back I’ll re‑evaluate the river and look to extract a small value bet only vs clearly worse hands or just check down for showdown.

Minni’s small flop c-bet looks like a high-frequency range bet, and her turn check on the 3rd club is a strong indication of range weakness (unpaired broadways, some medium pairs without a club, or pot-control with marginal made hands). We picked up a little showdown value with bottom pair, but it’s too weak to comfortably face river aggression if we give her a free card and let her realize equity with overcards.

I’ll apply pressure with a small-to-medium stab that targets her give-ups and denies equity, while keeping my risk controlled. If she check-raises (especially with her remaining stack), we can comfortably fold—our hand isn’t strong enough to continue versus that line.

Turn analysis: I hold 9♠9♦ on Tc‑2c‑8d‑3c — a medium pair that I checked to keep the pot under control. FiveTwo’s 2,000 into a 7,700 pot is a very small bet (~25% pot) and gives excellent pot odds: I must call 2k to win ~9.7k (break‑even ≈17%). Against the BB’s calling range to my 3‑bet and flop c‑bet I’m ahead of plenty of one‑pair hands, weaker pairs and many single‑club combos; two‑club combos that make a turn flush exist but are relatively rare. Raising OOP with a vulnerable middle pair on a three‑club board would bloat the pot and is unnecessary — calling keeps bluffs and worse hands in, realizes equity, and matches my pre‑turn plan to call small turns. Plan: call the 2,000, re‑evaluate the river (value bet small on safe rivers, fold to a large polarizing shove or raise).

Opponent called my 3‑bet pre, called my flop c‑bet and then fired a small bet on the turn which I called. That line is wide — medium pairs, weak tens, one‑card club combos, occasional two‑club flushes and air. The river 4♥ is a harmless blank (no extra club) and the villain checked, showing either weakness or pot control. With 9♠9♦ I beat a lot of single‑pair hands below tens (8x, 4x, 3x, 2x) and many Kx/Qx/one‑card club hands that will thin‑call a small bet. I prefer a small value probe to extract from those hands while keeping the pot controlled versus the parts of his range that beat me. Bet 2,500 (~25% pot). If I get a large check‑raise or a shove (>> my bet, e.g. ≥ ~6,000) I will fold — I don’t want to commit big with a vulnerable middle pair.

River comes 4h on Tc 2c 8d 3c, and Minni leads tiny (2500) into a large pot after taking the c-bet flop / check-call turn line. That turn check-call caps her range a bit: lots of unpaired broadways, hands with a single club (missed on river), and some medium-strength one-pair hands trying to get to showdown. This small river sizing is very often a blocker/thin-value or cheap bluff attempt rather than a polar value shove.

With Ad3d we have a weak pair, but we only need to be good a small fraction of the time given the price. We beat all her AK/AQ/KQ type hands that picked up (and missed) a club draw or simply floated, and we’re not getting pressured off by a big bet. Raising would be an unnecessary bluff into a range that still contains plenty of Tx/overpairs and occasional traps, so the best EV is to take the pot odds and bluff-catch.

Call and show down.

I am holding Ten-Three offsuit (Tc 3h) in the UTG position in a 4-handed game. This is a very weak starting hand and does not justify an open raise or a call, even with a relatively healthy stack of 12,900 chips. Given the stack sizes of my opponents, particularly the larger stacks of Minni and FiveTwo, there is no strategic advantage to playing this hand. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Button with A♦4♠ and two players to act (SB 20400, BB 5325). With position and a decent ace I want fold equity and to take the initiative — opening rather than just calling. A standard 3x open (300 total) pressures the blinds, isolates or defines ranges, and keeps postflop play in position where A4o has value. If I face a large 3-bet from FiveTwo I’ll fold to avoid playing dominated out of position; if Pro (shorter stack) shoves I’ll only call with a read for a very wide shove — otherwise fold. Plan: raise to 300, c-bet appropriate flops and use position to realize equity and pick up pots.

Button open to 3bb is standard and Minni appears active, but Th2o is near-bottom of range with poor playability and no meaningful blockers. Calling from the small blind would be dominated and difficult to realize equity OOP, and turning it into a 3-bet bluff risks too much versus a competent opener who can continue or 4-bet. Preserve stack and wait for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind with Pocket Eights (8h 8c), a strong hand against a Button open, especially given the active player Minni. With 53 BBs, I have a healthy stack but am out of position against the chip leader. 3-betting here has merit for value, but it risks isolating myself against a stronger range or facing a difficult 4-bet decision. Calling is the standard play to keep the opponent's range wide, control the size of the pot, and avoid being blown off a good hand pre-flop. I will call the additional 200 chips to see a flop.

I have flopped a set of Eights on a board of [9h, 2s, 8s]. This is an incredibly strong hand, but the board is dynamic with flush and straight draws present. Being out of position against the pre-flop raiser (Minni), the standard and most effective play is to check to the aggressor. Minni has been active and aggressive, so there is a high likelihood she will make a continuation bet. If she bets, I can look to check-raise to extract value from draws (spades, straight draws like JT, 67) and top pairs (A9, K9, Q9) or overpairs, while also protecting my hand against bad turn cards. Leading out (donk betting) would likely fold out her bluffs and weak hands that might otherwise bet.

I was the preflop raiser and this flop (9♥ 2♠ 8♠) is a good continuation-bet spot. I hold A♦4♠ — Ace-high with one spade for a backdoor flush. The board is fairly dry and misses a large portion of the BB’s calling range (broadways, weak A/x, many K/Q/J hands), so I can win the pot right now with a c-bet. I’ll size to about half the pot to apply pressure but not overcommit versus his short stack: bet 350 into 650. If he folds I take it down; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn (value-bet if an Ace appears, be cautious if a spade/9/8 lands). If he check-raises large I’ll give credit to strong made hands or nut spade draws and fold to avoid playing dominated with only Ace-high.

I have flopped a set of Eights on a draw-heavy board (9h 2s 8s). While checking to the pre-flop raiser was the correct first move, now that Minni has made a continuation bet of 350, I need to extract value and protect my hand. The board is wet with flush draws (spades) and straight draws (T7, JT, 67), which connects well with a Button opening range. Minni has been aggressive and has shown a tendency to call flop raises in previous hands (e.g., Hands 56, 96). Check-raising is superior to calling here because it charges draws while my equity is highest and builds the pot given my stack-to-pot ratio. I am short-stacked (starting hand with ~51 BBs), and building a pot now sets up a comfortable shove on the turn or river. Just calling risks a scare card (spade or straight completer) killing my action or beating me. I will raise to 1050 (3x the bet), which extracts maximum value from top pairs and draws.

I was the preflop raiser and c‑bet 350 on 9♥‑2♠‑8♠. Pro now check‑raised to 1,050; I must call 700 into the 2,050 pot. A call requires ~25.5% equity (700/(2,050+700)). Pro’s check‑raise is polarized toward strong value (sets: 99/88/22, strong 9/8 hands, overpairs) with some two‑spade semi‑bluffs mixed in. My A♦4♠ has essentially only ace‑high and very little realized equity vs a value‑heavy range, and I don’t block enough of the draw combos. Given the large sizing, the short stack behind the raise, and my earlier plan to fold to big check‑raises when I have only Ace‑high, calling or raising would be committing with insufficient equity. I fold to preserve my stack and wait for a better spot.

Four-handed and deep-stacked, K♠T♦ is a playable opening hand but not a limp. I want initiative and fold equity, and I don’t want to invite multiple callers by min-raising. Opens at this table are typically ~250–300, so I’ll raise to 300 (3bb) to define the hand and play heads-up where KTo performs well. Plan: avoid getting tangled in big 3-bet pots — fold to a large 3-bet or shove; if called, continuation-bet favorable flops and proceed cautiously multiway.

4-handed on the button, ATo is comfortably strong enough to continue versus an UTG open, and we benefit a lot from playing in position. Given Minni’s frequent opening pattern, I prefer a 3-bet here as a thin value / isolation raise: it pressures a wide open range, reduces the chance the blinds come along, and gives us initiative with a hand that has good high-card playability and blocks strong Ax.

I’m choosing a sizing that’s big enough to deny easy calls (and to make blind squeezes less attractive) but not so big that we’re pot-committed versus a 4-bet. If Minni 4-bets to a strong size, we can comfortably fold this combo.

I hold 3s 4d in the Small Blind, which is a very weak off-suit connector. The action has been heavy before me: Minni opened from UTG to 300 and FiveTwo 3-bet from the Button to 950. Facing a raise and a re-raise, my hand has virtually no equity and I am out of position. Cold-calling a 3-bet with this hand would be a significant mistake. The only correct play is to fold immediately.

I am holding 6h 4s in the Big Blind. Minni opened for 300 and FiveTwo 3-bet to 950. This is a very weak hand to defend against a 3-bet, especially out of position. Given the stack sizes and the strength shown by the opponents, there is no reason to commit more chips to this hand. Folding is the best play to preserve my stack.

I opened to 300 and FiveTwo 3-bet to 950, so I have to call 650 into a 1,400 pot (pot would be 2,050 after my call), which requires ~32% equity to break even. K♠T♦ OOP versus a button 3‑bet is often dominated by their value range (AK/AQ/KQ and pocket pairs) and has poor post‑flop playability out of position. A small 4‑bet to the shown min (1,600) is unlikely to fold enough of their range and a large 4‑bet or shove is unjustified with this holding. With deep stacks I prefer to conserve chips for better spots and avoid getting tangled in a big, OOP pot with a marginal, frequently dominated hand. Fold.

4-handed from UTG our opening range widens a lot, and 55 is comfortably ahead of the blinds’ random holdings and has good playability (can flop strong sets, can also realize equity in position vs blinds when called). With stacks deep (200bb effective vs Minni) we want the initiative and to avoid going multiway too cheaply, so I’ll open-raise rather than limp. Sizing 2.5x keeps folds coming while building a pot where we can c-bet favorable textures and still comfortably fold to heavy 3-bet pressure if needed.

I am on the Button with KsQd, a strong broadway hand. The UTG player (FiveTwo) has opened to 250 (2.5bb). Against a UTG opening range, which is typically tighter, KQo is strong enough to play but often not strong enough to 3-bet for value, as it is dominated by AK, AQ, KK, and QQ, which are core parts of a UTG continuing range. 3-betting might also fold out hands I dominate (like KJs, QJs). Calling allows me to play the pot in position, keeping the UTG range wider and allowing me to realize my equity without bloating the pot pre-flop with a hand that is likely behind a 4-bet calling range. My stack of ~60bb is sufficient for post-flop maneuvering.

I am in the Small Blind with A d J d. FiveTwo has raised to 250 from UTG, and Pro has called on the button. This is a very strong hand, especially suited. I have a stack of 12,750 chips. My options are to call and see a flop or 3-bet. Against an UTG raise and a flat call, a 3-bet is a strong play to isolate or take down the pot immediately, but with AJ suited, playing a multi-way pot is also acceptable. However, building a pot with a premium hand and reducing the number of opponents is usually more profitable. I will raise to 1,000 to put pressure on the callers and define the hand strength.

Fold. 2♥9♣ is a very weak, unsuited, disconnected hand that is routinely dominated by UTG open and SB 3‑bet ranges. Action: FiveTwo opened UTG, Pro cold‑called, and Flash squeezed to 1000 — likely a strong or aggressive squeeze. Pot is 1600 and it costs 900 to call (≈36% pot odds), but my hand has well under that equity vs two tightened ranges and will play poorly multiway and out of position. 4‑bet bluff or jam would be unnecessarily high variance with no read that justifies it. Preserve the ~20k stack for better spots and fold.

Flash’s small-blind squeeze to 1000 is a relatively small 3-bet given the dead money (our open + Pro’s flat + blinds), and his squeezing range in this spot can be fairly wide/aggressive. With 55 we don’t want to 4-bet (poor playability when called/jammed), but we’re getting a good price to continue and have strong implied odds versus an 11.8k stack when we flop a set, especially with Pro potentially coming along. Plan: call and play mostly fit-or-fold postflop, continuing aggressively when we hit a set and otherwise giving up to normal c-bets/pressure.

I originally flatted a UTG open with KsQd on the Button. The Small Blind (Flash) has squeezed to 1000 chips, and the UTG player (FiveTwo) has called the squeeze. While I am getting decent pot odds (calling 750 to win 2350, roughly 3.1:1), my hand strength relative to the action is poor. A squeeze from the SB against a UTG open usually signifies a strong range (TT+, AQ+, AK), and the UTG player's flat call confirms considerable strength (likely AK, AQ, JJ-QQ, or traps). KQo is frequently dominated in this spot by AK, AQ, KK, and QQ. In a 3-way pot with an SPR of less than 2, I have high reverse implied odds—hitting a King or Queen could easily lose my stack to a better kicker or an overpair. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my ~58bb stack.

I have the nut flush draw with two overcards (Ace and Jack) on a board of 3d-Td-5h. Having been the pre-flop 3-better, I have the range advantage here. I will lead out with a continuation bet of 800, which is approximately one-third of the pot. This sizing is standard in 3-bet pots to maintain pressure on the opponent's medium pocket pairs or non-diamond high cards while allowing me to efficiently realize my equity if called.

We flopped bottom set on Td3d5h in a 3-bet pot, which is near the top of our range after calling pre. Flash’s small 1/4-pot c-bet is consistent with a wide squeeze range (overpairs, Tx, broadways with/without a diamond), so we want to start building a pot for stacks while also charging the very real diamond/overcard equity. Raising now sets up a clean turn shove at a low SPR and avoids giving a cheap card when his sizing is invite-y.

I hold the nut flush draw with two overcards (Ace and Jack) on a 10-high board. This is a massive draw with approximately 45-55% equity against most of FiveTwo's range, including overpairs like JJ-QQ and top-pair hands like AT. The current pot is nearly 6,000 chips, and FiveTwo's raise to 2,800 shows significant strength, likely a set or a strong Ten. Given the stack-to-pot ratio, calling the 2,000 would leave me with only 9,000 chips behind, making it difficult to fold to a turn bet even if I miss my draw. By shoving now, I maximize my fold equity against his marginal made hands (like AT or JJ) and guarantee that I realize all five cards of my equity without being pushed off the hand on a blank turn. This is the optimal high-roller strategy for maximizing expected value with a semi-bluff of this magnitude.

Flash’s 3-bet pot flop line (small c-bet, then back-raise/jam to 11,800) is consistent with overpairs (AA–JJ), Tx, and a lot of diamond/broadway/overcard draws that want fold equity. Against that range, our bottom set (55) is near the top of our distribution and is a massive favorite; the only realistic hands that have us crushed are TT/33, which are very few combos compared to all the overpairs and draws.

Pot odds: we’re calling 9,000 to win ~25,950 (about 35% required). Our equity vs the jam range is far above that, so folding would be a huge mistake. Since Flash is all-in, the correct play is simply to call and let the board run out.

Current hand is 106/150. I am in UTG (first to act 4-handed) with 7s 6h. My stack is 5,825 (58 BB), which is the shortest at the table. 76o is a weak off-suit connector that performs poorly from early position, even in a short-handed game. Opening here would be too loose and risks bleeding chips against deeper stacks who have position on me. The standard play is to fold.

I am currently the chip leader with approximately 260 big blinds. On the Button in a 4-handed game, 7-high offsuit (7d 6c) is a marginal hand. While it's often within an opening range in 4-max play, both blinds have shown significant aggression in previous hands (especially Minni). FiveTwo is now relatively short-stacked (80BB) following the large pot in Hand #105 and might be looking for a spot to shove. Given my massive lead and the weakness of my hand, there is no need to play this speculative hand and potentially face a 3-bet or a shove. I will fold and wait for a stronger opening hand.

I’m in the small blind with 5♠4♦ — a weak, uncoordinated hand and out of position versus only the big blind (FiveTwo). Pot is 150 and I only need to put in 50 more (total 100) to see a flop. Raising to 200 to ‘steal’ is possible, but with this hand and a defender who often defends/3-bets, that’s thin and can get me into difficult spots. Calling is cheap (50 into 150 — ~25% pot odds) and lets me realize equity or fold to significant aggression post-flop. Plan: call to see the flop; if I miss and face heavy action, fold; if I connect (pair/straight/flush draw), play aggressively or pot-control depending on texture.

SB limp in a 4-handed game is usually a wide, capped range. A8o has solid high-card equity, dominates a lot of the limp/call hands (A2–A7, 8x), and benefits from taking initiative to win the pot pre or get a clearer postflop line. I’ll iso-raise to a size that pressures the limp and builds the pot when ahead while still allowing me to fold if Minni springs a big limp-3bet.

I’m on the small blind with 5♠4♦ — a weak, unsuited, low hand and I’m out of position against FiveTwo’s raise to 450. I’ve already posted 100, but calling another 350 into the 550 pot would require ~39% equity to break even. Versus a reasonable raising range from FiveTwo my hand’s equity is well below that and it plays poorly OOP post‑flop; 3‑betting is also inappropriate with this holding. Preserve stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

I am currently the chip leader with approximately 260 big blinds. In a 4-handed game, 87o is a very weak hand to open from UTG (which is effectively the cutoff position). While 4-handed play allows for wider opening ranges, the risk of getting 3-bet by an active player like Minni or a short-stack shove from Pro or FiveTwo makes opening this hand unprofitable. Post-flop playability is also poor for this hand when out of position. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to preserve my lead.

I’m on the button with Q♠8♥ facing only the blinds (SB 8,100; BB 5,725) and 100 to call. Button position lets me open a wide stealing range — Q8o is marginal but a standard open from the button. I’ll raise to apply pressure and take the initiative rather than limp/call and play a passive pot out of position. I choose a 3x BB-sized raise (to 300) — big enough to generate fold equity against the blinds but not so large as to overcommit with a marginal hand. Plan postflop: if I’m called I’ll use position to continuation-bet favorable textures (Q-high, dry boards) and play cautiously on coordinated/rainbow boards; fold to a sizeable 3-bet (large squeeze, e.g., >~700–800) unless reads indicate frequent light 3-bets, in which case I may mix calls. Overall goal: steal the blinds or realize my equity in position without bloating the pot with a weak hand.

4-handed, BTN is opening very wide and Minni has shown she can fold to 3-bets. Q9o is a marginal flat from the SB (OOP with BB behind), but it works well as a light 3-bet: we block some strong continues (Qx), can often win preflop, and when called we still have decent playability as the aggressor. Plan: 3-bet/fold vs a 4-bet or shove, and play postflop with initiative (small c-bets on favorable high-card boards, more checking on coordinated middling boards).

I am in the Big Blind with A2o. Minni opened from the Button to 300, and FiveTwo 3-bet from the Small Blind to 1100. This action demonstrates significant strength, or at least aggressive posturing, from the Small Blind. My hand is easily dominated by the 3-bettor's value range (Ax, pairs). Calling 1000 chips represents nearly 18% of my remaining stack, which is too steep a price for a weak offsuit ace in a potential multi-way or heads-up pot where I am likely behind. The correct play is to fold and preserve my stack.

I opened to 300 on the button with Q♠8♥ and FiveTwo (SB) responded with a 3-bet to 1,100. This is a sizeable 3-bet (>~700–800) and my preflop plan was to fold marginal offsuit hands to that size unless I had a specific read of frequent light 3-bets. Calling 800 into a 1,500 pot gives me only ~35% pot-odds; Q8o does not have that equity against a standard SB 3-bet range (pairs, A/Kx, broadways, suited A/K, some suited connectors). Yes I keep position postflop, but the hand is often dominated and I’d be playing a bloated pot out of position vs many turn/river decisions. I’ll fold and preserve my stack for better spots.

I have J♦T♦ UTG in a 4-handed game with a very deep stack (~198BB). This is a playable, aggressive-opening hand — good equity, suited, and can realize strong implied odds postflop. Limping is too passive in this spot and invites multiway pots where my equities drop; opening is preferable. I’ll open to 3x the BB (300) — a standard sizing here that applies pressure to the blinds, isolates weaker holdings, and keeps worse hands in to extract value. If I face a small 3-bet I can evaluate based on size and opponent (call or 4-bet occasionally), but I’ll fold to very large 3-bets out of position. Postflop plan: continue on favorable textures, pot-control or fold when checked to on dry boards, and leverage deep stack for implied odds when I hit straights/flushes.

Button gives us position, but Q3o is too dominated versus an UTG open even 4-handed. It has poor playability, makes weak top pairs, and suffers reverse implied odds—especially with both blinds still to act and capable of squeezing. Not a good candidate to call or 3-bet bluff here, so we preserve stack for better spots.

I hold 9c 5s in the Small Blind, which is a very weak hand (95 offsuit). Minni has raised to 300 (3bb) from UTG, indicating a potentially strong range. Being out of position with a trash hand against an UTG open makes this a trivial fold. The pot odds do not justify a call given the poor equity and playability of my hand.

I am in the Big Blind with T8o. Minni raised to 3x from the UTG/Cutoff position (4-handed). The pot is 450 and it costs me 200 to call, giving me 3.25:1 odds on a call. In 4-handed play, T8o is strong enough to defend the big blind against a standard open, especially since I am closing the action pre-flop and have a substantial chip lead. I will call to see a flop and evaluate based on board texture.

On a very dry board of 5d-2c-2s, I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor. My hand, Ten-high, has missed completely but retains some equity against a range of overcards. Standard play on this texture is to check to the raiser to see their continuation bet sizing and frequency. I do not want to lead into the aggressor here, as I have no made hand or significant draw.

Flop is 5♦-2♣-2♠ and I hold J♦T♦. As the preflop aggressor I have two overcards plus a backdoor diamond flush draw (needs two diamonds), so modest equity but not a made hand. This is a very dry paired board that favors a small continuation bet: it folds out pure air from the BB, gets value from worse cards and draws, and defines ranges without bloating the pot out of position. I’ll bet ~1/3–40% pot to maximize fold equity while keeping lots of hands that I beat or can outdraw in. If I get called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and only continue aggressively with a diamond or a real improvement; I’ll respect a strong check-raise and fold to heavy aggression since I’m currently just overcards with a backdoor draw.

I have Ten-high on a very dry board (5-2-2) and no real draws. Minni's small continuation bet of 250 into 650 is standard, but my hand has almost no equity against her range and lacks any backdoor potential (no flush draw, no straight draw). Floating here out of position is unlikely to be profitable. I will fold and preserve my large stack.

4-handed ranges are wider, but UTG is still the tightest seat and 92o is near the bottom of the deck: poor equity realization, dominated when we make a pair, and no meaningful blocker value. Limping invites isolation from the Button/SB with position and strong stacks, and raising just burns chips without sufficient fold equity or playability. Preserve stack for better spots.

I hold 9h 5c on the Button, which is a very weak, unsuited hand with poor playability. Although I am in position, the blinds (Flash and Minni) have massive stacks compared to mine and can easily defend or 3-bet. With 57 big blinds, I am not under pressure to make loose steals with bottom-tier trash hands. The standard and prudent play here is to fold.

I have a premium hand (Ace-Queen offsuit) in a blind-versus-blind situation after the UTG and Button folded. AQo is significantly ahead of the Big Blind's random range. I will raise to 300 to build the pot, take the initiative, and potentially win the blinds immediately. This sizing is standard for an open from the small blind.

J♥4♦ is a weak, uncoordinated offsuit hand, but defending the big blind vs a small‑blind open to 3bb is reasonable with deep stacks. The pot is 400 and I must call 200 more — calling to 300 total gives ~33% break‑even equity, which is acceptable against a wide SB opening range. I’ll call to see a flop, plan to play cautiously out of position (check most flops, fold to large continuation bets unless I pair/jam equity or have strong draws). I will not reraise with this hand.

I have top pair with a strong Queen kicker on a dry, Ace-high board. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and my hand is likely the best, I will lead out with a continuation bet for value. I expect to be called by weaker Aces (AJ, A9-A2) or a Ten. A half-pot bet of 300 is a standard sizing to build the pot and extract value.

I hold Jh4d and the flop is Tc Ad 4h — I’ve paired the 4 (bottom pair) and I’m out of position vs Flash (SB). Flash opened and made a small c‑bet of 300 into a 900 pot (to‑call 300), so I’m getting ~25% break‑even pot odds. A single low pair has enough showdown value against a reasonably wide SB c‑bet range to justify seeing another card, and raising OOP with this hand is a leak. I’ll call 300 to continue and reevaluate on the turn: if I see a blank I’ll proceed cautiously (check‑call small bets), and if the turn brings an Ace/Ten or Flash shows heavy aggression I’ll fold unless I improve to two‑pair or better.

I have top pair with an Ace and a strong Queen kicker on a board that is becoming increasingly coordinated. The King on the turn is a scare card as it completes a potential straight with QJ (though I block some of those combinations with my Queen) and improves hands like AT or KT to two pair. However, I am still likely ahead of Minni's range, which includes many weaker Aces (AJ, A9-A2) and pocket pairs. I will place a second barrel of 750 (approximately 62.5% of the pot) to extract value from those weaker Aces and to maintain my initiative in this blind-versus-blind encounter. If I am raised, I will have to carefully re-evaluate the strength of my hand.

I hold Jh4d — only bottom pair (4s) with a weak kicker on Tc Ad 4h Kh. I called the flop intending to fold to heavy turn aggression unless I improved to two‑pair or better. Flash’s sizable turn raise to 750 is exactly the kind of action I planned to respect: it is consistent with value-heavy hands that dominate me (Ax, Kx, Tx, AT, trips/two‑pair) and not enough pure bluffs to justify calling. The pot is 1950; calling 750 needs ~27.8% equity, and my realistic equity vs his raising range is well below that. Raising out of position would be reckless. I fold and preserve my stack for better spots.

I am in the UTG position with J3o (Jack of Spades, Three of Diamonds). This is a very weak off-suit hand. With 4 players remaining and a healthy stack of roughly 57 big blinds, there is no need to attempt a loose steal from early position. The hand has very poor playability and is dominated by most calling ranges. The standard and correct play is to fold.

I am on the button with 84o, which is one of the weakest hands in the deck. While opening from the button is generally a good strategy to steal blinds, especially with a large chip lead, this specific hand lacks both high-card value and connectivity. Given that Minni and FiveTwo have been reasonably active and defending their blinds, I will choose the low-variance route and fold this hand to wait for a better opening range.

I’m on the small blind with A♠J♥ heads-up vs the big blind (FiveTwo). This is a strong hand for an aggressive approach preflop — raising takes the initiative, narrows the BB’s calling range, and prevents them from seeing a cheap flop. Standard open size here is about 3x the big blind, so I’ll raise to 300 total. That builds the pot while keeping postflop decisions manageable given my deep stack (19,475) and the BB’s 8,450. If I face a large 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate (call small 3-bets sometimes, but won’t 4-bet light into heavy aggression).

Minni is opening from the small blind in a 4-handed game and should have a fairly wide range. From the big blind we’re getting a good price (200 to win 600 total after calling) and 8-7 offsuit has decent connectivity and playability to realize equity in positionless but heads-up blind-vs-blind dynamics. A 3-bet bluff is possible, but with our hand’s reasonable postflop equity and to avoid inflating the pot out of position versus a competent wide opener, the best default is to defend by calling.

I have top pair with a good kicker (A♠J♥) on Ac Kc 4h and I'm in position after opening preflop. Villain called a standard 3x SB raise, so his range is wide (Ax, Kx, broadways, medium pairs, some club combos). I want thin value and to deny equity to club/backdoor draws while keeping worse A/K hands and medium pairs in to call, so I place a half‑pot bet. Betting 300 extracts value from Kx and worse A‑hands, keeps the pot controllable with deep stacks, and sets up continuation decisions on the turn — if he raises big I’ll give strong weight to folding (he likely has two‑pair/set/AK), if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on turn.

Minni’s 1/3-pot c-bet in a blind-vs-blind pot can be very wide, but our specific hand is a bad continue: 8♠7♦ has no pair, no club backdoor on a two-club board, and only thin backdoor straight potential. Versus even a wide range we’re mostly relying on pairing (6 outs) and we’ll be out of position facing future barrels on A/K-high textures where our pair is often dominated. Raising as a bluff is also unattractive because we have no useful blockers to Ax/Kx or club draws and little equity when called. Best EV is to fold and preserve stack for better defend candidates.

In a 4-handed game, Ah6h is a strong starting hand from UTG. I have a significant chip lead and want to maintain pressure on the shorter stacks. A standard 3x open raise is appropriate to take control of the pot early and potentially win the blinds or see a flop with initiative.

Flash opened UTG to 300 (a 3x open). I’m on the button with J♠7♦ offsuit — a weak, often dominated hand vs an early-position raising range. Calling 300 into a 450 pot requires ~40% equity (300/(450+300)=40%), but J7o’s equity vs an UTG range is well below that and it performs poorly multiway. A light 3‑bet bluff is unattractive here because the blinds can squeeze or Flash can 4‑bet, and I don’t want to get stacked off with a marginal hand. With a healthy 19.8k stack I’ll preserve chips and wait for a better spot to exploit position or attempt a steal. Fold.

We’re in the small blind facing an UTG open to 300 with 93 offsuit. Even getting ~1.8:1 (call 250 to win 450), our raw equity versus an UTG opening range is marginal at best and we realize it very poorly out of position (often multiway if BB comes along), with heavy reverse implied odds when we make weak pairs. A 3-bet bluff is also too loose here given our hand quality and Flash’s demonstrated willingness to open/continue. Best EV is to preserve chips and fold.

I hold AsQd in the Big Blind with a stack of 5575 (approx 55 BBs). Flash, the chip leader, opened to 300 from UTG. While AQo is a strong hand, 3-betting or shoving from the BB against a UTG open at this stack depth can be risky; shoving 55 BBs is an overbet that folds out worse hands and isolates against a stronger range (AK, pairs). A standard 3-bet would build a large pot out of position. Flat calling keeps Flash's range wide, including hands I dominate (AJ, KQ), and controls the pot size. Thus, calling is the most prudent play to maximize value while managing risk.

I am out of position against the UTG opener (Flash) on a J-7-2 rainbow board (two hearts). I hold Ace-Queen offsuit, which gives me two overcards. This board texture is relatively dry but hits some of the UTG range (JJ, AJ, KJ). It is standard to check to the pre-flop aggressor to see their action. Leading out (donk betting) doesn't accomplish much here as I likely only get called by better hands or fold out hands I dominate. I will check and evaluate based on Flash's sizing.

I have the nut flush draw on a J-7-2 board with one heart on the board and two in my hand. After raising pre-flop and being checked to by the Big Blind, a continuation bet is the most effective play. It allows me to take the pot down immediately if Pro has nothing, while building a pot for my high-equity draw. Given Pro's short stack, a standard-sized bet of 400 (roughly 60% of the pot) applies appropriate pressure.

I hold AsQd on a board of 2h-7c-Jh. I checked to the pre-flop aggressor (Flash, UTG), who bet 400 into a pot of 650. This bet size (approx. 60% pot) is significant and suggests a polarized range of strong value hands (sets, overpairs, strong Jacks) or strong draws (flush draws). Against a UTG opening range, my Ace-Queen high is often dominated (by AJ, AA, QQ, KK) or drawing thin. I have no flush draw to fall back on. Even if my overcards are live, I only have about 6 outs (approx. 12% equity on the turn), and I am getting pot odds of roughly 27% (400 to call 1050). Being out of position with just overcards against a large bet from a strong range is a losing situation. Folding preserves my stack of over 50 BBs for better spots.

Pocket eights in 4-handed are a clear open-raise hand — good equity vs typical blind defending ranges and worth playing for value and fold equity. The table has active players behind (button and two blinds) who often defend/3-bet, but I’m deep (≈198 BB) and can comfortably play postflop. Standard sizing to build the pot and discourage too many callers is 3x the big blind. Raise to 300: gets value from worse pairs, broadways and suited connectors, maintains initiative, and gives me room to fold or call/4-bet if I see aggression. If I face a small 3-bet I’ll call and evaluate flop; against a large shove I’ll fold/call depending on exact sizing and pot odds.

4-handed, UTG is effectively a lojack/cutoff range and Minni has been opening frequently. On the button with Q6 suited we have decent playability (flush potential, some top-pair equity) and we realize equity well in position. A 3-bet bluff is possible but Q6s is fairly dominated when called and I’d rather keep the pot controlled against a strong continuing range while using position postflop. Call and proceed, folding later on unfavorable textures/pressure.

I hold 6s 2c in the Small Blind, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. Facing a raise from UTG (Minni) and a call from the Button (FiveTwo), I am being offered pot odds of 3:1 to call (250 to win 750). However, my hand has very low equity against their ranges, and I would be playing out of position against two opponents. With 53 big blinds, there is no reason to defend this hand. The correct play is to fold immediately.

I am in the Big Blind holding Ah 4d. Minni raised from UTG to 300 and FiveTwo called from the Button. The pot is currently 750, and it costs me 200 more to call. I am getting roughly 3.75-to-1 odds on a call (21% equity needed). A4o is strong enough to defend the Big Blind in this spot to close the action. I'm the chip leader, so seeing a flop for a small additional investment is the correct play.

I have flopped three of a kind with Aces, but my kicker (4) is very weak. On a board of As-Ac-Kc, there is only one Ace remaining in the deck. Given that Minni raised from UTG, her range significantly includes strong Aces (AK, AQ, AJ, AT) and pocket Kings, all of which currently beat me. However, if neither opponent has the last Ace or pocket Kings, I am currently in a dominant position against hands like pocket pairs or broadway cards. I will check to the pre-flop aggressor to see how the action develops and control the pot size, as this is a classic 'way ahead or way behind' scenario.

Flop A♠ A♣ K♣ vs two callers: my 88 is an underpair and heavily vulnerable. Any Ace in opponents’ calling ranges makes trips; Kings and most pocket pairs beat me too. The board is relatively dry (few realistic draws) so a continuation bet won’t fold many better hands — it mainly loses value or builds a pot I don’t want to be bloated in while likely behind. With deep stacks I prefer pot control and to see a free turn card rather than commit chips now. Plan: check to keep the pot manageable, fold to large, non-giving bets if faced with heavy aggression (unless pot odds/reads justify a call), and consider a small float or a delayed bet on a safe turn card to regain initiative or extract value from weaker Kx/pairs.

Multiway on A♠ A♣ K♣ after both opponents check, our Q♦6♦ has virtually no immediate equity (no club, no straight draw) and any stab is likely to run into a lot of Ax/Kx in both ranges—especially the BB’s defend range. This is also a common trap/check line for trips. With low fold equity and poor barreling prospects when called, the best play is to take the free card and look to pick up a backdoor diamond or a favorable turn to bluff later.

On the turn, the board is As-Ac-Kc-Ts. I have three of a kind with Aces (Ah-As-Ac), with a King and Ten kicker from the board. My hole card (4d) is currently irrelevant as the Ten on the board is a better kicker. While Trip Aces is a very strong hand, this board is extremely dangerous. Minni raised from UTG, which suggests a range heavy with high cards. I am currently losing to any Ace with a better kicker (AJ, AQ), any full house (AK, AT, KK, TT), and any straight (QJ). Since the flop was checked around, it's possible my opponents are trapping or also have marginal hands. I will check to maintain pot control and see how the action develops, as I am 'way ahead or way behind' in this spot and don't want to bloat the pot against a range that potentially dominates me.

Check — pot control. I have pocket eights on A♠ A♣ K♣ T♠ with two callers from preflop. I’m an underpair to any Ace and behind most pocket pairs K+; I only have two outs (two eights) to make a set (~4% on the river), so calling or building the pot here is a poor long‑term play. Both opponents checked flop and turn, which can be weakness or a trap — betting into two players risks folding out the very hands I beat while getting called or raised by hands that crush me (A×, K×, bigger pairs). With deep stacks I want to keep the pot manageable and see the river for a cheap equity shot or a better bluffing opportunity. I will check and be prepared to fold to any meaningful bet; I can consider a targeted bluff or a small value bet on the river only if the card and reads make it favorable.

Turn goes check/check again in a multiway pot on A♠ A♣ K♣ T♠. With Q♦6♦ we have essentially no showdown value and no meaningful draw, so checking is mostly just conceding the pot. In position we have a strong range advantage (we can credibly have Ax/Kx and even some Tx that wants protection vs clubs), and the double-check line is often capped at medium pairs, broadways, and missed hands. I’ll take a stab sized to (1) fold out those weak holdings and (2) charge/fold out some club draws, while keeping the risk controlled and folding to any check-raise.

I am holding Ah-4d on a board of As-Ac-Kc-Ts. This gives me trip Aces, but my kicker (4) is very weak. However, since the Ten and King on the board are higher than my 4, my current five-card hand is AAA-K-T. I am only beaten by a player holding the final Ace with a better kicker (Ad-Jx, Ad-Qx), a straight (QJ), or a full house (KK, TT, or an Ace-King/Ace-Ten combo). Given that the flop was checked around by everyone, it is less likely that someone is holding a monster like KK or an Ace with a strong kicker, though a slowplay is possible. FiveTwo's bet of 600 into the 950 pot on the turn could be a value bet with a King or a Ten, or a semi-bluff. As the chip leader, I have plenty of room to call here to see the river and evaluate the final action. Folding trip Aces for one bet is too conservative, but raising would be overplaying a hand that is 'way ahead or way behind.'

I stick to the plan I wrote earlier: this board (A♠ A♣ K♣ T♠) turned me into a pot-control/checking mode with 8♢8♡. My hand is an underpair that only beats non‑paired hole cards and small pocket pairs — it loses to any A, any K, and any pocket pair 9+. FiveTwo’s turn stab to 600 (with Flash calling) is a meaningful piece of aggression into two players rather than a one‑off probe. Calling 600 into the current pot (~2150) requires roughly ~22% equity to break even; I only have ~4% to improve to a set and limited showdown equity versus the likely combined ranges here. Raising as a bluff is ill-advised multiway (no strong blockers and low fold equity), and calling commits chips to a multiway pot where I’m frequently behind. Preserve the deep stack for better spots — fold.

On the river, the board is As-Ac-Kc-Ts-9d. I hold the third Ace (Ah), giving me three of a kind. My fifth card (the kicker) is the Ten on the board, so my hand is AAA-K-T. Given the pre-flop action where Minni (UTG) raised and FiveTwo (Button) called, and the fact that the flop was checked around, it's possible that someone is slow-playing a monster (like KK or an Ace with a higher kicker), or they have marginal hands like a King or a pocket pair. FiveTwo's turn bet of 600 suggested some interest in the pot. By checking the river, I control the size of the pot and allow FiveTwo to bet with weaker hands (bluffs or a King) that might fold if I were to lead out. I am 'way ahead or way behind' here; I lose to a straight (QJ), a full house (KK, TT, 99, AT, AK), or an Ace with a Queen or Jack kicker. Checking is the most prudent move to see a showdown or evaluate a bet from my opponent.

River is a relative brick after my small turn stab gets called. Flash’s turn-call range is heavily weighted to Ax (never folding), strong Kx (often calling), and some Tx; those hands won’t fold enough to justify a river barrel, especially since I don’t hold good blockers to Ax/Kx. Importantly, when he *does* have the portion of range that might fold (missed club draws / random floats), my Q-high can already win at showdown. Turning that thin showdown value into a bluff risks getting looked up by any pair. Best is to take the free showdown and preserve stack.

4-handed UTG ranges are fairly wide and A6o is comfortably ahead of random holdings, with decent playability (top pair potential, wheel draws). With no ante, I want to apply pressure but keep the pot manageable versus two bigger stacks in the blinds who can 3-bet liberally. A small open accomplishes that: we pick up the blinds often, and we can fold cleanly to significant 3-bets without burning much stack.

I hold 7h 3c on the Button, which is an extremely weak hand (off-suit, unconnected, low cards). The player 'FiveTwo' has raised to 250 from Under-The-Gun (UTG). An UTG opening range is typically strong, and my hand has very poor equity against any reasonable range. Despite having position, calling or 3-betting with such a weak holding is not profitable. I should preserve my stack of 53 BB for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with 7-3 offsuit, which is an extremely weak hand. FiveTwo has raised from UTG. Given the poor strength of my hand and being out of position, there is no reason to call or raise. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

FiveTwo opened to 250 from early position. I’m in the big blind with Th9c — a marginal connected offsuit hand. Pot is 400 and I need to call 150. With deep stacks and a long tournament left, I don’t want to turn this into a high-variance spot by 3-betting bluff OOP against an early-position opener who likely has a reasonably strong range. Calling 150 is cheap to see a flop, realizes equity, and keeps the pot manageable; if I hit (pair, two pair, strong draw) I can apply pressure postflop or extract value, and if I miss I can fold to sizeable aggression. I’ll call and re-evaluate on the flop.

Flop is 7♠-4♠-2♥; I have T♥9♣ — no pair and no realistic draw. The board is dry but with two spades, so opponents can have spade draw/holdings I can't contend with. FiveTwo was the preflop raiser and checked here (uncertain strength: either a controlled/medium hand or a slowplay), so betting OOP as a bluff is high variance against a likely sticky range. With deep stacks and only backdoor, runner-runner straight outs at best, I want pot control and to avoid turning this into a big bluff-or-fold spot. Plan: check and fold to sizable aggression unless I improve; if checked to me on later streets I can evaluate a delayed bluff or value bet if I pair up.

Heads-up vs the BB, this 7♠4♠2h flop is one where I retain a meaningful range advantage (overpairs, A7s/A4s, some strong suited broadways) even though BB connects with a lot of 7x/4x. With Ah6s I have ace-high plus some backdoor improvement (runner-runner spades, occasional wheel runouts), and a small c-bet will fold out a chunk of BB’s air/overcards while keeping the pot controlled versus a player who can check-raise strong. If I get raised, I can fold cleanly; if called, I’ll re-evaluate turn cards (barrel some good turns like A/6/5/spade; otherwise often take a check-back line).

Fold. On 7♠-4♠-2♥ holding T♥9♣ I have no pair, no spade and only weak runner-runner outs. After FiveTwo’s 200 bet the pot will be ~950, so calling 200 requires ≈21% equity. UTG’s c-bet range is weighted toward made pairs and spade combos where my equity is well below the break-even point. A bluff raise OOP is high-variance and unnecessary with deep stacks and many hands left. Preserve stack and fold.

I am in UTG position (effectively Cutoff in 4-handed play) with a stack of 53 BBs. My hand, Ace-Ten offsuit, is well within the standard opening range for 4-handed play, as it blocks other Aces and dominates many broadway hands that opponents might defend. Folding would be too passive given the hand strength and short-handed table, and limping invites aggression from the larger stacks behind me. A standard raise to 2.5 BB (250 chips) allows me to take the initiative, potentially win the blinds immediately, or play a pot in position against the blinds if called.

I am on the button with 7-2 offsuit, which is the weakest possible starting hand. Pro has opened with a raise to 250 from UTG. Given my significant chip lead and the poor quality of my cards, there is no strategic reason to call or raise. I will fold and wait for a better hand to protect my stack.

I’m in the small blind with A♣Q♦ facing an UTG open to 250 (pot 400). This is a strong hand that plays well as a 3-bet: I can get value from worse Ax and broadways, gain fold equity against marginal opens, and avoid a multiway pot where AQs loses equity. Calling 200 keeps me out of position and is passive; a min-raise to 400 is too small and invites callers. I have a big stack (≈19225) and Pro (opener) is relatively short (≈5075), so a sized 3-bet will often isolate or take the pot now. I’ll raise to 1000 (a ~4x open) — large enough to pressure weaker opens and limit multiway action while leaving plenty of chips behind to play postflop or fold to a very strong 4-bet/shove if necessary.

Facing an UTG open and a strong SB 3-bet to 10bb, K8o is far too dominated and plays terribly out of position (often making second-best top pair). The price is 900 to win ~2250 (~40% required), but our equity versus typical SB 3-bet + UTG continue ranges is nowhere near that, and 4-bet bluffing with K8o is a poor candidate given stack sizes and likelihood of getting called/jammed. Preserve stack and wait for a better spot.

I opened ATo from UTG (Cutoff equivalent in 4-handed) and faced a large 3-bet to 1000 (4x my raise) from the Small Blind. While I would have position post-flop, ATo is easily dominated by the typical 3-betting range (AQ, AK, TT+, AJs) and plays poorly in 3-bet pots due to reverse implied odds. The price to call requires about 36% equity, which is marginal against a strong range, and the large sizing denies me profitable odds to peel. Folding preserves my healthy 50bb stack for better spots.

I am UTG in a 4-handed game with Ac3c. In a short-handed format, a suited Ace is a strong opening hand from any position. Opening with a raise allows me to take control of the pot and potentially win it pre-flop, or set up a post-flop play with nut-flush potential. I will raise to 300, which has been my standard opening size.

Facing an UTG open to 300, Kc6c is a marginal, often-dominated hand vs an UTG raising range. Pot is 450 and calling 300 requires roughly ~40% equity to be profitable vs a single caller; against an UTG range (and with likely action from the blinds) K6c has much less equity and poor implied odds. A min-raise to 500 would be a weak bluff with limited fold equity against a deep-stacked opener (Flash) and risks a 3-bet or multiway pot where this hand performs poorly. I preserve my stack for better, higher-expected-value spots — fold preflop.

4-handed, UTG open is relatively wide and K9o is too strong to just fold getting a good price, but flatting in the SB invites the BB in and leaves us playing dominated top pairs out of position. I’ll take the aggressive line and 3-bet as a blocker/semi-bluff to leverage fold equity versus Flash’s wide open and to deny the BB an easy call. If Flash 4-bets, we can comfortably fold K9o this deep.

I am in the Big Blind with K6o. Flash opened from UTG to 300, and FiveTwo 3-bet from the Small Blind to 1100. This action indicates significant strength from both opponents. My hand is dominated by the ranges of a UTG opener and an SB 3-bettor. Additionally, Flash is still to act behind me and could 4-bet. Calling would commit a significant portion of my stack (1000 chips out of 4975) with a hand that has very poor equity in a multi-way 3-bet pot. This is a clear fold.

I opened UTG with Ac3c, a strong suited ace in a 4-handed game. FiveTwo, who has been active and aggressive throughout the tournament, 3-bet to 1100 from the Small Blind. FiveTwo has a relatively short stack of 6400 (about 64 big blinds). I am getting decent pot odds to call (800 more into a pot that will be 2300) and I will have the advantage of position for the rest of the hand. While Ac3c can be dominated by stronger Aces in a 3-betting range, its suited nature and the potential to flop the nut flush or a wheel draw make it worth a call given the stack sizes and my chip lead. I will call to see a flop and re-evaluate based on the board texture and FiveTwo's post-flop aggression.

3-bet pot, heads-up, low SPR (~2.8). This Q-T-9 two-tone board connects well with Flash’s call range (Qx, JT, KJ, draws), so we don’t want to bloat the pot with a big c-bet. With K9 we have decent equity (pair + gutshot to J) and some showdown value, but we also don’t want to give a totally free card to his overcards/heart draws. Best is a small range c-bet to deny equity and set a reasonable price to continue, while keeping our decision simple versus aggression.

The flop texture of Th-9h-Qs is extremely coordinated and hits many of the hands in FiveTwo's pre-flop 3-betting range, such as overpairs, top pairs, or straight draws like KJ or J10. Holding only Ace-high (Ac3c) with no direct draws or backdoor flush possibilities on this board, I have very little equity to continue. FiveTwo's bet of 600 into 2300 is small, but given the strength of their range on this board and the fact that I have completely missed, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my chip lead.

UTG with J♠3♥ — a weak, unsuited, low-playability hand. From early position I should be opening a much stronger range; calling 100 to see a flop or raising with this hand will routinely put me in tough, dominated spots versus button/SB/BB ranges. I have a deep stack and no reason to gamble with a negative-EV holding here — preserve chips and wait for a better spot.

Button spot after UTG folds is normally a wide-steal, but 72s is at the very bottom of playable holdings and performs poorly when either blind continues. With Flash deep in the BB (likely to defend/3-bet often) and Pro in the SB (shorter, incentivized to fight for pots), our fold equity isn’t high enough to justify opening this hand, and limping just invites an isolation raise where we’re forced to give up. Best to preserve chips and take better steal spots.

The action is folded to me in the Small Blind. I hold T8o (Ten of Clubs, Eight of Spades). The blinds are 50/100 and there are likely no antes given the pot size of 150. My stack is roughly 49 BBs, but I am the shortest stack at the table compared to my opponents, particularly the Big Blind (Flash) who has over 260 BBs. In a structure without antes, the pot odds and incentive to steal or play from the Small Blind are reduced. T8o is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position, especially against a deep-stacked and aggressive opponent who can apply pressure. Standard deep-stack blind-vs-blind ranges typically fold T8o from the Small Blind when there is no ante. Preserving chips is preferable to playing a weak hand out of position.

4-handed UTG plays much wider than full ring, and ATo is comfortably ahead of the blinds’ defending ranges and has good blocker value versus strong Ax. With no raise in front, I want to take initiative, isolate the weaker blinds, and apply pressure to Pro’s shorter 49bb stack on the Button. I’ll use a relatively small open to keep my risk down while still generating folds and building a pot when called.

I am holding Q4 offsuit on the Button facing a UTG raise from FiveTwo. Q4o is a very weak hand with poor playability and equity against a standard UTG opening range. Although I have position, the gap between my hand strength and the raiser's likely range is too large to justify a call or a bluff 3-bet. Folding is the only prudent decision to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with 3d 4s, which is a very weak starting hand. FiveTwo has raised from UTG. Even though I have the largest stack at the table, calling a raise with 4-3 offsuit out of position is a significantly negative expected value play. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

UTG opened to 250 (2.5x). I’m on the BB with K♠8♦ — a marginal hand out of position. I have deep stacks (≈195bb vs his ≈84bb) so there’s room to realize equity, but I don’t want to inflate the pot preflop with a weak offsuit K. A 3‑bet bluff with K8o is thin (not enough blockers and difficult to play OOP if called or 4‑bet). Calling costs 150 more (250 total) for a pot of 400 — about 2.7:1 immediate pot odds — which is reasonable to see a flop and keep options open. Plan: call, fold to a sizable 3‑bet, and on the flop only continue with middle/top pair, strong redraws, or a clear equity realization line; otherwise control the pot and preserve my deep stack.

Flop 2♥-5♦-5♥ and I hold K♠8♦ — no pair, no meaningful draws. Pot is only 550 and both stacks are deep (I ~194bb, villain ~84bb), so there’s no reason to inflate the pot out of position with air. My preflop plan was to realize equity or fold to aggression; on this dry paired board I don’t meet the criteria to barreI (no middle/top pair or strong redraw). I’ll check to control the pot, preserve my deep stack, and fold to a meaningful c-bet (only continue if I pair the K, get a clear improvement, or the opponent gives a very weak / tiny probe).

Heads-up versus the BB with position after opening preflop. On a paired 5-5-2 flop, my range still contains all the big overpairs and strong Ax, while BB has a lot of random overcards that will just fold to a small c-bet. With A♠T♣ I also block the nut-heart draws less, but I do hold the A♠ which reduces some strong continuing combos and gives me good turn equity (A/T).
