I’ll use a small continuation bet to deny equity, pick up the pot often, and keep risk controlled if BB continues or check-raises.

Preflop I called 250 with K♠8♦ intending to realize equity, but on the 2♥-5♦-5♥ flop I have no pair and no meaningful draws. Villain opened UTG preflop (a relatively strong range) and barreled a small 200 into a ~750 pot. My stated plan was to check and fold to a c-bet unless I improved — I didn’t. Although the bet is small, I don’t want to play a bloated OOP pot with air against an early-position range that often has a pair or better. Preserving my stack and waiting for a better spot is the +EV line here.

I am in UTG position with King-Deuce offsuit (K2o), which is a very weak hand. With three opponents left to act, including two large stacks, the probability of being dominated or facing a re-raise is high. My stack of ~49 BBs allows me to wait for better spots. There is no strategic merit in opening this hand from this position.

I am the chip leader with a significant margin. Holding Ten-Six offsuit on the button is a weak hand. While it's a potential stealing position, both Minni and FiveTwo are capable of defending or re-raising. With a stack of 267 tokens and only 32 hands remaining in the tournament, there is no need to play speculative hands and risk chips against the blinds. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here.

Only the big blind remains — I'm effectively heads-up. A♣T♦ is a strong heads-up opener and I have positional advantage (I act first preflop but will be last postflop). I want initiative and fold equity rather than just completing to 100, and a min-raise (200) is too small and invites cheap flops and wide defenses. I raise to 300 total (3x the BB) to charge calling hands, isolate the big blind, and play postflop with position. If FiveTwo 3-bets big I can fold ATo or proceed depending on sizing; if he calls I can use position to continue.

Minni is opening the small blind very frequently, so we should defend the big blind quite wide. A♠8♦ has good raw equity versus a typical SB steal range and we’re getting a very good price (200 to win 400). While a 3-bet is possible, A8o plays awkwardly when called (dominated by better Ax, reverse implied odds) and we don’t want to inflate the pot out of position with a middling offsuit ace. Calling keeps ranges wide, realizes equity, and lets us navigate postflop based on texture and Minni’s continuation tendencies.

I was the preflop aggressor vs the BB (they called a 3x). Flop 5c-6h-8c is relatively dry/low and misses a lot of a BB’s defending range (many broadways, suited connectors and weak pairs). I hold A♣T♦ — two overcards and a backdoor club possibility but no made hand. As the preflop raiser I have initiative and decent fold equity, so a continuation bet is the default play to take down the pot and to charge any draws. I size to ~60% pot (360 into 600): that price (~360 to win 1320 → ~27% threshold) forces marginal hands to fold and makes calling with draws or single-pair hands costly. Plan if called: proceed cautiously — continue only with improvement (Ace/Ten) or on very favorable turn cards, and fold to a substantial check‑raise (which usually represents sets/straights on this board).

We flop top pair (8s) with the best kicker (A♠) on a very dynamic texture (5c-6h-8c) where Minni’s SB steal/c-bet range is wide and contains lots of worse one-pair hands plus straight/flush draws. Calling keeps bluffs in, but it also lets a large chunk of her range realize equity (clubs, 97/74, etc.) and puts us in tough spots on many turns (club/7/9/4). Raising takes initiative OOP, gets value from worse 8x/6x/5x and draws, and forces incorrect prices for the drawing portion of her range while still being small enough to keep dominated hands continuing.

I have A♣T♦ on a 5♣-6♥-8♣ board: two overcards and a backdoor club draw. I was the preflop aggressor and c‑bet 360; FiveTwo’s check‑raise to 1250 is a strong line (sets, made straights, or big two‑card combos) but also contains semi‑bluffs (club combos, floats). Calling costs 890 to win a 2210 pot (~28.7% break‑even). My equity to hit a pair by river is roughly 24% and runner‑runner club possibilities push my realistic equity into the high‑20s — borderline with the pot odds, but I have position. Re‑raising as a bluff with only Ace‑high is too thin and folding surrenders too much equity. With a deep stack (18715) I can call, use position to see the turn, and fold to clear strength if I don’t improve; if I pair an Ace/Ten or pick up a club I can proceed for value. Therefore I will call the raise (total 1250).

FiveTwo’s large flop check‑raise (1250) strongly pointed to a very strong made hand (sets like 55/66/88 or flopped straights such as 4+7 or 7+9) or a polarized semi‑bluff with two clubs. I called the flop to use position and to see a turn because I had two overcards and backdoor club equity. The turn 7♣ gives me A♣T♦: I still don’t have a made hand, but I now have a four‑club combination (three clubs on board + Ac) so one club on the river completes a flush (~9 club outs). I also have 3 remaining aces and 3 remaining tens (some overlap with club outs), so my chance to improve to a potentially winning hand by the river is roughly ~30%. The pot is 3,100 and it’s checked to me on the turn. Betting here would bloat the pot and commit chips against a range that likely contains many hands that currently beat me; it would also get called by many marginal hands and punished by raises from sets/straights or made flushes. Given my earlier plan (only continue with clear improvement or a strong draw), the modest improvement on this turn and the fact that there is no bet to call means the best line is pot control: check and realize equity for a free river card. If I hit a club or pair on the river I can bet/raise for value; if I miss I’ll be prepared to fold to significant aggression. Action: check to keep the pot manageable and preserve options on the river.

We raised flop for value/protection on a very dynamic board, but the 7c is a major texture-shifter: it completes the most common straight draws (9x and 4x) and also completes flushes for any hand that continued with two clubs. When Minni checks, her range is a mix of (1) traps/strong made hands, (2) medium pairs like 8x/6x/5x that don’t love this card, and (3) give-ups.

With A♠8♦ (one pair, no club), we don’t want to polarize with a big barrel and get put in a miserable spot versus a check-raise. But we also shouldn’t always check and give free rivers to hands that can improve or bluff us off the best hand. A small turn bet functions as thin value + a block: it gets called by worse 8x/6x/5x often enough, folds out pure air, and keeps our loss small when she springs a check-raise (which we can comfortably fold to on this card).

Board: 5c-6h-8c-7c. I hold AcTd — nut-flush draw plus two overcards. Unique outs: 9 clubs + 3 aces + 2 remaining tens (Tc counted among clubs) = 14 outs → 14/46 ≈ 30.4% to improve on the river. Pot is 4200 and the bet to me is 1100, so my break-even equity is ~1100/(4200+1100) ≈ 20.8%. Equity (≈30%) > required (≈20.8%) so this is a +EV call. Opponent’s flop check‑raise and turn lead show a strong/polarized range (sets/straights or club semi‑bluffs), but with position and the sizable draw I should see the river rather than folding or over‑committing with a bluff raise. Plan: call to realize equity; if I hit a club or pair Ace/Ten on the river I can extract value, if I completely miss I’ll fold to significant river aggression.

I check. I hold A♣T♦ on 5♣-6♥-8♣-7♣-K♠ — no made hand, just Ace‑high after missing the club flush on the river. FiveTwo’s line (call preflop, large flop check‑raise, turn bet) is strongly polarized toward very strong made hands (sets, straights or club flushes) or the rare committed semibluff. He checked the river, which can be pot‑control or a trap, but it’s not a clear sign of weakness given his earlier aggression. Betting as a bluff here is thin — we have limited fold equity against that range and would only get value from worse hands rarely. Checking keeps the pot small, realizes showdown for free when he was bluffing or checked back with a marginal hand, and preserves chips to exploit later spots. If he bets now I’ll fold to meaningful sizing since Ace‑high is unlikely to be best.

River (5c-6h-8c-7c-Ks) is extremely unfavorable for a thin value bet: the turn already completed most straight draws (9x/4x) and any 2-club floats are now flushes, and the K river also improves some hands that continued (e.g., K8 to two pair). After Minni check-calls our flop raise and turn bet, her range is heavily weighted toward made hands that beat one-pair 8x, and the portion we beat (worse 8x/occasional 6x/5x) is unlikely to both reach river and then pay off on this scary runout. Betting mostly gets called/raised by better and folds out worse, while checking realizes our showdown value and avoids getting blown off our hand by a check-raise.

So we take the free showdown.

I am holding pocket Jacks (Jh, Jd) in the UTG position (effectively the Cutoff) in a 4-handed game. This is a premium starting hand. Being the chip leader with a stack of 267 big blinds, I want to play aggressively to maximize value. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate here to build the pot, define my opponents' ranges, and potentially isolate the short stack (Pro). I am comfortable calling a shove from Pro or playing against the other two stacks post-flop.

4♠7♠ on the button vs an UTG open to 3bb (300). This is a marginal but playable suited hand — good postflop playability and strong blockers to some backdoor combinations. With deep effective stacks and position I prefer to keep the pot small and see a flop rather than risk a small isolation raise that could bring a 3-bet from a stronger UTG range. I will call 300 to play in position; plan to continue with strong draws or pairs and fold to heavy preflop 3-bets or large turn/river aggression when I miss.

We’re in the small blind facing a 3bb open from Flash and a flat from Minni, holding 8♣3♦ offsuit. This hand has very poor playability multiway and will be dominated whenever we make a pair. Calling creates a tough out-of-position spot versus two likely wide-but-strong-enough ranges, and squeezing is too ambitious with this blockerless trash—especially with Pro in the big blind short enough to reshove and punish a light squeeze. Best EV is to preserve stack and fold.

I hold Pocket Aces (Ah Ad) in the Big Blind, the strongest starting hand. Facing a raise from UTG and a call from the Button, I have an excellent opportunity to 3-bet (squeeze) for value. Calling would allow a multi-way pot out of position, which is risky. Shoving my entire stack of 4825 (48 BBs) into a pot of 750 is an overbet that likely folds out hands I want action from. A 3-bet to 1200 is appropriate; it is 4x the initial raise, accounts for the caller and my positional disadvantage, and sets up an SPR of ~1.1 for the flop, allowing me to easily commit the rest of my chips on most boards.

I am holding pocket Jacks, a premium hand in a 4-handed game. Pro, the short stack in the Big Blind, has 3-bet to 1,200, which represents nearly one-third of their remaining stack. This indicates significant strength but also suggests they are likely committed to the hand. Minni has already called my initial raise. To maximize my chances of winning and to isolate Pro, I will raise to 3,725, effectively putting Pro all-in. This move forces Minni to fold any marginal hands and allows me to play a heads-up pot against the short stack with a very strong hand. As the chip leader, I can afford to take this high-equity spot to eliminate an opponent or further increase my lead.

I already cold‑called 300 on the button with 4♠7♠. Pro squeezed to 1200 and Flash 4‑bet to 3725, leaving me an extra 3425 to call into a 5275 pot. Calling would require ~28% equity (~2.54:1 pot odds) against two very strong, 3‑bet/4‑bet ranges (UTG open then 4‑bet and a squeeze), and 4♠7♠ has very poor equity and is easily dominated. Raising would be a large, thin bluff that commits too much of my stack with a marginal hand. I have position, but the price and the opponents’ line make this a clear fold — preserve chips and wait for a better spot on the button.

I hold Pocket Aces (Ah Ad), the strongest starting hand in Hold'em. After 3-betting to 1200, the original raiser (Flash) has 4-bet to 3725. This raise commits most of my stack, as I started the hand with 4925 and have 3725 remaining. Calling would leave me with only 1200 chips in a pot of roughly 7800, creating an awkward SPR of less than 0.2. With the best hand, my goal is to get all chips in pre-flop to maximize value against likely strong holdings like KK, QQ, or AK. I will 5-bet jam (raise all-in) to 4925.

I am holding pocket Jacks (Jh, Jd) and have already committed 3,725 chips to the pot. Pro, the short stack, has moved all-in for a total of 4,925 chips. This requires me to call an additional 1,200 chips into a pot that will be approximately 10,150 chips. Given the strength of pocket Jacks in a 4-handed game and the exceptional pot odds I am receiving (calling 1,200 to win a total pot of over 10,000), this is an automatic call. Even if Pro has a higher pair or Ace-King, I have the necessary equity and pot odds to justify the call.

UTG with 8♥5♣ offsuit in a 4‑handed game is a very weak holding. Out of position against the button, small blind and a deep big blind, this hand has poor equity, lots of reverse implied-odds and is frequently dominated. Calling 100 to see a flop or opening with it would put me in marginal spots postflop out of position against multiple players. I have a healthy stack (16425) so there’s no need to risk chips with such a hand — wait for a better spot in position or with real playability. Fold preflop.

Button vs blinds 3-handed (UTG folded) is a prime steal spot with 150 dead in the pot. Even though T2o is near the bottom of my opening range, position lets me realize equity and apply pressure postflop, and I can comfortably fold to a 3-bet. I’ll use the minimum raise size to risk less while still forcing both blinds to respond and to keep my range wide.

I am in the Small Blind with Pocket Deuces (2s, 2c), the lowest pair in the game. We are 4-handed, and the Button (FiveTwo) has opened with a min-raise to 200. Although the price to call is excellent (150 chips to win 350, offering nearly 3 to 1 pot odds), playing 22 out of position is notoriously difficult. Without hitting a set (approx. 12% chance), I will likely face overcards on the flop and be forced to fold to a continuation bet. As the current short stack (though still deep with ~100BB), chip preservation is important, and bleeding chips in spot where I cannot easily realize my equity is suboptimal. The Big Blind is also yet to act and could squeeze or call, making the pot multi-way where 22 performs poorly. The disciplined play is to fold and wait for a better spot or a hand with better playability.

I am in the Big Blind with 6c3d. The button (FiveTwo) has raised to 200, making it 100 more for me to call. While 63o is a very weak hand, I'm getting 2.5:1 odds on a call in a tournament where stack preservation and occasional defense are important. However, out of position against an aggressive player like FiveTwo, 63o is rarely profitable even with these odds. Looking at the tournament stage (Hand 120 of 150), I have a comfortable chip lead. I should play conservatively with trash hands. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack advantage.

4-handed, KQs is a clear open from UTG (effectively CO) with good playability and blocker value versus 3-bets. I want to apply pressure to the blinds while keeping the sizing small enough to comfortably respond to a 3-bet from the deeper, aggressive stacks (especially Flash in the SB). A 2.5bb open is a solid standard here.

I am on the Button facing an UTG raise from FiveTwo to 250. My hand is 8d 5s, which is an offsuit, unconnected, low-strength hand. Against an UTG opening range, this hand has very poor equity and playability. There is no justification for calling or 3-betting here. I will fold to preserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with 7s4d, which is a very weak starting hand. FiveTwo has raised from under the gun to 250. This is a standard open from a player who has been active and aggressive. Given my position and the weakness of my hand, calling or raising would be highly unprofitable and would put me in a difficult situation out of position against an aggressive opponent. Folding is the only sensible play here to preserve my stack.

FiveTwo opened UTG to 250; SB and BTN folded so this will be a heads-up pot and I'll be out of position postflop. I hold Qc2h — a weak, offsuit hand that fares poorly against an UTG opening range and has very limited postflop playability. To call I must put in 150 into a 400 pot, which requires ~27% equity to break even; Q2o vs a typical UTG range has considerably less equity. 3-betting as a bluff here is thin and will often get me in a dominated or difficult spot. With deep stacks and plenty of hands left, preservation + waiting for a better spot is the right long-term play. Fold.

I am in UTG position with King-Four offsuit (K4o). This is a weak hand that is typically folded from early position, even in a 4-handed game. With a stack of over 100 big blinds (10,150 chips), I am not under pressure to make loose moves to survive. Opening here risks bleeding chips or getting isolated by better hands while out of position. The standard and most profitable play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with 9-6 offsuit. While this is a good position to attempt a steal, 9-6 offsuit is a very weak hand to open with, especially against aggressive opponents in the blinds like Minni and FiveTwo who have shown a willingness to defend and 3-bet recently. Given my chip lead, there is no need to take unnecessary risks with such a low-equity hand.

AQo in the small blind heads-up vs the big blind is a clear open — strong hand with good equity and high blocker value. With UTG and the button folded I want to take the initiative, deny the BB good odds to see multi-card runs with junk, and build a pot when I’m likely ahead. I will open to a standard 3x BB (300 total) rather than the minimum raise to 200 to get better fold equity and discourage loose calls/3-bets. If I face a moderate 3-bet I can call or 4-bet depending on sizing and my read; I’ll proceed cautiously postflop out of position and c-bet favorable textures.

Minni is raising from the small blind in 4-handed play, which is typically a very wide opening range. Ks7s is a strong enough suited king to defend, and it performs well as a light 3-bet because it blocks stronger Kx hands, can flop top pair with good backdoors, and has solid playability when called. With deep-ish stacks, I prefer applying pressure and taking initiative rather than flatting out of position. If Minni 4-bets, we can comfortably fold; if called, we proceed with range advantage and c-bet on favorable boards.

I opened the SB to 300 with A♣Q♥ and the BB responded with a standard, moderately-sized 3-bet to 1000. AQo is a strong hand here (ace-blocker, good equity) and I don’t want to just fold; calling is viable (700 into 1300 gives ~26% pot-odds), but calling leaves me out of position with the initiative surrendered. A tiny 4-bet to 1700 would be too cheap and called too often — I want a size that gets medium pairs and speculative hands to fold while still leaving room to play postflop if called. I’ll 4-bet to ~3x the 3-bet (3000 total) to buy fold equity, isolate the BB, and take the initiative. If FiveTwo folds I scoop the pot; if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously OOP (c-bet favorable A/Q-high textures, pot-control on wet boards). If he jams a polarized 5-bet I will fold this holding absent a very specific read, since AQo doesn’t have the equity against a shove range.

We 3-bet K♠7♠ as a light, blocker/playability hand versus a wide SB open, but once Minni 4-bets to 3000 the game tree changes: her range tightens significantly and we’re out of position with an awkward SPR if we call (~1.3). Calling needs ~33% equity and good realization; versus a typical value-heavy 4-bet range (QQ+/AK with maybe some A5s bluffs), K7s is too dominated and will often put us in “top-pair-but-kicked” spots where we lose stacks. A 5-bet jam isn’t attractive either because the price she’d get to call is very good, so we don’t generate enough fold equity. This was planned as a 3-bet/fold line, so we take the disciplined fold and preserve the stack for better spots.

In a 4-handed game, Ace-Trey offsuit is a standard opening hand from the Under-the-Gun position (which is effectively the Cutoff). Although FiveTwo has been playing aggressively recently, I should maintain my standard opening range to avoid being exploited for being too tight. If I face a significant 3-bet, I can comfortably fold this marginal hand, but for now, I will raise to 300.

Button facing an UTG open to 300 with Kc2c. K2s is weak and often dominated by an early-position raiser, but it’s suited and we have maximum positional advantage. Calling 300 is small relative to our 17k stack and lets us see a flop in position with flush/king potential; a min 3-bet here is a poor bluff vs an UTG range and a larger 3-bet would be too speculative with this hand. Plan: call to see the flop, fold to significant preflop 3-bets, and continue postflop only with club equity, a pair, or good board texture — otherwise cede the pot to aggression.

We’re in the small blind facing an UTG open (300) plus a button flat, with the big blind still to act behind. 9♠7♦ offsuit is too weak to defend here: it’s dominated often, lacks suitedness, and plays poorly out of position in a likely multiway pot (or we risk getting squeezed by the BB). Even with decent pot odds to complete, the postflop realization is bad, so this is a disciplined fold to preserve stack and avoid reverse-implied-odds spots.

I am in the Big Blind with [Td 6h] facing a raise from Flash (UTG) to 300 and a call from Minni (Button). The pot is 750 and I need to call 200 to see the flop. My pot odds are approximately 21% (200/950). However, T6o is a very weak hand with poor playability, especially out of position against two opponents in a multi-way pot. The likelihood of realizing my equity is low, and I face significant reverse implied odds if I hit a marginal pair. Despite the attractive direct price, defending this hand is -EV in the long run. I will fold and preserve my stack.

On this 8s-9s-3d flop, I have bottom pair with an Ace kicker. This is a marginal hand that doesn't want to build a large pot, especially since the board is quite coordinated (straight and flush possibilities) and hits a button-calling range well. By checking, I can control the pot size and see how Minni reacts. If Minni bets small, I can consider a call, but if the action gets heavy, bottom pair is easily discardable.

Check. On 8s-9s-3d with Kc2c I have only K-high and no realistic draws. Because the flop has zero clubs and I hold both clubs, it’s impossible to make a five-card club flush by the river (only two cards remain), so my preflop suited equity is dead. I have only ~6 clean outs (three kings, three deuces) and we’re deep (SPR ≈ 20+), so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand against the UTG raiser. Flash checked the flop — could be weakness or an inducing check — but I’ll conserve chips and check to see a turn. Plan: fold to any meaningful bet and only continue if I pair K or 2 (or see a clear read to bluff later).

On the turn, the Ten of Hearts is added to a fairly coordinated board. I still have bottom pair with an Ace kicker. Given that the flop went check-check, it is unlikely that Minni has a monster, but the Ten hits a lot of cards that a Button caller might have (like Jack-Ten, Queen-Ten, or Ten-Nine). My hand has very little value for betting, and I would likely only be called by better hands. I will check again to keep the pot small and attempt to reach a cheap showdown or fold if facing significant pressure, as bottom pair is not a hand I want to commit many chips with on this texture.

Board is 8♠-9♠-3♦-T♥, I hold K♣2♣. With the turn being a heart (not a club), it’s impossible to make a five-card club flush — my suited equity is dead and I only have 6 clean outs (three kings, three deuces) to improve. Flash (preflop raiser) checked flop and turn heads-up, which looks like pot control or weakness but could also be a cautious line with a pair; my hand has very little showdown value and almost no fold equity for a thin turn bluff. Pot (750) vs my stack (17k) is small, so I should conserve chips and see the river where I still have fold/river-bluff options or can value-bet if I pair. Action: check and fold to any meaningful bet; only continue if I pair or get a clear, favorable river.

On the river, the board is 8s-9s-3d-Th-9c. I hold 3s-Ah, giving me two pair: Nines and Threes with an Ace kicker. While this hand looks decent, it is actually quite fragile in this specific texture. Any pocket pair (44 through 77) has a better two pair (e.g., 9-9-7-7-T), and any player holding an 8 or a 10 also beats me. Since both the flop and turn were checked through, Minni's range likely consists of weak showdown value or missed draws (like spades or Q-J). If I bet, I am unlikely to get called by worse hands, and I might get raised by a 9, a 10, or a slow-played monster. Checking allows me to reach a cheap showdown or potentially catch a bluff from a missed draw. Given the pot size and the board texture, checking is the most prudent action.

I check. I have Kc2c and never improved: only three kings and three deuces (6 clean outs). Because the flop had no clubs and only two cards remained, a five-card club flush was impossible by the river, so my preflop suited equity is dead. Flash (UTG) led preflop and has played very passively postflop (check-check-check), which reads as pot control or a medium-strength made hand (T,9x, pocket pair) that will often call a thin river bluff. With a 750 pot I would need roughly >35% fold equity to profitably bluff a ~400-sized bet — I don’t assign that much given his line. Losing a small extra bet here is immaterial to my stack, but the long-term +EV play is to conserve chips and not bluff marginally; I’ll check and fold to any meaningful river bet.

Four-handed with deep stacks, 8♠6♠ is a playable suited connector — good postflop and worth opening from UTG in a 4-max game. I want the initiative and to deny the blinds good odds to see cheap flops, so I’ll make a standard 3x open to 300 (bigger than the min-raise but not so large it gives away info). Plan: take the lead with continuation bets on favorable boards and use postflop playability to extract value or fold to heavy 3-/4-bet aggression; defend cautiously if I face a large re-raise out of position.

Minni’s UTG open to 3bb is a relatively strong range in 4-handed, and 5♠3♣ offsuit has very poor playability and no useful blockers for a profitable 3-bet bluff. Calling invites tough multiway spots with dominated one-pair holdings and weak realization even in position. Best chip-EV decision is to let it go and wait for better steal/3-bet candidates.

I am in the Small Blind with [Ac Qh] (AQo), a premium hand in a 4-handed game. Minni opened to 300 from UTG. Given my positional disadvantage (OOP) and the strength of my hand, a 3-bet is preferable to a call to define ranges, isolate the opener, and potentially take down the pot pre-flop. In Hand #19, I successfully 3-bet Minni with 99 to 1100, and Minni folded. Minni has shown discipline in folding to 3-bets. Calling invites the Big Blind and leaves me playing a tricky pot out of position. I will 3-bet to 1100 to apply pressure.

Holding K9o in the Big Blind, I am facing an open raise from Minni (UTG) and a 3-bet from Pro (Small Blind). K9o is significantly behind the ranges of both an UTG opener and a Small Blind 3-bettor. With a comfortable chip lead and being only 26 hands away from the end of the tournament, there is no strategic reason to commit chips to a dominated hand out of position. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack.

I opened to 300 UTG with 8♠6♠ and Pro (SB) 3-bet to 1,100. With deep stacks (my 16,725 vs his 8,950) and a moderate 3-bet size, 8♠6♠ has enough post‑flop playability and implied odds to continue. Folding is overly tight here and 4‑betting OOP with this hand would bloat the pot with marginal equity. I’ll call the extra 800 (total 1,100) to see a flop heads‑up, plan to play cautiously OOP: pursue strong draws or made hands, fold to heavy pressure when completely missed, and extract value when we connect. Calling keeps options open and leverages our deep stack for implied odds.

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (AcQh) on an Ace-high board [9c Ad 7c] in a 3-bet pot. Having 3-bet pre-flop from the Small Blind, this board heavily favors my range. My opponent, Minni, has shown a tendency to call down with marginal hands (e.g., Hand #118). Therefore, betting for value is superior to checking. I want to extract value from worse Aces (AJ, AT) and charge flush/straight draws. Although I hold the Ac (blocking the nut flush draw), there are still plenty of combinations Minni can continue with. A continuation bet of roughly 1/3 pot is standard to deny equity and build the pot while keeping my range uncapped.

Heads‑up vs Pro who 3‑bet pre and now c‑bets 750 into a 3,050 pot on 9♣ A♦ 7♣. I hold 8♠6♠ — I have an open‑ended straight draw (need a 5 or 10; 8 outs) with ~31–32% to hit by the river. Calling 750 will make the pot ~4,550, so my required equity is only ~16.5% (750/4,550) — calling is +EV. With deep stacks there are good implied odds if I complete; by contrast raising OOP with just a draw bloats the pot and invites bigger aggression or a shove. I’ll call the 750 to see a turn, plan to extract if I hit (value bet/raise), and fold to heavy turn aggression if I miss and he barrels hard. Therefore: call 750.

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (AcQh) on a board of [9c Ad 7c 4h]. The turn card (4h) is a brick that does not complete any straights and leaves the flush draw open. In Hand #58, Minni showed a tendency to call down with strong top pairs but check back the turn when the aggressor checks. Since I beat the majority of Minni's calling range (AJ, AT, weaker Ax, and flush draws), checking risks giving a free card and missing value. Although I hold the Ac (blocking the nut flush draw), Minni can still have other flush draws or worse Aces. A bet of roughly 45% of the pot builds value and sets up a reasonable river decision while denying equity to floats.

Fold. The turn bet is too large for my draw to continue profitably. Numbers: pot = 5,550, to call = 1,750 → pot after call = 7,300, so required equity ≈ 1,750/7,300 ≈ 24.0%. I have 8 clean straight outs (5s and 10s) = 8/46 ≈ 17.4% to hit the river. My raw equity (≈17.4%) is well below the price (≈24%), so a call is -EV. Opponent profile/line (SB 3‑bet preflop, c-bet on the flop and a second barrel on the turn) is weighted toward Ax/strong pairs and will not fold enough to a raise for me to profitably semi‑bluff; the min‑raise (to 3,500) would overcommit OOP and invite a shove or call with dominating hands. With a healthy stack (15,175) it's correct to preserve chips for better spots rather than paying incorrect odds to chase.

4-handed UTG ranges do widen, but Td5d is still a low-equity suited hand that performs poorly when called/3-bet: it makes dominated tens, weak pairs, and marginal draws that are hard to realize OOP against competent opponents. With ~109bb and no ICM desperation, preserving stack and waiting for better openers is higher EV than trying to force a thin steal into the deeper stacks behind (especially the aggressive big stack in the SB/button dynamics).

I am on the Button with pocket Fours (4s, 4c), a playable hand especially from late position when folded to. With 120 BB effective stacks, I have plenty of room to maneuver. My goal is to steal the blinds or play the pot in position. Folding is too passive for the Button, and limping caps my range. A standard open raise to 2.5 BB (250) applies pressure on the blinds (Flash and Minni). If they call, I can navigate post-flop with position, looking to hit a set or use the initiative to take down the pot on favorable textures.

I am currently the chip leader with 21,925 chips. Pro raised from the button to 250. My hand, Q2o, is extremely weak and plays poorly out of position. Since I am in the small blind and will be out of position for the rest of the hand against the button opener, there is no reason to risk chips with such a marginal hand. I will fold and wait for better opportunities.

I hold 4♥7♦ — a very weak, easily dominated hand. Pro raised to 250 from the button (SB folded) and I'm facing a 150 call into a 400 pot to play out of position. With deep stacks and a small raise size I can expect postflop play to be difficult and my hand has poor equity and playability. No profitable reason to defend here — fold and preserve chips for better spots against this opponent.

I am in UTG position holding 6s2s, which is a very weak hand (low disconnected suited cards). With 121BB, I have plenty of chips and no need to take unnecessary risks from early position. Opening this hand has a very negative expectation given the players left to act behind me. The standard and correct play is to fold.

I am on the button with King-four offsuit in a four-handed game. With Pro folding under the gun, it is only me and the blinds remaining in the hand. Opening from the button with any King is standard in short-handed play to capture the blinds and maintain aggression, especially as the chip leader. I will raise to 300, a standard 3x open. If either blind shows significant resistance (a 3-bet), I can comfortably fold given my weak kicker, but the positional advantage makes the initial raise profitable against their wide defending ranges.

6♦2♥ is the absolute weakest hand — no playability, poor connectivity and easily dominated. Flash has raised to 300 from the button; I’m out of position as the small blind and would face difficult postflop decisions (often against both the button and the big blind). Calling 250 more into a 450 pot with this hand is negative EV and 3‑bet bluffing 150 BB deep is unnecessary and high variance. Preserve my 15,025 stack and fold.

Button open to 3bb is wide; getting a very good price (200 to win 650 after calling). K9o has enough equity vs a BTN range and plays reasonably as a defend, while a 3-bet would bloat the pot OOP with a hand that can be dominated by better Kx and risks facing a 4-bet from the big stack. I’ll take the cheap defend and navigate postflop.

We defended BB vs a wide BTN open and now see an A-high, fairly dry flop (Ah Ts 3d) that strongly favors the preflop raiser’s range advantage (lots of Ax, strong broadways). Our K9o has no pair and no meaningful immediate draw, so leading would mostly fold out only hands we already beat and get called/raised by better. Best line is to check and realize equity; if Flash cbets at high frequency we can respond selectively (mostly fold) and look for better turn cards to probe when checked through.

I am the pre-flop aggressor from the button and the board has come Ace-high and dry (uncoordinated). This board texture heavily favors my range as the button raiser, as I have all the strongest Aces and broadway hands in my range. FiveTwo checked from the big blind, which is a standard defensive play. I will place a continuation bet (C-bet) of approximately 40% of the pot to represent the Ace and attempt to take the pot down immediately. If FiveTwo has a weak Ten or a small pocket pair, this bet puts them in a difficult position, and I can often fold out better King-high hands or air.

Flash’s small c-bet (275 into 650) looks like a high-frequency range bet on an A-high board that favors him, but the sizing gives us a good price: we need ~23% equity. Even though K9o is mostly air here, versus a wide BTN range we still have meaningful equity (overcards to some Tx/3x, and we can improve on K/9; plus turn-heart gives us a real backdoor flush draw, and Q/J can add gutshot equity). Folding everything without a pair vs this sizing would be overfolding and lets him print with range-bets. I’ll defend by calling once and plan to fold to substantial turn pressure on bricks, while continuing on favorable turns (K/9/Q/J/heart) or if he checks and we can realize equity/probe.

Turn is a low brick (Ah Ts 3d 4s) where my actual hand (Kh9d) has very little showdown value and can’t comfortably check/call again versus a big-stack BTN who can barrel. However, this 4 improves BB’s relative range (more 44, A4s, 43s, 52s/straight possibilities) and I can credibly represent those after check/calling flop. A small probe denies equity to his broadway floats and picks up the pot often versus his high-frequency flop c-bets that are now giving up. Plan: bet small; fold to a raise; if called, proceed cautiously and mostly give up on blank rivers unless I improve.

On the turn, I have improved to bottom pair (fours) with a King kicker. However, the board remains very dangerous with both an Ace and a Ten present. My opponent, FiveTwo, has led out with a small 'donk bet' after check-calling the flop. This lead often indicates that the turn card helped them (perhaps they have a hand like 4-5, or they now have a pair with a straight draw) or they are trying to block me from betting larger. While the pot odds are somewhat attractive (calling 500 to win a pot of 1700), my hand is extremely weak in this specific board texture. Calling here likely forces me into a difficult decision on the river if they bet again, and I am currently losing to any Ace, any Ten, and any pocket pair higher than fours. Given my position as the tournament chip leader and the marginal nature of bottom pair on an Ace-high board, the most disciplined play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am dealt 3c 6h in the UTG position. This is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em and provides almost no post-flop equity or playability, especially when acting first in a 4-handed game. Given my healthy stack and the remaining hands in the tournament, there is no strategic reason to open this hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I’m on the button with K♡7♦ — a marginal but playable hand in late position with both blinds left to act and deep stacks (≈150bb). This is a textbook spot to open for value and fold equity: the pot is small relative to our stack, opponents have shown a wide raising/defending frequency previously, and raising lets us isolate or take the pot down preflop. I’ll use a standard 3xBB open (300) which matches Minni’s common sizing in this game — enough fold equity to pick up the blinds and keeps post-flop SPR manageable. Plan: if both fold we take the blinds; if we get calls proceed cautiously on the flop (c-bet dry boards, check/call or pot-control on dangerous textures); fold to a large 3-bet unless it’s from a player who’s been sticky and I have specific reads to continue.

Minni is opening the button wide 4-handed, but 8♦3♦ is still a very weak defend from the small blind: it plays poorly out of position, is often dominated when we make a pair, and can create reverse implied-odds problems even though it’s suited. It’s also not a great 3-bet bluff candidate compared to hands with blockers (Axs/Kxs). With plenty of stack behind, I’d rather preserve chips for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Queen offsuit (KQo) facing a standard Button raise from Minni. With 120BB effective stacks, KQo is a strong hand that dominates much of a wide Button opening range (Kx, Qx, Jx). While a 3-bet to ~1000-1100 is a valid option to isolate and take initiative, it risks bloating the pot out of position against a range that might only continue with hands that flip with or dominate me (AK, AQ, pairs). Calling is a lower-variance play that keeps Minni's range wide, offers excellent pot odds (calling 200 to win 450), and allows me to realize equity without over-committing pre-flop. I recently 3-bet Minni in Hand 124, so flatting here balances my defending frequency.

I am in the Big Blind with QdKs on a flop of Tc-2s-8h. The pot is 650. This texture is relatively dry but connects somewhat with a Button opening range (AT, KT, QT, JT, pairs). As the pre-flop caller out of position, checking to the aggressor is the standard line. I have two overcards and some backdoor straight potential, but no made hand. Donk betting here is unnecessary and likely unprofitable. I will check and evaluate based on Minni's action.

I opened to 300 from the button with K♥7♦ and got one caller in the big blind. The flop T♣ 2♠ 8♥ is very dry and misses most of the caller’s likely range. As the preflop aggressor this is a textbook spot for a small continuation bet to collect the pot now or to charge weak pairs/overcards while keeping the SPR low with a marginal hand. The BB has shown a tendency to check/call postflop rather than frequently raise, so a ~1/3 pot c-bet should pick up many hands or get called by worse. Plan: bet small to seize the initiative; fold to a large raise and proceed cautiously if called (barrel only on safe/beneficial turn cards or with improved equity).

In the Big Blind with QdKs facing a 250 chip continuation bet (approx 38% pot) from Minni on the Button. The board is Tc-2s-8h. I have two overcards and runner-runner straight potential. Against a wide Button opening and c-betting range, my hand retains significant equity (likely 35-45% against a value range, and I am ahead of pure bluffs). The pot odds required to call are roughly 22% (250 to win 1150). Folding KQ high here would be over-folding against this sizing. Check-raising is an option but risks isolating myself against top pair or better. Floating the flop to re-evaluate on the turn is the most standard and EV+ line given the price and stack depth.

I am in the Big Blind with QdKs. The board is Tc-2s-8h-6d. I checked and called a small bet on the flop with two overcards. The turn 6d connects somewhat with the board (bringing straight possibilities like 97 or 75) but does not help my hand directly, leaving me with just King-high. My overcards have some equity, but I am out of position and likely behind any pair or Ace-high. Leading out here (donk betting) is not advisable as the turn card favors the aggressor's range or changes little. I will check to the raiser and evaluate their action. If they check back, I get a free river to try and hit a King or Queen. If they bet, I will likely have to fold given I have no draw and only overcards.

Turn card (6♦) is a clean, non-threatening blank — it doesn’t give me a pair or a draw with K♥7♦. I was the preflop aggressor and c-bet the dry T♣-2♠-8♥ flop; Pro’s cold call to that bet suggests he has at least a pair, a decent pocket, or a sticky draw/float. I have very little equity and no backdoor draw (only a single heart) and don’t block many of Pro’s value combos. Betting the turn would bloat the pot with a hand that will be behind most of Villain’s calling range and will get called by worse or shoved on. The prudent line is pot control: check and realize showdown value (if any), while keeping options to bluff the river only if Villain shows weakness. If Villain leads into me on the turn, I’ll fold to a sizable bet; if he checks back we get to a cheap river with a capped range.

On the river, the board is [Tc 2s 8h 6d 2d]. The turn went check-check. I have King-high (QdKs). My opponent's range likely consists of marginal made hands (weak pairs, Ace-high) checking for showdown, or missed draws (straight draws) giving up. My hand beats all missed draws and loses to all pairs and Ace-highs. Betting to bluff out Ace-high or small pairs is risky as opponents often station on paired boards after a checked turn. Checking allows me to realize my showdown value against her air/missed draws for free. If she bets, I can easily fold as she likely has value. Therefore, checking is the highest EV play.

Check. I never improved — K7 on T‑2‑8‑6‑2 is just K‑high. I was the preflop aggressor and c‑bet the dry flop, and Pro cold‑called that bet then checked the turn; that line and his table history (frequent check/calls) strongly indicates at least a pair or another made hand. A river bluff here has very little fold equity and will be called by most of his range, so betting would bloat a pot I’m almost certain to lose. I’ll check to realize showdown (and fold to any sizeable river shove).

UTG in a 4-handed pot with 144 BB deep. Tc6s is a weak, offsuit, disconnected hand with poor playability out of position against three players to act. Limping or a tiny open invites aggression and 3-bets from the deep, sticky blinds (especially the big stack Flash), and I’d rather preserve my stack for a better spot. Fold and wait for a stronger UTG/late opportunity.

4-handed on the button after UTG folds, T9o has good playability in position and benefits a lot from stealing blinds and realizing equity. The blinds are fairly aggressive (especially Flash), so I prefer a smaller, standard open that keeps my risk down while still applying pressure and allowing me to defend versus some 3-bets and play postflop in position. I’ll open-raise rather than limp/call to maximize fold equity and initiative.

I am in the Small Blind with J6o, facing a standard 2.5x raise from the Button (FiveTwo). J6o is a very weak hand with poor playability and is easily dominated. Being out of position against the opener makes this hand even harder to play profitably. The pot odds do not justify a call given these factors. Defending here would be a long-term losing play. Therefore, the best action is to fold.

Q3 offsuit is a very weak hand to defend from the Big Blind against a button raise. Even though I have favorable pot odds, the hand realizes equity poorly out of position. Given my healthy stack and the late stage of the tournament, I prefer to wait for better spots rather than playing a marginal hand out of position.

4-handed UTG is effectively CO, so A7s is a profitable open: it has decent high-card value, plays well postflop with nut-flush potential, and blocks strong Ax continues from the blinds. I don’t want to limp and invite the button/SB to iso with position. A 2.5bb open pressures the blinds while keeping risk controlled versus aggressive 3-bettors.

I hold Pocket Queens (Qc Qh) on the Button, which is a premium hand. The UTG player (FiveTwo) has opened to 250. With only 4 players left, QQs are extremely strong. I want to build the pot immediately for value and isolate the opener, discouraging the blinds from entering the pot cheaply. A flat call risks a multi-way pot where high cards can hurt my hand. Based on previous play (e.g., Hand #119 and #124), a 3-bet size of roughly 3.5x the open is standard and effective here. Raising to 850 puts pressure on the opener while keeping the range strong.

I have pocket Kings in the Small Blind, which is the second-best starting hand in poker. There is an initial open from FiveTwo and a 3-bet from Pro on the Button. This is a classic spot for a cold 4-bet for value. Calling would put me out of position in a multi-way pot and allow the Big Blind a cheap entry. By raising to 2500, I take control of the hand, protect my equity, and build a pot against what are likely strong ranges from my opponents. If either opponent shoves, I am never folding.

I’m on the big blind with 9♦3♦ facing three-way preflop aggression: UTG opened to 250, Button 3-bet to 850 and SB 4-bet to 2500. The pot is 3700 and it costs me 2400 to call — I’d need roughly 2400/(3700+2400) ≈ 39% equity to break even. 9-3 suited has very poor equity and playability multiway, is frequently dominated by the opponents’ ranges (UTG open and a button 3-bet are both strong; the SB 4-bet polarizes to very strong hands or a shove), and raising here would be reckless. There’s no reason to invest ~24 BB with a marginal rag hand in a four-way pot; preserve the stack and wait for a better spot.

We opened A7s in a good spot 4-handed, but the sequence BTN 3-bet (to 850) then SB cold 4-bet (to 2500) is a very strong range in practice. Calling 2250 creates a low SPR pot (~1.5) out of position where A7s is dominated by the Ax value region (AK/AQ) and we’ll frequently be forced into bad stack-off decisions when we make a pair. Turning this into a 5-bet jam bluff needs an unrealistic amount of fold equity (we’re risking ~11k to win ~3.7k) and when called our equity is poor. Best chip-EV is to let it go and preserve our stack for better spots.

I hold Pocket Queens on the Button. After my 3-bet to 850, Flash in the Small Blind has cold 4-bet to 2500. This demonstrates significant strength, likely a range of TT+, AK, and potentially AQs. In a 4-handed game, QQ is too strong to fold. However, 5-bet shoving over 100BB might isolate me against AA/KK which crush me, or AK which is a flip. Calling is the superior option here: I get good pot odds (calling 1650 to win a pot that will be 5350, needing ~31% equity), I retain position post-flop, and I keep Flash's range wider, including hands I dominate like JJ/TT. I will call to see a flop.

I am in a 4-bet pot with pocket Kings, but the flop has come with an Ace, which is the worst card for my hand. Pro called my 4-bet pre-flop, and his range includes many hands that contain an Ace (like AK and AQ) or possibly a set (JJ). By checking, I am exercising pot control and seeing how Pro reacts to the board. If I bet, I'm unlikely to get called by worse hands, but I will certainly be called or raised by hands that have me beat. Checking allows me to evaluate whether to fold or call a potential bet based on the sizing and my reads on Pro's aggression.

Pre-flop, I called a cold 4-bet from the Small Blind with QQ, putting the opponent on a very strong range of AA, KK, QQ, JJ, AK, and perhaps AQs. The flop comes [6c Jc Ad]. The Ace is a very bad card for my Queens as it connects heavily with the 4-bettor's range (AA, AK). The opponent checks. Betting here achieves very little: better hands (AA, AK, JJ) will call or raise, and worse hands (KK, TT) will likely fold. Checking back allows me to exercise pot control, avoid being check-raised, and potentially get to showdown against hands like KK. It is the standard play with second pair/underpair in a 4-bet pot on an Ace-high board.

The turn 9 of spades is essentially a blank. After checking the flop for pot control because of the Ace, I continue to check. Pro's check-back on the flop could indicate he is also wary of the Ace (holding a pair like QQ or JJ) or that he's playing a weak Ace cautiously. By checking again, I maintain pot control and give Pro the opportunity to bluff with hands I beat or check back with marginal showdown value. Betting here likely only gets called by hands that have me beat (Aces or sets).

Pre-flop action involved a cold 4-bet from the Small Blind, narrowing their range to premium hands like AA, KK, QQ, JJ, and AK. The flop brought an Ace, which is very bad for my Pocket Queens. The opponent checked the flop, and now checks the turn. This double check often indicates either a trap with a strong Ace/Set or a hand like KK/TT trying to get to showdown. Betting here serves little purpose: worse hands (KK, TT) likely fold, and better hands (AK, AA, JJ) will call or raise. My goal is to get to showdown cheaply to beat KK or split with QQ. Checking back maintains pot control and avoids isolating myself against a superior range.

The board has run out 6c Jc Ad 9s 8h. After the 4-bet pre-flop, the action has been check-check on both the flop and the turn. The Ace on the flop is a major concern for my pocket Kings, but Pro's willingness to check back twice suggests he might not have a strong Ace or is also playing a marginal showdown hand like QQ or TT cautiously. There is no value in betting the river; I won't get called by worse hands, and I only get called or raised by hands that beat me (Aces or sets). By checking, I hope to reach a cheap showdown and win against his pocket pairs lower than Kings or missed high-card hands.

Pre-flop involved a cold 4-bet from the Small Blind, creating a large pot of 5350. The flop [6c Jc Ad] brought an Ace, which hits the opponent's 4-bet range (AA, AK) hard. The opponent has checked three streets (flop, turn, and now river). My Pocket Queens are currently a marginal made hand. If the opponent has KK, I win at showdown; if they have QQ, we chop. If they have AK, AA, or JJ, I lose. Betting here is unlikely to get value from worse hands (KK would likely fold to a bet on an Ace-high board) and only isolates me against better hands that might check-call. Checking back is the optimal play to realize equity and minimize loss against trapped strong hands.

I am in UTG position (effectively Cutoff in this 4-handed game) with J7 offsuit. This is a statistically weak hand that falls well outside of a standard opening range, even in a short-handed format. With a stack depth of over 100 BBs, there is no pressure to make desperate moves, and playing trash hands like this often leads to difficult post-flop situations or bleeding chips. The standard and disciplined play is to fold.

I am on the button with Ten-Deuce offsuit (Td 2c), which is one of the weakest starting hands. Although I am the chip leader, there is no strategic reason to open-raise with such a poor hand into active blinds like Minni and FiveTwo. Preserving my stack and waiting for better spots is the optimal play with 20 hands remaining in the tournament.

Heads-up preflop with the button folded, I have Ace‑7 offsuit in the small blind. In two-handed play the small blind is effectively the button postflop, so I want to take the initiative and play this ace in position. Limping gives the big blind cheap odds and invites random calls; a standard raise to ~3x the BB isolates, applies pressure, and extracts value from worse aces/overcards. Stacks are deep (140+ BB), so I can play postflop. Plan: c‑bet favorable flops if called; fold to a large, polarized 3‑bet but consider calling a reasonable-sized 3‑bet. Raise to 300 to take initiative.

Minni is opening from the small blind, which is typically a wide range 4-handed. ATo is comfortably ahead of that range and plays well as a value/merge 3-bet. By 3-betting we deny equity to hands like suited connectors and weaker aces, pick up the pot often preflop, and get initiative in an otherwise tricky out-of-position spot. If Minni 4-bets large, we can fold without burning too much since ATo doesn’t perform well vs a strong 4-bet range.

I opened to 300 from the small blind with As7h and the big blind (FiveTwo) 3‑bet to 1,100. Heads‑up I'll have postflop position and stacks are deep (~140BB), so A7o has good playability and an ace blocker is valuable. The 3‑bet size is moderate and I’m getting ~26.7% break‑even pot odds (call 800 to win ≈2,200), so calling to realize equity and play in position is the best line. I will fold to a large, polarized 4‑bet shove, but otherwise continue postflop: c‑bet favorable A‑high or drawy flops and play cautiously on highly coordinated boards.

I have top pair (A♠7♥) but a weak kicker on A♣ Q♥ 9♠. This flop is favorable to my preflop calling/raising range, so I want to take the initiative and extract value while keeping the pot controlled. A small-to-medium c-bet (~35–40% pot) will get called by worse A-x and many underpairs, deny free cards, and allow me to reevaluate on the turn. If I face a large, polarized check‑raise I’ll give up; I’ll call or proceed cautiously against a small raise and re-evaluate on the turn. Bet 800 into the 2200 pot (~36%).

We have top pair on a dry-ish A-Q-9 rainbow board with a middling kicker in position. Minni’s small donk (800 into 3000) can be a wide range: some Ax, Qx/9x, and occasional stabs/probes. Raising here tends to fold out the hands we dominate (weaker Ax/Qx) and get action primarily from better aces (AJ+), AQ, and sets, which puts ATo in a tougher spot at this SPR. Calling keeps her bluffs/probes in, controls pot size, and lets us reassess on the turn (value when she slows down; caution on K/J/Q/9 or heavy barreling).

I have top pair (A♠7♥) on A♣ Q♥ 9♠ 5♥ but a very weak kicker. Opponent 3‑bet preflop and then check‑called my flop bet, so his range is fairly strong (AK, AQ, overpairs, mid pocket pairs, some suited connectors/floaters). The turn 5♥ doesn’t change much for most of his range (it mostly gives trips to 5x and a few backdoor draws), so this is still a good spot to extract value from worse A‑x and single pairs while keeping the pot manageable vs hands that beat me. I’ll make a small‑to‑medium value bet (~40% pot) to keep worse Ax and underpairs calling and deny free river cards. If I face a large, polarized raise I’ll fold; I’ll re‑evaluate if I get a small raise. Bet 1,600 into the 3,800 pot to achieve these goals.

Minni’s turn sizing (1600 into a 5400 pot) is still a small, pressure-light barrel. In a 3-bet pot her range contains plenty of hands we beat or chop with that will keep betting for protection/value (worse Ax like A8s–AJs, some Qx like KQ/QJ, and occasional heart backdoor-to-real FD holdings), plus some probes that don’t want to face a raise. Raising with AT mostly folds out worse and gets action from the part of her range that dominates us (AQ, AJ/AK, sets, A9/Q9), so it’s a poor value/merge spot. With good pot odds (~23% needed) and position, the best line is to call and re-evaluate rivers (call small, fold to big polar bets on bad runouts; value-bet selectively if she checks).

I have top pair (A♠7♥) but a very weak kicker on A♣ Q♥ 9♠ 5♥ K♥. FiveTwo 3‑bet preflop and then check‑called my flop (800) and turn (1600) bets — a fairly strong, sticky calling range (AK/AQ, KQ, mid pairs, some two‑card heart combos). The river K♥ actually helps that range: it gives two‑pair combos (KQ, K9, K5) and completes possible two‑card heart flushes. Betting for thin value here is unlikely to earn many folds from worse hands and will get called by hands that now beat me; bluffing is also risky because a raise/crash would be very expensive. With the pot already 7,000 and my kicker weak, I prefer pot control and a showdown. Check behind and take the showdown rather than bloating the pot.

River goes check to us in a 3-bet pot after Minni led small twice (block/protection sizing). That line is usually capped to marginal showdown hands (weaker Ax, some Qx/9x) more than nutted hands, and we can extract thin value with our top pair. We don’t want to polarize or bloat the pot because better Ax/two-pair/sets exist and K completes JT; so we choose a small value bet that gets crying calls from worse aces (A8/A7/A6 etc.) and occasionally stubborn Qx, while keeping our bet/fold clean vs any check-raise.

I hold top pair (A♠7♥) but a very weak kicker on A♣ Q♥ 9♠ 5♥ K♥. FiveTwo 3‑bet preflop, then check‑called my flop (800) and turn (1600) bets — a fairly strong, sticky line — and now posts a relatively small river bet (1500) into the ~7000 pot. That small river bet looks like thin value / a probe from a range that contains both hands that crush me (AK, AQ, KQ, sets, some heart flushes) and worse hands that I beat (weaker A‑x, some medium pairs or missed floaters). Calling 1500 to win ~10,000 requires only ~15% equity. Given I still have meaningful showdown equity and raising would dangerously bloat the pot with a weak kicker (and could be met by a jam or call from hands that already beat me), folding is too tight. I’ll take the cheap showdown and call the 1500.

I am in the UTG position with 8d 4s. This is a very weak hand, and in a 4-handed game, it does not have enough equity or playability to justify a raise or a call, especially with three opponents left to act behind me. I will fold to preserve my stack.

Qc3d is a weak, often dominated hand and I have the positional advantage on the button. With only the blinds in (pot 150) calling 100 to see a flop in position is the lowest-variance, most profitable line: it gives me cheap equity and postflop initiative without bloating the pot with a marginal hand. A min-raise here would invite defensives/3-bets from SB (FiveTwo) and BB (Pro) who have shown willingness to defend and raise; I don’t want to face a reraise out of position with Q3o. Plan: call, see flop, then use position to continue or fold based on the texture and opponent actions.

We’re getting an excellent price to complete (50 more to win a 250 pot), and 6c5c has strong multiway/implied-odds value (straights/flushes, plus decent playability). From the small blind I don’t want to bloat the pot OOP versus both the limper and a competent big blind who can 3-bet/squeeze; completing keeps variance controlled while still realizing equity very profitably.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Jack offsuit (KJo), a strong hand, facing a limp from the Button and a completion from the Small Blind. The pot is currently 300. While raising to isolate is a valid option given the strength of my hand, playing a bloated pot out of position against the Button with a hand that often makes just one pair can be tricky. Limping ranges can sometimes trap, but more often contain speculative hands that play well post-flop. By checking, I realize my equity for free, keep the pot manageable, and avoid bloating the pot out of position. If I hit top pair, I will likely have the best hand and can extract value then. Therefore, checking is the prudent play.

On Th-Jh-Qs we have essentially no made hand and no real draw (only a backdoor club flush and very remote runouts). This is a coordinated board that smashes both opponents’ limp/check ranges (Qx/Jx/Tx, K9/98, strong Broadway), and we’re out of position multiway, so a stab will get called/raised too often and burn chips. Best line is to check, keep the pot small, and continue only if we improve significantly.

I have flopped middle pair (Jacks) and an open-ended straight draw (needing an Ace or Nine) on a very coordinated board of [Th Jh Qs]. In a limped pot with three players, the ranges are wide, and straights (AK, 98) or two-pair hands are well within opponents' ranges. Being out of position against the button and sandwiched, leading out could expose me to raises that would force me off my equity or put me in a difficult spot. Checking allows me to control the pot size, see a free turn if the button checks back, or evaluate a bet from the button with the intention of calling given my strong equity (pair + OESD).

Flop T♥ J♥ Q♠ with Q♣3♦ — I have top pair but a very weak kicker and two opponents to beat. Both checked to me and the pot is only 300. In position I should try to extract thin value and deny equity to straight/heart draws rather than check and give free cards. A small-to-medium c-bet (~1/3–1/2 pot) is best: it gets called by worse Qx, Jx, Tx and small pairs, folds pure floats, and keeps the pot controllable if someone continues. I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal kicker, and I will fold to a large raise (I’m vulnerable to KQ/AQ/AK/K9 straights). Bet 150 (≈50% pot) — if called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn; if everyone folds I pick up the pot.

We have complete air on Th-Jh-Qs with essentially no credible draw (no OESD/gutshot, only a very remote backdoor club flush). This is a board that heavily connects with Minni’s limp/btn range (broadways, Qx/Jx/Tx, K9, 98) and Pro can also wake up behind or apply pressure, making our equity realization terrible OOP multiway. Even though the sizing is small (150 into 300), calling just sets money on fire because we’ll have to fold most turns and we have minimal implied odds when we do improve (often to a weak pair). No reason to bluff-raise either with no blockers/equity. Best play is to cut losses now.

I hold middle pair (Jacks) and an open-ended straight draw to the nuts (an Ace or Nine completes a straight) on a [Th Jh Qs] board. The opponent bets half-pot (150 into 300) after limping the button. This board texture hits limping ranges hard, so my opponent likely has a piece of it, but my equity is very strong (8 clean straight outs plus potential trips/two-pair outs). The price is excellent, offering me 4:1 pot odds while I likely have >30% equity even against strong made hands. Raising would likely only get action from hands that beat me or strong draws, potentially forcing me to fold to a re-raise. Calling keeps the opponent's range wide and allows me to realize my equity in a controlled pot.

The turn card [9s] creates a four-card straight on the board (9-10-J-Q). Holding a King, I have completed a King-high straight, which is the effective nuts given that Ace-King is extremely unlikely in a limped pot. The board texture is very scary for any hand that doesn't have a straight. Leading out here would likely only get folds from hands I beat (like sets or two pair) and only get action from chops or the rare 8x. By checking, I allow the opponent, who bet the flop, to potentially bluff, value bet a lower straight (8x), or mistakenly value bet a set. This maximizes expected value by keeping their range wide.

Check. I have top pair (Qc) with a very weak 3 kicker on T♥ J♥ Q♠ 9♠. I c‑bet the flop and Pro called, but the 9 on the turn now completes obvious straight combos (Kx and 8x) and also helps two‑pair/set combinations — my hand’s relative strength dropped. Pro checked the turn, which can be either a slow‑played strong hand or a pot‑control/call with a pair or a missed draw. With the pot ~600 and many hands that beat me still in range, I want to control the pot size and avoid building a big pot with a marginal kicker. I’ll check and: if Pro fires a small bet (≈≤1/3 pot) I’ll call and re‑evaluate on the river; if Pro puts significant pressure or raises I’ll fold to avoid being priced in against straights/two‑pair/sets. If Pro checks back I’ll go to the river and decide whether a small value bet is appropriate on a safe card or to check down.

The river card is the 3h, making the final board [Th Jh Qs 9s 3h]. This completes a potential flush draw (three hearts on board) while maintaining the four-card straight on the board (9-T-J-Q). I hold the King-high straight, which is the second nut straight (beaten by AK and now any flush). My opponent bet the flop but checked back the turn. Their turn check suggests they likely do not have an Ace or King, and are either pot-controlling a made hand or were on a draw. The river flush card makes it difficult to value bet, as worse hands (like 8x straights or two pair) will likely fold or check back, while flushes will raise. Betting would likely only get action from hands that beat me or chop. Checking allows me to get to showdown cheaply or induce a bluff from missed spade draws, while giving me the option to evaluate if the opponent bets representing the flush.

The river 3♥ paired my 3 and I now have two pair (Q♣3♦) on T♥ J♥ Q♠ 9♠ 3♥ — a reasonably strong showdown hand but still vulnerable to straights (Kx or 8x), some higher two‑pair combos (Q with T/J/9) and to a completed heart flush. Pro’s line (called my 150 c‑bet on the flop, then checked turn and river) looks passive — more consistent with single pairs, missed draws or pot‑control with medium hands than with a confident made straight/flush. With the pot at 600, I want to extract thin value from worse one‑pair hands (Jx, Tx, single Qx) while keeping the pot controllable against the few hands that beat me. A small value bet (~1/3 pot) is the best line: it gets called by many worse hands yet doesn’t commit me if I face strong resistance. I’ll bet 200. If Pro raises big I will fold; if he calls I go to showdown.

I hold a King-high straight on a board of [Th Jh Qs 9s 3h]. The river 3h completes a potential flush draw. My opponent limped the button, bet the flop, checked the turn, and now bets small (200 into 600) on the river. The turn check makes it highly unlikely they hold the nut straight (AK). The small river bet could be a flush trying to induce a call, a chop (Kx), a lower straight (8x), or a blocking bet with a set/two-pair. Raising offers little value as worse hands likely fold on this scary board, and better hands (flushes) will call or raise. Calling is the safest play to win against lower straights or bluffs, chop with other Kings, and minimize losses against a flush.

UTG with Jc2c — a very weak, low-card suited J. There are three players to act behind (Button, SB, BB), including a very deep and sticky big blind (Flash 24k) who will defend or 3‑bet often. With ~89BB I can afford to be selective: this hand is frequently dominated and has poor playability OOP. A limp (call 100) just invites pressure and a min‑raise to 200 is committing to playing out of position with a marginal holding. Prefer to preserve my stack and wait for a better spot. Fold.

4-handed on the button after UTG folds, this is a prime steal spot. Ah4d isn’t premium but it has strong blocker value (removes many strong Ax continues), reasonable postflop playability, and benefits a lot from initiative/position versus the blinds. I prefer opening rather than limping to apply pressure and realize fold equity while keeping sizing modest against deeper stacks.

I am in the Small Blind with Q8 offsuit, facing a standard 2.5x raise from the Button (FiveTwo). Q8o is a marginal hand that is easily dominated by the opener's range (KQ, QJ, A8, etc.). Additionally, playing out of position without the initiative is a significant disadvantage, making it difficult to realize my equity. Given the stack depths and the late stage of the tournament, preserving chips by avoiding marginal spots out of position is the optimal strategy. The pot odds of 2:1 are not sufficient to overcome the positional disadvantage and reverse implied odds.

I am in the big blind with pocket deuces. In a four-handed game, pocket pairs have significant value, and folding to a standard button opening raise would be too tight. FiveTwo's raise to 2.5x the big blind is a standard play. I will call to defend my blind and see a flop, looking to hit a set or potentially play the hand post-flop depending on the board texture and my opponent's aggression.

The flop of 8-9-10 is extremely coordinated and connects well with the button's opening range. My pocket deuces have very little showdown value on this board and no real draws. I will check to the pre-flop aggressor and evaluate their action. In this scenario, checking is the most standard and disciplined play out of position.

This flop (8♠ T♥ 9♣) is very connected and hits the BB’s flatting range hard (lots of Tx/9x/8x, two-pair like T9/98, and straight draws like J7/QJ). With A♥4♦ I have essentially pure ace-high with no meaningful backdoors, so a c-bet will get called or check-raised a ton and burns equity. Checking back keeps the pot small, realizes my ace-high showdown value, and lets me bluff/value-bet later on better turns (especially an A).

The board has become extremely dangerous with a four-card straight (7-8-9-10) now present. My pocket deuces have no showdown value and no potential to improve to a winning hand on this texture. Since the pre-flop aggressor checked back on the flop, they might have some marginal equity or be trapping, but regardless, I cannot continue with this hand if faced with any aggression. I will check and plan to fold to any bet, as I am at the absolute bottom of my range.

Flash has checked twice on one of the most dynamic textures (8♠T♥9♣7♥). After defending BB, a lot of his strongest hands (two pair/straights/strong pair+draw) will often start building earlier or look for a check-raise on the flop; the double-check caps him a bit toward marginal pairs (8x/9x), small pocket pairs, and various draws/overcards. My A♥4♦ has very poor showdown value here (and no real equity), so checking back just gives him a clean river decision to bluff/value-bet into me. A small delayed c-bet leverages fold equity vs his air and some weak/marginal holdings while keeping risk controlled; if he check-raises we simply fold.

The board is now 7-8-9-10, meaning any Jack or any Six has a straight. My pocket deuces have zero showdown value and no potential to improve on this texture. FiveTwo's bet on the turn, after checking back the flop, suggests they have at least some piece of the board or are capitalizing on the straight-heavy runout. Given I have the literal worst possible hand on this board, I have no reason to continue.

4-handed from UTG (effectively CO), KTs suited is a clear open: strong high-card equity, good playability, and blocks some Kx/Tx continues. With position postflop versus the blinds and deep stacks, I want initiative and to start building pots against wider blind-defense ranges. I’ll use a standard ~2.5bb open that pressures the blinds without bloating too much versus potential 3-bets.

I am holding 3s 2h on the Button, which is arguably the worst starting hand in poker. Facing a raise to 250 from FiveTwo in the UTG position, I have no pot odds or equity to justify entering the pot. Even with position, playing 3-2 offsuit against a strong range is a losing play. I will fold immediately to conserve chips.

I am currently the chip leader with 23,800 chips. Hand #133 is deep into the tournament (Hand 133 of 150). My hand, 5s 6d, is a weak offsuit connector that performs poorly out of position. FiveTwo, who has the second-largest stack, opened for a raise to 250. Calling from the Small Blind would put me in a difficult spot post-flop, and 3-betting into the second-largest stack with such a marginal hand is an unnecessary risk when my goal is to maintain my lead toward the end of the tournament. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

FiveTwo opened UTG to 250 and I'm in the big blind with 4♦9♥. The pot is 400 and it costs me 150 to call (I need ~27.3% equity to break even). 4‑9 offsuit is a very poor hand out of position and will be dominated by most of an UTG opener’s range; postflop play will be difficult and frequently negative EV. Rather than bleed chips with a marginal hand I’ll preserve my stack for better spots and fold here.

I am in UTG (effectively the Cutoff in a 4-handed game) with [9h Ah]. With 107 big blinds, this is a standard opening hand due to its flush potential and high card value. The table has seen standard opens of 2.5x-3x. My recent showdowns have been premium hands (AA, QQ, AQ), so my image is relatively strong. I will raise to 250 to steal the blinds or play a pot in position against the blinds if the button folds.

I am on the button with Jack-Five offsuit. Pro has raised from UTG. Given the weakness of my hand and the fact that I am facing a raise from a player in early position (relative to this 4-handed setup), folding is the standard and most profitable long-term play. There is no need to commit chips to the pot with such a marginal hand.

I have Ace of Clubs + Ten of Diamonds (AcTd) in the small blind facing an UTG open to 250. I'm ~87.8BB deep — ATo is not a monster but is a good hand to play aggressively in this short-handed, deep-stacked spot: it has an Ace blocker (reduces combos like AA/AK/AQ) and plays well on ace/broadway flops. I don't want to limp-call and be stuck out of position in a multiway pot, so I'll 3-bet to take the initiative and isolate the opener. I size to 1,000 chips (total) — big enough to discourage speculative calls from the big blind and give meaningful fold equity, but small relative to effective stacks so I can continue postflop without overcommitting. Plan: if everyone folds I take the pot; if I'm called I'll continuation-bet favorable ace/ten/broadway textures and proceed cautiously on coordinated low boards; if I face a large, polarized 4-bet I will fold ATo rather than commit.

KQo is strong enough to defend getting a good price (900 to win 1350, ~29% immediate) and it keeps Pro’s range wide; folding is too tight. A 4-bet is awkward here because Minni is short (7.8k) and likely value-heavy 3-betting SB vs UTG, so we’d often get jammed on and hate our spot (can’t 4-bet/fold profitably). I’ll flat and play postflop cautiously out of position, avoiding stacking off lightly versus a tight 3-bet range.

I opened with A9s from UTG/CO. Minni 3-bet from the SB, and FiveTwo cold-called from the BB. This action indicates significant strength from both opponents. Minni's 3-bet range likely includes strong Aces and pairs (99+, AJ+), while FiveTwo's cold call in the BB is a very strong signal, often implying hands like TT-QQ, AQs, or trapped AK. Against these ranges, A9s is frequently dominated (by AK, AQ, AJ) or behind overpairs. Although I would have position post-flop and the pot odds are roughly 3:1 (25% equity needed), the reverse implied odds are high; if an Ace flops, I could easily lose a large pot to a better kicker. Given the strong action and being sandwich-ed by premium ranges, folding is the prudent long-term play to preserve chips.

I was the preflop 3-bettor and got one caller. Flop K♠ 9♣ 2♠ is fairly dry and I hold A♣ T♦ — no pair but a live overcard (ace-high) and the club blocker to strong A-combos (AK/AA). As the preflop aggressor this is a good spot to take the initiative: a small continuation bet pressures many of the caller’s missed broadways and speculative hands, extracts equity from hands that cannot continue comfortably, and keeps the pot manageable with only ace-high. I’ll size small (~1/3 pot) to maximize fold equity while limiting risk if called. Plan: bet 750 chips; fold to a large (pot-sized or bigger) check-raise, and if called proceed cautiously on the turn, looking to barrel favorable runouts (A, T, or clubs) or check/control vs heavy action.

We flop top pair with a strong kicker in a 3-bet pot (KcQd on K-9-2 rainbow). Minni’s 1/4-pot c-bet looks like a range/probing bet that keeps in all her underpairs and broadway/Ax air, and it also exists with her strongest hands (AK/AA/KK). Raising here mainly folds out the hands we beat (AQ, QQ-JJ/TT) while getting action or a shove from better (AK/AA/sets), which is a bad value/protection tradeoff at this SPR. Calling keeps her bluffs in, controls variance versus a tighter 3-bet range, and lets us re-evaluate on turn cards/bet sizing with position disadvantage.

Board is 9c 2s Kh 9s; I hold A♣ T♦ — only Ace-high. I was the preflop aggressor and c-bet the flop (750) and got called. The turn (9s) drastically improves villain's calling range (any 9, many Kx, pocket pairs, some disguised sets) so my raw equity is very low. My Ac is a useful blocker to some strong A-combos but not enough to make a second-barrel profitable often — betting here will bloat a pot I am behind in and will be called by most Kx/9x/pairs. I prefer to control the pot and see a reaction: check to keep weaker holdings and floats in, and be prepared to fold to meaningful turn aggression. If villain checks back I can reevaluate the river and barrel only very specific runouts (A/T/club) or induce with a targeted sizing; if villain fires a small probe I may call to realize equity only when odds are correct, but fold to large bets. Overall: check for pot control and to avoid committing with just Ace-high.

Turn pairs the 9 and brings a second spade, but overall it’s a low-draw, high-“range-strength” texture for SB’s 3-bet range.
