Minni’s flop 1/4-pot c-bet followed by a turn check does not cap her much (she can still have slowplays like AK/AA/KK or trips), and with her short stack the main danger of betting is getting check-jammed and being forced into a very poor call/fold spot with KQ.

By checking back we (1) realize our equity with a clear showdown-oriented hand, (2) keep her bluffs in for the river, and (3) avoid turning a good hand into a thin value bet that can’t comfortably continue versus a shove. We can evaluate river sizing: comfortably call many reasonable bets and fold to very polarized large ones on bad runouts.

Check. The river 9 completes a very scary board (9♣-2♠-K♥-9♠-9♦) and I have A♣T♦ — no pair and essentially no showdown value against the range that called my flop c-bet and checked the turn. FiveTwo’s line (call flop, check turn) is consistent with Kx, middle pairs, small/medium pocket pairs or slow-played sets; the 9 on the river gives villains quads/full houses in some cases and trips or better in many others. Bluffing here has poor fold equity (many of his range that can call the flop will also call a river bluff) and value-betting is impossible with just Ace-high. Pot is 3,750 and my stack is 7,075 — I won’t bloat the pot with virtually no equity. I’ll check and fold to any meaningful river bet; if he checks back we’ll see a showdown (but I expect to lose).

River improves us to a very strong made hand: 9-9-9-K-K (nines full of kings). Minni’s check twice (turn, river) looks like a capped/SD-oriented range a lot of the time (AA/QQ-JJ/AQ-type hands, some AK/Kx that now chops), and a small value bet should get looked up by overpairs and induce calls from hands that would fold to bigger sizing.

At the same time, this board is very polarized for raises: if Minni check-raises, it’s overwhelmingly KK (the only combo left) or a rare 9x, so I want a sizing that extracts value when called but doesn’t pot-commit me versus a check-jam (where I can make a disciplined fold).

So I’ll go small/medium for value.

I checked the river with A♣T♦ on 9♣‑2♠‑K♥‑9♠‑9♦ and have essentially no showdown value. FiveTwo’s line (called my flop c‑bet, checked the turn, then bets the river) is exactly what I’d expect from Kx, medium pocket pairs or a slow‑played 9 — hands that beat me. The river bet is 1,300 into a 5,050 pot (~25% pot), so calling 1,300 would require the villain to be bluffing >~20% of the time to be +EV. Given their flop call range and the coordinated turn/river, I assign far more value combos than bluffs. I won’t risk chips with only ace‑high, so I fold.

Holding Queen-Deuce offsuit from under-the-gun in a four-handed game is a standard fold. Even with a significant chip lead, there is no strategic advantage to entering the pot with such a weak hand out of position, particularly as we are in the closing stages of the tournament (Hand 135 of 150).

Button with 7h7s and ~70BB effective — this is a clear open-raise spot. Limping hands loses value and invites multiway callers who crush pocket pairs; raising isolates the blinds and gives fold equity and position postflop. Table standard opening size is ~3x here (lots of 300 opens), so I’ll raise to 300. That sizing is large enough to get value from worse hands and deny equity to overcards, but not so large as to bloat the pot unnecessarily vs deeper stacks. If I get single- or multi-calls I’ll play positionally: c-bet dry boards, check/control or set-mine on coordinated/low boards, and fold to heavy, committed 3-bet aggression unless pot odds/implied odds justify a call. If faced with a large 3-bet I’ll often fold to preserve my 70BB stack.

Minni is opening very frequently on the button and is the shortest stack at the table, so applying pressure preflop is high EV. Flat-calling K8o from the small blind creates a tough, low-realization spot out of position (often multiway vs Pro), while a 3-bet can win the pot immediately and denies equity. I’ll use a sizing that pressures Minni’s stack without risking too much myself; if Minni 4-bet jams, K8o is not strong enough to continue and I can comfortably fold after taking this stab.

I am in the Big Blind with 9d 5d facing a Button open to 300 and a Small Blind 3-bet to 1000. My hand is a weak suited gapper. Calling a 3-bet cold from the Big Blind with this holding is generally -EV due to being out of position against a strong range from the Small Blind, and the Button still has to act behind me (potentially 4-betting or calling). The price to call (900 chips) is too expensive for the equity I realize with this hand. Conserving my 100BB stack for better spots is the correct strategy.

I opened to 300 on the button with 7h7s and FiveTwo (SB) 3-bet to 1,000. With ~67–70BB effective and heads‑up in position, the correct line is to call (total 1,000). 77 is too weak to 4‑bet‑polarize here and too valuable to fold outright — calling preserves stack, keeps the better postflop position, and gives reasonable implied odds to set‑mine against a player who 3‑bets a fairly wide range. Immediate pot odds on the call (~33%) are worse than the ~12% chance to flop a set, but implied odds + position make this a profitable continue. Postflop plan: use position to control the pot — c‑bet thin on dry, low boards to deny equity and extract; check/control or fold to heavy aggression on ace/high or coordinated boards; if I flop a set I will extract maximum value. I will call to 1,000 total.

This is a 3-bet pot versus Minni’s wide button open/call range, and the flop (As Js 4h) heavily favors my perceived range (lots of strong Ax, overpairs, and suited broadways). With Ks8c I have mostly air, but the Ks is a useful blocker to some strong continues (AK/KJ/KQs with a spade component) and I can c-bet at high frequency on this texture. A small continuation bet applies max pressure to her shorter stack, can fold out her whiffs (small pairs, suited connectors that missed, some weak J-less hands), and keeps my risk controlled; if she jams, I comfortably fold.

Board is J♠ 4♥ A♠. I hold 7♥7♠ — an underpair with one spade (only a backdoor flush draw) against a player who 3‑bet preflop. FiveTwo’s range from that line contains a lot of A‑x, J‑x and overpairs, so I’m behind to a big portion of their range. However their flop sizing (600 into a 2700 pot, ~20–25% of pot) is very small — consistent with a probe/block or mixed value/bluff c‑bet. Calling is the best option: it’s cheap (600 into 3300 total, ~18% immediate pot odds) and I keep position to control the pot and realize my equity. I have a small chance to hit a set (~8% by river) and some showdown value vs weaker holdings and bluffs; folding here would be too nitty and raising would be spewy given villain’s range and the risk of a big shove. Plan: call the 600, re‑evaluate on the turn — fold to heavy turn aggression without improvement, and extract value if I hit a 7 or villain barrels very light.

Turn in a 3-bet pot vs Minni’s wide BTN open/call range, this board (As Js 4h 6s) still strongly favors my perceived range (strong Ax, big broadways, and suited spade combos). The third spade is a good barreling card because it adds credible nut/near-nut flushes to my story and will put a lot of her marginal flop continues (pocket pairs, Jx without a spade, some floats) in a tough spot—especially with her short stack. I don’t want to overbet/jam because she’ll continue with most Ax and some spade draws; instead I’ll use a controlled pressure sizing that only needs a modest fold rate to profit. If she check-jams, I simply fold Ks8c and preserve stack; if she calls, I can choose selective river barrels on scary cards (spade/Q/T) and otherwise shut down.

Call 1,200. I’m on the button with 7♥7♠ facing a 3‑bettor who has made relatively small continuation bets. On J♠–4♥–A♠–6♠ I’m an underpair but I have one spade (four to a possible flush) plus two outs to a set. My chance to improve by the river is roughly 19–20% for the flush + ~4–5% for a set (combined ~24%), and calling 1,200 into a ~5,700 pot gives me ~21% pot‑odds — my raw equity meets/exceeds the immediate price. Calling also keeps position and preserves stack flexibility; folding here surrenders too much equity and raising would be spewy (I’m often behind to Ax/Jx/overpairs and would just inflate the pot). Plan: call the turn, evaluate the river — fold to a large shove if I miss and face heavy action, but extract or raise for value if I make my hand or villain shows clear weakness.

River is a brick for my Ks (no spade comes), so I’m at pure K-high. After Minni called flop and especially called the turn on the 3-spade texture, her range is heavily weighted to Ax (often with a spade), made flushes, and some strong draws/straights; she’s also short enough that a river shove would be for her whole stack and is likely to get looked up by most of that continuing range. The Td is not a great bluff card here because it completes KQ and improves some Ax/Tx holdings, reducing fold equity. Checking maximizes EV by (1) avoiding a low-success triple barrel, and (2) still winning sometimes when she has missed 1-spade draws or other floats that give up. If she bets big, I can comfortably check-fold.

Board is J♠-4♥-A♠-6♠-T♦, pot 5,700 and FiveTwo checked the river. I have 7♥7♠ — an underpair with essentially no river improvement and only thin showdown value. FiveTwo 3‑bet preflop and used very small probe bets on flop/turn; that line is weighted toward A‑x, J‑x and overpairs that beat me, plus some bluffs. Betting here to fold out better hands is unlikely to be profitable — a meaningful bet would commit a large portion of my 4,275 stack and can be exploited by a raise, while a tiny bet will fold out very few of the stronger hands that actually beat me. With no bet to call and minimal fold equity, the prudent line is to check back and go to showdown rather than barrel off chips with a marginal holding.

Pot is 150, to-call 100, min‑raise 200. In a 4‑handed game UTG ranges widen, and K9o is marginal but playable heads‑up. I don’t want to limp and invite multiway action or a squeeze, so I open to a standard 2x BB (200) to seize initiative and isolate the button/blinds. With ~100bb effective I can continue postflop if called; I will fold to a large, committed 3‑bet (K9o has poor equity vs a tight 3‑bet range) and otherwise c‑bet on favourable K/9 or dry boards and pot‑control on dangerous textures.

4-handed, Minni’s UTG open is effectively a CO open and can be fairly wide, especially given their frequent raising history. On the Button with QTh (suited broadway with strong playability), I prefer a 3-bet rather than a flat: we leverage position, put immediate pressure on a shorter stack (effective ~9.8k), and deny the blinds a cheap peel. This also keeps my range aggressive and balanced versus Minni’s min-open sizing. If Minni 4-bets, QTs is not strong enough to continue for stacks, so I can comfortably fold.

I am in the Small Blind holding Ten of Spades and Eight of Hearts (T8o). Minni raised to 200 from UTG, and FiveTwo 3-bet to 700 from the Button. Facing a raise and a re-raise with a weak offsuit hand, I am significantly behind the ranges of both opponents. Even if the 3-bet is light, T8o plays poorly out of position and lacks the equity to call. The correct play is to fold immediately to preserve my stack of 10,300.

I am in the Big Blind with QTo. Minni opened from UTG with a min-raise, and FiveTwo 3-bet from the Button to 700. QTo is a marginal hand that performs poorly against an UTG opening range and a Button 3-bet range. Furthermore, I would be playing the rest of the hand out of position. Given my current chip lead and the proximity to the end of the tournament (Hand 136/150), there is no need to take a high-variance, disadvantageous spot. Folding is the safest and most disciplined play here.

FiveTwo 3‑bet to 700 after my 200 open. Pot is 1,050 and I must call 500 more (700 total). K♦9♣ is a playable heads‑up hand with good blockers to Kx and reasonable equity versus a button's likely wide 3‑betting range. I don't want to 4‑bet to the small min‑raise (1,200) because that gives little fold equity, bloats a pot out of position and needlessly commits chips with a marginal hand. Folding is too tight heads‑up. Plan if I call: see a flop, c‑bet on favourable K/9 or dry textures, pot‑control or check back on ace/overcard heavy boards, and fold to large committed 4‑bets or strong flop shove/raising action. Therefore I call to realize equity and play the flop.

I’m out of position with K♦9♣ on J♠5♠2♣. This is a relatively dry flop (two low cards + a Jack) and my K is a strong overcard with a small backdoor club possibility. FiveTwo’s 3‑bet range is wide here (pocket pairs, A‑x, broadways, some Jx and spade draws), so a small continuation bet can fold out a lot of his weaker holdings (small pairs, A‑high, offsuit broadways) while keeping the pot controllable if called. I don’t want to overcommit with a marginal hand, so I’ll make a compact c‑bet (~1/3 pot). If he raises heavily I’ll fold; if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn (check for pot control and reevaluate). Betting 500 applies pressure, realizes fold equity, and preserves flexibility for the streets.

Minni’s small donk-lead (500 into 2050) looks like a range/probe bet after flatting my 3-bet—often weak Jx, small pairs/5x, or some A/K-high with a spade. With QhTh I have two live overcards to 5/2 plus backdoor hearts equity and strong positional leverage, so folding is too tight given the price. Raising here would bloat the pot with no made hand and mostly gets action from the part of Minni’s range that already connects (Jx/FD), while folding out the weaker hands I can keep in. Calling keeps their bluffs in, controls variance versus a shorter stack, and lets me apply pressure on favorable turns (Q/T/A/K/hearts) or if they check.

I check for pot control. After my 1/3-pot c-bet on J♠5♠2♣ FiveTwo called, and the 6♦ on the turn changes nothing for K♦9♣ — I still have no pair and no realistic draw. FiveTwo’s calling range is weighted toward made hands (Jx, pocket pairs, occasional sets or broadways) and will call turn bets that I need folds from, so barreling here simply bloats a pot I’m likely behind in. Checking keeps the pot manageable out of position, preserves fold equity on the river when a favorable card appears, and lets me fold to significant aggression now. Plan: check the turn; fold to large lead sizes, consider calling very small probes to see a cheap river, and if checked to the river re-evaluate for a delayed bluff only on a clear scare card or value bet if I pair up.

Turn checks to me after Minni’s small flop lead/call line. Their check range contains a lot of give-ups (A/K-high, some underpairs) and some marginal made hands (weak Jx/5x) plus occasional spade draws. With QhTh I have 6 clean outs versus one-pair hands and I can credibly apply pressure as the preflop 3-bettor. I’ll take a small, range-advantage stab to pick up the pot immediately at a good price and deny equity, while keeping the risk controlled versus a potential check-raise (which I would fold to).

Fold. Pot is 3,450 and I must call 900 — calling 900 to win 4,350 requires ~20.7% equity. With K♦9♣ I only have 6 clean outs (3 Ks + 3 9s) to pair on the river, ~12–13% equity, well below the required ~21%. FiveTwo’s line (preflop 3‑bet, called my small c‑bet on the flop, now betting the turn when I checked) is weighted to value (Jx, pocket pairs, some two‑pair/sets or strong broadways) so this is not a bluff-heavy spot enough to justify a call or a bluff‑raise out of position. Preserving my ~100bb stack for a better, +EV opportunity is correct — fold.

4-handed UTG we can open fairly wide, but 82o is at the very bottom: poor playability, dominated when we make a pair, and it performs badly versus the likely defend/3-bet ranges of these opponents. With no need to take a thin -EV spot this deep, folding preserves stack and keeps our open range stronger for the remaining hands.

I am on the Button with a very weak hand (4c 5d). The UTG player has folded. While being on the Button offers a positional advantage, 45o is too weak to profitably open-raise, especially considering the blinds' tendencies. The Small Blind (Flash) is the chip leader and can apply pressure, while the Big Blind (Minni) has shown a willingness to defend. There is no strategic merit in attempting a steal with this holding given the stack depths and stage of the tournament (Hand 137/150). Folding is the standard and correct play.

I am in the Small Blind with J8o. The action has folded to me. In a blind vs blind situation, J8o is a reasonable hand to attack the Big Blind with, especially since I have a significant chip lead over Minni. I will raise to 300 to try and take the pot pre-flop or to take control of the hand. If Minni fights back with a three-bet, I can easily fold without much loss.

I’m in the big blind with Kh Js facing a 300 SB open. Pot is 400 and I only need to put 200 more (300 total) — roughly 33% pot odds. KJ off is a strong playable hand heads-up vs a wide SB opening range, but it’s marginal to 3‑bet out of position given Flash’s stack and the postflop disadvantage. I’ll call 300 to keep the pot manageable, realize equity on flops, and proceed cautiously OOP (look to c-bet small when favorable or check/call and fold to heavy pressure on dry, hostile boards).

On a monotone flop of Qh 6h 2h, I hold the Jh, which gives me a draw to a strong flush. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor, I will follow up with a continuation bet of 300 (half pot). This allows me to maintain initiative and potentially win the pot immediately if Minni lacks a heart or a pair. If she calls, I have the equity of the flush draw to fall back on for future streets.

Flop Qh‑6h‑2h, I hold Kh Js. I have a 4‑card heart draw (Kh + three hearts on board) = 9 outs (~35% to make a flush by river) plus backdoor pair outs. Flash bet 300 into the ~600 pre‑bet pot (pot is 900 now); calling 300 commits 300 to a 1200 pot if called, so my required break‑even is ~25% and my equity (~35%) > break‑even → a call is +EV.

Raising as a semi‑bluff is possible, but I’m out of position, Flash has a big stack and will call/reraise with many hands (including nut hearts or Qx), making OOP play difficult. I’ll call to realize equity, keep the pot manageable, and see the turn. Plan: if a heart hits the turn I’ll proceed aggressively (value/commit); if I miss and face a large turn bet I will fold most non‑improved hands; if I pair a K or J I’ll proceed cautiously for value.

Action: call 300.

The turn 8c improves my hand to a pair of eights while I still maintain the Jh for a high flush draw. Given the monotone flop, Minni's call on the flop suggests she likely has either a heart, a pair of queens, or a smaller pair. By checking the turn, I control the pot size and allow myself to realize equity. If Minni checks behind, I get a free card to complete my flush. If she bets, my pair of eights plus the flush draw provides more than enough equity to call. Betting again might isolate me against better hands like a queen or a completed flush, whereas checking allows me to keep her weaker range in and evaluate the river.

Turn: Qh‑6h‑2h‑8c and I hold Kh Js. I missed completing the flush (I still have a 4‑card heart draw — 9 outs, ~9/46 ≈ 19.6% to hit on the river) and I didn’t pair. Flash c‑bet the flop then checked the turn, which can be pot‑control or a disguised strong hand; with Flash’s very deep stack a bet here risks a check‑raise that would put me OOP and committed with only a one‑card draw. Pot is 1,200 and there’s no bet to call, so the lowest‑variance, +EV line is to check and control the pot: realize my equity on the river, avoid getting raised off my draw, and keep river decision flexibility. Plan: if the river brings a heart I’ll bet/raise for value; if I pair a K/J I’ll proceed cautiously; if I miss and face heavy pressure I’ll fold.

The river 3s is a complete brick, and the final board remains monotone with three hearts. My pair of eights now has moderate showdown value. Minni's check on the turn after calling the flop suggests she likely holds a marginal hand like a small pair (6 or 2) or a single high heart (like the Ah or Kh) that she was content to take to showdown. By checking, I allow myself to win against these missed draws and small pairs without risking more chips against a potential Queen or a slow-played flush. Betting would likely only get called by hands that beat me, so checking is the most profitable line to realize my equity.

Board is Qh‑6h‑2h‑8c‑3s, I hold Kh Js. I flopped the nut heart draw but missed on the turn and river and never paired. Flash opened and c‑bet the flop, then checked the turn and checked the river — a line consistent with a missed one‑barrel (air/weak pair/float) or cautious medium strength hand; a slow‑played made flush is possible but unlikely given the passive turn/river. With only K‑high now I have very little value — I beat mainly missed bluffs/draws and lose to any pair or better. Betting as a bluff risks a check‑raise from a very deep stack and is unlikely to fold enough better hands to be +EV. The lowest‑variance, highest EV line is to check to showdown and avoid committing chips with no made hand.

I am in the UTG position (which is also the Cutoff in a 4-handed game) with [8h 7c] (8 of Hearts, 7 of Clubs). We are in the late stage of the tournament (Hand 138/150) with blinds likely at 50/100. 87 offsuit is a weak hand to open from first position, even four-handed. It lacks the flush potential of a suited connector and can easily be dominated or put in difficult post-flop spots out of position if called by the button. Given my stack size of ~103 BBs, there is no pressure to make loose steals. The standard and prudent play here is to fold.

I am on the Button with pocket fives. After Pro folded, the action is on me. Pocket fives are a strong hand to open with in a 4-handed game, particularly from the button where I will have positional advantage for the rest of the hand. Opening for 3x (300) has been my standard raising size throughout the tournament and should effectively narrow the field or win the blinds outright.

I have 5♣2♥ — a worthless, unplayable hand out of position. Flash (button) has opened to 300; I’ve already posted the 50 small blind and would need to call 250 into a 450 pot. With no real equity, poor playability, and deeper stacks behind (no reliable fold equity or implied odds), defending or 3-betting would be spewing chips. Preserve the 8125 stack and fold to the raise.

Flash is opening the button to 3bb and has shown plenty of aggression throughout the match, so our BB defend range should be quite wide. KTo would be a pure call, but KTs has strong playability (good top-pair potential plus suited equity) and benefits from denying equity to his wide open range. I prefer mixing in a 3-bet here to start building a pot against dominated Kx/Tx and suited broadways, while still being able to fold comfortably if he comes back with a big 4-bet (his value range dominates us). Sizing OOP: 1000 total puts pressure on his opens without overcommitting.

I have pocket fives on the Button in a 4-handed game. After I opened for 300, FiveTwo 3-bet to 1000 from the Big Blind. I am getting good odds (700 to call into a pot of 1350) and I have the advantage of position for the rest of the hand. Pocket fives are strong enough to see a flop here, primarily looking to hit a set (set-mining) or play a low-card board favorably. Given my stack size and position, calling is the most profitable and standard play compared to 4-betting or folding.

We’re in a BTN vs BB 3-bet pot and this A-high flop strongly favors the 3-bettor’s range (we have all the big Ax/sets more often than Flash does after flatting pre). With KTs we also have decent showdown value (pair of tens) but we don’t want to give free cards to his broadway floats and random overcards/diamonds. A small range c-bet keeps our line consistent, applies pressure to his air, and lets us comfortably fold if he comes back with a raise (his raise range will be very Ax-heavy here).

FiveTwo 3-bet pre-flop, which typically represents a strong range including high pairs and big Aces. The flop [Ad Td 6s] is very favorable for a 3-bettor's range. Holding pocket fives, I am now an underpair to the board with only two outs to improve to a set. FiveTwo's lead on the flop is a standard continuation bet that likely has me beat. Continuing in this pot with a marginal underpair against a demonstrated show of strength on an Ace-high board is risky and likely negative EV. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my chip lead.

I am currently the chip leader with 23,300 chips and 11 hands remaining in the tournament. In a 4-handed game, holding Jack-Five offsuit (Js, 5c) under the gun is a very weak position to play from. Given my stack size and the proximity to the end of the tournament, there is no strategic advantage to playing such a marginal hand and risking chips against aggressive opponents like FiveTwo and Minni. Folding is the standard and safest play here to preserve my lead.

I’m on the button with J♦9♥ in an unopened pot. With deep stacks (~81bb) and only the blinds behind this is a standard open-raise spot to steal the blinds and play post‑flop in position. A 3x open to 300 gives me good fold equity while keeping the pot manageable if called. Plan: apply pressure now, fold to a large cold 3‑bet that commits too many chips (e.g. a big 3‑bet >~900 total), but continue post‑flop and use position to c‑bet favourable boards or realize equity when checked to.

Minni is opening from the button and likely has a wide range. Getting 250 to win 700 (after I call) gives me decent direct odds, and 3h2h has some playable upside (flushes/wheel straights) when stacks are still relatively deep. A 3-bet bluff here would need an unrealistically high fold frequency (no blockers, and Minni has shown she can fight back with 4-bets), so I’d rather keep the pot small and realize equity. Plan postflop: mostly check to the raiser, continue on strong draws/paired boards where I can fight, and otherwise play tight (check/fold).

I am in the Big Blind with Q9o facing a Button raise to 300 and a Small Blind call. The pot is currently 700, and it costs me 200 to call. This offers me pot odds of 3.5:1, requiring approximately 22% equity to call profitably. Q9o has sufficient equity against a typical Button opening range and a Small Blind flatting range to justify a call at this price, despite being out of position. Folding would be too tight given the excellent price to see a flop.

Multiway flop on A♠-8♣-3♠ is a strong range advantage board for the preflop raiser (Minni). With 3♥2♥ I have a weak pair (bottom pair) with no meaningful draw (only very thin runner-runner straight possibilities) and I’m out of position versus both players. Donk-betting risks getting raised by Ax/strong draws and I can’t profitably continue; checking keeps the pot controlled and lets me play straightforwardly (mostly check/fold versus real sizing, maybe continue only versus a very small stab).

I hold Queen-high (Qs 9c) on an Ace-high board (8c As 3s). I have missed the flop completely with no immediate draws, though I have a weak backdoor flush potential. As the pre-flop caller in the Big Blind in a multi-way pot, this board texture (Ace-high) favors the pre-flop raiser (Minni) significantly. Donk betting here serves no purpose and would likely just lose chips. The standard play is to check to the aggressor. If faced with a bet, I will likely fold given my lack of equity.

I raised preflop and both blinds checked to me on an 8c As 3s flop. J♦9♥ completely missed (no spade, no pair, no real draw), so my raw equity is low. As the preflop aggressor in position I still have fold equity — many calling ranges contain weak overcards, small pairs or floats that will fold to pressure. I want to apply pressure while keeping the pot manageable: a small c‑bet (~1/3 pot) targets folds and preserves chips if I run into strength. Bet 300 into the 900 pot. Plan: fold to a substantial raise; if called, re‑evaluate on the turn and proceed cautiously (check‑back unless I improve or pick up additional information).

Minni’s 300 into 1200 is a very small 25% c-bet in a multiway pot, and I’m getting 5:1 immediate odds (need ~16.7%). With 3♥2♥ I at least have bottom pair plus some thin improvement (2 outs to trips, 3 outs to two pair, and backdoor hearts). I don’t want to bloat the pot with a raise (I’ll get continued on by Ax/8x/flush draws and be in rough shape), but folding everything to this sizing is too tight and lets the button print with small stabs. Plan: call once, then play very straightforward—fold to significant turn pressure, continue on improving turns (2/3/heart, etc.) and be cautious if Pro wakes up behind.

I hold Queen-high (Qs 9c) on an Ace-high board (8c As 3s). The pre-flop raiser (Minni) bet 300 into 900, and the Small Blind (FiveTwo) called. While the price is attractive (300 to win 1500, or 5:1), my hand has negligible equity. The Small Blind's call indicates they likely have at least a pair or a strong draw, leaving me drawing dead or extremely thin. I have no pair and no significant draws. Continuing out of position against two opponents with this holding is not profitable.

Turn 6♦ doesn’t materially improve us (still just bottom pair) and it’s a much better card for Minni’s c-bet range (Ax, 8x, spade draws, and some 66/76 type hands if she opens wide). With no meaningful draw and being OOP, leading is low-EV and inflates a pot where we’re rarely ahead when called/raised. Best is to check, keep the pot controlled, and respond based on sizing (mostly fold versus any real turn barrel; only consider continuing versus a very small stab).

I have J♦9♥ on 8c As 3s 6d — no pair and no meaningful draw. I was the preflop aggressor and c‑bet the flop (300) but was called; that call suggests a range containing Ax, medium pairs, 8x, or draws that will not fold lightly to a second barrel. The turn 6♦ didn’t improve me and the opponent checked, so betting is a pure bluff with little fold equity against boats/aces/pairs and will inflate the pot unnecessarily. I’ll control the pot and realize equity by checking back, preserving chips and keeping the option to bluff or value-bet the river if the card is favourable (or to check down if it’s a blank).

River pairs the 8, giving me two pair (8s and 3s) with an Ace kicker on board. This is a decent showdown hand, but it’s in rough shape versus Minni’s most natural value region: any Ax (two pair Aces+8s), any 8x (trips), and boats (A8/66/33). If I lead, worse hands (missed spades / random broadways) usually just fold, while better hands call/raise—so betting is mostly value-owning myself.

Checking keeps the pot controlled, allows me to realize my showdown value, and gives Minni a chance to bluff missed spade draws or take a stab with hands that can’t win at showdown. Plan: bluff-catch versus small sizing; fold versus strong polar barrels.

I opened preflop and c‑bet the flop (300) but was called. My J9 missed completely and the turn was a blank — I checked to control the pot and see a cheap river. The river pairing an 8 (8c/8d) actually improves many hands in my opponent’s calling range (Ax becomes two pair, any 8 becomes trips, pocket pairs remain ahead). I have no showdown value and very little fold equity vs a caller who continued on the flop. Betting here is a thin bluff that will be called too often or bled out by value hands; with ~75bb effective and only 1500 in the pot, pot control is the +EV play. Check and accept showdown (or fold to any river bet).

Qc4c is a weak, highly dominated holding to open UTG in a 4‑handed game. Calling 100 to see a flop would put me out of position multiway against deep, aggressive players (BTN and BB) and has poor realized equity / reverse implied odds. Raising to 200 (or larger) with this hand is too loose and invites comfortable 3‑bets or calls from bigger stacks. With ~75bb effective and only 10 hands left in the tournament, preserving chips and waiting for a better positional spot is the +EV line. Fold and avoid marginal OOP spots.

Minni has folded, so this is a late-position steal spot with only the blinds left to get through. 7♥6♣ is not a premium, but it has reasonable connectivity/playability in position, and raising here profits from fold equity plus keeps initiative against two relatively capped blind ranges. I’ll use a small, standard open size to risk little while still pressuring the blinds; if I face a 3-bet, this hand is an easy fold.

Current situation: 4 players left, I am in the Small Blind with As9h (A9o). The Button (FiveTwo), who is the second chip leader, opens to 250 (2.5 BB). I have about 100 BBs. 

Strategic analysis:
1.  **Hand Strength**: A9o is a decent hand 4-handed, often ahead of a wide button opening range. However, it suffers from reverse implied odds and playability issues out of position.
2.  **Opponent Analysis**: FiveTwo has shown a tendency to use mixed opening sizes (sometimes 250, sometimes ~1000). The smaller size likely indicates a standard wide range or speculative hands. While I am ahead of a random hand, A9o is easily dominated by hands that continue against aggression (AT+, pairs).
3.  **Position**: Playing out of position (OOP) against a deep-stacked aggressive opponent is difficult. Calling invites the Big Blind to come along, leading to a multi-way pot where A9o performs poorly.
4.  **Action**: A 3-bet would define the pot but risks bloating it OOP against the chip leader. Calling is passive and leaves me vulnerable. Folding is the conservative, low-variance play that preserves chips for better positional spots in the final 10 hands. Given the marginal nature of A9o facing a raise and the positional disadvantage, folding is the prudent decision to minimize mistakes.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Seven of spades, a strong suited hand that plays well post-flop. FiveTwo (Button) has raised to 250. With 150 more to call into a pot of 400, I am getting approximately 2.6:1 odds, which is more than enough to defend my blind against a Button open. I am the chip leader and can afford to see a flop and play a pot in position relative to the board, even though I'll be out of position against FiveTwo. I will simply call and see the flop.

I have King-high on an Ace-high board with very few draws. Checking is the standard play here as the out-of-position player against the pre-flop aggressor. I will see how FiveTwo responds to the check.

Flop comes A♦6♦5♠ giving me a strong combo: middle pair (6x) plus an open-ended straight draw (4 or 8). BB’s check contains a lot of overcards, weak Ax, 5x/6x, and diamond/gutter draws. I want to leverage my preflop initiative, deny equity to random overcards/diamonds, and also get value from worse pairs and draws while building a pot for when I improve. A medium c-bet accomplishes this without overinflating versus strong Ax/sets. If check-raised, I can continue versus reasonable sizes due to my equity; otherwise I’ll barrel good turns (4/8, diamonds, some 7s) and take my free card on bad ones when appropriate.

I have King-high on an Ace-high board with no pair and no real draws (only a very weak backdoor straight possibility). FiveTwo, as the pre-flop aggressor from the button, is very likely to have an Ace in their range or at least a better hand than King-high. My King of Spades and Seven of Spades do not interact with this board at all. Calling a 325-chip bet into a 550-chip pot with nothing but King-high is not profitable long-term, especially when out of position. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

4-handed UTG is effectively a cutoff spot, but 9♠5♥ offsuit is still at the very bottom of playable opens and performs poorly versus 3-bets/defends. With our aggressive image and deep stacks, we’re likely to get played back at and end up in tough postflop spots with a dominated, low-connectivity hand. Best EV is to pass and preserve maneuverability for stronger steal candidates.

I am on the Button with 6h 5d (offsuit connectors). The UTG player has folded. While being on the Button offers a positional advantage and a chance to steal the blinds, 65o is a weak hand with limited post-flop playability. The Small Blind (Flash) has a very large stack and may defend widely, and the Big Blind (Minni) is also sitting on a decent stack. Given the stage of the tournament (Hand 141/150) and my healthy stack size (~100 BB), there is no desperate need to force action with marginal holdings. Standard play here is to fold.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Eight suited, a very playable hand in a blind-versus-blind situation. Since both UTG and the Button have folded, the action is on me. Raising is the preferred strategy here to take initiative and potentially win the pot immediately against Minni, who has a shorter stack of around 74 big blinds. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate for this stack depth and position.

Flash opened to 300 from the small blind; pot is 400 and I need to call 200. 2♦3♠ is a weak hand but calling is cheap — I need ~33% equity (200/(400+200)) to justify the call and versus a likely wide SB opening range this is a reasonable defend. I don’t want to 3-bet bluff out of position with such poor playability and deep stacks (raising to 500 would commit an extra 400 total and put me in tough spots postflop). Plan: call to see the flop, fold to big, polarizing continuation bets if I completely miss, and only continue if I pair, pick up a usable draw, or can exploit a small bet.

On a flop of Q-T-9, I have middle pair (Tens) and a gutshot straight draw (needing a Jack). This board is highly coordinated and hits many hands in the Big Blind's calling range (like J8, KJ, or various two-pair combos). As the pre-flop aggressor, I have the option to continuation bet, but checking here allows me to control the pot size with a medium-strength hand that has good equity but is vulnerable to raises. I intend to check-call most reasonable bet sizes to see if I can improve on the turn or reach showdown cheaply.

I defended 2♦3♠ preflop as a cheap call, but on Q♦ T♥ 9♣ I completely miss — no pair and essentially no useful backdoor equity. Flash was the preflop raiser and checked the flop; that can be weakness but also a sign of slowplay with a made hand. This is a very connected, high board that heavily favors the opener’s range, so a bluff here has low fold equity and is likely to be called or raised by many hands (Q/T/9/Jx, overcards with backdoors, draws). With deep stacks and a small pot (600) I want to control the size of the pot and avoid getting multi-street barreled with zero equity. I’ll check to see how Flash proceeds and will fold to any sizable bet; only continue if I pair, pick up a real draw, or can exploit a very small bet on later streets.

On the turn, the board pairs the Queen (Qd-Th-9c-Qh). I currently have a pair of Tens with an Eight kicker and a gutshot straight draw (needing a Jack). Since the flop went check-check, it is unlikely either of us has a monster hand like a straight or a set of Queens, though Minni could certainly be slow-playing or have a Ten herself. My hand has decent showdown value but is not strong enough to bet for value, as most hands that call me will likely have me beat (like a Jack for the straight, a Queen, or a better Ten). Checking again is the most prudent play to control the pot size and potentially see a cheap river. If Minni bets, I will likely call a reasonable amount given my pair and the gutshot equity.

I defended preflop cheaply with 2♦3♠ but I have nothing on Q♦ T♥ 9♣ Q♥ — no pair and essentially no backdoor equity. Flash (the preflop raiser) checked the flop and checked the turn; that can be weakness but more often with this connected, high board it represents a queen, ten, nine, Jx or a made two-pair/set/hand with showdown value. With deep stacks and a small pot (600) a bluff has very poor fold equity and high downside if called or raised. I’ll check to keep the pot small and preserve my stack; plan is to fold to any meaningful bet on the turn and re-evaluate the river only if a very favorable blank appears and Flash shows more weakness.

On the river, the board is Qd-Th-9c-Qh-6d. I have a pair of Tens (Tc-8c). Both players checked the flop and the turn, which suggests that neither of us has a particularly strong hand like a Queen, a straight (any Jack), or a full house. My pair of Tens has significant showdown value in this spot, but it is not strong enough to bet for value because it's unlikely a worse hand will call. By checking, I can reach a cheap showdown and win against missed draws or lower pairs (like a Nine or a small pocket pair) that Minni might have. If Minni bets, I will evaluate the size, but for now, checking is the most consistent and safe play to maximize my long-term stack.

I have 2♦3♠ on Q♦ T♥ 9♣ Q♥ 6♦ — I missed completely. The pot is small (600) and Flash (preflop raiser) checked the flop and turn, then checked the river. That check can be weakness, but this board heavily favors the opener’s range (Q/T/9, two-pair, sets, Jx, or showdown-value hands). I have no pair, no meaningful blockers, and essentially zero showdown equity. Bluffing here has very poor fold equity and unnecessary downside given deep stacks. Best line is to keep the pot small and check. If Flash bets the river I will fold to any reasonable bet — there’s no price to continue.

I am in UTG position (first to act) with Ace-Eight offsuit (A8o). At a 4-handed table, A8o is a marginal hand. While having an Ace is decent, the kicker is weak, and playing out of position against three opponents (two of whom have large stacks and have been active) is risky. Opening here often leads to being dominated or facing difficult post-flop decisions if called. Given I have a healthy stack of nearly 100BB, there is no need to force action with a mediocre hand from the worst position. The standard and disciplined play is to fold and wait for a better hand or position.

I am on the button with a weak hand (Jack-Four offsuit) in a four-handed game. While it is often tempting to steal from the button, my hand is significantly below the standard opening range. Additionally, Minni in the small blind has a relatively short stack and might shove over an opening raise, and FiveTwo in the big blind has been playing aggressively. With the tournament nearing its end and my chip lead solid, there is no need to play a marginal hand out of position or risk chips on a weak steal attempt.

I have K6s in the small blind with deep stacks (~72BB). Button and UTG folded so I’m heads-up to the big blind (FiveTwo), who defends wide but also 3-bets occasionally. Out of position with a marginal but suited hand, I prefer to keep the pot small and see a flop rather than open a weak steal and risk a 3-bet. I’ll complete to 100 total (call the extra 50) to realize equity and evaluate the flop — be ready to c-bet/aggress or fold to heavy action depending on the board and villain’s response.

SB limp in a 4-handed, short-handed spot is typically a wide/capped range. From the BB we’ll have position postflop (SB acts first), and with a big stack we can apply pressure and win the pot preflop or with a high frequency c-bet on favorable textures. J3o isn’t a value hand, but it’s fine as part of a wide isolation-raise range versus a limp, especially with good playability from position and strong fold equity versus the weakest ranges.

FiveTwo's raise to 400 is a standard-sized, wide heads-up raise. I'm the small blind (dealer) so I will have positional advantage postflop — that makes defending with a marginal suited king attractive. K♠6♠ has reasonable equity vs a wide raising range and playability (backdoor spades, can realize equity well in position). Pot is 500 and I must call 300 more (break-even equity ~37.5%), which is within K6s's expected equity vs a wide range. I could 3-bet as a bluff, but with a marginal hand and deep stacks I'd prefer to keep the pot controllable and realize equity in position. Plan: call to 400 total, see the flop, and proceed based on texture (c‑bet when favorable, fold or check/controlled when heavy resistance).

I have K♠6♠ on 2♠ J♠ A♣ — the nut-flush draw (K-high) and I'm in position. Villain checked to me into a pot of 800 with no bet, so this is an excellent spot to semi-bluff: bet to take the pot down now against hands without a pair and to build the pot when I hit. I have 9 outs to the flush (~35% to complete by the river); a 400 chip bet (½ pot) means a caller must put in 400 to win ~1600, so they need ~25% equity to justify a call — my draw + fold equity makes this +EV. Plan: bet 400 as a semi-bluff; if called I’ll use position to continue on safe turn cards (or barrel spades/low cards) and fold to very large raises that indicate a made Ace/strong two‑pair/set.

Minni’s small donk (400 into 1200) from a limp/call range is wide and can include weak Jx, some Ax, and lots of spade/air stabs. With Jd3s we have a pair of jacks plus a backdoor-ish but live spade draw (3♠ with two spades on board gives us a flush draw to any spade), and we’re getting a very good price (need ~25%). Raising here bloats the pot against a range that often continues with Ax/better Jx, where our kicker is dominated; calling keeps in bluffs, realizes our equity in position, and lets us respond better on the turn based on sizing and runout.

Turn is A♦, which pairs the board and significantly increases the chance my opponent holds an Ace / trips. I currently have K♠6♠ — only a flush draw (9 spade outs), ~19.6% to hit on the river — and no pair. On the flop I semi‑bluffed; my flop plan was to only barrel safe turn cards or spades/low cards. A♦ is neither, so barreling here loses much fold equity vs hands that improved (or already had an Ace) and unnecessarily builds a big pot when I’m mainly a drawing hand. I’m in position, so the lowest‑variance +EV line is to check and control the pot: if FiveTwo checks back we go to the river with our ~19.6% chance; if he bets a small–medium size (≈≤½ pot ≈800) I can call to realize equity; if he fires a pot‑sized or larger bet/raise I’ll respect that as a strong line (likely trips/full houses) and fold. Checking preserves stack, avoids getting exploited by a turn raise, and keeps options for the river.

Turn pairs the Ace, giving us Aces-up (AAJJ) with a weak kicker. Minni’s flop donk was small and wide; her turn check caps a lot of her range to Jx/2x/spade draws and some slow-played Ax/full houses. As the preflop raiser we have a big range advantage on AAJ2, so a small bet is profitable to (1) charge spade draws, (2) get thin value from worse pairs/weak holdings that continue once, and (3) pick up the pot immediately versus her air.

I’ll use a small sizing to keep the pot controlled because many Jx that call will have us outkicked, and I’m comfortable bet/folding if she check-raises (that line heavily concentrates on Ax/full houses).

Turn summary: I have K♠6♠ on 2♠ J♠ A♣ A♦ — only a K‑high flush draw (9 outs, ≈19.6% to hit on the river). Pot = 2150, to call = 550. Pot odds: 550 /(2150+550) ≈ 20.4% required. Purely by pot odds the call is borderline (my direct equity ≈19.6% vs required ≈20.4%), but other factors push this to a call: I’m in position, already invested as a flop semi‑bluff, and Villain’s line (check‑call flop then a small 550 on the paired‑Ace turn) looks fairly wide — containing Ax, Jx, spade draws and floats. Raising to 1100 is an option to try to fold out some hands, but it bloats the pot against hands that beat me (Ax/trips) and risks a shove or a large reraise that I should fold to. Folding surrenders ~19% equity and is too tight. Plan: call 550 to realize my equity in position, evaluate the river — if I hit the flush I’ll extract value; if I miss I’ll fold to a large value bet but can look for a cheap bluff opportunity if Villain shows weakness (checks or makes a very small bet).

River: I missed my K♠6♠ flush and currently have K‑high on 2♠ J♠ A♣ A♦ 4♦. Villain’s line (raise preflop, check‑call my 400 flop bet, a small turn raise to 550, then check river) is wide — it contains Ax, Jx, some small pairs, floats and missed draws. Checking here hands me a losing showdown vs most Ax/Jx holdings that beat me; a small river bluff can fold out a sizeable portion of his marginal range. Pot = 2700, a bet of 900 is ~1/3 pot, so I need ~25% fold equity to break even. That sizing is large enough to fold Jx/pocket pairs/missed floats but small enough to limit my downside if called or raised (I still have ~5.9k behind). Plan: represent a turned Ace/trips and apply pressure with a small bluff — if he folds I scoop the pot, if he calls I lose but I haven’t overcommitted, and if he raises big I’ll fold. Action: bet 900 to try to take the pot down.

River comes 4d, so our hand stays AAJJ (two pair) — no improvement to a boat. Minni’s line (small flop donk, check/call small turn bet, then small river lead 900 into 2700) looks a lot like a block/value bet with a wide range: Ax for thin value, some 2x/medium showdown hands trying to set price, and a non-trivial portion of missed spade draws that decide to stab.

We’re getting excellent pot odds: call 900 to win 3600+900=4500, needing ~16.7% equity. Even if she shows up with Ax a lot, we should still clear that threshold because her limp/call range contains plenty of missed spades and some weaker showdown hands that take this sizing. Raising is poor: worse hands rarely call, while Ax/full houses snap and some Jx can even have us outkicked.

So we take the cheap showdown and call.

I am in the UTG position with 8s 3c, which is a very weak hand. Given that there are only a few hands remaining in the tournament and I have a significant stack lead, there is no strategic reason to enter the pot with trash cards from early position. My priority is to protect my chips and avoid unnecessary variance.

I’m on the button with Tc7h and ~50bb effective. Facing only the blinds (pot 150, 100 to call), this hand is marginal but position is a big advantage. A standard 3x open to 300 applies pressure to the blinds, avoids a multiway limp pot, and gives me post‑flop initiative to steal or realize equity in position. If I face a large 3‑bet I can fold this hand; if I get single‑called I’ll play cautiously with positional advantage. Folding is too tight here and just calling surrenders my positional edge, so I open to 300.

Button open is likely wide 4-handed and Minni is the short stack. A♠9♣ is too strong to fold and calling from the SB invites the BB in, putting us OOP multiway. I prefer a value/pressure 3-bet to isolate Minni, leverage our stack advantage, and often win the pot preflop; when called we can still realize well with an ace blocker and decent top-pair potential. If Minni jams, the pot odds will be reasonable and we can evaluate vs her shove range, but the default is to apply pressure now.

I am in the Big Blind with Pocket Sixes (6c 6s). Minni opened from the Button to 300, and FiveTwo 3-bet from the Small Blind to 1000. This is a cold 4-bet situation for me, or a cold call of a 3-bet. While my stack is healthy (98.5 BB), calling 900 chips (roughly 9% of my stack) to set-mine is a marginal to losing play here. I do not close the action, as Minni is still behind me and could 4-bet shove or call, leading to a multi-way pot where 66 plays poorly without a set. The immediate pot odds are not sufficient to justify a call solely for set value given the SPR would be around 4 post-flop, which is awkward for maneuvering. FiveTwo's range as the chip leader 3-betting the SB is likely strong or balanced enough that 66 is well behind. Preserving my stack in this late tournament phase is preferable to taking a high-variance spot out of position relative to the opener (though in position vs SB) with a low pair.

I opened to 300 on the button with Tc7h, then FiveTwo 3‑bet to 1000. Calling 700 into a 1400 pot requires ~33% equity; T7o has poor equity and limited post‑flop playability against a typical 3‑bet range. 4‑betting (min to 1700 or larger) would be a thin bluff with weak blockers and unnecessarily commit chips with a marginal hand. With ~47–50bb effective and plenty of better spots to press my advantage in position, I fold and preserve my stack.

Short-handed (4 players) with J♥9♥ and ~46.7bb: this is a hand I can comfortably open. J9s has playability (jack-high, suited, straight/backdoor potential) and in a short-handed game I want to take the initiative. I’ll open-raise to 2bb (200 total) to deny the blinds free equity and keep the pot manageable. If I face a large 3-bet that commits a significant portion of my stack I’ll fold; against a small/standard 3-bet I may call depending on who 3-bets (more willing to call the Button, fold vs heavy pressure from the blinds) and proceed very cautiously OOP. Postflop plan: c-bet favorable textures, pursue backdoor heart/straight equity, and fold to strong resistance when I miss.

4-handed and on the button getting a great price versus a small 2x open, I want to defend fairly wide with position. K6o is too weak/dominated to bloat the pot with a 3-bet (and I don’t want to face a shove from Minni’s 44bb stack or create a spot where I’m forced to barrel multiple streets with poor playability). Calling keeps the pot manageable, realizes equity in position, and lets me outplay postflop while also avoiding giving the blinds an easy profitable squeeze target with an inflated pot.

I hold 9d 6d in the Small Blind. Minni opened with a min-raise to 200 from UTG, and FiveTwo flat-called on the Button. The price to call is very cheap (150 chips to win a pot that will likely be around 800 if the BB calls), offering excellent pot odds of better than 4:1. Although I will be out of position in a multi-way pot, suited one-gappers have good playability and implied odds deep-stacked, particularly against the button's calling range. Given the low cost (1.5% of my stack) and the need to accumulate chips to catch the leaders in the final hands, I will call and look to hit a strong flop or fold if I miss.

I am holding pocket nines in the big blind, which is a very strong hand in a four-handed game. There was a min-raise from Minni and two callers, creating a multi-way pot. Pocket pairs like nines lose significant value in four-way pots as it becomes much harder to navigate post-flop without hitting a set. This is a classic squeeze situation. By raising here, I can either take down a decent-sized pot immediately or isolate one of the players, making it easier to play the hand post-flop. I'll raise to 1000 to put pressure on the callers and the initial raiser.

I opened to 200 with J♥9♥ and Flash (BB) 3‑bet to 1000. The pot is 1600 and I must call 800, which requires ~33% equity to break even (800/(1600+800)=33%). J9s OOP against a BB 3‑bet range is often dominated and has well under that equity vs a likely value/polarized 3‑bet. There’s also a real risk of a multi‑way pot or facing further aggression, and I have ~44bb effective — no reason to turn a marginal, often dominated hand into a difficult spot. A small 4‑bet is thin and an all‑in is too committal here. Fold and preserve my stack for better spots.

Flash’s squeeze to 1000 over a 2x open + two callers is representing a fairly strong range (even if it contains some bluffs). With K6o I’m dominated by all better kings and many broadways, and I’ll realize equity poorly even in position because I’ll often make second-best top pair and face heavy pressure on many boards. Calling also invites Pro (SB) to continue getting a great price, increasing the chance we go multiway in a bloated pot with a weak offsuit holding. A 4-bet bluff isn’t attractive this deep versus Flash’s stack, as I risk running into a jam or having to play an inflated pot out of line with K6o. Best decision is to cut losses and preserve stack.

I initially called the small raise due to excellent pot odds, but the squeeze to 1000 from the Big Blind changes the situation drastically. Although I only need to call 800 more into a pot of 1600 (2:1 odds), playing a suited one-gapper (9d 6d) out of position against a deep-stacked aggressor is a losing proposition. My realized equity will be poor, and I cannot afford to bleed nearly 10% of my stack on a speculative hand with only 6 hands remaining, especially while trying to maintain my chip lead over the short stack.

4-handed from UTG, A2o is near the bottom of an opening range but still a profitable open with this stack depth: it blocks strong Ax holdings, can win immediately versus blinds, and plays reasonably with initiative (can represent many boards and has some wheel potential). I’ll use a small, standard open size to pressure the blinds (especially the shorter BB) while keeping risk controlled versus a 3-bet from the deeper stacks.

I am on the Button with K4o facing a 2.5 BB raise from FiveTwo in the UTG position. With only 4 players, ranges are wider, but K4o is still a very weak hand to cold call an open raise, even in position. It is often dominated by better Kings (KQ, KJ, KT) and performs poorly against pairs and Aces. Furthermore, the chip leader is in the SB and a short stack is in the BB, increasing the risk of a squeeze or shove behind me. Given there are only 5 hands left and I have a healthy stack, there is no need to get involved in a marginal spot with negative expected value. Folding is the prudent choice.

In Hand #145 of 150, I am currently the chip leader with 23,900, followed closely by FiveTwo with 21,675. Being 4-handed, a King-high suited hand (Ks 6s) in the Small Blind is strong enough to continue against a standard UTG open. FiveTwo raised to 250, and it costs me 200 to call. While K6s isn't strong enough to 3-bet for value against the only other big stack who is opening from under-the-gun, it has sufficient equity and playability to see a flop. I will call to keep the pot manageable and see how the board develops, while also being mindful of the short-stacked Big Blind (Minni) who may choose to shove.

I’m in the big blind with 5♥4♦ facing an UTG open to 250 and the small blind calling. 5-4 offsuit has very poor playability and will be heavily dominated by an UTG opening range; in a multiway pot my equity is well below what’s required. Calling 150 into a 600 pot demands roughly 20% equity to break even, and I’m unlikely to have that here. Raising (min to 400) would be a reckless bluff out of position and needlessly risks a big chunk of my ~43BB stack. Preserve chips and wait for a better spot — fold.

I'm out of position against the pre-flop raiser (FiveTwo) on a paired board (Th-7s-Tc). My King-high with a backdoor flush draw doesn't warrant a bet here. Checking is the standard play to see how the aggressor handles this texture. I'll re-evaluate if they bet, but I'm prepared to fold to significant pressure.

Paired T-high flop (Th7sTc) heavily favors the preflop raiser’s range: I have all the strong Tx (AT/KTs/QTs/JTs/TT) and overpairs that the SB flat often lacks. Flash’s check caps him a bit, and a small range c-bet should fold out a lot of his whiffs/overcards and deny equity while keeping risk low with A-high. I’ll use a small sizing and be ready to give it up versus a raise (most raises here are Tx/boats).

FiveTwo bets 200 into 600 (1/3 pot) on a dry, paired board of Th-7s-Tc. My King-high (Ks 6s) has some showdown value and a backdoor flush draw. I am getting 4:1 pot odds to call, which is sufficient against a wide continuation betting range on this texture. I will call this small bet and see how the turn develops, while remaining cautious of FiveTwo's stack size.

I have improved to a pair of sixes on the turn, giving me two pair (tens and sixes). While this is an improvement, I am out of position against the other major stack, FiveTwo. Checking is the best strategic move here to control the pot size and see how FiveTwo reacts. My hand has showdown value against his potential bluffs or lower pocket pairs, but it remains weak against any Ten or a higher pocket pair. I will check to evaluate the action.

After c-betting small on a paired T-high flop and getting called, Flash’s range is now much more weighted toward some kind of pair (Tx, 7x, 6x, pocket pairs) than pure whiffs. The 6h turn also improves a chunk of his flop-calling hands (76/66/6x), and with A2o I have very little equity. Betting would mostly fold out the hands I already beat (unpaired overcards) and get called by hands that crush me, which is a poor value/bluff setup. Checking back keeps the pot manageable, realizes whatever showdown equity A-high has versus missed broadways, and still allows me to represent strong hands on many rivers if needed.

On the river, the board is Th-7s-Tc-6h-Ah. My final hand is two pair, Tens and Sixes with an Ace kicker (Th-Tc-6h-6s-Ah). While I improved to two pair on the turn, the river Ace is a very significant card for an UTG opening range, which is likely to contain many Ax hands (like AK, AQ, AJ). Additionally, I lose to any pocket pair 77 or higher (77, 88, 99, JJ, QQ, KK), which are also well-represented in FiveTwo's range. Given that I am the chip leader with only 5 hands remaining in the tournament, my priority is to protect my stack and avoid unnecessary risks against the second-largest stack. My hand has some showdown value against missed broadway cards (like KQ or KJ) which now only have a pair of Tens, but those hands are likely to check behind. Checking allows me to reach a cheap showdown or potentially catch a bluff, though I must be wary of any significant value bets from FiveTwo now that the Ace has hit.

River (Th 7s Tc 6h Ah), after SB check-calls flop and both of us check turn, Flash’s range getting here contains a lot of Tx (trips), some slowplays, and a meaningful chunk of Ax floats/backsdoor hands that now improved. With As2d we do make two pair (Aces and Tens), but it’s the weakest possible kicker (7 from the board), so any A8+/broadway ace beats us, and Tx crushes us. If we bet, worse hands (7x/6x/medium pairs) are under heavy pressure from the river Ace and will fold often, while better hands (Tx and higher-kicker Ax) will continue—so betting is high reverse-implied-value. Checking takes the free showdown and realizes our equity versus his non-ace, non-ten hands without exposing ourselves to a raise.

I am holding Qd 8c in UTG position with 4 players remaining. This is an off-suit Queen-high hand which is generally too weak to open from the first position, even in a short-handed game. With a stack of nearly 100 big blinds and approaching the very end of the tournament (Hand 146/150), there is no need to take unnecessary risks with marginal hands that are easily dominated. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

On the button with J7o, I'm facing a fold from the UTG player. My hand is marginal and generally not worth a raise in this spot, especially with the short-stacked Minni in the Small Blind and a fairly active FiveTwo in the Big Blind. However, as it's near the end of the tournament (Hand 146 of 150), I can look to fold and protect my current position and stack, as J7o is unlikely to win a significant pot against two defenders without a very favourable board.

8♥2♦ is one of the weakest starting hands — offsuit, raggy and very unlikely to realize equity out of position. I'm in the small blind against a very deep, capable big blind (FiveTwo) who has shown aggression throughout the tournament, so calling 50 to see a flop will often put me in difficult spots and raising here with 8‑2 is a pure bluff with little fold equity. With ~43BB in a four‑handed late stage, preserving chips and waiting for a real spot is the correct play. Fold and conserve the stack for a better opportunity.

With the tournament nearing its end (hand 147 of 150) and me holding the chip lead at a 4-handed table, I want to maintain aggression. Ace-Five offsuit is a strong enough hand to open from under-the-gun in a short-handed game. Opening here allows me to apply pressure to the shorter stacks, particularly Minni (43 BBs) and Pro (95 BBs), while maintaining my lead over FiveTwo. A standard 3x raise is appropriate.

I’m on the button with T♠7♥ facing an UTG open to 300 in a 4-handed game. UTG’s raise here represents a fairly strong opening range; T7o is weak and frequently dominated. Calling 300 (the pot is 450) requires roughly 40% equity to break even immediately, which this hand doesn’t have vs an UTG range (and the blinds can still come along and make the postflop decision messy). A light 3‑bet would bloat the pot with a marginal holding and risks a 4‑bet or a tough multiway pot. With ~43BB I can wait for a much better spot in position — fold and preserve my stack.

4-handed, Flash’s UTG open is relatively wide and QJo is strong enough to play, but calling from the SB invites the BB in and leaves us out of position without initiative with a hand that can be dominated (AQ/KQ/QT/JT). A small 3-bet here is a good merged/pressure play: we often win preflop, we reduce BB’s ability to realize equity, and when called we can c-bet many boards with range advantage. If Flash 4-bets, we can comfortably fold and avoid high-variance spots.

I am in the Big Blind with [6d Ac]. Flash opened from UTG to 300, and FiveTwo 3-bet from the Small Blind to 1100. My hand, A6o, is significantly dominated by the likely ranges of a UTG opener and an SB 3-bettor (often JJ+, AK, AQ). Cold calling a 3-bet with weak offsuit aces is a long-term losing play, especially given I would be sandwiched or heads-up against a strong range. With only a few hands left in the tournament, preserving chips is better than engaging in a marginal spot with low equity.

FiveTwo's 3-bet from the small blind is a strong signal, especially since we are the two largest stacks at the table. While A5o has an Ace blocker, it plays poorly post-flop in 3-bet pots, especially when we are over 200 BBs deep. Calling 800 more risks a significant portion of my chip lead if I'm forced to fold on later streets or if I'm dominated. With only four hands remaining in the tournament, preserving my lead over FiveTwo is the priority. I will fold and wait for a more advantageous situation.

4-handed table — I should be opening wider from UTG. T9o (Ts9c) is playable in 4-max: connected, decent equity and postflop playability. Limping/calling 100 is too passive and invites multiway action out of position; a standard 3x-ish open applies pressure and gives fold equity. I’ll raise to 300 to take the initiative, keep the pot manageable relative to my ~43BB stack, and discourage callers. Plan: fold to a large isolated 3-bet, call or continue vs single caller and c-bet favorable flops; if multiway, play cautiously OOP and look for realizable equity.

Minni’s UTG 3x open 4-handed is likely fairly strong/tight, and Q3o has poor playability even in position. A 3-bet bluff would need a very high fold frequency to be profitable given the risk, and if Minni continues (calls/jams) we’re forced to abandon the hand. With blinds behind and no compelling isolation spot, the best chip-EV decision is to pass.

I am in the Small Blind with [2d, 4c], a very weak offsuit hand. Minni, the short stack, has raised to 300 from UTG. Given the tightness of UTG ranges, my poor hand strength, and being out of position, I have no incentive to enter this pot. The hand is 148/150, so chip preservation against a likely stronger range is prudent. I will fold.

I have pocket Kings, the second-best starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Minni, who is playing a relatively short stack of about 4,000 chips, raised from UTG. I want to build the pot and maximize value from this premium hand. A 3-bet to 900 is a standard size that puts pressure on Minni and sets up a potential all-in on future streets or if they decide to 4-bet shove. With only a few hands left in the tournament, securing this pot or doubling through a short stack is a high priority.

I opened UTG to 300 with T9o as a standard 3x open in a 4‑max spot. Flash in the big blind 3‑bet to 900 — I must call 600 to continue or raise to at least 1500. Calling 600 into the 1250 pot (final pot ~1850) requires roughly ~32% equity to break even; T9o offsuit is frequently dominated by a BB 3‑bet range (broadways, Ax, mid/high pairs) and plays poorly OOP. A 4‑bet here would be a large polarized move with a weak hand and bad blocker coverage. I’ll fold and preserve my ~40BB stack for better spots (avoid playing a marginal hand out of position against a strong isolation 3‑bet).

4-handed from UTG (effectively hijack), JJ is a premium opening hand. Limping invites multiway and gives up initiative; raising builds the pot for value and helps narrow ranges. I’ll use a slightly larger-than-min open to reduce overcalls from the blinds while still keeping worse pairs/broadways in and making it easier to call off if Minni (39bb) or Pro (95bb) shoves/3-bets too wide.

Current context: Hand 149 of 150. 4 players left. I am on the Button with 95bb (9500 chips). FiveTwo (UTG/CO) opens to 2.75bb. 

Hand Analysis: Pocket Sevens (7d 7h) is a strong starting hand in 4-handed play, well ahead of a standard Cutoff opening range. However, it is vulnerable to overcards and re-raises. 

Strategic Decision: 
1. Folding is too passive; the hand has positive expected value.
2. 3-betting might isolate the opener, but it bloats the pot against a chip leader who covers me easily, and 77 plays tricky in large pots without hitting a set.
3. Calling is the most balanced line. It allows me to use my position (Button) to navigate post-flop, keeps the pot manageable, and gives me a good price to set-mine or call down on favorable boards. Given the deep stacks (everyone >35bb), flatting is standard to realize equity without over-committing pre-flop.

With A3o in the Small Blind facing a raise from FiveTwo (UTG) and a call from Pro (Button), I am out of position against two active players. Although the pot odds are tempting, A3o performs poorly multi-way and is frequently dominated by the raising ranges of UTG. Given that this is the penultimate hand of the tournament and I have a significant stack to protect, I will choose to fold this marginal hand to avoid unnecessary volatility against the other big stack.

I have KK in the big blind facing an UTG open to 275 with the button calling — a premium hand and a spot where I want to 3-bet to build the pot and isolate. The min-raise (450) is too small and leaves me in a multiway cheap pot where overcards and draws can reduce KK's equity. With ~39 BB effective I’ll raise to a size that gets fold equity and extracts value from worse aces/pairs while still allowing callers: raise to 1500 total. I’m prepared to call or shove over a very large 4-bet given KK’s strength; if called I’ll proceed postflop looking to charge draws and get value.

We opened JJ UTG 4-handed and now face a BB 3-bet to 1500 from Minni who only has ~2525 total. With her leaving ~1k behind, her sizing is effectively a commitment raise; JJ is well ahead of her stack-off range (many worse pairs and broadways) and we want to (1) get it in for value vs Minni, (2) deny Pro the very attractive price to peel in position, and (3) still keep some flexibility if Pro comes back over the top with only the very top of range.

The cleanest is a small 4-bet: it puts Minni all-in, applies maximum pressure to Pro to fold his flats, and avoids needlessly jamming 200+bb effective into Pro. I’ll use the minimum legal 4-bet size.

It is Hand 149 of 150. After I flat-called the opener, the Big Blind (short stack) squeezed to 1500, and the original opener (FiveTwo) put in a 4-bet to 2725. This action from FiveTwo typically indicates a strong range (TT+, AK) attempting to isolate the short stack and push me out of the pot. My hand, pocket sevens, performs poorly against a 4-bet isolation range. Calling 2450 more chips (about 25% of my remaining stack) to set-mine is not profitable given the low Stack-to-Pot Ratio post-flop and the high likelihood of being dominated. Preserving my healthy stack for the final hand is the prudent play.

I’m on KK in the BB. Action: UTG (FiveTwo) opened to 275, I 3-bet to 1500 and he 4-bet to 2725. Pot is 4550 and it costs me 1225 to call, leaving me ~1300 behind (I have 2525 left). Folding is not an option with KK. With an effective stack around 25 BB and facing a narrow, value-heavy 4-bet range (AA, KK, AK, QQ...), the correct play is to commit now. Shoving (raising to my full 4025 total commitment) maximizes fold equity vs hands like AKo and medium pairs, isolates the 4-bettor, and avoids tough post‑flop decisions where I’d be pot‑committed anyway. I’ll shove all‑in to get maximum value and deny equity to drawing/bluff candidates.

Minni’s 5-bet is effectively all-in for 4025 total and Pro has already folded, so this is a heads-up decision with very strong pot odds: we’re calling 1300 to win 7075+1300 = 8375 (~15.5% required equity). Pocket Jacks are far ahead of any reasonable short-stack jam range here (all pairs, broadways, some Ax), and folding would be a massive EV mistake. We call and realize our equity.

This is the final hand (150/150). I am in UTG with K6o, a weak hand. Stack sizes are: Flash ~23.5k, FiveTwo ~18.8k, Me ~9.2k, Minni ~8.3k. I am currently in 3rd place, significantly behind 2nd but only about 900 chips ahead of Minni in 4th. Opening with K6o risks losing chips and potentially falling to 4th place if things go wrong, while the upside of moving up to 2nd is unlikely given the large chip gap. The prudent play is to fold to preserve my current standing.

It is the final hand of the tournament and I am currently the chip leader with 23,500 chips. My closest rival is FiveTwo with 18,800. Holding King-Jack offsuit on the button in a four-handed game is a very strong starting position. While my primary goal is to maintain my lead, the objective is to 'maximize stack size'. A standard opening raise to 300 allows me to pick up the blinds and antes or play a pot in position against the blinds. If faced with a large three-bet or an all-in from FiveTwo, I can safely fold to preserve my tournament lead while still having attempted to increase my total stack.

Facing a button open to 300 from the small blind with A♣7♠. This is a defendable hand — ace-blocker and decent showdown potential — and calling keeps pot size manageable while letting me realize equity out of a likely wide BTN opening range in a 4-handed game. To call I need to commit 250 more (total 300), which gives ~35.7% pot odds (250/(450+250)) — reasonable vs a wide range. I avoid a small, postureless 3-bet out of position that bloats the pot; plan is to call, see the flop, and proceed cautiously OOP (fold to heavy action preflop/large 4‑bet, c‑bet selectively or check back on dry boards).

We’re getting a very good price closing the action: 200 more to win a pot of 700 (and it’ll be 900 after we call), so we only need ~22% equity. Even Q6o generally clears that threshold multiway versus a Button open and SB flatting range. A squeeze here doesn’t make sense with this hand (poor playability when called, dominated too often), so the highest chip-EV line is to defend and realize equity postflop, playing mostly check-to-caller on favorable textures and bailing on high-pressure multi-street lines.

Flop: I have top pair A (A♣7♠) on A♠ Q♣ 6♥ in a 3-way pot. Button opened and BB defended — both ranges are wide and include worse A×, Q×, and pocket pairs. I'm out of position, and my kicker is weak, so I don't want to bloat the pot but I do want to extract thin value and deny free cards to unmade hands. A small c-bet (~1/3 pot) accomplishes that: it gets value from worse aces/Qx and folds a lot of pure air, while keeping the pot manageable OOP. Plan: bet 300; if I face a large raise I will strongly consider folding (dominated by better A×), if I get one caller I'll re-evaluate on the turn and pot-control or proceed for value depending on the card.

We flop two pair (Q6) on a very dry A-Q-6 rainbow board versus a small SB donk/probe (300 into 1200). This lead is typically a wide range (many Ax/Qx/6x and some stabs), and we want to start building a pot while we’re far ahead of most of it. Raising also pressures the Button’s position to continue correctly and prevents him from realizing equity cheaply with hands like KJ/JT or random floats. Given Minni’s shorter stack, a medium value-raise sets up a comfortable turn shove line on many runouts without only getting action from the very top of range.

This is the final hand of the tournament. I am currently the chip leader with 22,900 chips (after my pre-flop investment), while my nearest competitor, FiveTwo, has approximately 17,400. On a board of As-Qc-6h, I have King-high with a gutshot straight draw. Minni led out with a bet and FiveTwo raised to 1,200. Given that both players called from the blinds pre-flop and are now showing aggression on an Ace-high board, it is extremely likely one or both of them has at least an Ace or a Queen. Calling here would risk a significant portion of my lead on a low-probability draw (approx. 8.5% to hit the Ten on the turn). To maximize my final stack and secure my position as the tournament leader, the most prudent move is to fold and avoid unnecessary risk.

I have top pair (A♣7♠) with a weak kicker on A♠ Q♣ 6♥. I c‑bet small to 300 to take a stab and fold equity; FiveTwo (BB) check‑called preflop and now raised to 1,200. Given I still have position, decent showdown value, and the price to continue (900 more for a total of 1,200) is reasonable, I’ll call and keep the pot controllable while seeing the turn. Folding top pair to a single raise here is too tight; re‑raising would unnecessarily polarize and bloat the pot versus a likely range of Qx, pocket pairs or draws. Plan: call now, use position on the turn to either extract thin value on safe cards or fold to clear, strong aggression that clearly represents two‑pair/set or a better ace.

Check to control the pot. I have top pair (A♣7♠) with a weak kicker and am out of position after FiveTwo’s big flop check‑raise to 1,200. The turn Q pairs the board and hugely improves the hands that likely raised on the flop (Qx -> trips, AQ/AA/66 -> full houses or quads). Betting now would mostly get called or re‑raised by hands that already beat me while folding the few worse hands I have edge on. Pot is 3,300 and my stack is 6,875, so I don’t want to bloat the pot OOP with a marginal holding. Plan: check and see how FiveTwo acts — call small/medium bets if priced cheaply to the river, but fold to large aggression or a shove (I’m behind to most of his value‑raising range).

Turn pairs the Q and upgrades us to a very strong full house (QQQ66). Minni’s flop donk/call line is heavily weighted to Ax, Qx, and some 6x that will still continue for at least one more street; checking back would miss a lot of value and let her realize equity for free.

I’ll bet a size that (1) gets called by most Ax/Qx, (2) keeps worse hands in, and (3) sets up a clean river jam for her remaining stack. We do need to be aware that only a small part of her range (AA/AQ) beats us, but it’s too narrow to justify slowing down now.

I have A♣7♠ — top pair with a weak kicker on 6♥ A♠ Q♣ (turn Q♠). I c‑bet the flop, FiveTwo check‑raised to 1,200 and I call; on the turn he now bets 2,400 into the pot (5700) and I must call 2,400. That line/sizing is very value‑heavy (Qx that now has trips or a full house, QQ quads, AQ full house, sets) and heavily dominates my hand. Pot odds: I need ~30% equity (2,400 to win a final pot of ~8,100) but versus his polarized/value range my actual equity is well below that. A raise would over‑polarize and commit a large chunk of my stack with a poor kicker, and calling leaves me in a dominated spot facing further aggression. I’ll preserve my stack and fold.
